case_info: C.R No. 204-P of 2022 Abdul Qayum Khattak  Vs  Abu-ul-Hassan & others
remarks:
Order XXI rule 99 & 101 CPC.
The legal framework governing such disputes is comprehensively provided under Order XXI Rules 99 to 103 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, which constitute a complete code for adjudication of resistance or dispossession during execution. The provisions of order XXI rule 97 to 101 are mandatory in nature and that the executing court cannot summarily dispose of such objections without conducting a proper inquiry because of the bar of fresh suit. In accordance with rule 99 of Order XXI where a person other than the judgment-debtor alleges dispossession during execution, the executing court is duty-bound to conduct a full inquiry under Order XXI Rules 99 to 101, C.P.C., and decide the matter conclusively. An executing court is competent to adjudicate questions of title, possession and interest raised by a third party under Order XXI Rule 101, and such determination bars a separate suit. Rule 101 expressly mandates that all questions relating to right, title or interest in, or possession of immovable property arising between the parties shall be determined by the executing court itself, while Rule 103 places an explicit bar on the institution of a separate suit. This statutory scheme leaves no discretion with the executing court to relegate the aggrieved person to another forum. The object of Rules 99 to 103 is to avoid multiplicity of litigation and ensure that disputes arising during execution are finally settled by the executing court itself.

case_info: Eh. Cr.A No. 09-P of 2003 Zafar Baig Bhittani Vs  The State & others
remarks:
A.	Having surveyed the matter in its entirety, we find no escape from the conclusion that amendments, if relied upon, must be examined in light of their object, purpose, and underlying legislative intent, and not as instruments for undoing concluded or substantially advanced judicial processes. Only by such a calibrated approach can this Court meaningfully respond to the contention raised, without unsettling the settled or inventing a principle unknown to law and unheard of in jurisprudence.
B.	Equally instructive is what these judgments do not say. They offer no warrant for the proposition that an amendment lacking retrospective reach, or one enacted after rights have crystallized, may be pressed into service to reopen concluded matters. That silence is neither accidental nor insignificant, it reflects a settled judicial reluctance to disturb vested rights in the absence of a clear legislative mandate.
C.	The legislative intent in this behalf is neither obscure nor capable of competing interpretation. Once such exclusion is provided in clear terms, the interpretative exercise stands significantly narrowed. In this backdrop, those cases which had been finally adjudicated upon by the learned Accountability Court prior to the coming into force of the amendment, even though presently pending in appeal before this Court, partake the character of past and closed transactions insofar as the applicability of the amendment is concerned.

case_info: W.P No. 1081-M of 2022 Muhammad  Sadiq  Vs  Govt of KP
remarks:
Court allowed a writ petition filed by Muhammad Sadiq and Ghani Ur Rahman, retired employees of the Education Department, challenging recoveries made by the respondents based on a withdrawn policy
(Finance Department letter dated 06.11.2001). The court held that the withdrawal of the policy (letter dated 13.01.2000) couldn`t be applied retrospectively to divest vested rights, and the respondents couldn`t
recover benefits already drawn by the petitioners. The court directed the respondents to stop further recoveries, refund amounts already deducted, and treat the Selection Grade granted to the petitioners as
valid for pension and post-retirement benefits.

case_info: Cr.A No. 244-P of 2024 Muhammad Tahir  Vs  Wali Muhammad & another
remarks:
i. 	The real question, therefore, is not whether their testimony should be excluded altogether, but whether they had placed the entire truth before the investigating agency and the trial court, or whether material aspects relating to the genesis of the occurrence were suppressed.
ii. 	Accordingly, the focus of this Court now shifts to examining whether, in the peculiar facts and circumstances of the case, the sentence awarded by the learned trial court meets the ends of justice, or whether any interference is warranted on the question of quantum of punishment.
iii. 	These injuries, taken cumulatively, indicate that at the time of occurrence both sides were actively involved in a free fight. The next question which arises is whether the existence of a free fight entitles the appellant to the extraordinary concession of acquittal, or whether it merely warrants consideration for reduction or modification of sentence.
iv. 	Thus, when the conduct of both sides is assessed in its true perspective, it becomes apparent that the appellant and his co-accused exceeded the limits of any plausible right of self-defence and, instead of restraining themselves, chose to use lethal weapons.

case_info: W.P No. 3756-P of 2025 Private Education Network (PEN)  Vs  Government of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa & others
remarks:
Writ Petition
Held:       Issue / Section of Laws. Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Employees Social Security Act, 2021, Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Private School Regulatory Authority Act, 2017 —It is held that Private Educational Institutions fall within the statutory definition of “establishment” in light of the relevant statutory provisions and settled legal principles. It is an inescapable conclusion that KP-PSRAA, 2017 is confined to the regulation and supervision of Private Schools and have no nexus whatsoever with the provision of social security benefits to employees. The provisions relied upon by learned counsel for the petitioner do not advance the petitioner’s case, as they neither exclude Private Schools from the definition of “establishment” nor bar the application of KP-ESSA, 2021. On the contrary, KP-ESSA, 2021, being a beneficial welfare legislation, is rightly applicable to Private Schools, which undeniably fall within the ambit of “establishment.” Consequently, the point for determination is answered in the affirmative; and it is held that the impugned notification has been validly issued in exercise of statutory authority, and that no illegality or infirmity can be attributed thereto. The writ petition is, therefore, dismissed.
Doctrine of consistency-explained- Judicial discipline and the doctrine of consistency require that this Court should not take a view contrary to its own earlier judgment on the same issue between similarly placed parties, particularly when the earlier decision has not been set aside or suspended by a superior forum.

case_info: W.P No. 4045-P of 2025 Hamid Ullah  Vs  Government of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa & others
remarks:
Writ Petition
Held:          Issue / Section of Laws. Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Child Protection and Welfare Act, 2010, Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Child Protection and Welfare Rules, 2016 —Vires of Rules- The government has the power to frame rules (we would avoid using the word “discretion” for the reason of its being inappropriate for rule making), there could be no escaping from the fact rules made must be in consonance with the aims and objects of the parent law. The impugned amendment plainly falls short of the standards mandated under the parent statute. In this context, it appears entirely misconceived on the part of the respondent-department to suggest that the rules were framed in the so-called “best interest of the state children.” The deletion of clauses (b) and (c), relating to age, educational qualification, and experience, is wholly unjustified and cannot be reconciled with the object and purpose of the Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Child Protection and Welfare Act, 2010. Equally unpersuasive is the contention that widening the scope of appointments to include persons from across the country enhances the selection process. No evidence has been placed on record to show that a comprehensive country-wide search was undertaken, nor could it be realistically expected that such persons, from outside the Province, would possess the requisite familiarity and hands-on experience necessary to discharge the complex functions entrusted to non-official members of the Institute Management Committee.

case_info: Cr.M BBA No. 893-A of 2025 Amanat Shah  Vs  The State
remarks:
(a) It is a well-settled principle of law that the grant of pre-arrest bail is an
extraordinary and exceptional relief and is not to be allowed as a matter of routine.
The underlying purpose of this relief is to protect the liberty of an innocent person in
rare and exceptional circumstances where the Court is satisfied that criminal
proceedings have been initiated with mala fide intent, ulterior motives, or for the
purpose of harassment. The consistent judicial exposition on the subject makes it
clear that pre-arrest bail is not meant to shield the guilty from the due process of
law, nor to obstruct or frustrate a lawful investigation. An applicant seeking such
relief carries a heavy burden to demonstrate that the conditions contemplated
under Section 497 of the Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898 (“Cr.P.C.”), are
satisfied—namely, that there are reasonable grounds to believe that he is not guilty
of the alleged offence, or that the matter calls for further inquiry. Bald or
unsubstantiated allegations of false implication, unsupported by prima facie
material on record, are insufficient to warrant the discretionary indulgence of the
Court.
(b) Section 498-A, Cr.P.C. imposes a statutory restriction and prescribes a
mandatory precondition for the exercise of the Court’s jurisdiction in matters of pre-
arrest bail, namely the personal appearance of the petitioner before the Court. In
the absence of such appearance, the Court lacks the legal authority to grant pre-
arrest bail, rendering the petition liable to dismissal on this ground alone. This
requirement reflects the legislative intent to prevent abuse of the process of law
and to ensure that the extraordinary concession of pre-arrest bail is not availed by
absconders or individuals seeking to evade the jurisdiction of the Court. However,
the provision does permit a narrow exercise of judicial discretion where a cogent,
plausible, and satisfactory explanation for non-appearance is duly placed on
record.
(c) Any practice whereby police authorities treat the mere filing of a petition before
the High Court as an implied stay or restraint on arrest—despite the rejection of
pre-arrest bail by a competent court—reflects a fundamental misunderstanding of
the scope and purpose of pre-arrest bail. This relief is designed as an exceptional
safeguard against arbitrary or mala fide arrest, applicable only where
circumstances clearly justify such protection. Once a competent court has declined
pre-arrest bail, it has necessarily concluded that no exceptional circumstances exist
and that arrest is lawful and required to facilitate an effective investigation.
Permitting the mere act of filing a subsequent petition to operate as a de facto stay
would render judicial determinations nugatory, undermine the administration of
justice, frustrate prompt and fair investigation, and encourage abuse of process by
enabling accused persons to evade arrest indefinitely without legal sanction.
Judicial orders remain binding and enforceable unless expressly suspended or set
aside by a competent court. Interim protection is neither automatic nor implied; it
must be specifically sought and expressly granted. In the absence of such an order,
the refusal of pre-arrest bail remains fully operative and must be implemented
promptly and in good faith by the investigating authorities. It is, therefore, the
foremost duty of the police to protect society and to apprehend fugitives who
remain at large despite the rejection of their pre-arrest bail applications by the
Sessions Courts or High Courts.

case_info: C.R No. 592-P of 2019 Syed Mutahir Shah  Vs  Syed Wakeel Badshah
remarks:
(i)	 In pre-emption cases, a decree is passed conditionally in line with the provision of Order XX, Rule 14, CPC, whereby, a pre-emptor is required by the Court to pay the purchase money on or before a date fixed in the decree and as such, after performance of his part of obligation of payment of purchase money, the judgment debtor/vendee is bound to deliver possession of the purchased property on the same day or soon thereafter and simultaneously, from that date of payment of purchase money, the revenue authorities are bound to enter the decree in the revenue record, however, in case of failure of the judgment debtor/ vendee to deliver the possession or the revenue authority to enter the decree in the revenue record, as the case may be, the decree-holder would require to file the execution petition within three (03) years from the date of such refusal of the judgment debtor or of the revenue officials, as the case may be.
(ii)	Generally, the limitation period for filing of an execution petition is to be reckoned from the date of judgment of the appeal court and as such, even on the aforesaid yardstick, the execution petition in the present case was time barred as the original judgment and decree was passed in favor of the respondent/plaintiff by the trial court on 26.06.2009 and the appeals thereagainst were dismissed on 28.02.2011, whereas, the instant execution petition has been filed on 05.07.2014 and as such, it was beyond three (03) years of period by 128 days.
(iii)	There is no cavil that if the appeal court or the revisional court vary, modify or alter the decree then the decree-holder can file a second execution petition, even if, he had already filed an execution petition before any alteration, modification or variation by the appeal or the revisional court but such, subsequent application must be made within six 06) years from the date of judgement/ decree.

case_info: Cr.A No. 294-P of 2023 The State  Vs  Malik Sadam Jan
remarks:
1.	Where the accused was fined Rs. 200,000/- by a Special Magistrate in a purported summary trial under the Price Control and Prevention of Profiteering and Hoarding Act, 1977 read with the COVID-19 (Prevention of Hoarding) Act, 2020, the order was appealable, as the fine exceeded the limit prescribed under section 14-A, Cr.P.C. In appealable summary cases, section 264, Cr.P.C. mandatorily requires recording of the substance of evidence and a reasoned judgment. Mere endorsement of \"pleads guilty\" or a vague handwritten statement on a printed fine-deposit proforma does not constitute a lawful confession, nor can it substitute statutory requirements of trial and judgment.
2. Failure of the Special Magistrate to produce any complaint, show-cause notice, record of proceedings, statement of accusation, substance of evidence, or a formal order of conviction renders the entire proceedings void and vitiated. Summary trial does not dispense with the right of fair trial and due process, which must be observed even where special statutes exclude certain procedural limitations. Article 10-A of the Constitution applies to all proceedings, including summary trials, and mandates notice, opportunity of defence, lawful procedure, and adjudication by a competent forum.
3.	Held: The Sessions Judge committed no illegality or jurisdictional error in entertaining and allowing the appeal and ordering refund of the fine. Criminal revision dismissed.

case_info: B.A No. 572-B of 2025 Naqib Ullah  Vs  The State etc
remarks:
Bail—Post-arrest bail—Dishonour of cheque—Section 489-F, P.P.C.—Scope—Non-prohibitory offence—Principles.
Offence under section 489-F, P.P.C. carries maximum punishment of three years’ imprisonment
and does not fall within the prohibitory clause of section 497(1), Cr.P.C.; therefore, grant of bail
is a rule and refusal an exception. Bail in such offences can only be declined in extraordinary and
exceptional circumstances, which must be specifically shown by the prosecution or complainant.
Dishonoured cheque—Security / guarantee cheque—Business transaction—Further inquiry.
Contention that amount was advanced as investment/share in business venture and cheque was
issued as guarantee-cum-security; absence of prima facie dishonest intention at the time of
issuance of cheque; determination of nature of transaction and purpose of cheque requires
evidence—case calls for further inquiry within the meaning of section 497(2), Cr.P.C.
Held:
In absence of exceptional circumstances justifying refusal of bail and keeping in view settled law
laid down by the Supreme Court regarding non-prohibitory offences, accused entitled to
concession of post-arrest bail, subject to furnishing of surety.

case_info: WP No. 9645-P of 2025 M/s Khyber Tobacco Company Ltd etc   Vs  Federation of Pakistan through Federal Secty. MOF Islamabad
remarks:
It is evident from the notice dated 12.12.2025 that the Assistant Commissioner was merely suspecting that duty-paid unmanufactured tobacco might be used in the manufacturing of cigarettes. Such suspicion, however, cannot be equated with the statutory requirement of “reasons to believe” as provided under sub-rule (6) of Rule 28-A ibid, for sealing the manufacturing unit of the petitioner. 
	Article 18 of the Constitution of the Islamic Republic of Pakistan, 1973, recognizes the right to freedom of business and profession, subject to certain regulations. Such regulations, as well as any executive action or intervention in lawful trade, are obviously subject to judicial review by the superior courts. Any misuse or excess of authority by the executive can be corrected by a constitutional court through the issuance of appropriate directions, ensuring that the lawful trade conducted by citizens of Pakistan is not unnecessarily hindered.
	A constitutional petition is competent if an order is passed by a court or authority in excess of its jurisdiction, even where the remedy of appeal or revision against such order is available, depending upon the facts and circumstances of each case.

case_info: W.P No. 570-P of 2025 Awais Khan  Vs  Govt of KPK and others.
remarks:
Appointment of a Member of the Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Public Service Commission made by the Chief Secretary without approval of the Governor held that:-
1.	A petition for quo warranto is maintainable at the instance of any member of the public, irrespective of personal grievance. Strict rules of locus standi do not apply where the legality of occupation of a public office is questioned.
2.	Under section 3(3) of the Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Public Service Commission Ordinance, 1978, the power to appoint the Chairman and Members of the Commission vests exclusively in the Governor. Neither the Chief Secretary nor the Provincial Government possesses statutory competence to make such appointments.
3.	Where a statute prescribes a specific manner for performing an act, it must be performed strictly in that manner, or not at all. Any departure renders the act void ab initio.
4.	Even if the appointee otherwise fulfills the qualifications, an appointment made by an incompetent authority does not acquire legality. Illegality cannot be cured by eligibility, past practice, or executive convenience.
5.	Where executive action is ultra vires the Constitution or statute, courts are duty-bound to strike it down to preserve constitutional supremacy and institutional independence.

case_info: W.P No. 6634-P of 2024 Muhammad Kamal  Vs  Government of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa through Chief Secretary, Peshawar and others
remarks:
The aim and object of the statutory period of 90 days before approaching the Service Tribunals is that the grievances of the civil servants are firstly to be considered at the departmental level and where such matters are not decided in the period provided by law, only then such matters are to be brought before the Service Tribunal concerned, irrespective of the fact whether the issues relate to the transfer orders or other terms and conditions of service of the civil servants; besides, the Service Tribunals have all the powers to grant interim relief as well as final orders in the matters.
The recent emphasis of the Courts as well as of the Government is to deliberate the matters firstly out of the Courts and in case, parties still find themselves aggrieved of any order then to approach the proper forums in the matter. The aim and object of prescribing period of 90 days’ wait is mainly for the purpose that the issue relating to the terms and conditions of service of a civil servant is to be deliberated at the departmental level and it can, under no circumstances, be considered to be violative of Article 10A of the Constitution of Islamic Republic of Pakistan, 1973, because, after the decision of the departmental authorities or on completion of 90 days, such civil servant gets a further fair trial before the Service Tribunal concerned and under no circumstances, Article 10A debars the departmental authorities from deliberating the matters at the departmental level. Even otherwise, for maintaining discipline in the Departments, the Departments shall be given space firstly to decide the matters relating to civil servants at the departmental level. We are, therefore, of the view that Section 4 of “the Act”, under no circumstances, is violative of Article 10A of the Constitution of Islamic Republic of Pakistan, 1973.

case_info: C.R No. 362-P of 2014 Mian Saeed Ullah Jan etc  Vs  Mst. Nusrat Begum etc
remarks: If a decree or order adversely affects a person, he may file an appeal thereagainst even if he is not a party in the original proceedings.

case_info: C.R No. 215-P of 2025 Ansar Ullah  Vs  Rafi Ullah
remarks: Decision of a Jirga may be relied upon as a piece of evidence, even if same has not been declared as a rule of Court under section 14 of Arbitration Act, 1940.

case_info: C.R No. 1364-P of 2025 Pakistan Telecommunication Company Ltd  Vs  KP Economic Development & Managment Company through its CEO etc
remarks:
i.	If one public functionary has a grievance against another public functionary, then in such cases, grant of an interim injunction has to be regulated on the touchstone of Section 56 of The Specific Relief Act, 1877, and the grant of an interim injunction or otherwise would not be weighed on the yardstick/criteria regulating cases of private parties.
ii.	While regulating an interim injunction in cases where public functionaries are parties, the main consideration is that as to whether the public functionary against whom an interim relief has been sought has acted within its lawful authority or not, and if it has acted within its lawful authority then such public functionary could not be restrained from performance of its lawful duty as such public functionary too acts in the larger interest of the public at large.
iii.	For grant of temporary injunction, three essential ingredients i.e. prima facie case, balance of inconvenience and the factum of irreparable loss in case of refusal of interim injunction has to be established by the parties seeking an interim injunction.

case_info: W.P No. 5296-P of 2022 “Employees Old Age Benefits Institution through Regional Head Peshawar  VS Peshawar Model School for Boys-V through its Director”
remarks:
i. When a special law has provided a complete and comprehensive forum/fora for the redressal of grievance(s) for their subjects and when a grievance(s) also falls and covered under the powers and functions of such Authority(ies)/Forum/Fora then an aggrieved person has to approach to those Authority(ies)/Forum/Fora by availing the available remedies. 
ii. Under The Employees Old Age Benefit Act, 1976, a complete and comprehensive mechanism has been provided for the redressal of the grievance(s) of an aggrieved person as provided under Sections 33, 34 and 35 of the ibid Act. The ibid Act has also provided the functions and powers of those Authority(ies), therefore, when a grievance of an aggrieved person falls within the domain of such Authority(ies) then such an aggrieved person has to approach to the Authority(ies) established thereunder and such aggrieved person cannot directly approach to the Civil Court by passing such forum/fora as allowing such practice would amount to defeat the very intention of the legislatures who have provided such forum/fora by enacting necessary legislation / as well as by appointing public functionaries.
iii. The jurisdiction of the Civil Court would not only be barred in cases of express bar but the same would also be barred impliedly and grievance(s) which fall under the mandate of an Authority under the special law then such cases would prima facie attract the implied bar.

case_info: Cr.MiSc. BA No. 562-B of 2025 Ijaz Khan  Vs  State etc
remarks: Held: Bail sought on the ground of statutory delay in conclusion of trial --- Third proviso to section 497 (1) Cr.P.C. ---- The petitioner’s earlier bail application was dismissed on merits, including a subsequent application filed on statutory grounds of delay in conclusion of trial. Petitioner remained in continuous detention for over two and half years, without seeking any adjournment. Prosecution cited ten witnesses, but only four were examined. Delay was due to external factors (e.g. polio campaign, non-production of accused from jail, absence of Presiding officer) not attributable to the accused. No evidence suggested the petitioner is a hardened or dangerous criminal, previously convicted for a capital offence, or involved in terrorism. Continued detention without trial would amount to abuse of the judicial process. The Court held that the petitioner is entitled to bail on the ground of statutory delay in prosecution.

case_info: W.P No. 84-B of 2024 Fahim Ullah  Vs Mst. Palwasha and others
remarks:
Family Law—Dower (Haq Mehr)—Nature of dower—Execution
proceedings—Payment in installments—Judicial discretion—Financial capacity of
judgment-debtor—Balance of equities—Compassionate approach in family
matters. Dower (Haq Mehr) is an obligatory debt upon the husband and is
enforceable through legal process; however, execution proceedings are meant to
secure compliance with a decree and not to punish the judgment-debtor.
Discretion to allow payment of decretal amount in installments is judicial in nature
and must be exercised fairly, reasonably, and equitably, keeping in view the
financial capacity of the judgment-debtor, the nature of the decretal amount, and
the likelihood of effective satisfaction of the decree within a reasonable time.
Dismissal of an application for installments, without fixing a reasonable amount
after balancing the rights of the decree-holder with the paying capacity of the
judgment-debtor, amounts to non-exercise of discretion in accordance with law.
In family matters, courts are expected to adopt a compassionate and pragmatic
approach so as to avoid undue hardship and ensure effective enforcement of
rights.
Writ Petition allowed and the Petitioner directed to pay the decretal
dower amount in monthly installments of Rs.25,000/-. Arrears accrued from 01-09-
2023 to 31-12-2025 shall be cleared at the same rate, either in lump-sum or as
calculated accordingly, and thereafter monthly payments shall continue till full
satisfaction of the decree. In case of two consecutive defaults, the respondent
shall be at liberty to seek enforcement of the decree through coercive process in
accordance with law. Execution petition to proceed accordingly.

case_info: W.P No. 3343-P of 2025 Muhammad Khan & others  Vs  Federation of Pakistan through Federal Secretary, Islamabad
remarks:
Writ Petition
Held:          CITIZENSHIP LAW – PAKISTAN CITIZENSHIP ACT, 1951 (S. 14(1)) – DUAL NATIONALITY – CESSATION OF CITIZENSHIP – FOREIGN PASSPORT: Possession of a foreign passport, while relevant, does not, by itself, conclusively establish cessation of Pakistani citizenship under Section 14(1) of the Pakistan Citizenship Act, 1951. A comprehensive factual inquiry is mandated to ascertain whether an individual is, at the same time, a national of another country under the laws of that specific country, thereby triggering cessation of Pakistani citizenship.
CITIZENSHIP LAW – PAKISTAN CITIZENSHIP ACT, 1951 (S. 14(1A) & 14(4)) – MANDATORY EXCEPTIONS: The statutory exceptions provided in Section 14(1A) of the Pakistan Citizenship Act, 1951 (relating to minors/persons under 21 years of age), and Section 14(4) of the Pakistan Citizenship Act, 1951 (concerning married females), are mandatory provisions that must be scrupulously addressed and applied by all competent forums dealing with matters of citizenship cessation.
CITIZENSHIP LAW – PAKISTAN CITIZENSHIP ACT, 1951 (S. 14 & S. 14A) – RENUNCIATION OF CITIZENSHIP: Renunciation of citizenship under Section 14A of the Pakistan Citizenship Act, 1951, constitutes a distinct legal concept from the cessation of citizenship as stipulated in Section 14 of the same Act; Section 14A exclusively governs voluntary acts of renunciation.
NADRA ORDINANCE, 2000 (S. 18) – NADRA POWERS – IMPROPRIATE ACTION – PROCEDURAL SAFEGUARDS: Actions undertaken by the National Database and Registration Authority (NADRA) under Section 18 of the NADRA Ordinance, 2000, including the impoundment or cancellation of national documents, necessitate strict adherence to fundamental procedural safeguards such as issuance of formal notice, provision of a clear show-cause opportunity, and preservation of statutory appeal rights. Indefinite blocking of national documents without due process is impermissible and unlawful.
CONSTITUTION OF PAKISTAN – PROCEDURAL DUE PROCESS – NATURAL JUSTICE – FAIR HEARING – SPEAKING ORDER: Any adverse declaration or decision affecting a citizen\'s rights, whether issued by the Federal Government or NADRA, mandates the rigorous observance of procedural fairness and the principles of natural justice, including the fundamental right to a fair hearing. Such adverse declarations must be communicated through a reasoned, speaking order that specifically addresses all presented evidence and any invoked statutory defenses.
CITIZENSHIP LAW – VERIFICATION BOARDS – TIMELINES – SPEAKING ORDERS: Verification Boards tasked with adjudicating citizenship matters are under an obligation to render their decisions within forty-five (45) days from the date the matter is formally referred to them. Furthermore, all such decisions must be conveyed through reasoned speaking orders, detailing the basis for the determination.
CITIZENSHIP LAW – PAKISTAN CITIZENSHIP ACT, 1951 (S. 19) – CASES OF DOUBT: The statutory remedy provided under Section 19 of the Pakistan Citizenship Act, 1951, remains available and applicable for individuals seeking a definitive resolution in cases of doubt regarding their citizenship status.
(Writ Petition is Disposed of)

case_info: B.A No. 539-B of 2025 Atiq-ur-Rehman  Vs  The State etc
remarks:
Head-note
Criminal Procedure Code (V of 1898) — S.497(1), third proviso — Penal Code (XLV of
1860), Ss.302/34 — Constitution of Pakistan, Art.10-A — Bail, post-arrest — Statutory bail
— Delay in conclusion of trial — Prolonged incarceration — Non-production of prosecution
witnesses — Delay not attributable to accused — Right to fair and expeditious trial.
The petitioner, charged in a murder case, sought post-arrest bail on statutory grounds after his
earlier bail applications on merits had been declined by the trial Court as well as the High Court.
The petitioner had remained in custody since his arrest and, despite framing of charge, the
prosecution examined only three witnesses over a long span of time. Material witnesses,
including the complainant and the investigating officer, were yet to be examined. The order-
sheets revealed repeated adjournments owing to non-appearance of prosecution witnesses, strikes
by the local bar, official engagements of police officials, and proceedings relating to co-accused,
none of which were attributable to any act or omission on the part of the petitioner.
Held: Where an accused has suffered prolonged incarceration and the prosecution fails to
conclude its evidence within a reasonable time for reasons not attributable to the accused,
continued detention becomes punitive rather than preventive. Such a situation squarely attracts
the third proviso to S.497(1), Cr.P.C, which is reinforced by the constitutional guarantee of fair
trial and due process under Article 10-A of the Constitution. The seriousness of the charge, by
itself, cannot defeat the statutory right to bail in the face of inordinate and unexplained delay in
the trial. Consequently, the petitioner was entitled to the concession of post-arrest bail on
statutory grounds.
Result: Petition allowed; post-arrest bail granted on statutory ground.

case_info: B.A No. 559-B of 2025 Qari Farhad  Vs  The State etc.
remarks:
Headnote
Criminal Procedure Code (V of 1898) — S.497(2) — Penal Code (XLV of 1860), Ss.302, 324,
148 & 149 — Bail, post-arrest — Further inquiry — Delay in F.I.R. — Night occurrence —
Absence of source of light — Doubtful identification — General role — Single injury —
Forensic inconsistency — Benefit of doubt at bail stage.
The petitioner, nominated as an accused in an F.I.R. registered for the offence of murder, sought
post-arrest bail after the same was declined by the Court below. The occurrence was stated to
have taken place at night, whereas the F.I.R. was lodged with an unexplained delay of about one
hour and fifty minutes. The F.I.R. was silent regarding any source of light at the place of
occurrence and the site-plan reflected a considerable distance between the complainant party and
the alleged assailants, rendering identification doubtful. Only a general role of firing was
attributed to the accused persons, while the post-mortem report revealed a single entry wound on
the body of the deceased, thereby not supporting the prosecution version of indiscriminate firing
by multiple assailants. Furthermore, the forensic report opined that all recovered crime empties
were discharged from one and the same weapon, which was inconsistent with the allegation of
several armed accused.
Held: The cumulative effect of the unexplained delay in lodging the F.I.R., doubtful
identification in the absence of any stated source of light, general and non-specific attribution of
role, lack of corroboration by medical evidence, and the forensic finding indicating use of a single
weapon, prima facie brings the case of the petitioner within the ambit of further inquiry as
contemplated under S.497(2), Cr.P.C. At the bail stage, such circumstances cannot be ignored,
and the heinous nature of the offence alone is not sufficient to deny bail where the prosecution
version itself is open to serious doubt. Consequently, the petitioner was admitted to post-arrest
bail.
Case law referred:
• Noorzali v. The State and others (2021 P Cr. L J Note 12);
• Saeed Ullah and others v. The State and another (2023 SCMR 1397);
• Sikandar Hayat v. The State and another (2022 SCMR 198).
Result: Petition allowed; bail granted.

case_info: Cr.A No. 755-P of 2024 Sabir Shah  Vs  Amjid Khan etc
remarks:
A.	The Investigating officer noted the trail of blood in the street, and if the male deceased had received a gunshot wound inside the premises, it is unlikely that he could have travelled outside without leaving a corresponding blood trail within, therefore, these are the essential circumstances which this Court must give due weight to when determining the guilt, involvement, or innocence of the appellant.
B. 	The circumstances suggest that an intruder was found within the house of brother of the appellant, so in such a scenario, it would be natural for the family members, including the appellant, to react instinctively. The tumultuous conditions prevailing at the time evidently clouded the perceptions of the witnesses, who presumed that the male deceased had been fired upon inside the house, however, had he in fact been shot within the house and thereafter physically subdued, he would not reasonably have been able to flee.
C. 	Thus, even if the appellant is conceded to have engaged in physical contact with the male deceased, such behavior, given the provocative circumstances, cannot fasten upon him the liability for homicide. There is no basis for applying the doctrine of common intention, for the evidence does not reveal any shared design to kill, instead, it discloses an impulsive reaction to the presence of an intruder.
D.	 It is only natural that such harrowing circumstances, coupled with the intense physical struggle between the accused and the male deceased, would have severely disturbed their mental composure. In that state of shock, they might well have believed that the firing which ultimately struck the male deceased occurred inside the house, when in fact it transpired outside. These traumatic conditions could have clouded their recollection of certain details, but they do not render the witnesses untrustworthy.
E. 	Though the two incidents, one occurring inside the house and the other outside, are undoubtedly connected and arise out of the same underlying motive, but the evidence does not establish that the appellant acted with the same degree of intent, brutality, or interest as the other accused. The witnesses did not attribute to him the role of firing, either inside or outside the house, nor does the investigative evidence, including the absence of a blood trail inside the house, support the conclusion that the male deceased was shot therein.
F. 	We cannot turn a blind eye to his role in the occurrence or to his participation therein. However, upon a careful appraisal of the attending circumstances, we are unable to hold him responsible for sharing any common intention with the co-accused. At best, his liability is confined to the extent of his individual participation in the physical altercation. Even in that respect, he cannot be visited with penal consequences, as it was the deceased who forcibly intruded into the house, and such unlawful intrusion provoked a sudden free fight, which compelled him to act in the manner alleged.

case_info: C.R No. 59-B of 2025 Mst. Noomana  Vs  Director General NADRA
remarks:
---Ss,42 & 54--- Suit for declaration and injunction---Correction of name and Date of
birth---in NADRA record---Trial Court as well as Lower Appellate Court dismissed suit
and appeal filed by petitioner / plaintiff--- The learned appellate Court placed reliance
primarily on the NADRA record, while discarding the SSC on the ground that the
petitioner had passed the SSC examination in a private capacity. Ordinarily, NADRA
record does carry evidentiary weight; however, the same is not conclusive, nor
immune from judicial scrutiny, particularly where cogent and reliable documentary
evidence to the contrary is available on record.
The computerized marriage registration certificate of the petitioner’s
parents was available on the file. According to the said document, the marriage of the
petitioner’s parents was solemnized on 09-10-1991. If this document is taken into
consideration, the recording of the petitioner’s date of birth as 15-07-1990 in the
NADRA record becomes factually impossible and unnatural, as it precedes the
marriage of her parents. This glaring contradiction strikes at the very credibility of the
NADRA entry regarding the petitioner’s date of birth. Significantly, the aforesaid
marriage registration certificate was neither objected by the respondents through any
written document nor the same was challenged or objected during the course of
arguments by the learned Law Officer NADRA.
High Court directed respondents / defendants to correct the name and date of
birth of petitioner / plaintiff in NADRA record. Revision was allowed, in
circumstances.

case_info: W.P No. 96-M of 2025 Tahir Zeb  Vs  Mst. Bakht Mina Shah Alias Maham & others
remarks:
Court disposed of a writ petition filed by Tahir Zeb, challenging the
decision of the Additional District Judge-II/Izafi Zilla Qazi, Swat, which
maintained the dissolution of his marriage. The court held that while
the dissolution of marriage between the parties was not reversible
due to the expiry of the iddat period, the basis of dissolution (cruelty
or khula) required judicial determination. The trial court had granted
an ex parte decree of dissolution on the ground of cruelty, but the
remaining decree was set aside. The High Court directed the trial
court to afford both parties a fair opportunity to produce evidence on
the issue of cruelty and decide the mode of dissolution and related
claims (including dower) accordingly.

case_info: C.R No. 68-P of 2022 Asfandyar Khan and another  Vs  Mardan Shah and others
remarks:
i.	Where a decree has been passed with respect to a particular subject matter (plot), then any subsequent suit with respect to the same subject matter (plot) would not be maintainable unless and until the earlier decree is set aside through filing of a 12(2) petition.
ii.	Where a plaint filed by a party has been rejected under Order VII Rule 11 CPC, and when that order has attained finality, then any subsequent suit with respect to the same subject matter and on the same cause of action would not be maintainable.
iii.	Where a party had filed successive suits with respect to a particular subject matter, and when such facts have not been disclosed in the contents of the suit last in the chain, then such lastly filed suit would not be maintainable even for the concealment of such facts qua the filing of the earlier suits.
iv.	A party to the suit is required to include all reliefs and claims with respect to a subject matter and against a person or set of persons in a suit and as such, if any relief or claim is omitted, then such party would not be entitled to bring a subsequent suit with respect to the omitted claim/relief.
v.	A suit for declaration and a suit for specific performance could not be filed simultaneously as both these kinds of suits are mutually irreconcilable, as a suit for declaration could only be filed for declaring pre-existing rights which have been denied by a party, and the same could not be filed for creating a right, whereas a suit for specific performance is filed to seek direction from the Court to ask a party to perform his part of obligation.

case_info: W.P No. 4804-P of 2021 M/s Northern Bottling Company (Pvt) Ltd, Industrial Estate, Jamrud road, Peshawar through authorized person  Vs  The Federation of Pakistan through Secretary, Ministry of Defence, Islamabad & others
remarks:
Writ Petition
Held:         Cantonment Act, 1924- Section 60- Charging Provision-The Cantonment Board cannot extend the scope of the charging provisions beyond what is explicitly provided in the law. To do so would contravene the principle that taxing statutes must be construed strictly, and any ambiguities must be interpreted in favor of the taxpayer… The Cantonment Board did not possess the statutory authority at the relevant time to levy an advertisement fee, as defined under Section 60 read with Section 200 of the Act. Ref: Rajkumar v. Hyderabad Cantonment Board [2006 MLD 549]; Munuwar Younus and others v. Karachi Cantonment Board [2011 MLD 1006]; Hyderabad Cantonment Board v. Raj Kumar and others [2015 SCMR 1385].
Tax and Fee- differentiated- The key difference is that taxes are levied as a common burden, while fees confer specific benefits to the payee. Without a clear earmarking for services, a fee may be deemed a tax. Ref: Collector of Customs v. Sheikh Spinning Mills [1992 SCMR 1402]; Government of N.W.F.P. v. Rahimullah [1992 SCMR 750]; Muhammad Ismail v. Chief Cotton Inspector [PLD 1966 SC 388]; Federation of Pakistan v. Durrani Ceramics [2014 SCMR 1630]; Kewal Krishan Puri vs. State of Punjab [AIR 1980 SC 1008] & Hingir Rampur Coal Company Ltd. v. State of Orissa [AIR 1961 SC 459].
Cantonment (Amendment) Act, 2023-retrospective legislation- It is an established position that retrospective application of law cannot be made unless specifically provided for, particularly in tax cases. It is equally true that legislature has the power to legislate retrospectively but the same must be expressly provided…. The relevant provisions of the newly substituted Section 60 of the Act of 1924 grant the Board the authority to impose any or all taxes, fees, tolls, and charges as specified in Schedule VII. However, it is important to note that under the old legal framework, particularly in light of the case law discussed earlier, the Board did not possess the authority to levy advertisement fees Ref: Commissioner Inland Revenue v. National Public Welfare Society [2025 SCMR 1073]. Commissioner Inland Revenue v. Mekotex [PLD 2024 SC 1168].
Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Local Government Act, 2013 — Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Local Government Taxation Rules, 2016--- Section 42- No doubt, Section 42 of the Act of 2013 empowers the local government to impose fee listed in the Third Schedule, including “Fee on all types of advertisements including bill-boards other than on radio and television” under Part I, Item No. 11. This provision grants express authority to the local governments to impose such taxes, subject to compliance with the procedural safeguards stipulated therein, namely, publication of the proposed tax, invitation and consideration of public objections, and approval by the respective local council. The Rules of 2016 framed under Section 112 of the Act of 2013, further elaborate this process: Rule 3 mandates formulation of a preliminary taxation proposal; Rule 4 requires issuance of a public notice inviting objections and specifying enforcement dates; Rule 5 prescribes the mechanism for hearing and recording objections by the Finance Committee; and Rules 6 to 8 provide for council approval through resolution and post-sanction publication. These procedural requirements ensure that taxation measures are adopted transparently and with due public participation.
Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Local Government Act, 2013 — Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Local Government Taxation Rules, 2016--- Section 42- Quid pro quo- he petitioners assert that the imposition of advertisement fees by the local governments lacks the provision of any corresponding services, as required by the principle of quid pro quo. However, it is essential to recognize that it is not necessary for the corresponding services to be exactly measurable. The distinction between a tax and a fee has been clearly articulated by the Supreme Court in Durrani Ceramics (supra), where it was held that a “tax” is a common burden for raising revenue that becomes part of the public revenue of the State, while a “fee” is exacted for a specific purpose and for rendering services or providing privileges to particular individuals or communities. The court further clarified that so long as the levy provides some advantage to the payers, the consequential benefit to the community at large does not transform the fee into a tax. This principle aligns with the Supreme Court of India’s ruling in Hingir Rampur (supra), which emphasizes that a fee is charged for services rendered, and while the exact amount of service may be challenging to quantify, a substantial portion must be shown to be expended for the services provided…. These services are not necessarily intended for the petitioners alone but should be quantified in terms of their benefit to the community at large. The local governments and the respondents possess the authority to impose advertisement fees as outlined in the Act of 2013, particularly under Section 42, which empowers local governments to levy taxes and fees as specified in the Third Schedule. The relevant entry (Entry 11) explicitly includes fees on all types of advertisements. Thus, the respondents are acting within their legal competence in imposing these fees, fulfilling the statutory requirements and ensuring that the services provided are in line with the provisions of the law. Therefore, the claim that the imposition of advertisement fees lacks a corresponding service is not substantiated, and we hold that the local governments are indeed competent to levy such fees as prescribed by the applicable laws.
Cantonment Act, 1924— Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Local Government Act, 2013 –Advertisement- explained- The dictionary definitions reflect that: firstly, advertisement is an informational technique to persuade public in order that the public respond to it in a certain way as regards the content of the information. The information may be circulated through various means, such as, radio, T.V., newspapers and billboards. The word “persuade” may be emphasized here, which may, in the context of trade and commerce, be interchangeably understood as “promotion”. In contemporary context, in addition to the traditional media, such as radio, T.V., newspapers, and billboards, business concerns also use social media, for example, apps, search engines, etc.  Advertisements through media is liable to payment of charges by the media owners. The use of public space for it is also chargeable by the local official or semi-official authorities managing and controlling the public places under relevant laws. So, billboards installed in a public place are permitted subject to payment of fee levied by the municipal authorities. Ref: New Encyclopaedia Britannica, Vol. I, Chambers Dictionary, P. Ramanatha Aiyar’s the Law Lexion [Law Lexicon, 2nd Ed. 2002].
Cantonment Act, 1924— Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Local Government Act, 2013 –Taxable Advertisement- explained- - What is “taxable advertisement” itself needs legal and technical analysis. First, it has to be ascertained that the display of name or board is a statutory compliance, not a commercial choice. Second, the “Plus Factor” has to be seen or it is to say that the immunity or exemption is lost if the signboard goes beyond simple identification. For instance, a sign board saying “ABC Bank” is exempt because it only depicts simple identification. But when a sign board saying “ABC Bank - Best Loans @ 5% Interest” becomes an advertisement and is taxable… since it is the statutory requirement under section 22 of the Companies Act of displaying name outside the business place, therefore, such signboard, billboard etc. does not comes within the definition of advertisement and thus cannot be treated as taxable advertisement for the purpose of section 42 read with item 11 of the Third Schedule of the Act of 2013.  Ref: Soneri Bank Limited vs Province of Punjab [PLJ 2020 Lahore 239]. 
Cantonment Act, 1924— Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Local Government Act, 2013 –Taxable Advertisement- Private Schools - The case of private schools is legally distinguishable from that of the banking sector on two critical grounds. First, the exemption granted to banks in Soneri Bank (supra) was predicated on the supremacy of a Federal Statute (The Companies Act) over provincial local government laws; here, both the regulatory body (PSRA/BISE) and the taxing authority (Local Government) operate under the same Provincial legislative sphere, meaning no such supremacy exists to oust the taxation power. Second, the nature of a private school’s signage—often displayed on “panaflex” and placed strategically near roads to attract admissions—possesses an inherent commercial character distinct from the passive identification required of a bank branch. Mere compliance with a regulatory directive to display a name does not automatically transmute a commercial advertisement into a non-taxable statutory duty, especially when the display utilizes public space or targets a consumer base. Therefore, the local authorities are well within their jurisdiction to levy the advertisement fee, and the immunity claimed by the petitioners is misconceived.
Cantonment Act, 1924— Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Local Government Act, 2013 –Taxable Advertisement- Delivery Bikes - For assessing the taxability of each tangible item—whether it is a signboard, billboard, vehicle, or name board—it must pass the test of serving promotional purposes rather than mere identification. If the name, logo, or trademark is used solely for identification and does not indicate the promotion of a business, it cannot be taxed as an advertisement. With this in mind, we will first address the issue of delivery services through branded motorbikes operated by the petitioners. It is beyond question that the respondents or local governments lack the authority to levy such fees. However, the petitioners contend that their trademark and logo are used for identification and security purposes. The respondents have failed to provide any evidence that these displays are intended to promote their business. The test is straightforward: if the motorbikes display the logo and trademark of “foodpanda” solely for identification purposes, then they should not be subject to advertisement fees….  Conversely, if the vehicles are wrapped in bright promotional colors and feature specific product images, slogans, prices, or calls to action (e.g., “Order Now for 50% Off” or “Buy One-Get One Free”), this shifts the display from “identification” to “mobile advertisement.” Reference: M/s R-SC Internet Services Pakistan (Pvt.) Ltd. v. Government of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa [2024 YLR 2808]
Cantonment Act, 1924— Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Local Government Act, 2013 –Taxable Advertisement- Identification v. promotion - the fundamental test for differentiating between identification and advertising, as established in the preceding paragraphs, is equally applicable to this case. Upon reviewing the documents submitted by the petitioner, especially the images of their vehicles, it becomes evident that these vehicles display more advertising slogans than mere identification, such as “Your Assurance, Our Expertise” alongside the company name and logo. Such slogans clearly fall within the promotional category. Therefore, we are not inclined to accept the petitioner’s assertion that the display of their trademark or slogan is solely for identification purposes.
Cantonment Act, 1924— Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Local Government Act, 2013 –collection through contractor- The primary legal question arising from these contentions is whether the collection of a fee or tax through a contractor constitutes an unlawful delegation of statutory power. We find this contention unsustainable for two reasons. First, a distinction must be drawn between the delegation of discretionary power (such as determining liability or the rate of tax) and the assignment of ministerial functions (such as the physical collection of a pre-determined amount)…. the contractor or third party tasked with the collection of taxes or fees acts merely on behalf of the original authority, performing a mechanical or “robotic” function rather than exercising sovereign authority. Secondly, it is essential to recognize that such contractors are not decision-makers regarding the liability or quantum of the fees; rather, they serve as collection agents responsible for executing a predetermined collection process. The principle in Shell Pakistan Limited (supra) was also followed by this Court which affirms the distinction between assigning ministerial duties and delegating discretionary powers. In this context, it is not uncommon for fees or taxes to be collected by third parties or withholding agents. For instance, the Pakistan Television (PTV) fee is collected through electricity bills by electricity companies. Moreover, various private entities routinely undertake such ministerial functions, including Telecom/Mobile Companies collecting Advance Income Tax and Sales Tax on recharges/bills, Airlines collecting Federal Excise Duty and Advance Tax on tickets, and Educational Institutions collecting Advance Tax on fees. These examples further underscore that private entities frequently collect state revenue without exercising sovereign discretion. Similarly, in today’s technological and digital financial landscape, taxpayers frequently remit their bills, taxes, or fees through third parties or withholding agents. Ref: Zia Ullah v. Government of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa [2022 MLD 1705 Peshawar], Shell Pakistan Limited, Karachi v. Capital Development Authority [PLD 2015 Islamabad 36]. 
(a)	Cantonment Act, 1924— Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Local Government Act, 2013 –collection through contractor- Consequently, in light of the distinction drawn between the delegation of discretionary powers and the assignment of ministerial functions, we are compelled to take a different view as regards the Cantonment Board’s and the local government authorities’ mandate to collect fees through a contractor. The argument that such collection constitutes an unlawful delegation lacks merit when the contractor is merely executing a mechanical function of collecting a pre-determined fee. It seems worth clarifying that the (sovereign) power to impose and assess the fee must be duly exercised by the authority empowered as such. The fee for which the authority has provided details of sizes and the applicable assessed and approved rates through official notifications does not in any way create the impression that the fee is charged by the contractor. It is for this reason that the contractor merely performs ministerial function, such as, undertaking field measurement of means of the advertisement on the spot. The contractor’s sole duty here is to measure the sizes of the means of advertisements and apply the notified rates as per the prescribed sizes in respect thereof. Once, he completes such exercise, the last task is to give a cumulative figure of the fee and collect such fee under the direction of the authority. It follows that the contractor’s job to measure the sizes of the means of advertisement on the spot according to the already approved notified rates and asking for payment of its fee, is a ministerial job. Otherwise, the contract itself would be a futile exercise.  By ensuring this statutory requirement, the mere application and collection of the fee through contractor does not contravene the letter and spirit of the chargeability of such levy. Distinguished-- Dubai Islamic Bank Pakistan Ltd. v. Federation of Pakistan [2014 MLD 957 Lahore]; Messrs Coca-Cola Beverages v. Cantonment Board Chaklala [2011 MLD 1987]; Muhammad Munir Abdullah v. Tehsil Municipal Administration [2010 YLR 2543 Lahore]; & Messrs Coca Cola Beverages Pakistan Limited v. City District Government, Rawalpindi [2014 CLC 1135 Lahore]

case_info: WP No. 4284-P of 2025 M/s Malakand Oil & Ghee Mills (Pvt) Ltd and others vs. Federation of Pakistan through Federal Secretary, Ministry of Finance, Finance Division, Islamabad and others.
W.P No. 8823-P of 2025 Munir Khan  Vs  The State through Secretary, Home Department, Government of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa, Peshawar & others
remarks:
Writ Petition
Held:          Constitution of Pakistan, Articles 4, 9, 10A, 14 & 18 — Police Clearance Certificate — Distinction between Internal Retention and Outward Disclosure — Minor Convictions & Dignity— The State cannot use a routine administrative document (Police Clearance Certificate) to impose a “civil death” or permanent disability on a citizen for a minor, concluded error. Article 14 (Dignity) is not merely about physical custody; it includes the right to reputation and social reintegration. When the State discloses a minor, spent conviction (e.g., a small fine for a regulatory offence) to an employer or foreign visa officer, it is effectively labeling the citizen as a criminal in perpetuity. This “labeling” is a disproportionate state action that violates Article 9 (Right to Life/Livelihood). While the police may keep records for internal surveillance, they cannot outwardly certify a citizen as having a “criminal record” for ordinary civil purposes (jobs, travel) if the offence was minor and the sentence has been served.
Section 243 Cr.P.C. — Article 10A (Fair Trial) — Validity of Guilty Pleas in Petty Cases — A Guilty Plea in a summary trial must be informed and voluntary, especially when it carries long-term civil consequences like a stained police record. Magistrates often record guilty pleas mechanically in petty cases where the accused pays a small fine (e.g., Rs. 500) to end the matter quickly. The Court held that this practice is dangerous if the accused does not realize this \"small fine\" will permanently appear on their Police Character Certificate. Under Section 243 Cr.P.C. and Article 10A, the Magistrate acts as a guardian of fairness. They must ensure the accused understands that the plea might result in a lasting record, not just a momentary fine.
Differentiation of Record — FIR vs. Conviction —Adverse Material Defined— For the purposes of issuing Police Clearance Certificates, criminal records must be conceptually divided. An “FIR-only” entry (mere accusation without challan/cognizance) cannot be disclosed as adverse material. Similarly, favourable outcomes (acquittal, discharge, quashment) cannot be treated as adverse.
Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Probation and Parole Act, 2021 — Section 13 — Effect of Probation— Where a convict has successfully completed their probation period, Section 13 of the Act protects them from disqualifications attaching to a conviction. Executive authorities (Police) are bound by this statutory policy and cannot disclose the conviction as adverse material in a PCC after probation is successfully served.
Judicial Review of Executive Action — (i) the competent law making forum consider enacting a rehabilitation or spent convictions framework to regulate retention, disclosure, clean periods, and safeguards, particularly for first time and minor offenders, so that constitutional rights under Articles 9 and 14 do not remain dependent on case by case constitutional litigation. (ii) further recommended that offences be categorised, for certification purposes, into petty or regulatory matters and serious matters, with clear criteria. Minor fines for petty regulatory infractions should not operate as a permanent adverse label in routine certification. Disclosure should be aligned with purpose, and the least restrictive measure should be preferred. (iii) Until such legislation is enacted, the Inspector General of Police, Khyber Pakhtunkhwa shall, within three months, frame standing orders or administrative instructions within his lawful administrative competence, to implement a two tier certification model with purpose limitation, namely: 
(a)	A standard Police Clearance Certificate for ordinary civil purposes which excludes FIR only entries, excludes favourable outcomes such as acquittal, discharge, quashment or lawful compounding from being treated as adverse material, and excludes minor petty fines after a defined clean period, subject to absence of subsequent relevant offending.
(b)	An enhanced vetting certificate for sensitive positions only, issuable only where authorised by law or competent standing order, with limited disclosure confined to relevant pending proceedings where challan is submitted and cognizance is taken, and with disclosure being time bound and reviewable through a reasoned decision process.
(Writ Petition is allowed)

case_info: Cr.A No. 131-B of 2025 with M.R No. 04-B of 2025 Bakhmal Badshah  Vs  The State & others
remarks:
Penal Code (XLV of 1860)---
----S. 302 (b)---Qatl-e-amd---Appreciation of evidence---Prompt F.I.R.---Single accused----Clear motive
of a property dispute---- The prosecution evidence is consistent and free from doubts. Although the
eyewitness was subjected to lengthy cross-examination yet his credibility remained intact, and his testimony on all material particulars could not be shaken. The FIR was lodged promptly, soon after the deceased was shifted to KDA Hospital, Karak, leaving no room for deliberation or false implication. The record is also silent regarding any mala fide, ill will, or animosity significant enough to falsely implicate the accused---- According to the post-mortem report Ex.PM, the deceased sustained a single entry wound on the medial aspect of the left thigh, measuring 1 × 1 cm, about 0.30 cm below the iliac crest—a non-vital part of the body. This constitutes a mitigating circumstance, raising the question of why the accused did not target a vital part if his exclusive intention was to commit murder. The fact that only one injury was inflicted also supports the presence of mitigating circumstances warranting reduction of sentence--On such factor alone, capital punishment of accused could not be sustained---Prosecution had fully proved its case against accused---Appeal against conviction, in circumstances, was partially allowed and death sentence of accused was commuted to imprisonment for life---With such modifications, Murder Reference was replied in the negative.

case_info: CR No. 318-M of 2023 Akhtar Ali etc   Vs  Mst. Bakhti Farosha etc
remarks:
Court has dismissed a revision petition challenging a judgment that cancelled a General Power of Attorney and a gift mutation. The court held that the gift was not validly proved and the attorney\'s actions were not in the best interest of the principal.
The court found that the General Power of Attorney was executed for management and representation purposes, not for gifting property. The alleged gift was not proved through cogent evidence, and its essential ingredients were missing. The attorney\'s fiduciary duty required obtaining informed consent from the principal for the gift.

case_info: Cr.A No. 368-P of 2014 Mian Gul Vs Gulab Said and others
remarks:
A. This kind of careful consideration is not just a formality, it is fundamental to
ensure that the verdict rests on a strong and reliable foundation of fact and
principle, and that the integrity of justice is fully upheld. For this Court to
undertake such a review, it will be essential that the events, the circumstances,
and the testimony of the witnesses are all considered together. Only in this way
can this Court understand the precise conditions and context that led the
learned trial Court to conclude that the accused were innocent and to dismiss
the charges against them.
B. The material collected from the scene, including blood-stained earth, spent
cartridges, and bullet marks on the walls and windows, reinforces their
narrative. In this backdrop, it becomes essential to examine the reasoning that
persuaded the learned trial Court to conclude that the prosecution had failed to
prove its case, and to assess whether the evidence was correctly and fairly
appreciated.
C. There was no apparent hostility that might have deterred them from deposing
against the respondents rather, their refusal to appear seems to stem from a
desire to avoid personal conflict and the risk of retaliation.
D. Though the learned trial Court sought support from Article 129(g) of the Qanun-
e-Shahadat Order, 1984, it appears to have applied traditional reasoning
without taking into account the realities of contemporary society, in which
reluctance to testify, particularly in cases of serious violence, is neither unusual
nor indicative of falsehood. In such a social climate, the absence of
independent or injured witnesses should not, of itself, lead to the conclusion
that the case of prosecution is fabricated.
E. This rapid sequence of events, from the moment of the incident to the reporting,
excludes any possibility of prior consultation or deliberate fabrication by the
complainant or eyewitness.

F. The conduct of the accused reveals a striking recklessness and indifference to
human life. They did not take care to avoid harming others, carrying out their
attack immediately after Jummah prayer, resulting not only in the death of the
intended target, Sajjad, but also the death of another innocent person and
injuries to two others.
G. Notably, the timing of both incidents is nearly identical, the locations are in
close proximity, and the alleged motive in both cases is the same. When such
critical features are shared between the two incidents, and when the motive, the
illicit relationship between the female deceased Mst. Jasmin, and the male
deceased, Sajjad, is common, it becomes pertinent to ask whether, even in the
absence of direct eyewitness testimony, the circumstances can be relied upon
to establish a connection, and whether proof of the first episode may lend
credibility to the second.
H. Once the Court is satisfied that such a connection exists, the subsequent
events may be regarded as part of the same transaction. In such a scenario,
this Court will consider whether the evidence was collected and linked in such a
manner, whether the parties were related in context, and whether the alleged
motive was sufficiently consistent.
I. It is, therefore, incumbent upon this Court to determine whether the benefit of
doubt was rightly extended to the accused, and whether, even in the absence
of the eyewitness who refused to testify from the house in question, the
circumstances still point convincingly to the same accused as responsible for
both incidents.
J. In this case, the motive serves as a crucial connecting link, tying the deceased
Jasmin, to the deceased Sajjad. This connectivity allows us to conclude that no
other individual could have been responsible, rather the accused were, beyond
reasonable doubt, the perpetrators.

K. We are of the view that the learned trial Court committed a serious error in this
regard, failing to properly understand the circumstances. The reluctance of the
witnesses to appear was driven by fear of the accused, the fear was so real and
pervasive that the witness was unable to come forward boldly to testify against
them. Being a woman, her apprehension was reasonable and justified,
however, it cannot follow that the accused should be acquitted solely on this
basis.
L. Yet, the evidence shows a singular, powerful motive, the accused were
consumed by vengeance, to such an extent that they could not wait for the
deceased Sajjad, to exit the mosque before acting. Their thirst for revenge
drove them to act immediately, without pause, reflecting a mental state of
extreme agitation and determination.
M. The learned Court, in taking an adverse inference from their absence,
considered only one side of the matter, without appreciating that the absence
was a direct result of the intimidation of the accused, so how could a lone
female witness be expected to appear in such circumstances.
N. Guided by the settled principles of criminal jurisprudence, we are required to
determine the correct course, which clearly indicates that both incidents are
interlinked, much like two bodies forming a single whole.
O. We are convinced that after the firing in the mosque, the accused had not fully
achieved their objective, for their purpose could only be completed by the killing
of Jasmin, as well, thereby enabling them to claim, at least in their own
perception, that the stain of dishonor had been erased. When such
circumstances exist, and when the connection of the accused is supported by a
single, consistent motive rooted in perceived dishonor, the circumstantial
evidence becomes strong enough to firmly link the second episode with the
first.
P. Comparable circumstances were examined by the Court in Moorov v HM
Advocate (1930 JC 68), where, after an exhaustive analysis, the Court
articulated certain principles concerning the nexus between multiple incidents
and the commission of distinct offences occurring either in close proximity or
within a defined span of time.
Q. The Moorov doctrine, though persuasive and not binding, provides valuable
guidance in assessing the interrelatedness of the offences and the continuity of
the conduct of accused. This Court is inclined to adopt this approach in
examining the evidence and arriving at a holistic conclusion on the culpability of
the accused.

case_info: W.P No. 221-B of 2024 Farhan-ud-Din  Vs  Govt. of KP through Secretary E& SE Deptt Peshawar, etc
remarks:
Writ Petition.
Constitution of Pakistan, 1973 --- Article 199 --- Public Employment --- Merit --- Primary
School Teacher (PST) recruitment --- ETEA --- Impersonation/Use of unfair Means (UFM) ---
Blacklisting --- Waiting List --- Next Meritorious Candidate--- Back dated Appointment --- Mala
Fide --- Discrimination --- Articles 4, 25 & 27 --- Petitioner sought setting aside of appointment
of Respondent No. 5 as PST (BPS:12) in UC Mira Khel and claimed appointment on merit ---
Petitioner secured 110.20 marks and initially stood at Serial No. 19; candidates at Serial No. 3
and 4 did not join, whereas candidates at Serial No. 17 & 18 (respondent No. 5) were declared
impersonation/UFM cases and were accordingly blacklisted by ETEA --- ETEA’s declaration of
impersonation/UFM was supported by CNICs, roll numbers, profiles cards and test day
photographs --- Department ignored repeated ETEA communication and appointed respondent
No. 5 as PST (BPS:12) --- Visual mismatch between profile picture and test day photograph of
respondent no. 5 confirmed impersonation --- Held, impersonation vitiates entire selection
process; candidate guilty of impersonation/UFM stands disqualified ab initio and appointment
secured through fraud is void and non-est--- Where higher merit candidates fail to join or stand
disqualified, next meritorious candidate on waiting list must be considered --- Issuance of back
dated appointment to defeat interim order held mala fide and impermissible --- Denial of
appointment to petitioner while similarly placed candidate in another UC in identical
circumstances was appointed amounted to discrimination --- Appointment of respondent No. 5
declared illegal and set aside --- Petitioner directed to be considered for appointment against
vacant post --- Directions issued for disciplinary proceedings against DEO and concerned
officials under KP Government Servants (E&D) Rules, 2011 --- Recovery of salaries from
respondent No. 5 and criminal action directed--- Writ petition allowed.

case_info: W.P No. 636-D of 2025 Sakina (Late) through L.Rs  Vs  Govt of KP and others
remarks:
Held: That Section 9 of the Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Waqf Properties Ordinance, 1979 applies only
where a lease is valid and subsisting and is attracted solely in cases involving breach of lease
conditions. Once a lease expires by efflux of time, the lessee ceases to have lawful possession and
cannot invoke the procedural safeguards contained in Section 9. Mere acceptance of rent after expiry
of lease does not amount to renewal, nor does it create any vested or enforceable right in favour of
the occupant, particularly in respect of Waqf property.
Held further: That Section 9-A of the Ordinance, inserted through the 2021 amendment, is an
independent and overriding provision containing a non-obstante clause, empowering the Chief
Administrator, with prior approval of Government, to revoke a lease at any time during its currency
in public interest, without requirement of establishing breach or following the procedure prescribed
under Section 9.
Held further: That “prior approval of Government” under Section 9-A validly includes approval of
the Provincial Cabinet, and where such approval is duly recorded, the resultant notification cannot
be struck down in the absence of mala fide, arbitrariness or lack of jurisdiction.
Held further: That occupation of Waqf property after expiry of lease is unauthorized and does not
enjoy constitutional protection under Articles 4, 9, 10-A, 14 or 23 of the Constitution of the Islamic
Republic of Pakistan, and matters arising out of such occupation do not ordinarily warrant
interference in constitutional jurisdiction.
Held further: That although the writ petitions were dismissed, where the Provincial Cabinet itself
directed assessment and payment of compensation for constructions raised by leaseholders, such
direction is binding on the authorities and must be implemented strictly in accordance with law
within the stipulated time.

case_info: W.P No. 787-B of 2025 Faran Khan  Vs State and 09 others
remarks:
Criminal Procedure Code, 1898 ---S. 169 read with S. 63 --- Constitution of Pakistan, 1973,
Art. 199 --- Release of accused on personal bond during investigation --- Subjective
satisfaction of investigation officer --- Scope of judicial interference --- Held, that opinion
formed by the investigating officer under S. 169 Cr.P.C regarding insufficiency of evidence at a
particular stage of investigation is an investigative function, provisional in nature, and does not
amount to adjudication of guilt or innocence. Courts, while exercising constitutional jurisdiction,
do not sit as an appellate authorities over investigative assessments and will not interfere unless
mala fides, patent illegality, perversity, or reliance on extraneous considerations is established
through credible material. Mere existence of an FIR, presence of eyewitnesses, recoveries of
medical/post-mortem evidence does not ipso facto restrict the Investigating Officer from acting
under S. 169, Cr.P.C. Allegations of political influence or police bias remained unsubstantiated,
and the petitioner failed to demonstrate violation of any statutory command. The act of releasing
the accused on personal bonds being an interim investigative measure, always subject to reversal
on collection of further evidence, could not be equated with the trial court’s domain. Relief
sought would amount to undue interference in the investigative process, which is impermissible
under Art. 199 of the constitution.
Constitution petition dismissed in limine.

case_info: Cr.A NO. 26-D of 2025 Muhammad Shah Nawaz  Vs  The State and another
remarks:
Appellant was convicted and sentenced to death under Section 302(b) PPC as Tazir, with
compensation under Section 544-A Cr.P.C., in connection with the murder of Nisar Ali Khan.
Held:
1. Where the principal assailant is acquitted on compromise, the vicarious liability of a
secondary accused under Section 34 PPC must be established by independent and
unimpeachable evidence.
2. Material contradictions, omissions, and lack of corroboration in ocular testimony raise
serious doubts regarding the appellant’s participation.
3. Recovery of a household knife from the appellant’s house, without FSL confirmation, is
insufficient to establish guilt.
4. Alleged motive must be proved; failure to do so favors the accused.
5. Benefit of doubt extended in accordance with the principle: “It is better to acquit a hundred
guilty persons than to convict a single innocent one.”
Disposition: Appeal allowed; conviction and sentence of death set aside; appellant released if not
required in any other case.

case_info: Cr.A No. 06-D of 2024 Sharab Din  Vs  The State etc
remarks:
(Appeal against conviction)
(a) Criminal Procedure Code, 1898….S.302/324/34 PPC—re-appraisal of
evidence— According to prosecution story, the complainant alongwith his brother
were sitting in front of the shop of one Asghar at Tank-Jandola main road at early
morning when the accused duly armed arrived there, beaten the complainant who
sustained injuries and also made firing at them, as a result brother of complainant died
on the spot. It was a daylight occurrence and reported with promptitude, excluding the
possibility of false implication.
(b) Criminal Procedure Code, 1898…S.302/324/34 PPC—re-appraisal of
evidence— The mere fact that the deceased was brother of the complainant would not
