be a ground, especially when he himself sustained injuries, his otherwise confidence
inspiring testimony corroborated by the medical evidence could not be discarded on
this score alone. The complainant remained firm qua shifting of the dead body from
the spot to the hospital while disclosing the mode of transportation. The mode and
manner of the occurrence was explained in a natural and coherent manner and could
not be shaken in cross-examination.
(c) Criminal Procedure Code, 1898…S.302/324/34 PPC—re-appraisal of
evidence—The FSL qua crime empties recovered from the spot furnished
corroborative support to the prosecution case.
(d) Criminal Procedure Code, 1898.…S.302/324/34 PPC—re-appraisal of
evidence—The prosecuting succeeded to prove the motive setup in the FIR by placing
on file and exhibiting copy of FR No.71, dated 03.3.2016, registered under sections
452, 365-B, 496-A, 109, 148, 149 PPC of Police Station Gomal University (Ex. Pw
11/3). Similarly, a Madd Report No.12 (Ex. PW 11/4), entered in Police Station
Gomal University, a day prior to the present occurrence on the report of deceased
against the accused for beating him and his wife.
(Appeal was dismissed in the circumstances).

case_info: C.R No. 282-B of 2025 Gul Nawab Khan, etc  Vs  Police Khan, etc.
remarks:
Civil Revision
Held: Code of Civil Procedure, 1908---Section 115---Section 144---Restitution---Mutation---Effect of
Reversal of Decree---Actus Curiae Neminem Gravabit---Limitation---Concurrent Findings---Petitioners
challenged orders whereby mutation attested on basis of decree was cancelled and earlier Tamleek mutation restored---Mutation No.1674 dated 20.05.1997 was attested on strength of decree dated 07.04.1996---Said decree was reversed in appeal on 15.05.1998 and reversal was affirmed in revision, attaining finality---Despite reversal of decree, mutation continued to subsist on record---Respondents sought restitution under Section 144 CPC for restoration of status ante---Held, once decree forming basis of mutation is reversed, any benefit derived therefrom must be undone---Restitution under Section 144 CPC is mandatory and flows ex debito justitiae; no party should suffer due to act of Court---Plea of limitation under Article 181 Limitation Act held misconceived; restitution is not a fresh cause of action and subsisting mutation constituted continuing benefit of reversed decree---Courts below rightly cancelled mutation founded on reversed decree and restored earlier Tamleek mutation---Revisional jurisdiction under Section 115 CPC is supervisory and does not permit re-appraisal of evidence in absence of perversity or misreading---Concurrent findings of Courts below found lawful and justified---Civil revision dismissed in limine.

case_info: Cr. A No. 51-P of 2025 Muhammad Sabir  Vs The State
remarks:
Sections 9-D, 24,26 and 32 KPCNSA, 2019.
Articles 17,79, 129(g) of Qanun-e-Shahadat Order, 1984.
Section 410, 540 Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898.
Single Convention on Narcotic Drugs, 1961.
United Nations Convention against Illicit Traffic in Narcotic Drugs and Psychotropic Substances,
1988.

i. Article 12 of United Nations Convention against Illicit Traffic in Narcotic Drugs and
Psychotropic Substances, 1988 has been incorporated in section 7 of the CNSA,
1997, which prohibits import into Pakistan, export from Pakistan, transport within
Pakistan and transship of narcotic substances.
ii. Recovery of narcotic substances from the accused, both the marginal witness to
the recovery memo were not produced as witness in the trial court.
iii. In case of recovery of narcotics three fundamental elements must be proved;
firstly, recovery of narcotics substances, secondly; it must be demonstrated that
the recovered material is prohibited substance within meanings of law, thirdly;
conscious knowledge and possession of the accused. Every significant procedural
step be undertaken strictly in accordance with the principles laid by law and
dictum of the superior judiciary.
iv. Per record; one constable Hidayat who took samples to the FSL, instead of him,
one Qaiser Khan, Excise constable was produced as witness, whether the same
single aspect can be termed as “reasonable doubt” leading to the acquittal of the
accused.
v. Section 540 CrPC: purpose of section of section 540 CrPC is to empower and
enable the court to summon any witness at any state of proceedings to ascertain
the truth of the matter in order to arrive at just and proper conclusion.
vi. Powers of the appellate court under sections 418 and 423 and scope of section
537 CrPC elaborated.
vii. Guidelines and directions for adopting practice of audio visual recording of
testimony of witness during trial.

case_info: W.P No. 3170-P of 2025 Dr. Ikram Ullah Khan  Vs  Government of KP and others
remarks: A writ petition is maintainable only against final orders passed by the departmental authority. Interim measures or order of such authority could not be treated ripe for invoking the constitutional jurisdiction of this Court, unless same suffers from jurisdictional defeats, are mala fide, coram non judice, or otherwise likely to perpetuate injustice.

case_info: C.R No. 431-M of 2005 Muhammad Said Main  Vs  Govt of KPK
remarks:
Court has dismissed the revision petitions filed by Muhammad Said Mian and
Ashraf Gul, upholding the concurrent findings of the lower courts. The petitioners,
belonging to Miangan and Sheikhan castes, claimed ownership and entitlement
to forest royalty, but the court held that they are seri khors (service holders) and
not daftari owners.
The court found that the petitioners' claim is unfounded and not supported by
revenue records or settlement documents. Instead, the respondents, belonging to
Hassan Khel caste, are daftari owners and have been recognized as
concessionists/right-holders. The petitioner`s possession is permissive and
limited, and they have no proprietary title to the suit property.
The lower court`s findings were concurrent and reasoned, and the High Court
saw no reason to interfere with them. The revision petitions were dismissed, with
no order as to costs.

case_info: C.R No. 449-M of 2019 Noor Ghani  Vs  Bakhti Sherwan and others
remarks:
Court has allowed the revision petition filed by Noor Ghani, setting aside the
impugned judgments/orders passed by the courts below. The executing court is
directed to conclude the execution proceedings afresh, in accordance with law.
The petitioner, Noor Ghani, was decreed a share in the property against Alam
Said. Respondents No. 5 and 6 were found in possession of land exceeding their
entitlement. The court held that respondents No. 5 and 6 are \"persons bound by
the decree\" under Order XXI Rule 35, C.P.C. The executing court must determine
the extent of land in possession of each party and ensure delivery of possession
to the decree-holder.

case_info: Cr. A No. 279-P of 2025 Faiz-ur-Rehman  Vs  The State etc
remarks:
i.	The crucial issue is to determine who initiated the incident, who was the aggressor, and whether the appellant fired in the exercise of his right of private defence. These are the central matters which this Court must examine with care, for their determination will decisively affect the question of liability and the ultimate conclusion as to where the fault truly lies.
ii.	The circumstances show that both sides played a part in the escalation. The appellant was not free from blame, yet the conduct of the deceased also contributed to the incident.
iii.	The principal issue for determination is whether the trial court exercised its discretion correctly in awarding the sentence, having regard to the unique circumstances of the case, including the inter-relationship of the parties and the damage suffered by both sides.
iv.	Having considered the totality of the evidence, the nature of the incident, the role of the appellant, and the circumstances under which the tragedy occurred, this Court is of the opinion that the sentence of life imprisonment imposed under Section 302(b) PPC calls for reconsideration.

case_info: C.R No. 35-P of 2013 Rizwan Ullah Khan and others  Vs Muhammad Usman Khan and others
remarks:
i.	where a plaintiff or defendant dies during the pendency of a suit or appeal as the case may be, then an entry in this regard is to be made in the record and then the L.Rs of such deceased plaintiff or defendant is to be impleaded, however, if the factum of death of a plaintiff or defendant is not brought into the notice of the court or if the L.Rs of such deceased party are not impleaded in the case, then merely for this omission, the judgment if pronounced by a competent court of law would be as effective as if the death of such party has been occurred.
ii.	The question of limitation as against the impleaded L.Rs of the deceased plaintiff could not be relevant in view of Sub-Rule(2) of Order-XXII, CPC, nevertheless, such limitation would be relevant under Sub-Rule(1) of the ibid Order only where a plaintiff or defendant is substituted or added in a suit or appeal as the case may be.
iii.	Before the Law Reforms Ordinance, 1972, a suit used to be abated in case of death of plaintiff, however, after promulgation of the said ordinance, a suit will not abate and it will continue to proceed provided the L.Rs of such deceased plaintiff is having an alive cause of action as well against the deceased defendant If the cause of action is alive against such legal heirs.

case_info: W.P No. 122-M of 2024 Sana Ali Khan  Vs  Govt: of KP
remarks:
High Court, accepted a writ petition filed by Sana Ali Khan and others,
challenging notices issued by NADRA under Section 18 of the
NADRA Ordinance, 2000, which had led to the digital impounding of
their CNICs. The petitioners' CNICs were blocked based on vague
allegations of doubtful citizenship, with NADRA`s notices lacking
specific grounds or evidence, violating principles of natural justice.
Notably, NADRA had previously admitted the petitioner`s identity and
family lineage in a civil suit. The court held that NADRA`s actions
were arbitrary and lacked transparency, applying the doctrine of
estoppel to prevent NADRA from contradicting its earlier stance.
Consequently, the writ petition was accepted, the impugned notices
were set aside, and respondents were directed to restore the
petitioner`s CNICs forthwith.

case_info: Cr. A No. 348-A of 2023 Liaqat Ali etc  Vs  The state
remarks:
a) When credibility of scribe of report is shaken on account of fabricating
the record of the cross-case, his testimony cannot be relied upon for
the purpose of conviction.
b) If medical evidence diverges from the ocular account, the same is not
safe for reliance for recording conviction of an accused.
c) The empties recovered on the very day of occurrence were not
dispatched to the Forensic Science Laboratory at once. They were
sent only after the alleged recovery of the weapon of offence. Both
items travelled together. This practice destroys the evidentiary value
of the FSL report.
d) If complainant dishonestly and deliberately conceals cross-version of
the incident in his report and denies seeing injuries on the accused’s
side, such suppression of material facts destroys the credibility of the
prosecution case.
e) When facts of the case bring it squarely within the category of cross-
cases and free fights and where both sides conceal truth and
sustaining of injuries by each other, it becomes impossible to discern
the real aggressor. The benefit must go to the accused.

case_info: RFA No. 160-A of 2023 Mst Saira  Bibi  Vs  Mst Sughran Bibi
remarks:
(a) Order XLI Rule 17 of Civil Procedure Code, empowers the appellate court
to dismiss the appeal if the appellant fails to appear or comply with the court’s
directions. Rule 18 permits the court to dismiss an appeal where notice has not
been served due to the appellant’s failure to deposit the required costs, while
also allowing the appeal to proceed if the respondent appears. Rule 19
provides a statutory right to restore the appeal if the appellant can demonstrate
sufficient cause for non-compliance and mandates the court to re-admit the
appeal on such terms as it deems fit. The duties of the parties and the court under
these provisions are dual and complementary. The appellants are obliged to comply
with all procedural directions, deposit process fees promptly, and provide the
necessary documents to facilitate the service of notice. The appellate court, on its
part, must ensure that any dismissal of the appeal is procedurally proper, by recording
the facts of non-compliance, ensuring the appeal is called in open court, giving the
appellant an opportunity to explain their non-compliance, and exercising its powers in
accordance with Rules 14 to 20 of Order XLI CPC. Failure to observe these
requirements renders the dismissal order procedurally defective and legally infirm.
(b) It is a fundamental principle of civil justice that disputes should be adjudicated on
merits, rather than being decided or terminated on mere technicalities. The dismissal
of an appeal, without proper examination of the reasons for non-compliance, deprives
the party of its statutory right to have the case decided substantively.

case_info: Cr.A No. 69-D of 2024 Qaisar Abbas  Vs  The State etc
remarks:
(Appeal against conviction)
(a) Criminal Procedure Code, 1898….S.302 PPC—re-appraisal of evidence—The
allegation against the appellant was that he committed the murder of nephew of the
complainant by giving him knife blows. Stance of the prosecution was that the
accused was arrested from the spot by the SHO who was on patrol duty. However, the
SHO neither drafted any Murasila concerning alleged incident, nor signed any
recovery memo for alleged seized items. Had he arrested the accused from the spot, he
would have prepared relevant document on the spot, completed recovery proceedings
coupled with association of independent witnesses. The Investigating Officer admitted
that he had not inquired or confirmed how, by whom, or through what means accused
was brought to the Police Station. He also failed to record statements from local
residents regarding arrest of the accused. Similarly, he did not record statement of any
person who allegedly gave shelter to Mst. Zeenat Bibi, wife of the convict/appellant.
(b) Criminal Procedure Code, 1898…S.302 PPC—re-appraisal of evidence— Since Mst.
Zeenat Bibi, who allegedly witnessed the occurrence, was not produced as prosecution
witness during trial, hence, without undergoing the test of cross-examination, no
credence could be given to her statement recorded under Section 164, Cr.P.C. This
flaw in the case has seriously undermined the prosecution case.
(c) Criminal Procedure Code, 1898…S.302 PPC—re-appraisal of evidence—The
retracted confession was not corroborated by an iota evidence, hence, the same
could not be relied upon for sustaining conviction on a capital charge.
(d) Criminal Procedure Code, 1898.…S.302 PPC—re-appraisal of evidence—Since
testimony of SHO who allegedly recovered knife/Churri at the time of arrest of the
appellant was disbelieved, hence, no credence could be given to such type of recovery.
In addition a specific dagger—was mentioned by the complainant but later, recovery of
ordinary kitchen knife with broke tip reflected in recovery memo Ex.Pw-3/1 as well as
FSL report Ex-PZ.
(Appeal was allowed, conviction awarded to the appellant was set aside and the appellant was acquitted in the circumstances).

case_info: W.P No. 467-M of 2022 Najmuddin  Vs  Federation of Pakistan
remarks:
Court set aside the dismissal order of Najmuddin, a former Officer Grade-II of the National Bank of Pakistan, and reinstated him into service. The court held that the dismissal was vitiated by procedural
irregularities, including lack of fair trial and due process, as the petitioner wasn`t confronted with specific allegations and wasn`t allowed to cross-examine witnesses. The court also found the penalty disproportionate to the alleged misconduct, emphasizing principles of proportionality and fairness. The Bank is allowed to hold a fresh inquiry, ensuring due process and proportionality in awarding penalty,
with back benefits to abide by the outcome.

case_info: W.P No. 8365-P of 2025 Colonel (Retd) Dr. Gulzar Ahmad and another  Vs  Medical Teaching Institution Mardan etc.
remarks:
(a)	In a case when vested rights are created under an earlier notification then same may not be unilaterally withdrawn by an authority exercising its locus poenitentiae under section 21 of the General Clauses Act.
(b)	When an act of Departmental Authority is found to be so unreasonable that a man of ordinary prudence culd not find it logical in any sense, then same would furnish a ground for judicial review of administrative actions.

case_info: W.P No. 781-B of 2025 Zafar Ullah  Vs  Chairman, BISE, Bannu, etc
remarks:
Writ Petition
Held: Constitution of Pakistan, 1973---Article 199---Improvement Attempts---Rule 20, Chapter-V Part-II,
Calendar of Boards of Intermediate and Secondary Education, Khyber Pakhtunkhwa---KPBCC Uniform
Policy---Issuance of Roll Number Slip---Legitimate Expectation---Estoppel against Statute---Fundamental
Rights---Petitioner assailed action of BISE Bannu in withholding his HSSC improvement result with the
remark “Multiple Improvements Not Allowed”---Petitioner contended that Board had accepted his
improvement form, issued Roll No.802724 and permitted him to sit in Annual-II Examination 2024,
therefore, a legitimate expectation arose that result would be declared---Held, Rule 20 of Chapter-V, Part-II
of the Calendar of Boards restricts improvement to two successive examinations, whether annual or
supplementary, before taking the next higher examination---Right of improvement is a limited statutory
entitlement and cannot be exercised beyond the manner expressly prescribed---Record showed petitioner had already availed two improvement attempts (Annual-II 2023 and Annual-I 2024), appearance in Annual-II 2024 constituted third attempt, falling outside permissible scope---KPBCC in its 239 th meeting held on
29.05.2023 adopted a uniform province-wide policy restrictive improvements to two attempts, except for
third-division cases; this policy was reaffirmed in the 49 th Board Meeting of BISE Bannu on 03.11.2023 and again in the 268 th meeting of the KPBCC held on 12.10.2025, thereby binding all Boards in the province to a uniform standard---Such academic policy decisions are within the exclusive domain of statutory educational bodies and warrant judicial deference---Issuance of Roll Number Slip contrary to rules does not create any vested right or legitimate expectation---Estoppel cannot operate against statute---No violation of Articles 4, 10A or 25 Constitution established, as due process and equality operate within the framework of valid statutory regulation---Petitioner having exhausted his statutory improvement attempts, his third attempt was without lawful sanction---Board rightly withheld petitioner’s result---Writ petition dismissed; interim relief vacated.

case_info: W.P No. 405-B of 2020 Bibi Amina  Vs  D.P.O Karak & others
remarks:
Criminal Procedure Code (V of 1898)---
--- Ss. 22-A & 561-A--- Quashing of Order --- Constitutional petition filed under Article
199 of the constitution of Islamic Republic of Pakistan’ 1973 --- Petitioner seeks the
quashment of order dated 09.04.2020 passed by the learned Ex-Officio Justice of
Peace /Additional Session Judge-II, Karak, whereby the application under section 22-A
(6), Cr.P.C, have frequently been misused. The legislature never intended that police
officials, who take action in lawful discharge of their duties, be subjected to harassment
through such applications. Courts must exercise caution and ensure that such
applications are not entertained mechanically. --- we find no illegality or irregularity in
the impugned judgment of learned Justice of Peace --- It would be open to the
complainant to file direct complaint against respondent if so directed --- Impugned order
was maintained, in circumstances.

case_info: Cr. A No. 413-P of 2024 Asif   Vs  The State etc
remarks:
i.	This case is singular, both in its appreciation and in its evidentiary complexion as on one hand, it stands on the account of the eyewitnesses; while on the other, the evidence procured through modern technological devices. The core question before this Court is whether these two strands of evidence travel in harmony, and whether what the witnesses claim to have seen accords with what is depicted in the video recordings obtained by the investigating officer from the shop at the premises.
ii.	There can be no dispute with the proposition that evidence collected through modern technological means possesses evidentiary worth and relevance. Yet, such evidence cannot be assessed in isolation, particularly where the ocular account is tainted by concealment, contradiction and partial disclosure of truth.
iii.	In this backdrop, the Court is persuaded to hold that although one segment of the prosecution evidence i.e. the ocular account, fails to inspire confidence and is liable to be discarded, such rejection does not inevitably attract the doctrine of falsus in uno, falsus in omnibus.
iv.	It is now well settled that electronic evidence, if found to be genuine, lawfully collected, properly preserved, and proved in accordance with law, may be relied upon even in the absence of trustworthy testimony of eye witnesses. The crucial enquiry, therefore, is not whether the oral evidence has failed, but whether the electronic evidence itself meets the legal standards of reliability and admissibility.
v.	It would, therefore, be incorrect to assume that once electronic evidence is produced, it automatically becomes conclusive. Equally, non-compliance with the safeguards laid down in Ishtiaq Ahmed Mirza’s case cannot be cured by a mechanical reliance upon Zahir Zakir Jaffar’s case. The latter presupposes the former; it does not replace it.
vi.	Once witnesses admittedly erred in identifying the assailants and conceded their mistake upon viewing the video recording, their testimony ceases to be a dependable foundation. In this setting, the prosecution cannot selectively rely upon the same witnesses to authenticate, interpret, or identify persons in the electronic footage.
vii.	This Court is mindful of the evolving role of electronic and digital evidence in contemporary criminal adjudication. It is now well accepted that, where lawfully collected and properly preserved, such evidence may, in appropriate cases, form the sole basis of conviction, but this relaxation, however, is neither automatic nor unconditional.
viii.	The jurisprudence developed by this Court beginning with Ishtiaq Ahmed Mirza’s case and subsequently, refined and consolidated in Zahir Zakir Jaffar’s case, clearly delineates the parameters that must be satisfied before such evidence can be safely relied upon.
ix.	The conduct of the eyewitnesses, coupled with the inherent contradictions in their statements, reveals malafide considerations and to falsely implicate the appellant. Evidence emanating from such a tainted source, even if taken at its highest, cannot be afforded any serious evidentiary value.

case_info: Cr.A No. 64-D of 2025 Kaleem Ullah  Vs  The State etc
remarks:
(Appeal against conviction)
(a) Criminal Procedure Code, 1898….S.302 PPC—re-appraisal of evidence—The
allegations against the appellant was that he fired husband of the complainant due to which her
husband and brother of accused received fatal injuries and died. Presence of the complainant
and alleged witness was not established from the evidence produced by the prosecution. The
alleged eyewitness was subsequently introduced. Testimony of both the alleged eyewitnesses
was not corroborated by any other independent evidence.
(b) Criminal Procedure Code, 1898…S.302 PPC—re-appraisal of evidence— Statement
of Mst. Bakht Bibi, mother of deceased Irfan Ullah, under Section 164/364, Cr.P.C.
was recorded before the Judicial Magistrate after one and half year of the occurrence,
however, she was not examined during trial and the prosecution failed to justify the
reason for not producing her as an eye-witness of the occurrence or the reason for
recording her statement after 1½ years of the occurrence.
(c) Criminal Procedure Code, 1898…S.302 PPC—re-appraisal of evidence—No
evidence was led by the prosecution which could suggest that how and by whom, Irfan
Ullah deceased then injured was shifted from the spot to the hospital.
(d) Criminal Procedure Code, 1898.…S.302 PPC—re-appraisal of evidence—No
independent witness was associated with the recovery of crime empties, hence, no
weight could be given to positive report of FSL, particularly, when no crime weapon
was recovered.
(e) Criminal Procedure Code, 1898. .…S.302 PPC—re-appraisal of evidence—Motive
setup in the FIR was not proved.
(Appeal was allowed, conviction awarded to the appellant was set aside and the appellant was acquitted in the circumstances).

case_info: Cr.A No. 66-D of 2024 Muhammad Zubair  Vs  The State etc
remarks:
(Appeal against conviction)
(a) Criminal Procedure Code, 1898….S.302/34 PPC—re-appraisal of
evidence—Accused was charged for committing the murder of brother-in-law of the
complainant. The occurrence was un-witnessed and there was inordinate delay in
reporting the matter, hence, possibility of false implication could not be ruled out.
(b) Criminal Procedure Code, 1898…S.302/34 PPC—re-appraisal of evidence—
As per the testimony of Medical Officer, the injured was in critical condition, hence,
disclosure of the name of accused to the complainant was a mystery.
(c) Criminal Procedure Code, 1898…S.302/34 PPC—re-appraisal of
evidence—The testimonies of Pw-9 and Pw-10 could not be relied upon as none from
the persons mentioned by them in their statements come forward in support of their
depositions.
(d) Criminal Procedure Code, 1898.…S.302/34 PPC—re-appraisal of
evidence—Ehsan Ullah, who, as per deposition of the doctor Pw-7, had brought the
injured to the hospital, was not cited as a witness.
(e) Criminal Procedure Code, 1898.…S.302/34 PPC—re-appraisal of
evidence—The driver in whose rickshaw initially the injured was shifted to Jadeed
Pull, Panyala, was not cited as a witness.
(f) Criminal Procedure Code, 1898.…S.302/34 PPC—re-appraisal of
evidence—Motive as alleged by the prosecution was not proved.
(Appeal was allowed and the accused was acquitted in the circumstances).

case_info: Cr.R No. 51-D of 2025 Naveed Khan  Vs  The State
remarks:
(Criminal Revision under Section 435 & 439 Cr.P.C. Converted into criminal appeal)
1. Improper Recording of Plea of Guilt. Accused was arrested with 1050
grams charas and was convicted by the trial Court on the basis of plea of
guilt under S.265-E(2), Cr.P.C. after he had earlier denied the charge and
claimed trial. ---Held --- once the accused denies the charge and the trial
commences, the Court must proceed under S.265-F, Cr.P.C. to record
prosecution evidence. The trial Court cannot revert back to S.265-E to
record a plea of guilt after the accused has pleaded not guilty. Any
admission of guilt at a later stage can only be considered in the statement of
the accused under S.342, Cr.P.C. after completion of prosecution
evidencee.
2. Right of Appeal and Condonation of Delay. Right of appeal against
conviction is a fundamental and statutory right connected with the
protection of life and liberty under Article 9 of the Constitution of Pakistan,
1973. Therefore, technical objections like limitation cannot defeat the right
of a convict to challenge an unlawful conviction, particularly where the
conviction itself appears illegal. Consequently, the criminal revision was
converted into criminal appeal and delay was condoned.
3. Article 10-A of the Constitution of Pakistan. Convicting the accused on a
plea of guilt recorded at an improper procedural stage without recording
prosecution evidence violates the right to fair trial guaranteed under Article
10-A of the Constitution. It was the duty of trial court that before convicting
the accused on admission of guilt, to ensure:
a. the accused is mentally fit and fully aware of consequences,
b. the plea is voluntary and free from coercion or promise,
c. proper judicial safeguards and questioning are followed.
4. Section 412 Cr.P.C. Section 412 Cr.P.C. bars an appeal against conviction
based on a plea of guilt, --- Held --- this bar does not apply where the plea
of guilt itself was recorded in violation of law or fundamental rights. The
High Court can still examine the legality of conviction.
5. Benefit of Doubt: It is settled principle of criminal law, that even a single
reasonable doubt is sufficient to acquit an accused, and such benefit must
be given as a matter of right, not grace. On merits, there were serious
discrepancies in the prosecution case, particularly regarding:
a. preparation and custody of narcotics samples,
b. defective entries in Register No.19,
c. unexplained delay in sending samples to the forensic laboratory
which created reasonable doubt about the safe custody and transmission of
the case property, therefore, accused was entitled to be acquitted
(The Criminal Revision was converted into appeal against conviction and allowed. Consequently, the accused was acquitted of the charges)

case_info: B.A No. 526-B of 2025 Bakht Ullah  Vs  The State & other
remarks:
S. 497---Penal Code (XLV of 1860), Ss. 302, 336-B, 338-C, 201---Qatl-i-amd, attempt to
commit Qalt-i-amd. Bail refused. The complainant was not an eyewitness to the incident, on
14-07-2025, Mst. Ishal Bibi, a minor daughter of the deceased, appeared before the learned
Judicial Magistrate-II, Bannu, and recorded her statement under Section 164, Cr.P.C. In her
statement, she deposed that her father (the petitioner/accused) first assaulted her mother and
thereafter poured petrol on her and set her ablaze. Furthermore, the local police recovered
from the scene two pieces of burnt clothing belonging to the deceased, a 1½-liter bottle
containing approximately one-tenth of petrol, and a small lighter. The recovery of these items
aligns with the allegations leveled against the petitioner/accused and provides corroboration in
support of the prosecution’s version. The offences with which the petitioner/accused stands
charged squarely falls within the prohibitory clause of Section 497, Cr.P.C. It is well-settled that
at the bail stage, only a tentative assessment is permissible to determine whether reasonable
grounds exist for believing that the accused has committed the alleged offence. Where such
grounds are found to exist, bail is liable to be refused.

case_info: C.R No. 293-M of 2016 Mumtaz Ali  Vs  Abdul Wakil
remarks:
The Court held that upon the death of the petitioner, the right to continue the revision petition survived to his legal heirs under Order XXII Rule 1 CPC, as the cause of action was not personal. However, the three sons (respondents No.2-4) could not be substituted as legal representatives because they were already
judgment-debtors against whom the decree had attained finality. Substituting them would create a conflict of interest, as they would be arguing against their own established position and the final decree. The Court emphasized that only heirs with no adverse interest to the deceased’s estate could properly represent
it. The decree-holder’s concession that he was not pursuing the widow, daughters, or the fourth son (Muhammad Ayub) meant that no relief was sought against them, so they need not be substituted for the purpose of the revision. The revision petition was disposed of accordingly. The impugned judgments and
decrees were set aside with respect to the widow, daughters, and fourth son (Muhammad Ayub) of the deceased petitioner. The decree for recovery of 122 tola 8 masha gold or its market value was upheld and remained fully operative against the three sons (respondents No.2-4).

case_info: Cr. A. No. 178-P of 2025  Arshid Jaman  Vs  The State etc
remarks:
A.	It is beyond dispute that four innocent persons perished in the tragic incident. Equally incontrovertible, however, is the legal proposition that the number of victims, even if large, cannot, of itself, sustain a conviction. The prosecution remains under a continuing obligation to collect and lead independent, reliable evidence in support of its case and to produce such witnesses as are necessary to establish the guilt of the accused beyond reasonable doubt.
B.	The more reasonable inference is that the dead bodies were found at the scene, the police prepared the relevant documents, dispatched the bodies, and only thereafter the matter was formally reported. In this background, this Court is inclined to hold that the complainant did not disclose the complete truth, and the scribe too failed to demonstrate how the report could have been made in the manner asserted.
C.	The Investigating Officer himself, though recovering bloodstains from the room and the bathroom, made no meaningful effort to record the statements of the inmates of the house. Even more surprising is the fact that the prosecution, at a later stage, chose not to examine certain male and female inmates, explaining away their absence merely on account of their close relationship with the accused.
D.	In these circumstances, the prosecution cannot be permitted to rest its entire case upon the solitary testimony of the complainant, whose own account is riddled with inconsistencies, whose presence at the scene is doubtful, and whose stated time of reporting stands contradicted by the medical evidence.
E.	Although it appears that the Magistrate attempted to comply with the statutory safeguards, this Court cannot ignore the admitted fact that at the time of recording of the confession, the accused continued to remain in police remand, and was returned to the custody of the very investigating officer who had produced him. This circumstance casts a serious shadow on the voluntariness of the confession.
F.	When the alleged confessional statement of the appellant is examined in juxtaposition with the testimony of the complainant, it becomes evident that the two accounts are mutually irreconcilable.
G.	Equally perplexing is what relationship, if any, existed between the deceased and the female inmates of the house, particularly the mother and sister-in-law of the appellant, that would justify their alleged familiarity with his presence outside the premises.

case_info: W.P No. 172-B of 2024 Syed Akber Khan  Vs  Rehmat Ullah, etc.
remarks:
Writ Petition
Held: Pre-emption---Limitation---Sec. 31, Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Pre-emption Act, 1987---Registered sale
deed---Constructive notice---Date of knowledge---Order VII Rule 11 CPC---Rejection of plaint---Article
199 Constitution---Petitioner challenged judgment whereby Revisional Court set aside order of Trial Court
and rejected plaint under O.VII R.11 CPC in a suit for possession through pre-emption---Record showed that suit property had been transferred through registered sale deeds dated 30.12.2022 and 09.06.2023---Suit instituted on 28.08.2023---Held, Section 31 of the Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Pre-emption Act, 1987 provides a special and exhaustive limitation of 120 days; where sale is evidenced by a registered sale deed, clause (a) applies and limitation begins from date of registration, irrespective of alleged late knowledge---Clause (d) “date of knowledge” is residual and cannot be invoked where transaction falls within clause (a)--- Registration of sale deed constitutes constructive notice to all pre-emptors; plea of oral sale or later knowledge cannot extend limitation---Non-compliance with public notice requirement under Section 32 of the Act does not enlarge limitation---Trial Court erred in treating transaction as oral and applying clause (d); Revisional Court rightly held suit to be ex facie time-barred and rejected plaint---Impugned judgment free from illegality, material irregularity, misreading or non-reading---No ground to interfere in constitutional jurisdiction. Petition dismissed.

case_info: W.P No. 664-B of 2023 Adnan Ahmad  Vs  Muhammad Salim Khan
remarks:
Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Pre-emption Act (X of 1987)---
----S. 13---Pre-emption suit---inserting Khata No.73, Khasra No.380 and Khata No.73 along with
Khasra No.379 in the plaint---Plaintiff moved an application for correction/insertion of Khata and
Khasra numbers in the plaint which was dismissed by the Trial Court as well as Revisional court---
Validity---the petitioner sought pre-emption only in respect of Khata No.72, Khasra No.443, measuring
1½ kanal, part of Mutation No.3352. However, Mutation No.3352 reflects transfer of land from three
distinct Khatas, i.e. Khata No.69, Khasra No.380 measuring 1 kanal 3 marlas, Khata No.72, Khasra
No.443 measuring 1½ kanal and Khata No.73, Khasra No.379 measuring 1 kanal 4½ marlas. Neither
Khata No.69 Khasra No.380, nor Khata No.73/Khasra No.379, was mentioned in the plaint, nor were
they mentioned in the notice Talb-e-Ishhad, which is a mandatory prerequisite under the law of pre-
emption. The omission from notice Talb-e-Ishhad is fatal as a claim not asserted in notice Talb-e-Ishhad
cannot be introduced later on through amendment in the plaint. Constitutional Petition was dismissed in
the circumstances.

case_info: E.A No. 01-M of 2025 Barkat Ali  Vs  Akhtar Ali & others
remarks:
Election appeal filed by Barkat Ali against Akhtar Ali, upholding the Election Tribunal`s decision that declared Akhtar Ali the winner of the Local Council Election for General Councilor 17-VC Shalpin, Khwazakhela (Swat). Initially, Barkat Ali won by a margin of 2 votes, but Akhtar Ali challenged the result, citing 472 rejected ballots. The Tribunal ordered a recount, which showed Akhtar Ali won, and the High Court affirmed this decision. The court ruled that the Election Tribunal has inherent jurisdiction to order
recounts in exceptional circumstances, especially when the margin is narrow and rejected ballots are high, applying the principle of \"res ipsa loquitur\".

case_info: W.P No. 2313-P of 2023 Mir Hadad  Vs  Govt: of KPK through Chief Secretary, Peshawar and others
remarks: The Antiquities Act, 1975 addresses a specific and compelling public interest namely the Protection of archaeological and cultural heritage; section 70 thereof employ a non-obstinate clause therefore same Act shall operate notwithstanding anything contained in any other law. In case of safe distance for mining activities around antiquities the provisions of Antiquities Act shall have precedence over the regulatory regime provided in Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Mines and Minerals Act, 2017.

case_info: Cr.R No. 76-B of 2025 Ali Marjan alias Almar Jan  Vs  The State etc.
remarks:
Criminal Revision—Under Sections 435/439 Cr.P.C —Juvenility—Age Determination—.;
Section 8, Juvenile Justice System Act, 2018—CNIC not conclusive proof of age—Contradictory
documentary evidence—Mandatory inquiry—Medical opinion/ossification test.
Where the accused, facing trial under Section 302 PPC, sought a declaration of juvenility, the trial
court dismissed the application on the basis of conflicting documents, i.e., CNIC showing age
below 19 years and arrest card noting age 20/21 years. Held, a CNIC issued by NADRA is not by
itself a reliable or independent determinant of age; when documentary evidence is inconsistent,
the law mandates an inquiry under Section 8 of the Juvenile Justice System Act, 2018. Courts
must not decide the question of juvenility in a cursory manner but are required to undertake a
comprehensive inquiry, including examination of all documents and, where necessary, medical
assessment/ossification test, as held in PLD 2023 SC 536 and 2012 P.Cr.L.J 1032. Trial court
erred in dismissing the application without conducting the mandatory inquiry. Revision accepted;
impugned order set aside; matter remanded with direction to constitute a Standing Medical Board
for age determination in accordance with law.

case_info: W.P No. 113-D of 2025 Saeed Khan  Vs  Mst. Bibi and others
remarks:
Held:
S.25, Guardians and Wards Act, 1890 — Custody of minor —
Paramount consideration — Welfare of minor — Scope —
Held, the welfare of the minor is the paramount and overriding consideration
in proceedings under S.25 of the Guardians and Wards Act, 1890. The term
“welfare” is to be construed in its broadest sense, encompassing the minor’s
moral, spiritual, physical, psychological, educational, emotional and material
well-being. Factors such as the minor’s health, academic progress, religious
upbringing and emotional development are integral to the welfare test. The
minor’s happiness and emotional bonding with the proposed guardian must
also receive due consideration.
— Minor’s preference— Relevance
The minor strongly disliked the father and repeatedly declined to meet him.
Upon appearance before the Court, she unequivocally expressed her desire
to live with her mother. Such preference, being consistent and reflective of
her emotional comfort, could not be disregarded.
— Father serving in the Armed Forces — Effect on custody —
Petitioner, being a serving official in the Pakistan Army, was frequently away
from home for long durations, thereby limiting his ability to attend to the
minor’s daily needs. In such circumstances, the minor would inevitably
remain dependent on the paternal grandparents. Welfare of the minor was
better secured with the mother.
— Mother’s remarriage — Whether disqualification —
Mere remarriage does not constitute a disqualification unless specific
detriment to the minor is shown. No adverse material was produced;
apprehensions of the petitioner were speculative and unfounded.
— Constitutional jurisdiction—Interference
No misreading or non-reading of evidence, nor any jurisdictional error
established to warrant interference by the Court.
Result: Petition dismissed.

case_info: C.R No. 16-A of 2014 Muhammad Suleman etc  Vs  Mst. Tasneem Akhtar (widow & others)
remarks:
a) Once an entry is proved illegal, the previous lawful entry stands
restored. An illegal entry has no life of its own. It cannot displace a
valid one.
b) If the basic structure is illegal, the super structure raised upon it is also
illegal.
c) Every fresh wrong entry creates a fresh cause of action for a person in
possession. Possession may be actual or symbolic.

case_info: C.M (T.A) No. 01-D of 2025 Nadeem Sohail  Vs  Mst Naghmana Tabassum
remarks:
Transfer of family suit – Section 24 C.P.C. read with Section 25-A, West Pakistan Family Courts
Act, 1964 – Earlier transfer application finally decided; Court had directed virtual proceedings to
avoid confrontation and ensure petitioner’s safe participation – Present application based on
identical allegations including alleged threats already examined in earlier proceedings – No new,
supervening, or exceptional circumstance disclosed – Objection that virtual cross-examination
compromises fairness held misconceived; virtual hearings are lawful, effective, and subject to
judicial safeguards – Access to certified copies and case record available under procedural rules –
Convenience of female litigant paramount; her residence determines Family Court jurisdiction –
Repetition of applications amounts to forum-shopping and delaying tactics – No demonstrable
likelihood of injustice – Application dismissed.

case_info: Cr.A No. 179-A of 2024 Ajmal  Vs  The State
remarks:
(a) When there is admitted prior enmity between the parties, any unexplained
delay in lodging the FIR assumes greater significance. Such delay is often
treated as fatal to the prosecution case because it gives rise to a reasonable
inference that the complainant may have had sufficient time for deliberation,
consultation, or embellishment before approaching the authorities. In these
circumstances, the possibility of a false implication or improvement in the
narrative cannot be ruled out, thereby weakening the evidentiary value of the
FIR.”
(b) In criminal jurisprudence, the testimony of chance or interested witnesses
is viewed with heightened caution because their presence at the crime scene
may be unusual or they may have a personal stake in the case. Courts
therefore require that their presence be explained naturally and convincingly,
and that their statements be supported by independent corroboration—such as
medical evidence, forensic findings, or other reliable circumstances—before
relying on them for conviction. While such evidence is not rejected outright, it
must inspire confidence and align with the broader factual matrix of the case,
consistent with constitutional principles of fair trial and due process.
(c) As a rule of criminal jurisprudence, prosecution evidence is not tested on
the basis of quantity but quality of the evidence. It is not that who is giving the
evidence and making statement; what is relevant is what statement has been
given. It is not the person but the statement of that person which is to be seen
and adjudged.
(d) The unnatural or inconsistent conduct of the prosecution witnesses casts
serious doubt on the credibility of their testimony. When the behaviour of a
witness is at variance with what would be expected in the ordinary course of
human conduct, the courts view such evidence with caution. In such
circumstances, their testimony becomes inherently doubtful, and it would be
unsafe to base a conviction solely upon such evidence without strong,
independent corroboration. The law requires that the prosecution must
establish its case beyond reasonable doubt, and when the conduct of material
witnesses itself appears suspicious or improbable, the benefit of doubt must
necessarily go to the accused.
(e) When the medical evidence is in direct and material conflict with the ocular
account, and the prosecution is unable to satisfactorily reconcile or explain the
inconsistency, the credibility of the prosecution version becomes gravely
undermined. Medical evidence, being scientific and objective in nature, often
serves as a reliable check on the veracity of eyewitness testimony. Therefore,
where the medical findings render the ocular version improbable or impossible,
the courts are justified in treating the prosecution case with suspicion. In such
circumstances, the prosecution fails to discharge its burden of proving the case
beyond reasonable doubt. Consequently, the benefit of such unresolved
contradictions must necessarily enure to the accused, in accordance with the
settled principles of criminal jurisprudence and the constitutional mandate that
no person shall be convicted on doubtful or shaky evidence.
(f) In criminal jurisprudence, reliance on circumstantial evidence requires a
complete chain of facts connecting the accused to the offence beyond
reasonable doubt. In the present case, the missing links in the forensic and
circumstantial evidence create serious doubt regarding the involvement of the
appellant. In view of these factors, the alleged recovery and related
circumstantial material cannot be treated as reliable evidence to sustain a
conviction, and without independent corroboration, reliance on such evidence
would be unsafe and unjust.
(g) It is well-settled that if direct evidence is doubtful and motive is not
established, the prosecution case cannot stand.
(h) Article 129(g) of the Qanun-e-Shahadat Order, 1984 allows the court to
draw an adverse inference when a party withholds the best available evidence
that could reasonably be produced. The principle is that when relevant,
material, and superior evidence is intentionally kept back—such as
eyewitnesses, medical documents, forensic reports, or official records—the
court may presume that the evidence, if produced, would have gone against
the party withholding it. Thus, if the prosecution fails to present the most
reliable evidence without a valid explanation, courts may extend the benefit of
doubt to the accused.

case_info: Quashment Petition No. 53-P of 2025 Mst. Shaima and 02 others  Vs  Muhammad Ali, etc
remarks:
i. If a document is produced or given in evidence in any proceedings
before a Court of law and if the opposite party considers such
document to be a fake and fictitious document then any complaint
filed by such opposite party/private person would not be
maintainable as under section 195 (c) of the Cr.P.C, it is for that
Court to file a complaint in this regard.
ii. Even otherwise, a complaint with respect to the using or giving of a
document allegedly being fake and fictitious could not be
maintainable in pending proceedings as the same complaint would
be premature being based on a subjudice document as the Court has
yet to form its opinion qua its genuineness or otherwise.
iii. Where proceedings have been initiated in a court of law, which are
against the mode and manner in which the law requires to be carried
out then such proceedings would not be sustainable and the same
would be liable to be quashed.

case_info: Cr.A No. 37-D of 2025 The State Vs  Khuda Bakhsh
remarks:
S.409, PPC; S.5(2), Prevention of Corruption Act – Acquittal under S.265-K Cr.P.C. – Scope –
Interference in appeal against acquittal.
Held: Prosecution witnesses, including the complainant, categorically absolved the accused of any
corruption, misappropriation, or misuse of authority. Material on record failed to establish even a
remote probability of conviction. Trial Court correctly exercised powers under S.265-K Cr.P.C. to
prevent a futile trial. No misreading, non-reading, perversity or illegality demonstrated to justify
interference under S.417 Cr.P.C. Appeal dismissed. Acquittal maintained.

case_info: Cr.A No. 1565-P of 2019 The State  Vs  Junaid Khan
remarks:
In a case involving a single accused, and where the complainant is the biological father of the respondent, the possibility of substitution is extraordinarily remote. It is further acknowledged that the complainant shared a close relationship with the deceased, yet, this does not diminish the inherent credibility arising from his equally close and natural relationship with the respondent.
The witnesses, weighing the consequences, evidently chose not to support the complainant, as doing so would expose their real brother to a capital charge for the murder of their stepmother. Regrettably, in our societal structure, stepmothers are often viewed through a different emotional lens, frequently colored by indifference or latent hostility. This observation is not confined to the facts of the present case but reflects the broader psyche prevalent in this region. In such a milieu, the present matter required a nuanced and context-sensitive judicial appreciation.
It is true that the eyewitness, on whose pointation the site plan was initially prepared, was declared as a won over and not produced before the learned trial Court, however, it is equally true that the complainant subsequently verified the site plan, therefore, the non-production of the said witness does not materially affect the case of the prosecution, as the Investigating Officer confirmed the preparation of the site plan and its subsequent verification by the complainant before the learned trial Court.
In such circumstances, this Court cannot, on the mere strength of an isolated medical assertion, fasten criminal liability upon the respondent for the death of the unborn child. The law mandates that the prosecution must affirmatively establish its case through credible and substantive evidence, conjectures, however distressing, cannot substitute proof. While the allegation evokes sympathy, judicial decisions cannot be shaped by emotion.
Why should a judgment that is arbitrary in its reasoning, mechanical in its approach, and which confers an unwarranted advantage upon an accused, merely on the pretext of a supposed double presumption of innocence, be allowed to remain in the field. The rule of presumption is not a shield for perpetuating illegality, nor is it intended to sanctify conclusions arrived at in disregard of evidence.
The jurisprudence is well-settled that though an acquittal carries weight, it is not immune from interference where the findings are artificial, speculative, or wholly unsupported by the record.

case_info: W.P No. 7655-P of 2025 Rana Shabbir Hussain  Vs  Inspector General of Police and others
remarks:
i. Where a person is accused of the commission of an offence
by reason of any act done by him at one place, and when
such offence has consequences in another place as well then
such offence may be inquired into and tried at either of the
places i.e. the place where the offence was initially committed
as well as at the place where its consequences occurred.
ii. In the present case, both the parties belong to Gujranwala,
Province of Punjab, and the cheque was issued at
Gujranwala, however, the same was presented at Peshawar,
therefore, the offence under section 489-F PPC could be
inquired into and tried either at Gujranwala or at Peshawar,
and the payee/drawee has the option to initiate such inquiry at
either of the place.

case_info: Cr.A No. 344-A of 2023 Khalid Mehmood  Vs  The State
remarks:
(a) Substitution of the real culprits—especially in cases where eyewitnesses have seen
their close relatives killed before their own eyes—is an extremely rare phenomenon.
(b) The prosecution`s evidence is to be evaluated on the touchstone of quality rather
than quantity. The credibility of testimony depends on its intrinsic worth, not on the
identity or social status of the witness.
(c) It is well-settled law that the prompt lodging of an FIR serves as a strong guarantee
of truth, particularly where the complainant brings the dead body directly to the
hospital without any intervening delay.
(d) The medico-legal evidence lends strong support to the ocular account. It establishes
that the cause of death was a direct firearm injury to the heart and left lung, resulting in
haemorrhagic shock and cardio-pulmonary arrest. The nature, direction, and location of
the injuries fully align with the prosecution’s allegation that the deceased was shot
from close range. The injury was sufficient in the ordinary course of nature to cause
death, satisfying the requirements of Section 302(b) PPC. The post-mortem report,
injury sheet, and inquest report corroborate the narrative of instantaneous death at the
spot. No contradiction, inconsistency, or gap was identified in the medical evidence,
and the defence failed to cast any doubt upon it. In addition, the ballistic matching
constitutes a highly incriminating piece of evidence directly connecting the appellant
with the offence, particularly when viewed alongside the consistent ocular account.
(e) It is settled law that abscondence alone is not sufficient to secure a conviction.
However, it is a strong corroborative circumstance when coznsidered alongside direct
and circumstantial evidence. Where an accused remains a fugitive for a considerable
period without offering any plausible explanation, such conduct becomes indicative of
guilt when read in conjunction with reliable ocular and circumstantial evidence.
(f) It is a well-established principle that testimony of related or interested witnesses
cannot be discarded merely because of their relationship with the deceased, provided
their statements are otherwise confidence-inspiring, coherent, and consistent on all
material particulars.
(g) The occurrence took place in 2013, and most of the depositions were recorded
several years later. Minor inconsistencies regarding timing or peripheral details are
natural due to the lapse of time. The courts must discourage “parrot-like” statements;
therefore, minor discrepancies that do not affect the core of the prosecution case should
not be given undue importance and are to be ignored.
(h) Motive becomes irrelevant once the prosecution proves the occurrence through
reliable direct evidence. Failure to prove motive may be treated as a mitigating factor
for reduction of sentence, but not as a ground for acquittal.
(i) The appellant chose not to appear as a witness under Section 340(2) Cr.P.C.,
thereby foregoing an opportunity to rebut the prosecution`s strong evidence. His simple
denial under Section 342 Cr.P.C. carries no evidentiary value unless supported by
defence evidence, which is entirely absent in the present case.

case_info: Cr.A No. 143-B of 2024 Taj Muhammad  Vs  The State etc
remarks:
Penal Code (XLV of 1860) — Ss. 302, 311 — Criminal Procedure Code (V of 1898) — Ss. 164, 364—
Qatl-e-Amd — Compromise — Honour killing allegation — Scope of S. 311, P.P.C. — Fisad-fil-Arz —
Retracted confession — Appreciation of evidence.
Accused not nominated in FIR; complainant charged unknown assailant. Sole legal heirs of deceased
appeared before Court and verified a genuine, voluntary compromise, waiving Qisas and Diyat.
Confessional statement, recorded belatedly while accused was already in custody, stood retracted, lacked
corroboration, and could not be safely treated as voluntary after compromise. Allegation of honour killing
was unsupported by any independent or tangible evidence; mere oral assertion does not establish Fisad-fil-Arz. Nothing on record showing brutality, habitual criminality, or social disruption as required under S.
311, P.P.C. Trial Court erred by invoking S. 311 without proof of its statutory ingredients. Reliance placed on Iqrar Hussain (2014 SCMR 1155), Ghulam Nabi (2020 YLR 1069), and Samreen (2015 PCrLJ 753). Held: Prosecution failed to prove charge under S. 311 beyond reasonable doubt — Appeal allowed;
conviction set aside; accused acquitted.

case_info: W.P No. 698-B of 2022 Khusdil Khan & others  Vs  Government of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa through Inspector General of Police Peshawar and others
remarks:
(a) Criminal Procedure Code (V of 1898)---Ss. 22-A (6) [as amended by
Criminal Procedure (Third Amendment) Ordinance (CXXXI of 2002)]---
Justice of Peace---Object, purpose and scope---Role of Justice of Peace
in Foreign Jurisdictions---Justice of Peace, in Pakistan---It is noteworthy
that the learned Justice of Peace observed that the petitioner had earlier
filed a complaint against petitioners (the then respondent’s No. 3 and 4)
before the RPO Bannu Division which was forwarded to the S.H.O.,
Police Station Haved, who then entrusted it to IHC Abdur Rehman for
inquiry, however, the respondent/complainant did not disclose this fact in
his petition, instead he only stated that on 27-08-2022 he visited Police
Station Haved to lodge an FIR against the petitioners and submitted a
complaint to respondent No.1, the S.H.O. of that police station.
Furthermore, in his petition under Section 22-A(6) Cr.P.C., the
respondent/complainant arrayed the DSP Circle Haved as respondent
No.2, however, neither the petition nor the entire case file reflects any
allegation explaining what illegality or misconduct was committed by the
DSP to justify his inclusion as a respondent. The accusations, therefore,
rest entirely upon verbal assertions unsupported by any documentary or
corroborative evidence. The learned Justice of Peace also relied upon an
observation that, according to an inquiry report allegedly conducted by
one Abdur Rehman, IHC, the petitioners were “notorious smugglers” and
“influential persons.” This Court has carefully perused the entire file and
finds no such material on record to substantiate that the petitioners are
involved in narcotics smuggling. Even assuming for the sake of argument
that the petitioners have a questionable background, there remains no
nexus between them and the present allegations, as the complainant has
failed to establish that the alleged occurrence was committed by them.
The writ petition is allowed.

case_info: W.P No. 729-B of 2025 Saleem Khan through Anwar Khan  Vs  The Provincial Police Officer, Khyber Pakhtunkhwa, etc
remarks:
Writ Petition
Held: Constitution of Pakistan, 1973---Article 199---Habeas Corpus---Section 491 Cr.P.C.---High Court
Rules & Orders, Chapter 4-F Vol-V---Bailiff’s Report---Alleged Illegal Detention---Misrepresentation of
Facts---Sessions Court’s Duty to Ascertain Truth---Summary Dismissal of Petition---Issuance of Rule Nisi--
-Scope of inquiry under Section 491 Cr.P.C.---Petitioner alleged that his son Saleem Khan had been taken
from home by SHO Kakki and kept at an unknown location; Bailiff recovered detainee handcuffed inside
police station but reported that SHO snatched him away and shifted him elsewhere; SHO denied allegations,claiming he was on exhumation duty---Bailiff’s report found self-contradictory, casually drafted, containing incorrect names, and not reflective of a properly completed recovery process---Sessions Judge treated Bailiff’s report as complaint but dismissed proceedings as “settled” without probing truth, despite apparent falsehood either by Bailiff or police---Held, where Bailiff claims recovery and subsequent removal of detainee, and police deny same, a clear conflict exists requiring judicial fact-finding; lower court was legally bound to determine which party misrepresented facts by recording evidence, allowing cross-examination, and deciding on merits---Petition under Section 491 Cr.P.C. cannot be dismissed summarily when liberty of citizen is at stake; Rules 1-18 of Chapter 4-F Vol-V of High Court Rules & Orders apply equally to habeas petitions under Article 199; proper statutory procedure not followed---Impugned order dated 02.10.2025 set aside; petition under Section 491 Cr.P.C. restored to file and deemed pending---Directions issued to lower court to ascertain true facts, take strict action against anyone found misrepresenting the situation, and decide application on merits within 30 days---Writ petition allowed.

case_info: Cr.A No. 25-B of 2024 Usman Ghani  Vs  The State etc
remarks:
HEAD NOTE
Criminal Appeal—Murder—Solitary injured witness—Interested witness—Material
contradictions—Medical evidence—Defective investigation—Motive—Benefit of doubt—Acquittal.
The appellant was convicted by the trial Court under sections 302(b)/34, 324/34 and 337-F(ii)/34, P.P.C.
and sentenced to life imprisonment along with other ancillary sentences. The conviction was primarily
based on the testimony of a solitary injured complainant, who was also the son of the deceased.
Held: Testimony of an interested and inimical witness, particularly when standing alone, requires strict
scrutiny and independent corroboration. In the present case, the ocular account suffered from material
contradictions and unexplained improvements regarding the manner of occurrence, relative positioning of
the parties, distances, surrounding features of the place of occurrence and post-occurrence events. The
ocular version was found inconsistent with the site plan and was not fully corroborated by medical
evidence, which ruled out close-range firing and suggested injuries possibly inflicted by a single assailant.
The investigation was tainted by serious lapses, including non-association of independent witnesses despite availability, failure to record statements of police officials present at the spot, post-occurrence modification of the site plan at the instance of an interested witness, doubtful recoveries without independent mashirs, and failure to reconcile physical evidence with the prosecution story. Such deficiencies materially impaired the credibility of the prosecution case.
Motive was not established against the present appellant and was admittedly confined to the absconding co-accused, which further weakened the prosecution case in a matter resting on solitary ocular testimony.
It is a settled principle of criminal jurisprudence that the prosecution must prove its case beyond reasonable doubt, and even a single circumstance creating reasonable doubt entitles the accused to acquittal as a matter of right. The cumulative effect of unreliable ocular testimony, lack of convincing medical corroboration and defective investigation rendered the prosecution case unsafe for sustaining conviction.
 Criminal appeal allowed; conviction and sentences set aside; appellant acquitted by benefit of doubt. Criminal revision for enhancement dismissed as infructuous.

case_info: Cr.M (B.A) No. 639-M of 2025 Dawood  Vs  The state and others
remarks:
If, in a case where the bail plea of the accused on the ground of
statutory delay in his trial has earlier been declined with specific
directions to the prosecution to conclude the trial expeditiously, the
prosecution still fails to do so even after the lapse of the statutory
period as provided under the third proviso to Section 497 Cr.P.C.,
the accused would be entitled to the benefit of the said provision of
law by enlarging him on bail.

case_info: C.R No. 59-P of 2018 Aziz Khan & others  Vs  Mst. Shamim Akhtar & others
remarks:
Under section 115 of the Civil Procedure Code, 1908.
 It is by now a well settled principle that res judicata under Section 11 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 is attracted only when the matter directly and substantially in issue has been heard and finally decided by a court of competent jurisdiction between the same parties and on the same cause of action. Mere similarity of issues or the fact that certain pleas were raised earlier, does not suffice to invoke the bar of res judicata unless those pleas were specifically put in issue and adjudicated upon on merits. In the present case, since the earlier court neither framed an issue nor recorded any finding with respect to the ownership claimed on the basis of the unregistered deed and mutation, the controversy cannot be deemed to have been decided previously. Consequently, the bar of res judicata or constructive res judicata does not operate in the circumstances of the case.
The petitioners/plaintiffs based their ownership claim on these documents, contending that both deeds were more than thirty (30) years old and, therefore, a presumption of truth was attached to them under Article 100 of the Qanun-e-Shahadat Order, 1984. However, this contention is legally misconceived. It is a well-settled principle of law that ownership or transfer of immovable property exceeding the value of one hundred rupees can only be effected through a registered instrument in accordance with Section 54 of the Transfer of Property Act, 1882, read with Section 17 of the Registration Act, 1908. An unregistered document purporting to convey title in such property does not confer any proprietary rights nor does it create, transfer, or extinguish any legal interest in the property.
it is equally well settled that a mutation, by its nature, does not create or extinguish ownership rights but merely reflects a fiscal adjustment for revenue purposes. A mutation which has not been sanctioned or incorporated in the record of rights carries no evidentiary value and cannot be used as proof of title.
Mere possession of unregistered documents or an unsanctioned mutation does not vest ownership nor can such defective evidence displace the presumption of correctness attached to the existing revenue entries in favour of the respondent/defendant, unless the same has been proven otherwise by producing cogent, trustworthy, reliable and admissible evidence, which in the present case is seriously lacking and the petitioners / plaintiffs have miserably failed to prove the aforesaid documents, relied upon in support of their case, through confidence inspiring evidence. In absence thereof, there is no occasion to dislodge the lawful entries duly incorporated in favour of the respondent / defendant on the basis of such unsubstantiated documents.

case_info: C.R No. 479-P of 2017 Akhtar Ali Shah and others  Vs  Muhammad Hussain and others
remarks:
1. If an appeal or revision filed against a preliminary decree succeeds, then
it will have the effect of setting-aside of the final decree or even the
execution of such final decree as the final decree is dependent on the
preliminary decree stating and applying what the preliminary decree has
declared and ordained.
2. If a preliminary judgment and decree is under challenged before the
higher forum through an appeal or revision and if during the pendency
of such appeal or revision, a final decree has been passed and if such
final decree has also not been challenged or even if the final decree has
been executed, even then the appeal or revision filed against the
preliminary judgment & decree has to be decided on its own merits.
3. When both parties are recorded as co-owners in a joint property duly
reflected in the revenue record and when the title of any of them is not
disputed, then in a suit for partition, the Court has no other option but to
proceed with the same in accordance with law.
4. Where the Courts below are at variance then the High Court in its
revisional jurisdiction has to analyze and evaluate the evidence of both
the parties and to determine that as to judgment of which Court is
legally sustainable.

case_info: C.R No. 1788-P of 2010 Muhammad Niab Khan and another  Vs  Miss Habib and others
remarks:
i. In an appeal filed u/s 96 C.P.C. against the judgment of the trial Court,
the Appellate Court under the mandate of Order XLI Rule 31 C.P.C., is
required to formulate either its points for determination or make issue-
wise findings with reference to the evidence on record as the Appellate
Court enjoys all the powers of the trial Court as envisaged u/s 107 C.P.C.
ii. It is the duty of the Appellate Court to examine as to whether the trial
Court has properly appreciated the evidence on record in its true
perspective. It must deal with both questions of law and fact arising from
the pleadings, evidence and findings of the trial Court.

case_info: C.R No. 283-P of 2025 Akbar Ali and others  Vs  Mian Shireen and others
remarks:
(i)	Where a transaction has been alleged by a beneficiary of a document from a Parda Nasheen, old and illiterate woman, then such a person has to administered extra care and precaution qua the identity of such a woman, through a close blooded relationship like her father, husband or brother etc. and such person has also to prove that the nature of the transaction was made understood to such woman and if the transaction is of sale, then he has also to prove the payment of sale consideration to her and conversely, if a beneficiary of document could not prove through his evidence, the aforesaid pre-conditions, then such transaction could not be a valid and lawful transaction.
(ii)	Concurrent findings are not sacrosanct and whenever, the High Court in its revisional jurisdiction comes to the conclusion that the same suffer from misreading or non-reading of evidence or mis-application of law of if the same suffer from illegalities or material irregularities, then, it conveniently set them aside.

case_info: B.A No. 493-B of 2025 Ihsanullah  Vs  The State etc
remarks:
Headnote
Criminal Procedure Code (V of 1898) — S.497 (2) — Penal Code (XLV of 1860), Ss.302, 324
& 34 — Bail, grant of — Further inquiry — Plea of alibi — General allegations — No
specific role — Non-recovery of weapon — Benefit of doubt at bail stage.
The petitioner, nominated in the F.I.R. along with co-accused for allegedly committing the
murder of the complainant’s brother, sought post-arrest bail after its denial by the Court below.
The F.I.R. contained only general and collective allegations of firing against the accused persons
without specifying whose shot struck the deceased. The medical evidence revealed a single entry
wound, thereby creating doubt regarding the petitioner’s specific role. Within three days of the
occurrence, the petitioner voluntarily surrendered himself and simultaneously moved an
application before the S.P. (Investigation) taking the plea of alibi, asserting his non-presence at
the spot. Such plea was supported by independent affidavits of two witnesses examined under
S.161, Cr.P.C. No weapon of offence was recovered from his possession, and the recovered
empties from the spot were not connected with him through any forensic evidence.
Held: In view of the general and non-specific allegations, the uncorroborated ocular account, the
plausible plea of alibi raised from the inception, and the absence of any incriminating recovery
from petitioner, the case of the petitioner squarely falls within the ambit of further inquiry as
contemplated under S.497 (2), Cr.P.C. The law is well settled that even at bail stage, the plea of
alibi may be considered when it is taken promptly and finds some support from the record.
Heinousness of the offence alone cannot be a ground to deny bail when the prosecution evidence
itself raises doubts about presence of accused at spot. Consequently, petitioner was admitted to
bail.
Case law referred:
(i) Asmatullah v. The State (PLD 2011 SC 178);
(ii) Zaigham Ashraf v. The State (2016 SCMR 18);
(iii) Khalid Javed Gillani v. The State (PLD 1978 SC 256);
(iv) Ch. Muhammad Shafi v. Ch. Muhammad Anwar Sama (1975 SCMR 219);
(v) Malik Muhammad Saliheen v. Arshad Siddiq (1997 SCMR 1829).
Result: Petition allowed; bail granted.

case_info: C.R No. 34-P of 2022 Ishtiaq Hussain and others  Vs  Jamshaid Khan and another
remarks:
Specific Relief Act, 1877 - Ss. 12-14
Suit for specific performance - Scope
A suit for specific performance is confined to enforcement of a contractual obligation in terms of the contract. Relief beyond contractual enforcement, including declaration of rights independent of the contract, is not maintainable.
C.P.C., 1908 - 0. VI, R. 17
Amendment of pleadings - Change in nature of suit
Amendment in a suit for specific performance introducing declaratory relief or challenge to another document alters the nature and cause of action of the suit and is not permissible.
Nikahnama — Dower (Haq Mehr)
Third-party rights
Fixation of immovable property as dower in a Nikahnama does not bind a third party who neither consented to nor was a party to such arrangement; any such recital gives rise to an independent civil dispute.
C.P.C., 1908 - S. 9
Civil Court jurisdiction
While validity of a Nikahnama per se is not justiciable before a Civil Court, civil consequences of its recitals affecting proprietary or contractual rights of third parties are triable by a Civil Court under Section 9, CPC.
Family Courts Act, 1964
Jurisdiction - Distinction
Disputes relating to marital status or validity of Nikahnama fall within Family Court jurisdiction, whereas disputes involving proprietary rights of third parties lie before Civil Courts.
Petition disposed of in view of the above terms

case_info: WP No. 4952-P of 2024 with CM No. 2083/2024 & CM No. 2420/2025 M/S Fast Track Express (Pvt) Ltd.    Vs   Gvot. of KP through Secretary Finance etc
remarks:
The charging provision, Section 3 of the Act, as amended by Section 14 of the Finance Act, 2024, makes the cess payable on the transportation, carriage, or movement of goods within, into, out of, or through the Province. The rate of cess is to be determined either ad valorem or on prescribed basis, and its proceeds are credited to the Provincial Consolidated Fund to be utilized exclusively for the maintenance and development of infrastructure. A close reading of Section 3 reveals that the charging event is the movement of goods whether produced, traded, imported, or exported across the infrastructure of the Province. The measure of the levy, however, is linked to the value of the goods as determined under the Sales Tax Act or the Customs Act. This distinction between the event that attracts liability and the standard used for quantification is crucial. The adoption of a federal valuation standard is merely an administrative convenience; it does not define the constitutional character of the levy.
In this backdrop, the determination of legislative competence must proceed by first identifying the true object and substance of the impugned IDC Act and then juxtaposing it against the fields expressly reserved to the Federation. The relevant entries of the Federal Legislative List: Entry 24 (Carriage of passengers and goods by sea or by air), Entry 27 (Import and export across customs frontiers, inter-provincial and international trade and commerce), and Entry 43 (Duties of customs, including export duties) define the outer boundaries of federal fiscal jurisdiction. Read together, these entries secure to Parliament the power to legislate on trade, transport, and taxation at the frontiers of the Federation and between Provinces.
The jurisprudence on fiscal measures akin to the IDC Act reveals that levies imposed on the movement or entry of goods within a province or local area, even where assessed on an ad valorem basis and collected in a manner similar to customs duty, have consistently been upheld as constitutionally valid provincial impositions. In Universal Merchants,  the levy of Octroi under the West Pakistan Municipal Committee Octroi Rules, 1964 charged on goods imported for consumption, use, or sale within municipal limits and collected “as if it were a customs duty” was sustained on the ground that its pith and substance related not to customs or importation but to local entry and use of goods. The Divisional Bench of the learned Sindh High Court held that although imported goods might incidentally cross federal frontiers, the taxable event was their entry for consumption within municipal boundaries, a matter squarely within the provincial field. This reasoning was reiterated in Shershah Industries  and Hyderi Ship Breaking,  where Octroi on imported scrap brought into Karachi was again upheld, the Court held that unless equated with customs duties, such local cesses remained valid provincial levies under Article 142(c).
In any constitutional scheme where the Federation and the Provinces either share legislative competence or there exists a division of legislative fields, incidental encroachment by one legislature upon the domain of the other is, at times, inevitable. The constitutional framework through the Government of India Act, 1935, envisaged three lists relating to the legislative fields of the Provinces as well as the Federation, and this phenomena was visualized by Sir Maurice Gwyer C.J. said in 1940 F.C.R. 188  that “It must inevitably happen from time to time that legislation though purporting to deal with a subject in one list, touches also upon a subject in another list, and the different provisions of the enactment may be so closely intertwined that blind adherence to a strictly verbal interpretation would result in a large number of statutes being declared invalid because the Legislature enacting them may appear to have legislated in a forbidden sphere. Hence the rule which has been evolved by the Judicial Committee, whereby the impugned statue is examined to ascertain its pith and substance or its true nature and character for the purpose of determining whether it is legislation with respect to matters in this list or in that.” Similarly, it was also a consistent view that where necessary, recourse could be taken to the doctrine of “pith and substance” for resolving the conflict.
