It was in this context that their lordships, in the case concerning the Central Province and Berar Sales of Motor Spirit and Lubricants Taxation Act, 1938 (AIR 1939 FC 1), declined to extend the scope of excise duty beyond the process of manufacture or production of goods.
Having regard to the foregoing principles, it becomes evident that in assessing the validity of a fiscal enactment, the constitutional focus must remain on its object and substance, rather than on the measure or machinery employed for assessment or collection. Fiscal statutes, by their very nature, often adopt valuation standards or administrative procedures from other enactments, such borrowing, however, does not determine legislative character. A provincial law may conveniently draw upon federal valuation criteria, such as those found in the Customs or Sales Tax Acts, without thereby assuming the constitutional colour of those enactments. What is determinative is the purpose for which the levy is imposed. This distinction was clearly articulated by the Privy Council in Prafulla Kumar,  wherein it was observed:
[37] Subjects must still overlap and where they do the question must be naked what in pith and substance is the effect of the enactment of which complaint is made and in what list is its true nature and character to be found. If these questions could not be asked, much beneficent legislation would be stifled at birth, and many of the subjects entrusted to Provincial Legislation could never effectively be dealt with. 
[38] Thirdly, the extent of the invasion by the Provinces into subjects enumerated in the Federal List has to be considered. No doubt it is an important matter, not, as their Lordships think, because the validity of an Act can be determined by discriminating between degrees of invasion, but for the purpose of determining what is the pith and substance of the impugned Act. Its provisions may advance so far into Federal territory as to show that its true nature not concerned with Provincial matters, but the question is not, has it trespassed more or less, but is the trespass, whatever it be, such as impugned Act is not money-lending but promissory to show that the pith and substance of the notes or banking? Once that question is determined the Act falls on one or the other side of the line and can be seen as valid or invalid according to its true content.

In this backdrop, the determination of legislative competence must proceed by first identifying the true object and substance of the impugned IDC Act and then juxtaposing it against the fields expressly reserved to the Federation. The relevant entries of the Federal Legislative List: Entry 24 (Carriage of passengers and goods by sea or by air), Entry 27 (Import and export across customs frontiers, inter-provincial and international trade and commerce), and Entry 43 (Duties of customs, including export duties) define the outer boundaries of federal fiscal jurisdiction. Read together, these entries secure to Parliament the power to legislate on trade, transport, and taxation at the frontiers of the Federation and between Provinces.
Applying the doctrine of pith and substance to the present case, it becomes evident that the IDC Act, when read as a whole, legislates within the legitimate domain of the Province. Its preamble, charging provisions, and fiscal framework collectively reveal that the Act’s dominant purpose is to generate revenue for the maintenance and development of provincial infrastructure. The charging event is the transportation, carriage, or movement of goods within, into, or out of the Province, reflecting a direct nexus with the use of provincial infrastructure rather than the act of import or export itself. The reference to valuation methods under the Sales Tax Act and the Customs Act serves merely as a mechanical standard of assessment and does not alter the nature of the levy.
 .	Universal Merchants v. Commissioner of Karachi (1980 CLC 704).
 .	Shershah Industries Ltd. v. Government of Sindh (PLD 1982 Karachi 653).
 .	Hyderi Ship Breaking Industries v. Sindh Government (2007 MLD 770).
 . 	Subramanyam Chettiar v. Mulluswami Coundan (AIR 1941 FC 47).
 .	Prafula Kumar Mukherjee and others v. Bank of Commerce Ltd., Khulna (AIR (34) 1947 Privy Council 60).

case_info: Cr.M (B.A) No. 634-M of 2025 Siraj alias Khaperay  Vs  State through AAG and others
remarks:
I. In a case involving multiple accused persons, where one accused is
alleged to have caught hold of the deceased while the co-accused
inflicted a single stab wound, the factum of catching hold by an
accused and his vicarious liability requires recording of prosecution
evidence.
II. In cases falling within the ambit of further inquiry u/s 497(2)
Cr.P.C., the grant of bail is a right, not a concession.
III.Every accused is presumed innocent until proven guilty and the
benefit of doubt may be extended even at the bail stage if warranted
by the facts of the case.
IV. Where one accused has already been granted bail by a competent
Court then another co-accused, having been assigned almost
identical role, cannot be denied the same concession in view of the
principle of consistency and the mandate of Article 4 read with
Article 25 of the Constitution of the Islamic Republic of Pakistan,
1973.

case_info: W.P No. 683-A of 2021 Najum Sohail Tanoli  Vs  Govt of KP & others
remarks:
(a) It is a settled principle that matters arising out of contractual obligations
cannot ordinarily be agitated or challenged in constitutional jurisdiction under
Article 199 of the Constitution.
(b) It is a consistently affirmed principle of constitutional adjudication that
disputed questions of fact, which require the recording of evidence,
examination of witnesses, cross-examination, or detailed factual inquiry, cannot
be entertained or adjudicated in writ jurisdiction. The writ court, in exercising its
powers under Article 199, is not equipped to conduct a full-fledged fact-finding
trial; its role is confined to examining questions of law, jurisdictional defects, or
violations of fundamental or statutory rights.
Where the controversy involves factual disputes—such as competing versions
of events, contested documents, allegations requiring evidentiary proof, or
matters necessitating technical assessment—the appropriate forum is the civil
court or any other competent tribunal empowered to undertake such factual
determination.
(c) The arbitration clause, in its scope and language, embodies the clear
intention of the contracting parties to exclude the intervention of ordinary civil or
constitutional forums in matters arising purely from the contract and to resolve
such disputes through the internal dispute-resolution mechanism provided
therein. Where parties have voluntarily entered into a contract containing a
self-contained dispute-resolution clause, they are bound by its terms and
cannot bypass the agreed procedure by directly invoking the constitutional
jurisdiction of this Court.
(d) Perusal of the contents of the writ petitions reveals that the petitioners have
not disclosed about the filing of earlier suits which amounts to concealment of
facts from the Court. Relief under section 199 of the Constitution, being
discretionary, therefore, the Court considering the conduct of the petitioners,
holds that they have not come to the Court with clean hands and do not
deserve the relief sought in the writ petitions.

case_info: W.P No. 580-D of 2015 Mst. Fahat Afza  Vs  Government of Pakistan through Federal Secretary.
remarks:
The petitioner challenged, through constitutional jurisdiction, the judgment dated 19.03.1952 passed by the Sessions Judge, Derajat, only to the extent of forfeiture of immovable property of a convict sentenced to death. The petitioner claimed rights through an alleged gift and will from the convict\'s sister.
Held that the petitioner had earlier instituted a civil suit claiming ownership of the property, which was dismissed on merits; the appeal there against was also dismissed. The subsequent civil revision was not decided on merits, but was partly accepted solely on the assumption that the land had allegedly been resumed by the Government under the Land Reforms (MLR-64), resulting in lack of jurisdiction of the civil court. Consequently, the judgments of the courts below were modified only to the extent that the suit was ordered to be returned, with permission to approach the competent forum strictly within the context of land resumption.
Held further that the return of a plaint does not confer unrestricted liberty; such permission is conditional and circumscribed by the express terms of the order. The doctrine of \"permission governs consequence\" applies, and the returned plaint cannot be used to reopen issues already concluded or to revive claims decided on merits.
Since the petitioner, from the inception of the present petition, disowned the plea of land resumption and asserted that it was a mistaken assumption, the very basis on which jurisdiction of the civil court was held to be barred ceased to exist. In such circumstances, the petitioner was legally required either to challenge the judgment in civil revision before the Supreme Court or to seek its review; having failed to do so, the judgment attained finality.
Held that the petitioner, not being a legal heir of the convict and having already failed to establish title in civil proceedings, had no locus standi to challenge the forfeiture or transfer of property. Constitutional jurisdiction could not be invoked to avoid final judgments, or indirectly reopen settled controversies.

case_info: W.P No. 8150-P of 2025 Syed Zahir Shah  Vs  The State through Chairman NAB etc.
remarks:
i.	The Court is mindful of the principle that what is unconstitutional is void ab initio. However, the determination of unconstitutionality of a judicial pronouncement is exclusively within the sphere of the appellate forum, not the Court that rendered the judgment. Allowing a High Court to sit in appeal upon its own earlier judgment, even partially, would obliterate the principle of finality, destabilize judicial hierarchy, and create a jurisdiction unknown to law.
ii.	A party that consciously elects to abandon a statutory remedy cannot thereafter invoke the constitutional jurisdiction as a means to circumvent the consequences of that election. To permit such a course would enable litigants to manufacture jurisdiction by their own omission or deliberate withdrawal, thereby unsettling the discipline of appellate hierarchy and undermining the finality attaching to judicial determinations. Jurisdiction is conferred by law, not created by parties.
iii.	A concluded judgment cannot be reopened merely because a litigant has, by choice, foreclosed the appellate avenue available to him. Such a proposition would strike at the very foundation of judicial certainty and would erode the structural coherence of the justice system. Hence, the contention premised on the withdrawal of the appeal does not advance the Petitioner’s case; rather, it reinforces the impermissibility of entertaining the present petition.
iv.	The petitioner did invoke that very jurisdiction but chose to withdraw the appeal. Judicial discipline does not allow this Court to re-assume the role of an appellate court over issues that were properly placed before, but not pursued before, the apex Court. The withdrawal of the appeal, whether strategic or otherwise, cannot be permitted to generate a fresh cause before this Court under the guise of inherent jurisdiction.

case_info: Cr.A No. 136-P of 2025 Waseem  Vs  Muhammad Hasnain & another
remarks:
i.	It is true only one person has been charged. However, this does not relive the prosecution of its duty to prove the case beyond reasonable doubt, nor does it excuse the Trial Court from applying its judicial mind to the evidence.
ii.	The statement of the complainant, rather than dispelling doubts, has heightened the apprehensions of the court regarding the circumstances in which the occurrence occurred and the manner in which the deceased was fired upon. It is, therefore, necessary to examine not only the conduct of the complainant, but also his relationship with the deceased.
iii.	In the presence of such rivalry, the conduct the complainant assumes critical significance. Under these circumstance, this Court is inclined to hold that the complainant was an interested, and possibly inimical, witness, and the possibility of his implicating the accused out of the personal motive cannot be ignored.
iv.	In view of these circumstances, this Court entertains serious reservation regarding the credibility of the narrative of the prosecution, the assertions of the presence of the complainant at the scene, and the trustworthiness of the account of the SHO.

case_info: W.P No. 832-B of 2022 Saqib Naveed  Vs  Govt of KPK through Chief Secretary, Peshawar etc
remarks:
Writ Petition.
Held: Constitutional Petition --- Recruitment to police service --- Physical standards --- Minor
deficiency in chest measurement --- condonation --- Scope of Judicial interference --- Article
199 of the constitution --- Petitioner applied for recruitment as police constable and qualified
written and physical test but was ultimately declared ineligible for having half-inch deficiency in
prescribed chest measurement. --- contention that shortfall was marginal and should have been
condoned --- Held, eligibility criteria for recruitment of constables are governed by Rule 12.15
of Police Rules, 1934, Standing order No. 26/2014 and Police Policy Board Order No. 74/2017,
which expressly prohibits relaxation of height and chest measurement even for minor variations -
-- where policy mandates strict adherence to physical standards, court cannot substitute or relax
such standards under Article 199 --- Recruitment to disciplined forces such as police demands
uniformity and compliance with prescribed benchmarks, which are essential to meet operational
and institutional objectives; judicial interference in such matters would amount to overreach and
compromise institutional discipline --- Petition dismissed.

case_info: W.P No. 281-B of 2025 Sikandar Ayaz  Vs  Federation of Pakistan through Secretary Communication Islamabad, etc.
remarks:
Writ Petition.
Held: Constitutional Petition --- Prime Minister’s Assistance Package --- Cancellation of
appointment --- Prospective overruling--- Vested Rights --- Article 199 of the constitution ---
Petitioner appointed as Postal Clerk (BPS:09) on 02.11.2023 under the Prime Minister’s
Assistance Package following retirement of his father on medical grounds. --- Later, appointment
was cancelled on 18.02.2025 on the ground that Islamabad High Court order dated 08.12.2022
had declared such appointments unlawful --- Held, the order dated 08.12.2022 shows that it was
interim in nature which did not final adjudicate upon the constitutionality of the Prime Minister’s
Assistance Package --- The Islamabad High Court only expressed prima facie concerns regarding
the manner in which appointments were being made under various office memoranda and
policies, and accordingly issued a notice to the Attorney General for Pakistan under OXXVII-A
CPC to assist the court on constitutional aspect of the scheme. --- Vide judgment dated
26.09.2024, the Supreme Court in General Post Office Vs. Muhammad Jalal (CP No. 3390 of
2021) declared such appointments unconstitutional but expressly held that the judgment would
not affect the appointments already made. --- Petitioner’s appointment having been made prior to
26.09.2024 stood protected under the doctrine of prospective overruling and the principle of
finality of administrative action. --- Respondents’ reliance on an interim order to
retrospectively invalidate an already crystallized appointment was legally misconceived --- once
the appointment had been validly made and acted upon, a vested right had accrued which could
not be divested in the absence of an express retrospective mandate.--- Impugned cancellation
order issued without lawful authority, contrary to binding pronouncements of the Supreme
Court, and void ab initio --- Petitioner reinstated in service with consequential benefits including
arrears of salary. --- Petition allowed.

case_info: Cr.A No. 1232-P of 2019 Fayaz Khan  Vs  Faizan Qazi & another
remarks:
i. While the non-production of the second eyewitness may at first appear to weaken the prosecution case, the fact that PW-3 was completely independent and not under the influence or control of the complainant, minimizes the impact of this omission. His presence at the scene stands naturally explained, and his version remains consistent throughout.
ii. Their testimonies harmonize on material particulars such as the occurrence, the movement of the injured, the manner in which information was conveyed, and the subsequent reporting of the matter. In such a situation, the relationship of the complainant with the deceased does not, by itself furnish a reason to discard his testimony especially when it stands reinforced by an independent eyewitness whose presence and narrative bear the hallmark of truth.
iii. While only one eyewitness was produced, it must also be noted that this witness stood on neutral ground, sharing an equal association with both the deceased and the accused. His relationship thus strengthens, rather than weakens, the reliability of his testimony. iv. Although the respondent/ accused attempted to mitigate his culpability by asserting that the pistol had gone off unintentionally, the nature and placement of the injuries leave no room for ambiguity. Had his version regarding lack of intention been correct, all injuries would likely have been in close proximity; instead, the deliberate selection of multiple areas of the body unmistakably indicates an intention to cause death.
v. It is a well-settled proposition that a conviction may rest solely upon a voluntary and truthful confessional statement.
vi. It is equally well-settled that an appellate court must exercise heightened care while hearing an appeal against acquittal, and even greater caution when it intends to reverse such a finding, as superior courts are ordinarily reluctant to disturb an acquittal rendered by a trial court.
vii. True that where an order appears arbitrary or perverse, the appellate court must act with restraint; but equally true the principle that no court of appeal may permit an illegal or unjustified order to stand, if upon re-appraisal, it is satisfied that the evidence on record was not properly appreciated.

case_info: W.P No. 8357-P of 2025 Sahibzada Muhammad Hamid  Vs  Federal Government through Secretary Ministry of Interior & 03 others.
remarks:
Petitioner seeks protective bail by invoking the jurisdiction of this court under Article 199 of the Constitution of Islamic Republic of Pakistan, 1973.
Once a competent court has rendered a judgment of conviction or acquittal, the individual’s status changes from an accused to a convict or acquitted person, and their custody or liberty stems from a judicial order. Thereafter, the only competent remedy lies before the appellate or revisional forum within the proper territorial jurisdiction.
A High Court of another Province has no authority under Article 199 of the Constitution to interfere with or suspend a conviction passed by a coordinate or subordinate court elsewhere. Protective bail, being a pre-arrest safeguard, cannot extend to post-conviction situations, as execution of a judicial sentence flows from lawful authority, not administrative discretion.
Only in exceptional circumstances—such as conviction in absentia or while on exemption from appearance—constitutional court of competent jurisdiction may grant / defer arrest briefly to enable the convict to approach and surrender before the appropriate appellate forum, in line with his fundamental right of access to justice. (PLD 2012 SC 923).
In this case, the petitioner, convicted by the Anti-Terrorism Court, Faisalabad, vide judgment dated 31.07.2025, and being a resident of Faisalabad, has no justification to invoke this Court’s jurisdiction at this belated stage.

case_info: W.P No. 41-M of 2021 Umar Hayat  Vs Mst. Nasreen Bibi and two others
remarks:
It is not an absolute or automatic right of the husband that upon
dissolution of marriage on the ground of khula, the wife must in
every case be directed or compelled to return all benefits including
the dowry already received by her rather, such return depends upon
the facts and circumstances of each case and decision thereof lies
within the discretion of the Court keeping in view the overall conduct
of the parties and the reasons leading to the khula.

case_info: W.P No. 303-B of 2025 Rafi Ullah  Vs  Mst. Qamar-un-Nisa
remarks:
Civil Revision.
Held:
Family Court--- Ex Parte Decree --- Application for setting aside --- Limitation --- Due
Service --- Substituted service --- Article 164, Limitation act, 1908 --- Article 199 of the
Constitution --- Petitioner assailed judgments of family court and appellate court
refusing to set aside ex parte decree passed in a family suit for the recovery of dower,
dowry articles, and maintenance --- Record showed petitioner was abroad during the
relevant period --- No material produced to establish personal service, service through
registered post, or effective substitutive service --- Alleged publication was made in a
local newspaper not circulated a petitioner’s place of residence, and no copy of said
publication was dispatched to the address mentioned therein --- Order sheet itself
recorded that defendant was abroad --- Held, no evidence of due service was available
on record --- Under Art. 164, Limitation Act, 1908, an application to set aside an ex
parte decree must be filed within 30 days of decree, or, where summons were not duly
served, within 30 days from date of knowledge --- Petitioner filed application
immediately after acquiring knowledge through attorney --- Application therefore within
time --- Courts favour decision on merits rather than non-suiting litigants on
technicalities --- Impugned orders set aside; ex parte judgment and decree recalled ---
Case remanded to Family Court for fresh adjudication after providing both the parties
fair opportunity of hearing --- Petitioner was directed to furnish fresh surety bond with
wo sureties covering entire claim, if decreed, and to deposit arrears of maintenance of
his son and two daughters from 2015 till date, subject to adjustment of amounts already
paid --- Family court was directed to determine maintenance of minor son in accordance
with law and conclude proceedings within four months --- Petition allowed.

case_info: B.A No. 461-B of 2025 Inayatullah  Vs  The State etc
remarks:
Penal Code (XLV of 1860) — Ss. 302/34 — Qatl-i-amd, common intention — Bail, post-
arrest— Refusal — Medical ground — Scope.
Report of Standing Medical Board did not disclose that the ailment from which accused was
suffering was life-threatening or that its treatment was not available in jail premises — Held,
Mere illness, without proof of it being life-threatening or of the unavailability of adequate
medical facilities, is not a ground for grant of bail in cases falling within the prohibitory clause —
Petition dismissed.
\"Mian Nazir Ahmad v. State etc" (2016 SCMR 1536); \"Abdul Ghafar v. Federation of Pakistan
etc\" (2021 P.Cr.L.J 1270 ref.)
Criminal Procedure Code (V of 1898) — S. 497(1) — Bail, post-arrest — Prohibitory clause
—Considerations.
Accused specifically nominated in promptly lodged F.I.R. with a specific role of firing upon
deceased — Incident took place in broad daylight — Parties known to each other — No question
of mistaken identity — No ground existed for grant of bail.
Principle — Illness simpliciter — Not a ground for bail.
Unless it is established that continued detention endangers life or that required treatment is not
available in jail or government hospitals, concession of bail on medical ground cannot be
extended in offences punishable with death or imprisonment for life.
[Bail Application dismissed]

case_info: W.P No. 7776-P of 2025 Hub of Private Educaiton Network (HOPE)  Vs  Government of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa & others
remarks:
Writ Petition
Held:        Issue / Section of Laws. Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Board of Intermediate and Secondary Education Act, 1990—Powers of Board- it is the Board alone which has the power to conduct examinations, including notably the constitution of examination centers. In this respect, the roles of the Chairman, the Controller of examinations and the Joint Action Committee have been defined.
Cluster Based System - conduct of examination by the Board is an administrative duty and devising an efficient and effective system is a policy matter of the Elementary and Secondary Education Department in tandem with the Board. It is in fact the consultation with parents and students that is missing in the law, the relevant rules and regulations, and the policy. In a rights-based perspective, the significance of public deliberation through a participatory approach could not be simply brushed aside as it will lend democratic legitimacy to the system. While such an approach will be more encouraging if adopted, the Board being the sole responsible institution may do it if considers necessary. On this score alone, however, the cluster system examination could not be struck down. On balance, however, the vested interest groups in the deliberative process may not venture to offer help that may threaten their stakes in the given milieu. The goals the cluster system seeks to pursue could not be doubted, nor could ill intention be attributed to the Board as a public institution.
As far the argument that the cluster system tends to compel students to travel long distances to reach the cluster examination halls, and widespread logistical problems having been resulted, suffice it to say that it is the bounden duty of the Board to address such issues. We cannot presume that the Board is oblivion to such problems which naturally arise in the conduct and management of examination. As far “psychological problems” to the students of the schools associated with it. High Court can’t go into this issue, too. If at all, any evidence has emerged, the petitioner or any aggrieved person may approach to the Board. The petitioner seems to perceive disadvantage to the students of its associated schools being used to a certain environment. That aspect, however, has to be palpably seen against the larger goal of weeding out widespread cheating and unfair means in examinations. Even otherwise, it becomes the responsibility of the petitioner’s associated schools to psychologically prepare their students for such an examination.
Fundamental rights and principles of policy- The Court do not see any indication of the cluster-based examination system’s conflict with such rights. The Board has the legal authority under the Act 1990, the Rules and Regulations. All the students of secondary and higher classes who stand as candidates for the examination are treated according to law; their clustering in one examination hall also negates discrimination. Article 25-A has no application at all as it obligates the State to provide free and compulsory education to all children of the age of five to sixteen years in such manner as may be determined by law. As far articles 37 and 38, those are part of the principles of policy which set out broader goals for socio-economic development. We are mindful of the fact that the principles of policy despite being programmatic in nature, requiring progressive realization, need to be seen in harmony with the fundamental rights and with a dynamic approach to meet the challenges of the day. It seems rather naïve to stretch such constitutional provisions so much as the petitioner prays before us.
Child Rights perspective- The 1989 United Nations Convention on the Rights of Child, and the 1990 UNESCO’s initiative spell out broader principles of access of all to education, equality of opportunity and quality consideration (CRC: article 28) and the full development of the child’s personality (CRC: article 29). True, article 12 of the CRC recognizes children’s right to express their views freely in all matters affecting them, with their views given due weight in accordance with their age and maturity. This can be interpreted as hearing children’s voices in decision-making processes related to their education, including potentially the design and implementation of assessment methods. This, however, is conditional to their age and maturity and understanding. The Board may do it if deems appropriate. On a more critical note, it may be observed that the cluster-based examination system may not be sufficient as the sole tool for discouraging cheating and unfair means in examinations; and that there is a dire need of uprooting rote learning and doing away with putting children in the stress of do-or-die environment.

case_info: Election Appeal No. 02-A of 2025 Abdur Rasheed   Vs  RO etc
remarks:
a. 	The requirement of Section 85 of The Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Local Government Act, 2013 is explicit: a person holding a local government office must resign before filing nomination papers, not by the date of scrutiny. The statute fixes a definite timeline, leaving no room for discretion. The statute speaks plainly. It requires that the resignation in writing be tendered to the respective Chairman. When it says a candidate must resign before filing nomination papers, courts cannot stretch it by common-law notions of effb. 	The intent of Section 85 The Act of 2013 is clear; to prohibit dual membership. The principle has constitutional recognition under Article 63(1)(p) of the Constitution. Moreover, sections 231 and 232 of the Elections Act, 2017, when read with Article 63(1)(p) of the Constitution, incorporate the qualifications and disqualifications provided in any law for the time being in force. The bar contained in Section 85 of the Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Local Government Act, 2013, therefore, squarely applies. The argument that this provision cannot extend to parliamentary elections is misconceived. The constitutional and statutory framework leaves no such distinction.ectiveness or by counting time till scrutiny. The timeline is fixed; the duty is absolute.
b. 	The intent of Section 85 The Act of 2013 is clear; to prohibit dual membership. The principle has constitutional recognition under Article 63(1)(p) of the Constitution. Moreover, sections 231 and 232 of the Elections Act, 2017, when read with Article 63(1)(p) of the Constitution, incorporate the qualifications and disqualifications provided in any law for the time being in force. The bar contained in Section 85 of the Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Local Government Act, 2013, therefore, squarely applies. The argument that this provision cannot extend to parliamentary elections is misconceived. The constitutional and statutory framework leaves no such distinction.

case_info: Election Appeal No. 01-A of 2025 Sajid Mehmood  VS  Babar Nawaz Khan etc
remarks:
a.	It is settled that electoral laws are to be interpreted in favor of enfranchisement, not dis-enfranchisement. More so, while dealing with the question of acceptance or rejection of nomination papers.

b.	It is evident that all objections concern reconciliation between Form-B and tax returns; both filed by respondent No.1 himself. There was, therefore, no concealment. The annexures submitted with nomination papers are an integral part thereof; a detail disclosed in an annexure cannot be treated as concealed merely because it was not repeated in the main form.

c.	Had respondent No.1 concealed an asset unearthed during scrutiny, the issue of honesty could arise. Here, there was none. Any lapse in Form-B appears inadvertent; an error of judgment, not deceit.

d.	There being no declaration by a court of law against respondent No.1, the presumption of honesty prevails. Neither the Returning Officer nor this Tribunal has the authority to declare otherwise.

case_info: RFA No. 327-P of 2024 Ali Asghar  Vs  Mst. Farkhanda Rani and another
remarks:
Cases in which Ombudsperson can and cannot assume jurisdiction:
1.	In the Act Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Enforcement of Women Property Act, 2019, the word \"possession\" has been used without any reference of symbolic or actual (physical), thus, whether in the cases of joint possession the possession would be simply interpreted as symbolic possession, if so, whether the complainant would be required to seek the possession through partition from the court of competent jurisdiction?
2.	Whether physical possession from the joint ownership could be directed/granted without partition?
3.	Whether intricate question of law and facts could be determined/decided (adjudicated) by the Ombudsperson while deciding the complaint by the \"aggrieved person\" as defined under the Act of 2019?
4.	Whether without impleading all the co-owners (co-shares) the complainant could request for actual possession of immovable property?
5.	Whether the Ombudsperson could exercise the jurisdiction in a matter which has been decided by the civil court or pending before the court, for determination of the plea of complainant as defined under section 2(a) of the Act of 2019?
Held that the Ombudsperson has limited, summary jurisdiction under the Women`s Property Rights Act, 2019. No order for delivery of actual possession could either be passed or executed by Ombudsperson against co-sharers in accordance with law. Any direction issued by the Ombudsperson for delivery of possession among co-owners in such like cases is perverse, illegal, and contrary to the settled principles of law and norms of justice. Likewise, in cases where civil or family litigation is pending or has been decided by a competent court, the matter must be taken to its logical conclusion before such forums. The learned Ombudsperson, not being a court of competent jurisdiction, could neither stay proceedings of a suit pending before the competent civil court nor adjudicate upon question to title.

case_info: Cr.A No. 361-P of 2025 Farman Ullah alias Kotay  Vs  The State & another
remarks:
i.	The learned counsel for the appellant attempted to argue malafide on the part of the Investigating Officer for failing to collect the footage. Even if the omission is viewed as an irregularity in the investigation, such deficiency cannot, by itself, be taken as a circumstance to undermine the otherwise consistent and corroborated prosecution evidence.
ii.	The testimonies of the witnesses are mutually corroborative, consistent on all essential aspects, and fully support the prosecution version that it was the accused who were responsible for the commission of the offence.
iii.	Undoubtedly, medical evidence, standing alone, may not be sufficient to fix culpability upon the accused, for its primary purpose is confirmatory in nature. However, when the medical findings are consistent with and corroborated by the testimony of reliable eyewitness and the surrounding circumstances, such concurrence substantially reinforces the prosecution\'s case and diminishes the possibility of false implication.
iv.	During cross-examination, the witnesses were questioned at length regarding their expertise in the game of volleyball, their physical height, and the usual number of players required for the game. However, such queries, in our considered view, were not of material significance. There existed no fixed rule or formal arrangement regarding the composition or physical attributes of the players, nor was there any requirement that only players of a certain height or skill level could participate. These questions, though designed to test credibility, cannot by themselves discredit the witnesses, especially when there is nothing on record to suggest that the game was organized under any formal or institutional supervision requiring specific qualifications.

case_info: W.P No. 2015-P of 2021 Rio Din  Vs  Ayaz-ud-Din & others
remarks: Legal partition is recognized by the legal mazim “Nemo in communione potest invitus detineri” no one can be kept in co-proprietorship against his will. Acceptance of partition application and due execution thereof is the transfer of physical possession/actual possession and not the symbolic possession. Partition is merely an arrangement whereby co-sharers having undivided interest in joint properties take by arrangement specific properties in lieu of the shares. The true  essence of a partition decree lies in separation of the share and delivery of  physical possession. In this case, only symbolic possession had been delivered  earlier, which did not fulfill the intent of the decree. After dismissal of the writ  petition in 2018, the decree-holder rightly sought actual possession to complete  execution of the decree.

case_info: C.R No. 133-M of 2012 Marid Khan & others  Vs  Tor Khan & others
remarks:
Court allowed the revision petition, setting aside the concurrent findings and
decree of the lower courts. The court held that Mst. Awal Jan and Mst. Zarina
were sisters of Muhammad Ayub, and the petitioners are entitled to their
respective shares in his estate. The court relied on the unchallenged testimony of
PW-3, corroborative documents, and the principle that failure to cross-examine
on a material point amounts to tacit admission. The omission of the ladies names
from the Shajra-i-Nasab was not considered conclusive proof of the absence of
relationship, given the prevalent practice of excluding female heirs from revenue
records in rural areas.

case_info: Cr.A No 309-A of 2023 Naseeb Ullah  Vs  The State and others
remarks:
(a) The offence under Section 279 PPC does not hinge merely on the speed of the vehicle.
The statutory phrase “driving in a manner so rash or negligent as to endanger human life”
establishes a qualitative rather than quantitative standard. Speed may be a relevant
evidentiary factor, but it is never the decisive criterion. The core inquiry is the manner of
driving—whether it was rash or negligent, and whether such conduct created a danger to
human life or the safety of others on the road. A vehicle may be driven slowly yet recklessly,
for example, on the wrong side of the road or without due regard to traffic conditions, which
would still fall within the mischief of Section 279 PPC. Conversely, high speed alone, if
accompanied by proper control and compliance with traffic norms, does not automatically
constitute rash or negligent driving.
(b) The settled legal position is that conviction under Section 279 PPC requires the
coexistence of three essential elements: (i) the accused must have been driving the vehicle;
(ii) the driving must have been rash or negligent; and (iii) such rash or negligent driving must
have been of a nature that endangered human life or caused hurt. Failure to establish any
one of these conditions renders conviction unsustainable. The assessment of rashness or
negligence must be made in light of all attendant circumstances and cannot be judged in
isolation. Even slow driving may amount to rash or negligent driving if done without proper
care or in a manner that creates danger, which is why the legislature used the expression
“manner so rash or negligent as to endanger human life.” Once these three ingredients are
satisfied, the penal consequences under Section 279 PPC necessarily follow.
(c) It is a well-established principle of criminal jurisprudence that even a single reasonable
doubt is sufficient to extend the benefit of doubt to an accused; multiple grounds are not
required for acquittal.

case_info: W.P No. 934-A of 2019 Sabir Sultan etc  Vs  Govt
remarks:
(a)The constitutional jurisdiction of this Court under Article 199 cannot be invoked to
question administrative or policy decisions of the Government where the competent
authority has acted within its lawful domain and in accordance with law. In addition, the
law is well settled that constitutional jurisdiction under Article 199 of the Constitution of
the Islamic Republic of Pakistan, 1973, cannot be exercised in matters involving disputed
questions of fact, which require recording of evidence. The scope of this Court under
Article 199 of the Constitution of Pakistan is dependent on the questions which are
devoid of factual controversies, therefore, this Court cannot interfere in such like matters
where disputed questions of facts are involved.
(b) The impugned notification, having been issued after observance of the due process
prescribed under paragraph 7.69 of the Land Record Manual, cannot be termed coram non
judice or without jurisdiction. It is well settled by the superior Courts that the constitutional
jurisdiction of this Court under Article 199 cannot be invoked to question administrative or
policy decisions of the Government where the competent authority has acted within its lawful
domain and in accordance with law. For a petitioner to qualify as an “aggrieved person” under
Article 199 of the Constitution of the Islamic Republic of Pakistan, 1973, there must be a
demonstrable and immediate violation of a legal right. The mere dissatisfaction of a few
individuals cannot nullify a decision undertaken in the larger public interest after due
compliance with law. If the petitioners or any other person considers themselves affected by the
administrative bifurcation, the Land Record Manual provides a complete and adequate
mechanism for redressal through the revenue hierarchy. Constitutional jurisdiction cannot be
invoked as a substitute for ordinary statutory remedies.

case_info: W.P No. 1300-A of 2021 Dr. Laiba Khan  Vs  Government of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa & others
remarks:
a. Regulation 20(e) of The Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Public Service Regulations, 2003 does not impose a compulsion. It recognizes choice. A married female candidate may retain her own domicile if she so chooses, or she may adopt her husband’s domicile. The impugned PGMI policy, however, removes that choice altogether. It presumes, without inquiry or consent, that marriage automatically transfers domicile; an assumption foreign to constitutional law and inconsistent even with the Regulation it purports to follow.
b. The impugned provision leaves no room for choice. It compels a married woman to abandon her own domicile and assume that of her husband; whether she wishes it or not. It presumes that marriage erases individuality. The law does not. The Constitution certainly does not. A woman’s domicile is not a transferable chattel, nor does it change hands upon marriage. It reflects residence, intention, and choice; all attributes of personhood. To impose an “alien domicile” upon her merely because she married is to deny her individuality. This, the Constitution forbids.
c. Section 9 of the Succession Act, 1925 provides that “the domicile of origin prevails  until a new domicile is acquired”.
d. Article 15 of the Constitution further guarantees every citizen the right to reside and settle anywhere in Pakistan. A woman cannot be denied the right to retain her pre-marriage domicile as that would infringe upon her fundamental right to freedom of movement and choice of residence protected under Article 15 of The Constitution.
e. A female candidate may marry yet wish to continue her education and career in her native province, where she has lived, studied, and intends to reside permanently. If her husband, too, chooses to make that province their home, the State has no business to displace that joint decision. Domicile is a question of intention and residence, not gender or marital status. By extinguishing her choice, the policy does not promote administrative order; it enforces gender subordination. It replaces merit with marital dependency. The Constitution knows no such hierarchy.
f. It is settled law that while matters of policy ordinarily fall within the domain of the executive, the courts will intervene where such policy is tainted with illegality, arbitrariness, or constitutional infirmity. A policy can be struck down if it is ultra vires the parent statute, inconsistent with constitutional provisions, discriminatory, irrational, or founded on extraneous considerations. Likewise, if it violates fundamental rights, offends the principle of equality under Article 25, defeats legitimate expectation, or constitutes a colourable exercise of power, it cannot stand judicial scrutiny. The executive cannot, under the guise of policy, amend or override statutory provisions, nor can it act contrary to the constitutional guarantees of fairness, reasonableness, and due process. The line of distinction is clear: courts do not sit in appeal over the wisdom of a policy, but they will strike it down when it crosses the boundary of legality.

case_info: W.P No. 515-A of 2020 Usman Ibrar and others  Vs  GM Forest development
remarks:
A contractual employee is bound by the terms of his appointment and cannot,
as of right, claim absorption or regularization unless such entitlement is
expressly provided by statute, rules, or policy. The mere fact of long service or
successive extensions does not confer a vested right to regularization,
particularly where the engagement is neither against a sanctioned post nor
under a regular budget, and where the employer is an autonomous corporate
body rather than a government department. The process of regularization is
also contingent upon the fulfillment of certain prerequisites, including the
availability of sanctioned posts, the fulfillment of qualifications and
conditions prescribed under the relevant rules, and the approval of the
competent authority. The financial implications must be considered and
approved as no post can be regularized or made permanent without proper
budgetary allocation, as it involves expenditure from the public exchequer.

case_info: W.P No. 583-A of 2025 Sajid Iqbal  Vs  Mst Farhat Moosa Hasany
remarks:
a). Section 13(6) of The Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Rent Restriction Ordinance, 1959 deals with two distinct situations; firstly: where the quantum of rent is not deposited and same is directed to be deposited in the
Court and secondly: where there is dispute regarding quantum of rent and Court proceeds to determine fair rent and directs its deposit in Court, which determination of quantum is subject to final outcome of rent case. Whereas, in the former case, the statute provides that the same is to be deposited before 15th of each month, in the latter case the statute does not provide the date itself rather it leaves it to the discretion of the Court to fix any date for the said deposit.
b). Any default in compliance of the tentative order passed by the Rent Controller under section 13 (6) of the Ordinance of 1959 has to be met with the penalty provided therein, i.e striking off his defence and passing of the eviction order without further proceedings. The law leaves no discretion with the Courts in this regard. The Rent Controller is bond by law to give affect to this provision.
c). If the tenant has any reservations about the tentative rent order, he may agitate the same before the Rent Controller but after complying with the order.

case_info: C.R No. 102-B of 2023 Govt of KPK through Secretary Elementary and Secondary Education, Peshawar Vs  Ilyas Khan and another.
remarks:
Civil Revision.
Held: Civil Revision--- Appointment to public post --- Possession of higher qualification---
Whether exclusion justified --- Scope of “minimum qualification” --- Jurisdiction of Civil court -
-- Section 9, Civil Procedure Code, 1908 --- Respondents, holding M.Sc (Computer Science) and
B.S (Computer Engineering), applied for post of certified Teacher (Information Technology)
(CT-IT) in the office of DEO (Male), Lakki Marwat pursuant to advertisement prescribing
Intermediate with one-year Diploma in information Technology (DIT) as minimum
qualification --- Despite securing second and third positions in merit list, respondents were
excluded from final selection for not possessing DIT Diploma --- Held, degrees possessed by
respondents were higher in nature and covered all components of DIT curriculum ---
Notification prescribing DIT as minimum qualification did not exclude candidate having higher
qualifications in same discipline --- Department’s restrictive interpretation was arbitrary and
contrary to settled recruitment principles--- Civil court’s jurisdiction under Section 9, C.P.C. not
barred, as claim related to right of fair consideration for appointment, not to terms of service of
appointed employee --- Findings of Courts below based on correct appreciation of law and
evidence --- No illegality or material irregularity found warranting interference in revisional
jurisdiction under section 115, C.P.C. --- Revision petition dismissed.

case_info: C.R No. 169-B of 2022 Govt of KPK through Secretary Elemantary & Secondary Education, Peshawar  Vs  Iftikhar Wali Khan
remarks:
Civil Revision.
Held:
Civil revision --- Appointment i public service --- Illegality in recruitment process ---
Effect--- Rule 10 (2), Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Civil Servants (Appointment, promotion and
transfer) Rules, 1989 --- Right to appointment --- Comparative illegality--- Non-
impleadment of necessary parties --- Scope of revisional interference ---
Respondent/plaintiff instituted suit for declaration and mandatory injunction seeking
appointment against a class-IV post, alleging that 74 appointments made by the
department were illegal, politically influenced, and contrary to rule 10 (2) of the Khyber
Pakhtunkhwa Civil Servants (Appointment, promotion and transfer) Rules, 1989 ---
Evidence revealed that no list was requisitioned from Employment Exchange, no
advertisement was issued, and appointments were made on the basis of Demi Official
(DO) letters issued by MNAs and MPAs --- Both departmental and Employment
Exchange officials admitted procedural violations --- Trial court decreed the suit, and
appellate court upheld the decree --- Held, although the appointments of others were
irregular and in breach of Rule 10 (2), such illegality could not create a vested right of
appointment for the respondent --- courts are not empowered to direct appointment
merely because others were appointed unlawfully --- Relief must be based on lawful
entitlement, not comparative illegality --- Respondent failed to implead appointed
candidates as parties, rendering proceedings defective and violative of natural justice ---
Registration with Employment Exchange alone does not confer enforceable right to
appointment --- Findings of courts below based on misconception of law and excess of
jurisdiction --- Prior illegal act cannot be invoked to justify another illegality --- Revisional
interference warranted --- Revision petition accepted, concurrent judgments and
decrees set aside, and suit dismissed.

case_info: Cr.A No. 874-P of 2025 Ajmal Sadiq  Vs  The State
remarks:
A.	The alleged discrepancies pointed out are minor and do not go to the root of the matter. The defence cannot invoke such trivialities to secure an advantage. On the contrary, the status of the accused, being employees of one of the most respectable departments, renders their conduct far more serious.
B.	The record further establishes their interconnectivity, showing not only involvement in the present occurrence but their involvement in similar activities in the past. It is merely their misfortune that they were apprehended on this occasion, otherwise, it appears they may have engaged in such activities previously but remained undetected.
C.	When circumstances of this nature surface, they invariably cause serious concern, for the accused are members of a force entrusted with the duty to defend, to protect, and to restore order in troubled areas. Their involvement in such activity is not only alarming but also corrosive to the very foundation of public trust.
D.	The participation of law-enforcement personnel in offences of this character shocks the conscience of all concerned and creates a profound sense of insecurity among the citizens.
E.	When public servants, tasked with enforcing the law, themselves become instrumental in destabilizing the area by supplying arms and ammunition, no leniency can be extended. Their cases cannot be allowed to pivot on minor discrepancies.

case_info: W.P No. 6068-P of 2025 Dr. Mian Tauseef-Ud-Din  Vs  Javed & others
remarks:
Writ Petition
Held:          Issue / Section of Laws. Section 21-Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Health Care Commission Act, 2015, Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Health Care Commission (Complaints Management) Regulation 2022 —Test for judicial review- The first test is to see whether the authority suffered with jurisdictional defect. Jurisdiction, as may be understood in light of the case law, is “a right” of an authority (including a court and/or tribunal) to decide a certain subject matter in a particular case as also the power and the manner in which it is exercised by an authority, and the person in respect of whom such power is exercisable…. The Second test is to examine that the jurisdiction must be exercised according to law… In a nutshell, an impugned order shall not suffer with procedural defect… Third test, it is a must to afford to both the parties an opportunity of fair hearing (Latin: audi alteram partem.  English: Let the other party be heard as well) … Finally, the High Court may exercise judicial review if the decision is not “based on evidence, or arrived at as a result of misreading of evidence.” The principle expounded from this ruling is that an impugned order shall be free of any adjudicatory defect.
                      Section 21-Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Health Care Commission Act, 2015- Criminal Negligence and Medical negligence-proceedings for-distinguished- As regard the criminal proceedings against the petitioner, in this respect we would not enter into the legality of such proceedings. The reason is that the criminal proceedings have not been separately challenged before us in the instant constitutional petition. Needless to say, the criminal proceedings are different in nature… So far as the propriety of the committee’s imposition of the fine, its findings reproduced in para 9, above, are self-explanatory. We find the same as based on proper appreciation of the relevant surgical protocols and their adverse effects triggered on the health of the patient owing to non-fulfillment. The learned appellate court examined those findings palpably and reduced the fine to Rs. 3,00,000/- keeping in view the petitioner’s long service career and first time default while conducting the surgery in question.
(Writ Petition is Dismissed)

case_info: W.P No. 186-P of 2023 Malik Jamshed  Vs  Ghafoor Khan
remarks: The pivotal question in proceedings under Section 12(2) CPC is whether fraud or misrepresentation was practiced upon the Court, and if so, who was allected by it. It defies logic why the petitioners, whose shares were subsequently reduced through the correction mutation, were not impleaded as parties to the original suit. This omission, coupled with the alteration of their ownership record, prima facie indicates that they were necessary parties whose exclusion vitiated the decree. Although learned counsel for the respondents argued that the petitioners had failed to summon the Patwari/AOK or challenge the order dismissing their application for additional evidence, such technical objections cannot override a substantive illegality. Once the Court concludes that a legal irregularity or material illegality was committed by the courts below, such defect cannot be allowed to persist, for it is a settled principle of law that one illegality cannot justily another.

case_info: C.R No. 307-M of 2024 with C.M No. 1156-M of 2024 Rahmat Karim & others  Vs Ghulam Muhammad & others
remarks:
The revisional jurisdiction u/s 115 C.P.C is corrective, supervisory and
exceptional: the court may interfere only where the subordinate court has (i)
exercised a jurisdiction not vested by law; (ii) failed to exercise jurisdiction so
vested, or (iii) acted in the exercise of jurisdiction illegally or with material
irregularity.
If no plausible and convening evidence is placed before the court qua alienation
of the property during his lifetime by propositus then the property is to be
considered as his legacy.
In the case of inheritance if alleged estoppel is not proved limitation for recovery
of possession to the extent of legal share of the heir would not run.

case_info: W.P No. 1454-P of 2022 Dr. Arshad Hussain  Vs  Sawabi Development Authority etc
remarks: Cancellation of plots of subsequent purchasers on account of non-deposit of dues or arrears by the original allottees was held to be illegal, unlawful and resultantly, null and void.

case_info: W.P No. 25-C of 2024 Mst. Bibi Razia Sultana  Vs  Muhammad Zubair
remarks:
Court has dismissed two writ petitions filed by Mst. Bibi Razia Sultana and Muhammad Zubair, related to a family dispute over dower, maintenance allowance, and other marital obligations.
The court held that the prompt dower of the wife was fixed at Rs.10,00,000/-, out of which Rs.9,00,000/- was outstanding. The husband was directed to provide a separate residence to the wife with all basic necessities of life.
The wife was not entitled to claim past maintenance allowance as she was living separately from her husband by her own will. The court interpreted the entry in column No.17 of the Nikahnama as part of the prompt dower, payable to the wife upon demand.
The husband`s claim that the entry was deferred dower or a gift was not supported by evidence.

case_info: Cr. A No. 463-P of 2025 Liaqat Ali etc   Vs The State etc
remarks:
i. If this Court comes to the conclusion that the injuries sustained by Imtiaz-ur-Rehman were indeed received during the same transaction, his presence at the scene would stand established beyond doubt. However, even with that acknowledgment, the role attributed to him and the other accused by the deceased and injured remains consistent throughout the record.

ii. Thus, while the possibility that Imtiaz-ur-Rehman sustained injuries during the occurrence cannot be entirely ruled out, the nature of those injuries, the evidence on record, and the consistent testimony of the prosecution witnesses do not materially undermine the prosecution case. Rather, the overall circumstances suggest that the counter-version was advanced as an afterthought to dilute the gravity of the accusation and to create confusion regarding the true genesis of the occurrence.

case_info: WP No. 287-P of 2025 ZTBL through Regional Business Head Peshawar  Vs  KP Govt
remarks: Zari Taraqiati Bank was not found to be an instrumentality of the Federal Govt. for the purpose of exemption from tax under Article 165 of the Constitution of Islamic Republic of Pakistan, 1973

case_info: TAx Reference No. 95-P of 2024 M/s Khan Gas Pvt Ltd Peshawar  Vs  Deputy Commissioner-IR Corporate Zone, RTO Peshawar etc
remarks: Although limitation provisions are related to procedural laws but create valuable rights for opposite party and cannot therefore be lightly ignored.

case_info: WP No. 870-A of 2025 Telephone Industries of Pakistan   Vs  Ghulam Abbas etc
remarks:
(a) Article 9 of the Establishment of the Office of Wafaqi Mohtasib (Ombudsman) Order, 1983 delineates the powers and jurisdiction of the Wafaqi Mohtasib. It authorizes the Mohtasib (respondent No. 3) to conduct an investigation upon a complaint filed by any aggrieved person or upon a reference made by the President, the Federal Council, or the National Assembly, as the case may be. The Mohtasib may also initiate an investigation on a motion made by the Supreme Court or a High Court during the course of proceedings before it, or even suo motu, into any allegation of maladministration on the part of any agency or its officers or employees.
However, these powers are not unfettered. The proviso to Article 9(1) expressly curtails the jurisdiction of the Mohtasib in specific circumstances. It prohibits him from entertaining or investigating any matter which, at the time of receipt of the complaint, reference, or motion, is sub judice before any court, tribunal, or board of competent jurisdiction in Pakistan. Likewise, the Mohtasib is barred from taking cognizance of matters relating to the external affairs of Pakistan, its relations or dealings with any foreign state or government, or matters connected with the defence of Pakistan, including those concerning the military, naval, or air forces, or issues governed by laws applicable to those forces.
These exclusions reflect the legislative intent to confine the Mohtasib’s jurisdiction to administrative maladministration within the civil sphere and to prevent encroachment upon matters of judicial, diplomatic, or defence sensitivity. Furthermore, Article 9(2) further circumscribes the Mohtasib’s authority by stipulating that, notwithstanding anything contained in clause (1), he shall not accept for investigation any complaint made by or on behalf of a public servant or functionary concerning matters relating to the agency in which he is, or has been, employed, particularly where the complaint pertains to a personal grievance arising out of his own service conditions or employment.
Thus, a holistic reading of Article 9 demonstrates that while the Wafaqi Mohtasib has been vested with wide powers to redress administrative injustice and maladministration, such powers remain subject to clearly defined statutory limitations, aimed at preserving the separation of powers and avoiding overlap with judicial or executive domains.

(b)The jurisdiction of the Wafaqi Mohtasib is not absolute and is expressly confined to matters involving maladministration by Federal Government agencies. Under Article 9(2) of the Order, the jurisdiction of the Mohtasib is excluded in respect of personal grievances or service-related matters of public servants or functionaries serving in any Federal Government agency or an organization controlled by it. It is a settled principle of law that any order or action taken without jurisdiction is void ab initio, coram non judice, and a nullity in the eyes of law.

(c)It is well established that jurisdiction cannot be conferred by the consent of the parties. Irrespective of whether an objection is raised by the parties, it is incumbent upon the forum assuming cognizance of a matter to determine, at the very outset, whether it possesses the requisite jurisdiction. There is no exception to the principle that an order passed or an act done by a court or tribunal lacking competence to entertain the proceedings is without lawful authority.
A defect of jurisdiction is not cured by the conclusion of the trial or inquiry, and an objection to jurisdiction may be raised at any stage of the proceedings. Likewise, where a mandatory precondition for the exercise of jurisdiction by a court, tribunal, or authority is not fulfilled, all subsequent proceedings become illegal and suffer from inherent lack of jurisdiction. Any orders passed in continuation thereof, whether in appeal or revision, are equally vitiated by illegality and are without jurisdiction.

case_info: W.P No. 669-M of 2018 Dr. Roohul Amin  Vs  Govt of KPK
remarks:
Court directed the respondents to consider Dr. Roohul Amin`s case for
proforma promotion to Chief Medical Officer (BPS-20) despite his
retirement, citing that the delay was due to the department`s inaction. The
court emphasized that a civil servant can`t be denied promotion due to
retirement when the delay lies with the department, and directed the
respondents to place the petitioner`s case before the Provincial Selection
Board for consideration within four months.

case_info: W.P No. 4017-P of 2024 Fida Muhammad  Vs  Said Muhammad Arif etc
remarks: Writ petition filed against a remand order passed in the revenue hierarchy was found not maintainable.

case_info: Cr.R No. 69-B of 2025 Imran Ullah  Vs  The state etc
remarks:
Criminal Revision.
Held:
Juvenile Justice System Act, 2018--- Determination of age--- Reconstitution of Medical Board--- Scope and legality--- Ossification test--- Forensic standard --- Accused charged under section 302, 324 & 34 PPC claimed to be juvenile --- Initial Medical Board at DHQ Hospital, Lakki Marwat assessed his age between 20 to 24 years based only on X-Ray of right hand --- Trail court allowed re-examination through fresh Medical Board at KGN Hospital, Bannu --- contention that Juvenile Justice System Act, 2018
contain no provision permitting re-examination repelled --- Held, law does not prohibit reconstitution of a Medical Board where earlier ossification report is defective, incomplete, or lacks forensic integrity --- Courts possesses inherent jurisdiction to rectify procedural deficiencies to secure the ends of justice --- initial report, based on single X-Ray and printed template, held unreliable --- trial court rightly exercised discretion to direct re-examination --- However, Medical Board to be newly constituted directed to follow standard forensic protocols by conducting radiological analysis of all requisite skeletal regions (left wrist, elbow, shoulder, pelvis etc.) and furnish structured report indicating epiphyseal fusion --- No jurisdictional error or material irregularity found --- Revision Petition dismissed in circumstances.

case_info: C.R No. 46-B of 2021 Abdul Jabbar Khan  Vs  Muhammad Gul etc
remarks:
Civil Revision.
Held:
Civil revision --- Declaration and Injunction --- Mutation entries --- Limitation --- Locus standi --- Effect of alienation by predecessor --- Correction of revenue record --- scope of recurring cause of action --- Plaintiffs/respondents filed suit for declaration, perpetual, prohibitory and mandatory injunctions --- Record showed that petitioners’ predecessors had already sold his entire holdings to defendant Lal Zaman vide Mutation No. 36429 attested on 13.12.1968 and ceased to own or possess any portion thereof --- Alterations in Khata/Khasra numbers and corresponding areas during settlement proceedings occurred long after such sale and could not relate to vendor’s property --- Held, predecessor, having divested himself of ownership, had no subsisting title at the time of impugned entries; consequently, his successor had no locus standi to challenge them --- Revenue entries questioned through suit filed in 2014, nearly four decades after attestation of disputed mutations, were hopelessly time-barred --- Principle reiterated that where predecessor acquiesced in or failed to assail revenue entries during his lifetime, successor-in-interest cannot reopen the matter without pleading and proving fraud or misrepresentation --- Each new adverse entry in the record of rights (Jamabandi) gives rise to a fresh cause of action only for a person who is in possession of the land to which such entries relates --- Concurrent findings of courts below regarding recurring cause of action, held misconceived, erroneous, and not sustainable in law --- Revision Petition was allowed in circumstances.

case_info: C.R No. 436-A of 2025 Muhammad Irshad  Vs  Rizwan Ahmad
remarks:
(a) In Islamic law, waqf signifies a permanent dedication of property to the pleasure of
Allah for the benefit of Ahl-e-Islam. Once a waqf is validly created, the ownership of the
property immediately vests in Almighty Allah, and the waqif (dedicator) irrevocably
loses all proprietary rights, control, or possession over it. The Mutawalli merely
functions as a trustee or administrator (Amin) to manage the property strictly in
accordance with the declared objectives of the waqf. Under Islamic jurisprudence,
neither the waqif nor his heirs can claim ownership of waqf property, as it belongs solely
to Almighty Allah and its benefits are meant for the Muslim community. Beside this,
Under Section 3 of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Waqf Properties Ordinance, 1979, all waqf
properties stand vested in the Chief Administrator of Auqaf, Khyber Pakhtunkhwa, for
their supervision and management, while Section 6 mandates that waqf properties are
inalienable and cannot be sold, gifted, mortgaged, or transferred except as expressly
provided by law. These provisions clearly reflect the legislative intent to preserve the
sanctity of waqf as a perpetual and inalienable trust dedicated to Almighty Allah.
(b) Where relief is sought in relation to administration or management of a waqf or trust
property, prior sanction in writing from the Advocate General is a precondition for the
institution of such a suit

case_info: C.R No. 415-A of 2025 JVD`s Mining etc  Vs  Subline Minerals etc
remarks:
(a)Trial court, while deciding the application under Order VII Rule 11 CPC, is required to
confine its consideration solely to the averments made in the plaint itself. The Court is
neither required nor permitted to examine the defence set up by the defendants, nor to
draw inferences from documents or pleas beyond the scope of the plaint. The rationale
underlying this principle is that the plaint alone forms the foundation of the plaintiff’s cause
of action, and the Court must determine, upon a plain reading of its contents, whether it
discloses a right to sue or falls within any of the contingencies enumerated in clauses (a) to
(f) of Rule 11 of Order VII, C.P.C. Fruitless or vexatious litigation must be curtailed at the
earliest possible stage, and if the plaint on its face reveals a legal defect such as being barred
by law, lacking cause of action, or suffering from any other infirmity as mentioned in Rule
11, the same should be rejected forthwith. This judicial power is not discretionary but
mandatory in nature once the conditions prescribed in Order VII, Rule 11, are satisfied.
However, at the same time, the power must be exercised with utmost caution, because
rejection of a plaint has the effect of depriving a party of the opportunity to prove its claim
through evidence.
(b) It is a well-established principle of law that the relief of temporary injunction is a
discretionary and equitable remedy, which cannot be granted as a matter of course. The
applicant must establish the concurrent existence of three essential conditions, (i) prima
facie case, (ii) balance of convenience, and (iii) the likelihood of irreparable loss or injury in
case the injunction is not granted. These ingredients are interdependent, and the absence of
any one is sufficient to defeat the claim for interim relief.

case_info: C.R No. 39-A of 2013 Misri Khan  Vs  Mst. Farooq Jan etc
remarks:
(a)The beneficiary of a gift must plead and prove the three essential ingredients of a valid
gift: Declaration or offer by the donor, Acceptance of the gift by the donee, and Delivery of
possession in pursuance of the gift. Where a gift excludes a legal heir—even if evidenced
through a registered deed—the donee bears a heavy burden to establish the original
transaction and justify the exclusion of the legal heir from the estate. In the absence of
proof of these mandatory ingredients, no valid gift of immovable property can come into
existence. Thus, the plaintiff’s failure to prove the twin requirements of acceptance and
delivery of possession was fatal to her claim.
(b) Mere passage of time does not extinguish the right of inheritance. Therefore, issues of
limitation do not arise in matters involving inheritance or allegations of fraud; such
objections become legally insignificant.
(c) Where a donee relies on an instrument purporting to gift immovable property in
praesenti, the document must be registered under Section 17 of the Registration Act, 1908.
Since a gift of immovable property also requires registration under Section 123 of the
Transfer of Property Act, non-registration is a substantive defect. In such a case, Section 49
of the Registration Act applies, preventing the unregistered instrument from creating any
right, title, or interest—vested or contingent—in the property. At most, the document may
be used for a limited collateral purpose, but not to establish ownership or any proprietary claim.

case_info: C.R No. 136-A of 2015 Mst. Tahira Bibi  Vs  M Saddique etc
remarks:
(a) It is also a settled proposition of law that partial partition cannot be
allowed unless the entire joint property is brought before the Court for
equitable division. No co-sharer can claim division of only a selected
portion of the joint holding, as that would disturb the equilibrium of shares
among the remaining co-owners. All joint holdings must be included in
proceedings for partition to ensure fairness and completeness.
(b) Private arrangement and partition deserves the same sanctity which a
lawful contract deserves and should not be interfered within any legal
proceedings unless the private arrangement or partition is otherwise not
legally permissible---Principles elucidated

case_info: Cr. MBA No. 475-D of 2025 Mudassir Abbas  Vs  The State etc.
remarks:
(Criminal Misc: Bail Petition)
(a) Criminal Procedure Code, 1898.S.376 PPC—bail grant of—further inquiry—The petitioner was cousin of the complainant and was on visiting terms to her house as they were having friendly relations for past five years. The petitioner was charged for committing zina with the complainant, recording video of the occurrence and viral the same on social media, however, the FIR does not mention specific date and time of the occurrence.
(b) Criminal Procedure Code, 1898.S.376 PPC—bail grant of—further inquiry—The medico legal report of the complainant/victim did not support the allegation against the petitioner as no marks of violence were noted on her body. No tears of semen were found on her body and clothes. The applicability of Section 376
PPC was to be determined by the learned trial Court.
(c) Criminal Procedure Code, 1898.S.376 PPC—bail grant of—DNA— By virtue of amendment made in the Code through Act No.XLIV of 2016, Section 164-B was inserted whereby in cases under Sections 376, 377 or 378-B of PPC or attempt thereto, the Deoxyribonucleic Acid (DNA) testing was made mandatory, if
practicable, from the victim and accused and sent to the Forensic Laboratory, however, no such exercise was done to bring on record sufficient material, connecting the petitioner with the offence.
(d) Criminal Procedure Code, 1898.S.21 of the Prevention of Electronic Crimes Act, 2016—bail grant of—In the USB containing video recording of the occurrence, allegedly delivered to the Investigating Officer by brother of the complainant, the male person was not identifiable while the female was identified to be the complainant. The mobile phone allegedly recovered on the pointation of petitioner did not contain any video of the occurrence. The USB and mobile phone were not sent to the FSL.
(e) Criminal Procedure Code, 1898.—bail grant of—Section 21 of the Prevention of Electronic Crimes Act, 2016, did not fall within restrictive clause of Section 497, Cr.P.C. and bail in such like cases was a rule and refusal thereof, an exception.
(Petition was allowed and the petitioner was granted bail in the circumstances).

case_info: Quashment Petition No. 09-P of 2025 Maj. Arshad Sohail (Rtd), Manager Security  Vs  The State & others
remarks:
i.	The court noted that Section 63 Cr.P.C. allows an arrested person to be released only on personal bond, bail, or by special order of a Magistrate, dealing solely with custody. Section 173(3), on the other hand, permits a Magistrate to discharge an accused from such bond upon receipt of a police report. The court emphasized that these provisions relate only to release from custody and do not authorize acquittal or termination of trial proceedings.
ii.	The court observed that while an order of discharge of an accused is administrative in nature and not judicial, it is nevertheless impermissible for a Magistrate to discharge an accused once cognizance of the case has been taken.

case_info: Cr.A No. 39-P of 2024 Sher Wali etc  Vs  Muhammad Ismail etc
remarks: It is true that the conviction of an accused in a prior trial cannot, by itself, be made the basis of conviction for those subsequently arrested, however, it is equally true that is lies upon the defense to show what substantial distinction, if any, exist between the two sets of accused, and whether the evidence now recorded warrants a different appreciation. Undoubtedly, both cases are to be treated as independent. The evidence recorded, conviction awarded, and findings reached in the previous trial were to be judged on their own footing. Likewise, the present case must be assessed independently on the basis of the evidence adduced herein. Were this Court to rely mechanically upon the previous record, the very purpose and utility of a subsequent trial would stand undermined.

case_info: Cr. MBA No. 442-B of 2025 Anwar Khan  Vs  The State
remarks:
Bail Petition.
Criminal Procedure Code (V of 1898)---
--- S. 497 --- Penal Code (XLV of 1860), Ss. 302/324/452/148/149 --- Qatl-i-amd, --- Bail, grant of -Statutory ground of delay in conclusion of trial --- Allegations against the petitioner was that he along with co-accused committed murder of complainant Ikramullah and deceased Hajra Bibi. Petitioner was arrested on 24.06.2023 and last charge was framed against him and his co- accused on 06.11.2024. It is evident from the record that in this case twenty witnesses are to be examined by the prosecution but till date only a single witness has been examined by the prosecution, In the instant case, the prosecution has not placed on record anything before the court to indicate that the delay in conclusion of trial was caused due to act of accused/petitioner or any person acting on his behalf or that the petitioner is a hardened, desperate or dangerous criminal or a previously convicted person for an offence punishable with death or imprisonment
for life or a person involved in the act of terrorism. Since the delay was caused in conclusion of trial particularly of the acts of co-accused for the reason that they were arrested one after the other, however, no one shall be condemned for the acts of others. Thus, the case of petitioner did not fall within exceptions mentioned in the fourth proviso to S. 497 (1), Cr.P.C. --- Bail application was allowed in circumstances.

case_info: Cr.A No. 20-P of 2025 Alam Khan  Vs  State
remarks:
i.	The focal issue before this Court is not merely the chain of custody of the recovered contraband or the transmission of the test-samples to the Chemical Examiner, but the personal involvement of the appellant, his participation in the occurrence and whether he had conscious knowledge of, and conscious possession over, the narcotics recovered from the vehicle.
ii.	This case demands that we go the extra mile in scrutinizing the record. the appellant faces life imprisonment, his liberty therefore calls for cautious and scrupulous appraisal of every material circumstance. Of particular concern is that two important witnesses whose statements were recorded by the Investigating Officer were thereafter not produced by the prosecution before the learned trial Court. The omission to place their testimony before the Court may be of considerable consequence, since the said evidence, if adduced, might have contradicted the case of the prosecution and tended to exonerate the appellant.
iii.
