It has, however, been the good fortune of the appellant that the said statements still form part of the prosecution record, and this Court has had the benefit of examining them with the valuable assistance of the learned Additional Advocate General. Had the learned trial Court properly considered these statements, alongwith the other material available on record, it could have arrived at a more just and correct conclusion regarding the liability of the appellant. Instead, the learned trial Court took the matter for granted, relying primarily on the depositions of official witnesses and overlooking vital evidence which, if duly appreciated, might have altered the entire complexion of the case.
iv.	Had the appellant been in the knowledge of the narcotics or had he been in any manner connected with the premises, he would naturally have mentioned the names of the watchman or the owner of the premises in his statement or requested their examination as defence witnesses. His failure to do so rather strengthens the inference that he neither knew them nor had any complicity in the transaction.
v.	If contraband was indeed found therein, then, in the absence of such knowledge, mere physical control over the vehicle cannot connect the appellant with the commission of the offence. The appellant never denied his association with the vehicle, nor did he dispute that the loading had been carried out by one Masood-ur-Rehman.
vi.	Undoubtedly, a driver in control of a vehicle is ordinarily presumed to have dominion over it, and consequently, any illicit article discovered therein may prima facie be attributed to him, but in the present case, the recovery was not effected from any concealed or secret cavity of the vehicle rather, it was made from a box lying openly in the body of the truck, a box which, as admitted by the prosecution own witness, had been placed there by Masood-ur-Rehman. The watchman of the premises corroborated this fact in clear terms, thus this Court finds sufficient material to conclude that the appellant was unaware of the presence of narcotics in the said box, and hence, conscious possession cannot be imputed to him.

case_info: W.P No. 205-M of 2024 Mst. Tahira Ihsan  Vs  District Education Officer(Male)
remarks: Court has allowed Mst. Tahira Ihsan`s writ petition, directing the District Education Officer (Male), Swat, to process her family pension case and release all admissible benefits, including perks and emoluments, related to her late husband's service. Her husband, Ihsanur Rahman, served as a Theology Teacher and Arabic Teacher for nearly 27 years until his death on November 17, 2009. The court declared the posthumous removal of Ihsanur Rahman from service, dated February 20, 2010, as null and void, citing lack of due process and disciplinary proceedings. The court ruled that Mst. Tahira Ihsan is entitled to family pension and other benefits, as her husband died during service, and disciplinary proceedings abate upon death. Additionally, the court held that the claim is not barred by laches, as pensionary benefits constitute a recurring cause of action. The District Education Officer is directed to complete the exercise within 60 days and submit a compliance report to the Additional Registrar of this court.

case_info: W.P No. 3550-P of 2023 Wajid Ali  Vs  Mohammad Ajmal Farooq, etc
remarks:
i.	When once a party has resorted for the proceedings under the Arbitration Act, 1940 and subsequently the same party for the same controversy or ancillary thereto also approached to the civil Court then it will amount to abandonment of arbitration proceedings by such party and the arbitration proceedings would lose its significance.
ii.	Filing of a suit with respect to a dispute which has already been agitated under the Arbitration Act would amount to participate in litigation on the arbitrable issues and as such it will amount to waiver of arbitration when a party institutes a Court action in violation of the arbitration agreement and it will further indicate that such party who has resorted to the civil Court by filing a civil suit in presence of arbitration proceedings has the intention not to arbitrate any further.
iii.	Filing of a suit before the civil Court after arbitration proceedings for the same controversy amounts to a presumptive waiver of his right to arbitrate then in such eventuality the arbitration proceedings could not proceed.

case_info: C.R No. 301-P of 2025 Muhammadi Gul  Vs  Sheikh Muhammad Javed
remarks:
Section 115, Code of Civil Procedure, 1908
The core questions before the Court are: (i) whether the sale transaction of the suit
property, executed between the petitioner and respondent No.3 during the pendency of
partition proceedings, is void in law; and (ii) what legal effect follows when such
transaction takes place despite a subsisting injunctive order.
Under Section 52 of the Transfer of Property Act, 1882, a transfer made pendente lite
is not void or illegal but remains subordinate to the result of the litigation. The doctrine
of lis pendens does not annul the transfer; it merely binds the transferee to the eventual
decree, confining his rights to those vested in the vendor at the time of transfer.
As regards the effect of an injunction order, any sale or alienation made in its violation
constitutes disobedience of a judicial directive, attracting contempt proceedings or
attachment under Order XXXIX Rules 1–2 CPC, yet such breach does not render the
transaction void ab initio. The object of an injunction is to preserve the status quo; its
violation invites penal consequences but does not extinguish or invalidate rights
created between the parties.
Hence, even if Rule 2(3) CPC were liberally construed to cover violations under Rule
1, the legal consequence would remain limited to penal sanctions, not nullification of
the transaction itself, as the legislature intended to uphold judicial authority, not to
annul private dealings executed in contravention thereof.

case_info: Cr.A No. 167-B of 2025 Hameed Ullah etc  Vs  The State etc
remarks:
Criminal Appeal— under Sections 334, 337-L(ii), 337-A(i), 332 P.P.C—Hurt.—Ocular account of
injured witness—Medical corroboration—Non-mention of eyewitness in FIR—Principle that quality,
not quantity, of evidence prevails—Scope of Arsh under Section 334—Absence of overt act/common
intention—Modification of conviction—Daman substituted—Acquittal.
The appellants were convicted under Sections 334 and 337-L (ii) P.P.C. for causing grievous hurt to the
complainant by means of assault and a bite injury on the right ear. Held, the testimony of the injured
complainant was natural, consistent, confidence-inspiring and duly corroborated by medical evidence,
which recorded loss of the lower half helix and an excoriation injury. The presence of the eyewitness (PW-
02), though not named in the FIR, was neither unnatural nor contrived; omission of a witness’s name in the
FIR is not fatal where his testimony bears the hallmark of truth and withstands cross-examination. Reliance
placed on the principle affirmed in Rahat Ali v. The State (2018 PCr.LJ 206) and reiterated in Muhammad
Sadiq v. The State (2022 SCMR 690) that it is the intrinsic worth of evidence—not its numerical
strength—that is determinative.
However, the injury, though grievous, did not constitute dismemberment within the contemplation of
Section 334 P.P.C, nor did the medical evidence suggest permanent deprivation or mutilation of an organ to justify Arsh. Conviction under Section 334 was therefore unsustainable and was rightly convertible to
Section 337-A(i) read with Section 332 P.P.C, attracting Daman. Conversely, no specific role, overt act, or
shared intention to inflict the proven injury was attributed to appellant Hameed Ullah; mere presence or
passive conduct cannot substitute for criminal liability under the penal law. Benefit of doubt extended in
view of the settled rule laid down in Muhammad Sadiq v. The State (2022 SCMR 690).
Resultantly, conviction of appellant Naimat Ullah modified to Section 337-A(i)/ 332 P.P.C. with a direction
to pay Rs.100,000/- as Daman, failing which he shall remain in custody until payment. Appellant Hameed
Ullah acquitted. Criminal Appeal partly allowed; connected Criminal Revision seeking enhancement
rendered infructuous and dismissed.

case_info: Cr.A No. 57-B of 2024 Rehmat Ullah & another  Vs  The State & another
remarks:
Penal Code (XLV of 1860)---
----Ss. 302(b) & 34---Qanun-e-Shahadat (10 of 1984), Art. 46---Qatl-i-amd, common intention---
Appreciation of evidence---Dying declaration---Scope---Accused were charged for the murder of
complainant by firing---Evidence produced by the prosecution is not sufficient to maintain conviction……
The place wherefrom the blood has been recovered, it shows the presence of the deceased then injured Sadar Nawaz, which has been shown in the middle of the ravineخڑ and at a distance of 350 feet from house of Khan Bahadar, so the dying declaration is contradictory to the site plan, firstly that the deceased then injured has been shown at point-A which is center of ravine instead of Kacha Rasta while it has been recorded in the report that “No sooner I reached to the house of Khan Bahadar, I was fired at by the accused party.” Now if we look again, his statement is contradictory to the site plan for the reason that he is very close to the house of Khan Akbar instead of Khan Bahadar, which is at a distance of 350 feet from the place of the deceased then injured----Another aspect of the case is that the distance between point-A and point-B is 110 feet from point-A to point-C is 125 feet. Now if the appellants had the intention to kill the deceased or if the occurrence has taken place in the mode and manner as narrated by the complainant then they would have come closer to fire at him-----Another aspect of the case is that the deceased then injured was brought to the police station for recording his report and he was lying inside the Datsun/Pick-up when allegedly his report was recorded and which fact has been confirmed by the hospital authorities that he was admitted in orthopedic ward meaning thereby that his thigh was fractured and because of severe blood for one hour and 20 minutes. Whether he was able to have been spoken or not, for that matter the doctor who examined the deceased then injured has not given his opinion or endorsed the MLC and injury sheet of the deceased that the deceased then injured was able to talk….In the FIR the complainant has charged two accused and there is one fire arm entry wound with its exit on the person of the deceased then injured---Appeal was allowed and the accused was acquitted in circumstances by setting aside conviction and sentence recorded against him by the Trial Court.

case_info: W.P No. 4627-P of 2021 Jehan Badshah and others  Vs  Govt. of K.P and other
remarks:
It is well settled by now that this Court in its constitutional jurisdiction cannot sit in judgment over the wisdom of the legislature or to struck down any legislative instrument except if such an enactment is:
a. repugnant to the injunctions of Isla as laid down in the Holy Quran and the Sunnah;
b. repugnant to any provision of the Constitution.
c. Violative of or contravenes any of the fundamental rights;
d. Not in consonance with the Principles of Policy.
The powers of the Legislature in passing a statute is beyond the scrutiny of Courts coupled with any irregularity of procedure under Article 69 of the Constitution.
The propriety, expediency and necessity of a legislative act are for the determination of the legislative authority and are not for determination by the Courts. An Act passed by the Provincial Legislature, within its constitutional sphere, stands on the same footing as an Act passed by the Federal Legislature, as provided in Article 127 of the Constitution.
(Writ petition was dismissed).

case_info: W.P No. 471-M of 2025 Dr. Fazli Subhan  Vs  Govt of KPK
remarks:
The court found that although the Khyber pakhtunkhawa Public Service
Commission (PSC) rejected his appointment for being past a 3-month deadline,
the delay was caused by the Health Department`s internal procedure, not by the
petitioner himself. The court held that a candidate should not be penalized for the
government`s own delays, especially when he was next on the merit list for a
vacant post. Technicalities should not override substantive justice and the right to
equality, directing the authorities to appoint the petitioner to the vacant position.

case_info: W.P No. 955-M of 2024 Habib Ur Rehman  Vs Toor Gul & others
remarks:
Court ruled that the Assistant Commissioner`s proceedings and order were
invalid due to lack of jurisdiction. The court held that the Assistant
Commissioner wasn`t a recognized \"court\" or \"referring authority\" under the
Khyber Pakhtunkawa Alternate Dispute Resolution Act, 2020, for ADR in
civil or criminal disputes. The court emphasized that jurisdiction is a matter
of law and can`t be conferred by consent or acquiescence of parties. The
impugned order was set aside, and parties were left to approach the
appropriate forum for redressal.

case_info: W. P No. 95-P of 2020 SNGPL PVT Ltd through General Manager and 03 others  Vs  Government of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa through Chief Secretary.
remarks:
(i)	Section 16 of the Land Acquisition Act, 1894, provides that upon completion of two essential steps (a) the making of an Award under Section 11, and (b) taking possession of the acquired land, ownership vests absolutely in the Government, free from all encumbrances. The phrase “shall vest absolutely” denotes complete transfer of proprietary rights, extinguishing all previous private ownership. Vesting occurs by operation of law and does not require any registered instrument or conveyance. Even when land is acquired for a company, ownership first vests in the Government; only, thereafter, is it transferred to the company under Sections 41 and 42 of the Act, read with the Land Acquisition (Companies) Rules, 1963.
(ii)	Section 51 of the Land Acquisition Act provide a statutory exemption from the payment of stamp duty and copying fee in respect of any award or agreement made under the Act. The legislative intent underlying this provision is to facilitate and expedite the process of land acquisition for public purposes by relieving both the acquiring authority and the affected persons from the fiscal burdens associated with the registration and stamping of acquisition documents. The phrase “no award or agreement made under this Act shall be chargeable with stamp duty” means that any award or agreement made by the Land Acquisition Collector under the Act, such as the award determining compensation for the acquired land or an agreement between the landowner and the acquiring authority (whether governmental or corporate) regarding such compensation, shall not be subject to payment of stamp duty. In other words, no stamp duty is payable on the award or the agreement itself. The expression “no award or agreement made under this Act” encompasses all awards passed by the Collector under Section 11 of the Act as well as any agreements executed between the landowners and the acquiring authority in consonance with its provisions. The words “shall be chargeable” are mandatory in nature, signifying an absolute prohibition against the imposition of stamp duty. Likewise, the latter part of the section exempts all persons claiming any right or interest under such award or agreement from the payment of any copying fee.
(iii)	The vesting of property occurs by operation of law under Section 16 of the Land Acquisition Act, rather than through any voluntary conveyance. Therefore, the imposition of a 2% stamp duty at the stage of mutation is without lawful authority and lacks legal foundation. Once ownership of land vests in the government or an acquiring body, pursuant to an award under the Land Acquisition Act, no stamp duty can be levied on such vesting, however, with respect to other provincial taxes, fees, or duties, Rule 37 of the Rules, 2020 clarifies that acquiring departments or companies, whether public or private, are not exempt from the payment of any other provincial levies, except the stamp duty applicable to the award itself.
(iv)	Section 51 of the Land Acquisition Act, 1894 specifically grants a statutory exemption from the payment of stamp duty and copying fees in respect of awards and agreements made under that Act. This exemption is confined strictly to such statutory awards and agreements and does not extend to mutation fees, registration charges, or transfer taxes imposed under any other law.

case_info: W.P No. 7516-P of 2025 with IR Shabbir Hussain  Vs  Pakistan Bar Council through its Secretary, Islamabad and others
remarks:
Lawyers are the backbone of the justice system. It is inherent in their legal training to stand for the uplifting of democracy and independence of judiciary, when and where the same is undermined. The legal fraternity has historically upheld constitutionalism, resisted authoritarianism, and played a central role in the restoration of democracy. As Justice V.R. Krishna Iyer aptly remarked about the honour of the profession “lawyering like justifying rises and falls in reputation and esteem according as they serve the great purpose of delivering justice to the people,” and when they fail in this duty, the consequences are captured in Shakespeare’s cautionary line: “The first thing we do, let’s kill all the lawyers.” This reminder tells us the nobility and sensitivity of the lawyer’s role and reinforces the need for a robust code of conduct, both to preserve the integrity of the profession and to shield its members from internal and external pressures. Besides, they represent clients before courts, advise them on legal rights and obligations, and ensure the protection of their fundamental right to a fair trial. In doing so, they not only safeguard individual rights but also contribute to the development and interpretation of law, thereby making justice more accessible.
Here, the petition concerns the fundamental rights of two advocates whose licences and authority to practice and appear before the courts have been suspended by bodies or authorities not recognized under the Act of 1973, as competent to exercise such power. It is axiomatic that every advocate possesses a fundamental right to represent a client before the courts, and correspondingly, every litigant or citizen of Pakistan has the fundamental right to be represented by an advocate of their choice, subject only to limited exceptions such as conflicts of interest. Any encroachment on these rights, whether directed at an advocate, a litigant, or a citizen, by an unauthorized person or body, constitutes a violation of constitutional guarantees and is fully amenable to enforcement by this Court in the exercise of its jurisdiction.
This principle is well embedded in our constitutional jurisprudence since long. In Ch. Manzoor Elahi 1, the apex Court ruled that the powers of High Court under Article 199(1)(c) of the Constitution are “expansive” and the Court “can exercise them not only where a citizen`s fundamental right is offended but also to enforce an individual`s right under Article 4 which is inalienable.” Likewise, in Human Rights Commission of Pakistan 2, the Hon’ble Supreme Court has elaborately delineated the scope of constitutional jurisdiction, particularly in cases involving serious violations of fundamental rights—even where the offending party does not perform a sovereign function.
In nutshell, the statutory scheme leaves no room for doubt: disciplinary control over advocates is a matter reserved exclusively for the Disciplinary Committees and Tribunals constituted under Sections 10, 15, 41 and 42 of the Act of 1973, read with the corresponding Rules. These bodies alone are vested with the jurisdiction to receive complaints, conduct inquiries, order interim suspensions, and impose penalties. Neither the Executive Committee nor the Appellate Committee of the Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Bar Council, nor for that matter any Bar Association, forms part of this legally mandated disciplinary hierarchy. Their functions are administrative and facilitative, not adjudicatory. Consequently, any action initiated or order passed by such bodies, whether suspending an advocate’s licence, restricting appearance before courts, or otherwise impairing the statutory right to practice, stands outside their conferred authority. Such actions are, therefore, ultra vires the Act, devoid of procedural legitimacy, and wholly unsustainable in the eyes of law.
It is an undisputed and settled position that an advocate who has accepted a brief owes a primary, non-derogable duty to his client and to the administration of justice: he must appear when the matter is called or make suitable alternative arrangements 3, he may not, by strike or boycott, thwart his client’s right to effective representation or cause avoidable adjournments that infringe the accused’s or litigant’s right to speedy and fair trial. This position is affirmed by the Hon’ble Supreme Court in Shahbaz Akmal 4, wherein it was held that a detained accused must not suffer because his lawyer elects to strike and reminded the profession of the Canons requiring appearance or satisfactory alternatives. Similarly, the High Court of Balochistan in Kaura Khan 5, which categorically held that lawyers have no right to compel courts to adjourn, and that courts are not obliged to wait for counsel who absent themselves pursuant to boycott calls. The worthy High Court further held that it is an inalienable right of every citizen “to seek justice and to be treated in accordance with law” guaranteed under Article 4 of the Constitution. Thus, strikes by lawyers “affect not only public right of access to justice, but also deprive the general public of their fundamental rights”. On the adverse implications of non-appearance of lawyers before Courts, the worthy Baluchistan High Court held that “non- appearance of lawyers, has many adverse implications i.e. wastage of time, unjustifiably adjournments, gives rise to backlog and hampering the course of justice.”

1 . Ch. Manzoor Elahi v. Federation of Pakistan and others (PLD 1975 SC 66)
2 . Human Rights Commission of Pakistan and 2 others v. Government of Pakistan and others (PLD 2009 SC 507).
3 . Rule 166 of the Rules of 1976: It is the duty of advocates to appear in Court when a matter is called and if it is so possible to make satisfactory alternative arrangements.
4 . Shahbaz Akmal v. The State through Prosecutor General Punjab, Lahore (2023 SCMR 421).
5 . Kaura Khan v. The State (2023 MLD 1260).

case_info: WP No. 5234-P of 2024 Haji Ameer Nawaz   Vs  Torkhel through Faith Khan
remarks: The forum of Assistant Political Agent was found non-existing after repeal of Article 247 of the Constitution of Islamic Republic of Pakistan, 1973 as well as implied repeal of Frontier Crime Regulations and if any fresh proceedings are to be conducted, same has to be conducted before the competent Civil Court.

case_info: Cr.A No. 417-P of 2024 Sarwar Shah & others  Vs  The State & another
remarks:
There is no denial to the fact that, with the death of the accused persons in the cross-version, the proceedings in DD-16 abated; however, the documents prepared during investigation do not lose their evidentiary value. The materials and evidence collected by the Investigating Officer, including the site plans, medico-legal certificates, and recovery memos, can still be taken into consideration for a just decision of the matter.
This Court is conscious of the fact that the defence, right from the inception of the trial, endeavored to explain the circumstances under which the unfortunate incident occurred, as well as the injuries sustained by two of the appellants. It is further noteworthy that during the recording of the statements of the accused under Section 342, Cr.P.C., specific questions were put to them regarding the injuries received by two of the appellants in the same episode. The accused, in their replies, reiterated that both sustained firearm injuries during the same transaction. Thus, it stands established that the accused did not conceal this fact; rather, they consistently admitted the occurrence of injuries and the circumstances in which the same were received.
The evidence clearly demonstrates that the altercation was sudden, arising out of a property dispute, and that both sides resorted to firing, which culminated in the tragic loss of lives. There is no denial to the fact that one side suffered more grievously, having lost three of its members; however, the other side also endured substantial harm, as two of the appellants sustained serious firearm injuries. In cases of this nature, it is not the extent of damage suffered by either side that determines culpability, but rather the circumstances leading to the incident and the conduct of the parties involved.
Since it stands established that both parties were equally responsible for the occurrence and that the incident took place in the heat of the moment, without premeditation, arising out of a property dispute, the sentence awarded to the appellants does not appear to be commensurate with the circumstances of the case. The learned Trial Court also failed to give due weight to the fact that two of the appellants themselves sustained serious firearm injuries during the same occurrence.

case_info: Cr.A No. 182-A of 2025 Muhammad Shahbaz Vs  SP Police etc
remarks:
(a) It is also well settled that failure to appear in response to a summons does not constitute an offence committed “in the presence or view of the Court.” Consequently, summary punishment under Sections 480 to 482 Cr.P.C. cannot be invoked. The proper course for the Court, in such circumstances, is to adopt coercive measures under the Cr.P.C. to compel attendance or, if prosecution is deemed necessary, to file a
complaint under Section 195 Cr.P.C. for trial before the competent Magistrate.
(b) Section 195(1)(a) Cr.P.C. clearly provides that no Court shall take cognizance of any offence punishable under Sections 172 to 188 PPC, except on a complaint in writing by the public servant concerned or by some other public servant to whom he is administratively subordinate. The purpose of this provision is to safeguard individuals from arbitrary prosecution for contempt-like disobedience and to ensure that only those cases which, in the opinion of the competent authority, warrant prosecution, are brought before a criminal Court.

case_info: Cr.A No. 199-A of 2024 Muhammad Shoaib  Vs  The State and another
remarks:
a) If foundation of the case i.e. Murasila / Crime Report, is doubtful and record suggests that the same was not prepared on the place, mode and manner as alleged therein, benefit of the same has to be extended to the accused.
b) Where presence of eyewitnesses on the place and at the time of occurrence becomes doubtful, their testimonies are not safe for reliance.
c) Unnatural and inhumane conduct of the eyewitnesses, who also happen to be close relatives of the deceased i.e. passivity and inaction, in the face of a grave assault on their beloved (deceased), would render their presence on the spot at the time of occurrence highly doubtful.
d) Where the distance between the assailant and the victim is considerable, the angle may lose its probative value. But where the distance is close and fixed, the trajectory becomes crucial. In case, the assailant is alleged to have fired from a distance of only four (4) feet, while the victim (deceased) was facing him; from such a short range, the entry wound ought logically to be on the front of the body, with exit towards the back. No sudden or defensive movement could reasonably place the victim (deceased) in a position to receive the injury in a straight line, entering from left shoulder and exiting from the right arm.
e) Where medical and ocular evidence is inconsistent then such ocular account cannot be relied upon and benefit of such conflict goes in favour of an accused.
f) If during post mortem examination, the concerned doctor takes some item(s), sealed the same into phial and hands over to police for forensic and DNA analysis, failure on the part of Investigation Officer to conduct and conclude investigation with respect to the same being significant in nature, would create a dent in the prosecution case.
g) Material inconsistencies in Crime Report and statement(s) of prosecution witness(es) as to the exact place of occurrence would indicate that the incident did not occur in the mode and manner alleged by the prosecution.
h) Mere recoveries of incriminating articles or for that matter weapon of offence and positive report of FSL, would be of no use to prosecution if it fails to establish its case against an accused by producing unimpeachable, trustworthy, reliable and confidence inspiring eyewitnesses of the occurrence.
i) Even one or two days unexplained delay in recording the statement of eye- witnesses in absence of valid and cogent explanation, would be fatal and testimony of such witnesses cannot be safely relied upon.
j) If, on one hand, prosecution failed to establish its motive against an accused for commission of the offence, while on the other, from the record, it transpires that the complainant party has sufficient motive to implicate the accused for the commission of said offence, his false implication cannot be ruled out.

case_info: W.P No. 6729-P of 2025 Mst. Saima Samdani etc  Vs  Director ACE & others
remarks:
i.	This Court finds it rather perplexing that parallel proceedings are being pursued on identical allegations before two different forums. If the Anti-Corruption Establishment is permitted to proceed further, leading to the registration of an FIR and subsequent prosecution of the petitioners, the question naturally arises as to what bearing such proceedings would have on the pending election petition.
ii.	This Court is not inclined to deliver an early knockout to either of the parties through any pronouncement made herein. The object of this adjudication is rather to facilitate the parties in pursuing their respective claims before the forum competent in law, in a manner consistent with the statutory scheme, so that both sides may achieve their legitimate legal objectives through due process.
iii.	The doctrine of election is not a novel proposition in law. It is a time-honored principle firmly rooted in the jurisprudence of English Common Law, deriving its force from the established maxims of equity, estoppel, and judicial propriety. Historically, courts of equity discouraged litigants from indulging in forum shopping, that is, seeking multiple adjudicatory forums sequentially in the hope of securing a favourable verdict.
iv.	The deliberate legislative inclusion of these provisions signifies that the Election Commission alone retains jurisdiction to address such matters in the first instance. Thus, the confusion that enveloped the Anti-Corruption Establishment is self-created, stemming from a misreading of the statutory framework.
v.	When read conjointly, Sections 190, 191, and 193 create a complete and self-contained mechanism for the determination of all election-related offences. The framework leaves no room for an external agency, including the Anti-Corruption Establishment, to assume jurisdiction in matters arising from acts or omissions committed by election officials during the conduct of elections.
vi.	Therefore, for all intents and purposes, the jurisdiction of the Anti-Corruption Establishment stands impliedly excluded.
vii.	The doctrine of implied exclusion of jurisdiction rests upon discerning the true intention of the legislature, an exercise that cannot be confined within the narrow bounds of literal construction. The issue does not ordinarily arise from explicit statutory words, but from the design, purpose, and policy underlying the enactment.
viii.	The literal rule is insufficient in this field, for the ouster of jurisdiction is seldom expressed in plain words; it must therefore be inferred by necessary implication from the overall structure and intent of the statute.

case_info: Cr.A No. 158-M of 2022 Muhammad Nasim Sahar through Legal heirs  Vs  Nasib Zada & others
remarks:
Court upholds the acquittal of the accused in a case filed under the Illegal Dispossession
Act, 2005. The Court affirmed the trial court`s decision to acquit the respondents under
Section 265-K of the Criminal Procedure Code, ruling that the prosecution`s case was
fundamentally flawed and lacked credible evidence. The key findings were that the
complainant`s own witnesses provided inconsistent testimonies, failed to establish
continuous possession, and, critically, admitted that the respondents were co-owners of
the disputed property with a longstanding presence there. The Court emphasized the legal
principle of the \"double presumption of innocence\"; favoring an acquitted accused and
declined to interfere, concluding that the evidence was too weak to support a conviction
and that the dispute appeared to be a civil matter over property ownership inappropriately
pursued as a criminal case.

case_info: C.R No. 126-A of 2011 Aftab Shah  Vs  Aurangzeb Khan
remarks:
1. In pre-emption case, when once vendee on acceptance of his application by the concerned Court, withdraws decretal amount (compensation deposited under decree) the same would amount to clear acceptance of the decree and acquiescence in its terms, therefore, he cannot be permitted to turn around and challenge that very decree. If pre-emption amount is deposited by the pre-emptor in Court towards the satisfaction of the decree, the same is held in trust by the Court while the litigation is pending. Its withdrawal by vendee clearly means that the amount was discharged to the him and the dispute concluded. His conduct constitutes waiver of the right to contest and he stands estopped from doing so. The principle is well settled that a party cannot both approbate and reprobate at the same time.
2. Where a party concealed material fact(s) from the Court, such concealment disentitles him from any discretionary relief as it is settled norm that he who comes into equity must come with clean hands.

case_info: W.P No. 170-M of 2024 Mst. Nazi Shaukat Ali Khan and others  Vs  Miangul Hilal through legal heirs and others
remarks:
The revisional powers u/s 164 of the Land Revenue Act, 1967 are wide, corrective and supervisory in nature, empowering the Board of Revenue to rectify jurisdictional errors, procedural irregularities or injustices committed by subordinate officers. The Board of Revenue, being the highest forum in the revenue hierarchy, possesses full authority to direct subordinate revenue officers to undo any wrong committed in the revenue record and to rectify the same.
High Court, while exercising its constitutional jurisdiction under Article 199 of the Constitution, ought not to interfere with intermediate, remand, or interlocutory orders passed by the competent authorities unless such orders are patently without jurisdiction, manifestly perverse, or in defiance of law.

case_info: C.R No. 581-P of 2022 Muqarab Khan and others  Vs  Akhtar Zeb and others
remarks:
(i) The person who has been appointed as attorney/ agent has to obtain a special permission from his principal while alienating the property of his principal either in his own favor or in the name of his close relative of which the ultimate beneficiary is the attorney himself and thus, in absence of such special permission, any transaction entered and executed by the attorney/ agent would be ineffective upon the rights of the principal.
(ii) Where a beneficiary alleged the disputed transaction as a gift then he has to prove the original incident of gift to the effect that as to when, where and in whose presence, the donor had declared his intention to gift the property and such beneficiary has also to prove the declaration on the part of donor, its acceptance by the done and of course, the delivery of possession and thus, if such pre-conditions for a valid gift has neither been pleaded by the beneficiary in his pleading nor later on, the same has been proved, then such beneficiary would not be legally entitled to derive any benefit from such transaction.
(iii)Where a party pleads a verbal transaction or a transaction on the basis of an unregistered deed then in order to attach any legal worth to such transaction, the beneficiary has to prove 
his stance, through a solid, strong and unimpeachable evidence and thus, any lacuna or omission in this regard would be fatal for the case of such person.
(iv)An act of gift is a personal transaction and it could not be performed by the agent/ attorney on behalf of its principal/ donor, as the consideration of gift is always love and affection of the donor for the donee.
(v)In cases where a transaction in the form of a mutation has been made but such mutation has been challenged by the opposite party being the result of fraud, then the beneficiary of such mutation has to prove the same as per the requirement of Article 17 read with Article 79 of the Qanun-e-Shahadat Order, 1984 by producing the Revenue Officials as well as two marginal witnesses of the said mutation and in case of non-production of its marginal witnesses, such transaction could not be stamped as a proved transaction within the meaning of the aforesaid Articles.
(vi)Civil cases are to be decided on the principle of preponderance of evidence and any party who proves its case as per their pleading shall be entitled for a favorable decree in his favor.
(vii) Concurrent findings of the fora below are not sacrosanct and whenever the High Court in its revisional jurisdiction comes to the conclusion that the same are the result of misreading, non-reading of evidence or the result of misapplication of law on the subject or if the same suffers from illegalities or material irregularities, then the same could be conveniently set-aside/ reversed.

case_info: Consumer Appeal No. 169-B of 2024 Vice Chancellor Khushal Khan Khattak University Karak etc  Vs  Neelofar etc
remarks:
Consumer Protection Act, 1997—Educational
services—Deficiency in service—University
admitted student to M.A. program on the basis of
a Bachelor’s DMC later alleged to be invalid;
student completed entire coursework over two
years, paid dues, and resided in hostel; at final
stage issuance of transcript/degree was
refused—Held: It was the mandatory duty of the
University’s Admission Committee to verify
academic credentials at the time of admission;
failure to do so amounted to administrative
negligence and deficiency in service, and the
institution could not subsequently penalize the
student for its own lapse. Private college
operator who misrepresented affiliation with a
recognized university and failed to disclose ban
on Al-Khair University was guilty of deliberate
concealment and gross deficiency in service.
Concurrent findings of Consumer Court
disclosing no illegality or perversity were
upheld; both appeals dismissed; application for
condonation of delay rendered infructuous.

case_info: W.P No. 5842-P of 2023 with IR Bank of Khyber Peshawar  Vs  Pakistan through the Secretary, Ministry of Finance, Islamabad and other
remarks:
Section 99D, as noted earlier, commences with a non obstante clause and constitutes a charging provision in its own right, imposing an additional tax upon a person whose income, profits, or gains arise from any economic factor or factors that yield windfall income. Though the Ordinance does not define the expression windfall income or windfall profits, the language of Section 99D leaves little doubt that what is sought to be taxed is an additional income of companies arising from unusual economic circumstances. By virtue of Entry No. 47 of the Fourth Schedule, read with Articles 142 and 165A of the Constitution, Parliament is empowered to legislate on the subject. This proposition, in fact, has not been seriously disputed by the Petitioner and already stands affirmed by the Hon’ble Sindh High Court in the case of National Bank of Pakistan (supra).
The authority of the Parliament to impose tax with retrospective effect as the matter has already been settled by the apex Court in the case of Mekotex (Pvt.) Limited. The apex Court, after undertaking an exhaustive survey of case law, lucidly held that there exists no constitutional limitation on the legislative competence conferred by Article 142 of the Constitution upon Parliament and the Provincial Assemblies to enact laws with retrospective effect, save that such power must always remain subject to the Constitution itself.
The format of the financial statement for banks and DIFs is being issued periodically by the State Bank of Pakistan, keeping in view changes in law, regulations and rules. However, the Second Schedule to the Ordinance of 1962 also provides specimens/guidelines for making financial statements of the financial position of a banking company, which should, inter alia, include assets, liabilities, net assets, interest earned, non-markup interest expenses, profit/loss before taxation, and profit/loss after taxation in the like matters. The financial statements should clearly provide for assets/total assets on one hand as well as liabilities, equities, share capital, retained earnings, and total liabilities on the other hand to balance the account of a banking company.
It is important to note that a banking company or listed company is not obligated to issue dividends against retained earnings. The decision regarding the utilization of such earnings rests with the Board of Directors, who must act in the best interest of the company. If the banking company elects to distribute dividends—whether through a payout ratio to shareholders or by issuing bonus shares—it may do so. Alternatively, it retains the discretion to reinvest the retained earnings or profits without issuing dividends. Once reinvested, the amount forms part of the company’s assets. However, if dividends are issued against the said earnings or profits, either by way of cash payout or bonus shares, the bonus shares shall contribute to the company’s equity.
Every company/person is required to file income tax returns in the manner as provided under Chapter X of the Ordinance, which is deemed an assessment order under Section 120 of the Ordinance. The banking companies are required to file returns in the manner as provided under the Ordinance. The annual financial statement of a banking company is translated into the tax returns. There are three stages in the imposition of a tax: there is the declaration of liability, that is the part of the statute which determines what persons in respect of what property are liable. Next, there is the assessment. Liability does not depend on assessment. That, ex hypothesi, has already been fixed. But assessment particularize the exact sum which a person liable has to pay. Lastly, come the methods of recovery, if the person taxed does not voluntarily pay. 2 Under the scheme of the Ordinance, liability to pay income tax accrues to the taxpayer on the last day of the income year/accounting year, though the tax became payable after it was quantified in accordance with the procedure laid down in the Income Tax law. 3 Subject to the enabling provision of the Ordinance for reopening of assessment, on filing the tax returns, the income tax affairs of a company are thus closed.
The payment of dividends is a distinct taxing event. Section 150 of the Ordinance envisages that every resident company paying a dividend shall deduct tax from the gross amount of the dividend paid at the rate specified in Division I of Part III of the First Schedule. Similarly, Section 5 of the Ordinance envisages that a tax shall be imposed, at the rate specified in Division III of Part I of the First Schedule, on every person who receives a dividend from a company. Section 5 speaks of the dividend being “received” by a person from a company, while Section 150 speaks of the dividends being “paid” by the latter. The former is a general expression, bringing to tax all that is received by way of dividend, while the latter is limited to the requirement of Section 150, i.e., requiring a deduction to be made on the dividend paid. 4 It is in this view of the matter, at the end of each tax year when the dividends are paid to the shareholders it is subject to the impost of taxing event, and once the share money goes in the hands of shareholders, it becomes past and closed transactions. If the legislature intends to unsettle these past and closed transactions, it must have been in specific words.
The language of Section 99D of the Ordinance does not explicitly impair/unsettle these past and closed transactions. However, in our humble view, it charges an additional tax on the earnings, profits, gains, if it remains with the company (as reflected in the financial statements) in any form, including in the form of investments and thus, in that event, it would be subject to the imposition of tax under Section 99D of the Ordinance. However, as far as the said profit which is out of the hands of the company and paid to the shareholder, either in cash or through issuance of bonus shares, it becomes a closed transaction for that tax/financial year, and Section 99D of the Ordinance does not give any impression to charge the income of the shareholders of a banking company.

case_info: Cr.M BA No. 448-B of 2025 Saad Ullah Khan  Vs  The State
remarks:
Bail Petition.
Held: Bail --- Dishonoured cheque --- Section 489 P.P.C --- Non-prohibitory clause--- Partial payment reducing subsisting liability --- Further inquiry --- Petitioner sought post-arrest bail in a case registered under section 489-F P.P.C --- Allegation that cheque of Rs. 73,50,000/- issued towards purchase of gold was dishonoured --- Record showed that Rs. 50,00,000/- had already been paid prior to registration of FIR, leaving balance of Rs. 23,35,000/ --- Held, once substantial portion of cheque amount stood paid, cheque no longer represented existing liability in full; element of dishonest intention diluted --- Case thus called for further inquiry within meaning of Section 407 (2) Cr.P.C. ---Petition allowed; accused admitted to bail.

case_info: Review Petition No. 110-P of 2024 in C.R No. 806-P of 2018 Mumtaz Ahmad  Vs  Sahib ur Rehman
remarks:
Scope and purpose of Review:
The literal meaning of “review” is re-examination of something for the sake of correction or improvement. In legal proceedings, the term review implies reconsideration or re-adjudication of an already decided case by the same court. The purpose behind investing a court with review jurisdiction is rectification of errors. The
courts tend to stand by the judgments rendered by them, as a general principle, in line with the doctrine of stare decisis. However, in interest of justice, courts across the globe can reconsider their earlier decisions. The concept of review is premised upon the fallibility of human beings that is to say that humans can never be immune to committing errors and mistakes. The doctrine of review therefore permits the courts to rectify such mistakes in order to avoid serious miscarriage of justice. The ultimate object of the court is to uphold justice therefore when injustice and illegality is bound to perpetuate due to a patent error, a court may not hesitate in reviewing its order. The Civil Procedure Code, 1908 (CPC), offers a structured procedure for an aggrieved party desiring to seek a review of a decree or order. Initially, the process commences with the submission of an application for review. The very notion of a review embedded in the Civil Procedure Code, 1908, is rooted in principles that prioritize the rectification of glaring errors and uphold the sanctity of justice. The CPC crystallizes the distinction between an appeal and a review, emphasizing that the latter is not an avenue to simply express dissatisfaction with a judgment or to re-litigate. Instead, the review is an exceptional tool, invoked only to correct substantial oversights or palpable errors. A cornerstone of this principle is that the error in question should be manifest and patent. It should not demand exhaustive scrutiny or extensive argumentation. The error should be glaring enough to be discernible without delving deep into elaborate debates. Essentially, any mistake that requires long-drawn discussions to identify isn’t a valid ground for review, as the process isn’t intended to serve as a re-hearing of the case.

case_info: W.P No. 3614-P of 2023 with IR with CM No. 1815 of 2023 with CM No. 1093/2025 M/S khyber Tobacco Company Ltd, through its authorized representative, Mr. Khurshid Wahab and others  Vs  Pakistan through Federal Secretary, Ministry of Finance, Revenue and Economic Affairs , Islamabad and others
remarks:
It is manifest from the foregoing constitutional trajectory that the legislative competence to levy duties of excise has consistently remained within the federal domain. What is, however, significant to note is that while Entry No. 45 of the Act of 1935 expressly included excise duties on tobacco along with other manufactured or produced goods, this express reference to “tobacco” disappears from the corresponding provisions of the subsequent constitutional instruments. Neither the Constitution of 1956, nor that of 1962, nor even the Constitution of 1973, make a specific mention of tobacco in their respective entries relating to excise duties, thereby marking a notable shift in the drafting approach, albeit without disturbing the overall federal competence in the matter.
In matters of complexity where competing jurisdictions of the Federation and the Provinces arose, particularly in determining whether a given tax fell within the field of taxation reserved for one or the other, their lordships were of the view that overlapping of legislative spheres was never the intention of the lawmakers. As Sir Maurice Gwyer C.J. said in 1940 F.C.R. 188 1 that “It must inevitably happen from time to time that legislation though purporting to deal with a subject in one list, touches also upon a subject in another list, and the different provisions of the enactment may be so closely intertwined that blind adherence to a strictly verbal interpretation would result in a large number of statutes being declared invalid because the Legislature enacting them may appear to have legislated in a forbidden sphere. Hence the rule which has been evolved by the Judicial Committee, whereby the impugned statute is examined to ascertain its pith and substance or its true nature and character for the purpose of determining whether it is legislation with respect to matters in this list or in that.” Similarly, it was also a consistent view that where necessary, recourse could be taken to the doctrine of “pith and substance” for resolving the conflict. It was in this context that their lordships, in the case concerning the Central Provinces and Berar Sales of Motor Spirit and Lubricants Taxation Act, 1938 (AIR 1939 FC 1), declined to extend the scope of excise duty beyond the process of manufacture or production of goods.
In addition to, in Hirjina & Co.’s case (supra), the Hon’ble Supreme Court was of the considered view that the framers of the Constitution had, by design, omitted the expression “tobacco and other goods manufactured or produced in Pakistan.” It was further the opinion of the Apex Court that since the Constitution does not define the term “excise duty,” no restrictive construction can be imposed so as to curtail the legislative competence of the Federal Legislature in this regard.
The definition of “un-manufactured tobacco” under the Act of 2005 is couched in broad terms, referring to tobacco usable for the manufacture of cigarettes after being processed and converted by Green Leaf Threshing Units. Thus, the taxable event under Section 3 of the Act of 2005 arises at the stage when tobacco, having undergone the threshing process, becomes capable of being used in cigarette manufacture. Similarly, the Impugned Provincial Act defines “un-manufactured tobacco” as raw or bulk tobacco which has not yet reached the stage of a finished product, such as cigarettes or cigars. The object of both levies is to charge excise duty on un-manufactured tobacco, and the said duty becomes part of the federal as well as the provincial revenue. There are three stages in the imposition of a tax; declaration of liability, which is the part of the statute that determines which persons are liable in respect of what property; assessment; and recovery. Tax could only be charged and levied when the taxing event squarely falls within the purview of the charging section. The concept of a taxing event is central to any fiscal statute, for it delineates and circumscribes the legislative field. Under our constitutional framework, taxing powers are not shared between the Federation and the Provinces; they are divided and allocated with precision. Once it is established that a particular taxing event falls within the exclusive domain of Parliament, the inevitable corollary flowing from Article 142(d) of the Constitution of 1973, is that the Provinces are precluded from legislating in respect thereof or imposing a parallel levy upon the same taxable event. 
In the light of the foregoing observations and findings, we hold that the Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Provincial Excise Duty (Un-manufactured Tobacco) Act, 2024, travels beyond the legislative competence of the Provincial Assembly and is accordingly declared ultra vires the Constitution and struck down. The imposition of excise duty on un-manufactured tobacco is a matter falling exclusively within the field of legislation reserved for the Federation under Entry No. 44 of the Federal Legislative List, read with Article 142 of the Constitution.

case_info: W.P No. 3437-P of 2022 Dr. Ghulam Sadiq and others  Vs  Govt of KP through Secretary HEC and others
remarks:
The doctrine of promissory estoppel and legitime expectation are equitable doctrine evolved by the superior Courts while adjudicating upon the complaint lodged by the aggrieved party against an unfair and arbitrary action of the authorities. The principle has been elaborately expounded in Fecto Belarus Tractors Limited Vs. Pakistan through Ministry of Finance Economic Affairs (2001 PTD 1829). 
Once a flaw has been rectified through official acknowledgment, the consequential benefits flowing therefrom must necessarily follow, particularly when the denial of such benefits results in unequal treatment among similarly placed employees. 
After acknowledging seniority from the original joining date, the respondents were legally obliged to extend corresponding monetary benefits. The subsequent contention of ineligibility or flawed selection cannot be raised at this belated stage, especially when the petitioners had successfully completed the selection process, fulfilled the eligibility conditions, completed their Ph.D. programs under University sponsorship, and were reinstated in service upon return. 
(Writ petition was accepted).

case_info: Custom Reference No. 07-A of 2025 Muhammad Nawaz  Va  Additional Collector etc
remarks:
(a)It is by now well-settled in law that once a person produces a valid and genuine registration book issued by the Motor Registration Authority (MRA), a presumption of regularity and authenticity attaches to such official document, thereby rebutting the initial presumption envisaged under Section 187. The onus then shifts upon the Customs Department to establish through cogent and admissible evidence that the vehicle is, in fact, smuggled or non-customs-paid. Moreover, it is also a recognized principle that if the import documents or allied records pertain to a period older than five years, their verification cannot form the basis for declaring such documents as bogus or fabricated merely on account of non-traceability in departmental record, as official record retention policies often limit the preservation period.

(b)The burden of proof under Section 187 of the Customs Act, 1969, holding that once a vehicle owner produces a genuine registration book issued by the Motor Registration Authority (MRA), a presumption of legality and authenticity attaches to it. The initial burden of proof then shifts to the Customs Department, which must establish through substantive evidence that the vehicle is smuggled or non-customs-paid. In this case, the petitioner successfully discharged the burden by producing all relevant and verified documents, while the Customs authorities failed to provide any contrary proof.

(c)The definition and scope of “smuggling” under Section 2(s) of the Customs Act, 1969, pertain to the act of unlawfully bringing goods into or removing them from Pakistan. The petitioner, being a subsequent purchaser and not the importer, could not be held guilty of smuggling merely on suspicion or on the basis of unverifiable departmental correspondence.

Section 211 of the Customs Act, 1969, relates to record retention and lawful excuse. Customs authorities cannot demand import records beyond five years. After this statutory period, the vehicle owner has a lawful excuse for non-production of such documents, and failure of verification after that period cannot form a valid basis for confiscation.

(d)Registration by the Motor Registration Authority under the Motor Vehicles Ordinance, 1965, carries a presumption of regularity and authenticity under Article 129(e) of the Qanun-e-Shahadat Order, 1984. Since the registration process itself requires verification of import and tax documents, once registration is completed and remains unchallenged, it is presumed lawful and valid.

(e)The principle of “Mushtri Hoshyar Bash” (buyer beware) cannot be applied to penalize a bona fide purchaser who buys a vehicle duly registered by the competent authority. Reliance on official registration satisfies the requirement of reasonable care and diligence expected from a prudent buyer.

(f)The Customs Appellate Tribunal misread and failed to consider vital evidence, including the Forensic Science Laboratory report, which confirmed that the vehicle’s chassis and engine numbers were genuine and untampered. The Tribunal also overlooked the statutory limitation under Section 211, which prohibits verification of import documents after five years, rendering its judgment legally untenable.

The distinction between “lawful authority” and “lawful excuse” shows that the latter, used in Section 156(1)(89) and (90) of the Customs Act, 1969, is broader and requires a lesser degree of proof. A vehicle that has been duly registered and verified under law provides a sufficient lawful excuse, even if original import documents are unavailable after the statutory period.

case_info: W.P No. 1211-M of 2023 Shahnaz Akhtar  Vs Govt: of KPK & others
remarks:
Writ petition filed by Shahnaz Akhtar, directing her reinstatement in service as a Primary School Teacher (PST). The petitioner`s removal from service was based on the cancellation of her domicile certificate, which has been set aside by a civil court decree.
The court held that the petitioner`s domicile of origin (Village Usurai) remains valid, and the cancellation of her second domicile (Wari) was misconceived. The respondents' assumption that the petitioner couldn`t hold two domiciles is misplaced, given the distinction between domicile of origin and domicile of choice.
The petitioner is entitled to reinstatement, outstanding salaries, and seniority benefits, but not back benefits. The court emphasized that every individual has a domicile of origin, which remains until a new domicile is acquired.

case_info: W.P No. 216-A of 2019 Javed Ehsan and others  Vs  Govt
remarks:
(a) It is a settled principle that a lessee cannot claim rights greater than those
conferred by the original lease deed. Any subsequent permission irregularly or
mistakenly granted by municipal staff cannot override the express terms of the
lease. Where the lease is granted solely for residential purposes, any change in
land use without lawful modification constitutes a breach that renders the lease
liable to cancellation. A lease that is misused or violated through acts contrary to
its covenants becomes voidable at the option of the lessor, and a defaulting
lessee cannot seek relief under Article 199 of the Constitution. Clause (a) of
Section 111 of the Transfer of Property Act specifies that a lease granted for a
fixed period automatically terminates upon expiry and does not confer upon the
lessee any implied right of renewal. Clause (c) further provides that no person
can transfer to another a greater interest in property than he himself possesses.
Accordingly, once respondent No.1 conceded—both in writing and verbally—to
deliver vacant possession of the premises, respondent No.2, being merely a
dealer or sub-lessee, could not lawfully retain possession any further.
(b)Sections 108 and 111 of the Transfer of Property Act, 1882 set out the rights
and obligations of both lessor and lessee, and the lawful modes through which a
lease may be determined. Section 108 establishes that the lessor must disclose
material defects, deliver possession, and ensure peaceful enjoyment of the
property, while the lessee must pay rent, maintain the property, disclose relevant
facts, and use the premises only for the purpose for which it was leased. The
lessee may transfer or sublet his interest unless expressly restricted, but remains
bound by all lease obligations. Importantly, the lessee is prohibited from using
the property for any purpose other than the one stipulated in the lease or
erecting permanent structures without the lessor’s consent. Section 111 outlines
the circumstances in which a lease terminates, including expiry of the fixed term,
occurrence of a specified event, express or implied surrender, and
forfeiture—such as when the lessee violates an express condition allowing the
lessor to re-enter. Collectively, these provisions ensure clarity in landlord-tenant
relations, uphold the sanctity of contractual obligations, and prevent arbitrary
termination while protecting the proprietary rights of both parties.

case_info: Cr.A (SPLA) No. 03-B of 2025 Idrees Khan  Vs  SHO Police Station, Norang Lakki Marwat etc.
remarks:
Criminal Appeal— Under Sections 3 & 4—Illegal Dispossession Act, 2005—Dismissal of complaint under Section 203, Cr.P.C.—Maintainability of appeal—Section 417(2) & 417(2-A), Cr.P.C.—Section 8-A of Act XXVIII of 2017—Scope of appellate and revisional jurisdiction—Conversion into writ petition—Police inquiry not substitute for judicial determination—Premature dismissal—Remand.
Complainant alleged unlawful dispossession from inherited land on the basis of forged sale agreements
executed during his absence abroad; complaint under Sections 3 & 4 of the Illegal Dispossession Act, 2005 dismissed as non-maintainable by trial Court on strength of police inquiry. Held, appeal under Section
417(2) Cr.P.C. lies only to Provincial Government, while Section 417(2-A) provides remedy to private
complainant against acquittal; Section 8-A of the Illegal Dispossession Act confers right of appeal only
against conviction under Section 3; no revisional remedy provided in the Act. Present appeal, therefore,
incompetent but convertible into writ jurisdiction of High Court.
Trial Court erred in dismissing complaint solely on a police report without independent judicial appraisal or
permitting evidence. Police inquiry is merely recommendatory and cannot replace judicial scrutiny. Object
of the Illegal Dispossession Act is to provide immediate protection to lawful owners/occupiers; complaint
cannot be dismissed at threshold unless no prima facie case is disclosed. Reliance placed on Shaikh
Muhammad Naseem v. Mst. Farida Gul (2016 SCMR 1931), holding that maintainability cannot be decided
in isolation and existence of civil litigation does not bar proceedings under the Act. Impugned order
suffering from material irregularity and legal infirmity—set aside. Matter remanded to trial Court to decide
afresh after recording evidence and affording full opportunity of hearing to both sides.

case_info: W.P No. 6004-P of 2024 Fazal Rehman  Vs  The State etc.
remarks:
A further and more nuanced question would then arise, namely, if the liability is discharged in Pakistani currency, whether the amount payable is to be calculated with reference to the value prevailing at the time of the occurrence, or at the time when the judicial proceedings culminate and the obligation is finally determined and enforced by the Court.
Even if, however, this Court were to proceed, purely for the sake of constitutional argument, on the assumption that the liability partakes the character of punishment, the issue would still persist whether fixation of its monetary equivalent at the time of adjudication amounts to the imposition of a greater penalty than that which existed at the time of the occurrence.
Upon a holistic consideration of these facets, this Court is persuaded to hold that it is not the monetary amount, in abstract terms, which constitutes the determinative standard. Rather, it is the quantity of silver, fixed, definite, and immutable, which has remained constant throughout and alone constitutes the true measure of the liability.
To insist that the monetary equivalent be frozen as on the date of occurrence would amount to replacing a legislatively and jurisprudentially fixed commodity standard with a volatile and depreciating substitute, thereby undermining the very rationale for adopting silver as the benchmark.
By contrast, where no compromise has been effected, and the matter is adjudicated solely upon conviction, the legal and moral landscape shifts profoundly. Here, the Court derives its authority not from the gracious consent of the parties but from the immutable command of the law itself.
(i) that Diyat shall be determined with reference to silver as the fixed standard, and its monetary equivalent shall be assessed contemporaneously, without affecting the extent of liability.
(ii) the constitutional prohibition against the imposition of enhanced punishment is inapplicable to such compensatory liability;>br>
(iii) inflation or devaluation of currency does not dilute, diminish, or extinguish the statutory obligation;
(iv) this Court lacks jurisdiction to grant concessions inconsistent with the statutory mandate and Islamic jurisprudential scheme.

case_info: W.P No. 56-D of 2024 Fatima Raees and another  Vs  Raees Ahmad and others
remarks:
Held:
(1) Family Laws—Maintenance—Educational expenses of minor—Scope—
The pivotal question for determination was whether a Muslim father is
under a legal obligation to bear the educational expenses of his children,
or whether such expenses fall within the broader ambit of “maintenance.”
To resolve this issue, the Court examined the true import and legal
definition of the term “maintenance” as recognized under Islamic law and
the statutory framework governing family relations.
(2) It is in the best interest of a child to have access to quality education,
including early childhood education. All decisions and measures
concerning a child must be guided by and consistent with the principle of
the child’s welfare, particularly with regard to education.
(3) A father is under obligation to maintain his children till they attain the age
of majority in the case of male and the daughters till they are married.
Law does not debar minor children to claim the maintenance allowance
even after attaining the age of majority.
(4) Educational expenses form an essential and inseparable component of a
child’s maintenance. A father is legally bound to bear such expenses, as
the provision of education is fundamental to the child’s welfare and
development. Accordingly, educational costs stand encompassed within
the broader concept of “maintenance” under Islamic law as well as
statutory family law.
Result. The W.P. No. 56-D/2024 is partly allowed to the extent that
petitioner No. 1, Ms. Fatima Raees (daughter), is held entitled to receive
Rs. 10,000/- (Rupees ten thousand only) per month as maintenance
allowance, with a periodic increase at the rate of 20% per annum. Except
to the above extent, since petitioner No. 2, Mr. Afaq Raees (son), has
attained the age of majority, he is no longer entitled to receive
maintenance allowance, and to his extent, the petition stands dismissed.

case_info: W.P No. 733-M of 2023 Akhtar Hussain  Vs  Govt of KPK
remarks:
Writ petition filed by a government employee, Akhtar Hussain, who was terminated from
his service for holding a dual government job. While the Court upheld the petitioner's
termination, it set aside the portion of the order that demanded the recovery of Rs.
1,012,000 in salaries already paid to him.
The Court based its decision on the principle of equity and a precedent set by the
Supreme Court of Pakistan in Shahbaz Latif v. DIG, Pakistan Railways (PLD 2025
Supreme Court 560). It ruled that in the absence of any fraud or dishonest conduct by the
employee, the retrospective recovery of salaries for services that were actually rendered is
unjust and impermissible. Therefore, the petitioner, who claimed he was unaware of the
rules against dual employment, was relieved of the financial recovery, though his
termination for the policy violation was sustained.

case_info: W.P No. 2012-P of 2025 Muhammad Hasham  Vs  Malik Jehanzaib etc
remarks: The Original forum of Assistant Political Agent, established under the Frontier Crimes Regulation, 1901 (FCR), has no more been in existence, after repeal of the FCR by way of 25th Amendment. Besides, the Assistant Commissioners posted in newly merged Districts cannot be deemed to be successors of the former Assistant Political Agents, therefore they are not having any authority to proceed with or decide any judicial proceedings under the repealed FCR.

case_info: Cr.A No. 1213-P of 2023 Muhammad Abbas  Vs  Abdul Qayyum etc
remarks:
i.	When the witnesses have confirmed that the incident occurred in the stated manner, and when the eyewitness has satisfactorily established her presence at the scene, her testimony, though that of a related witness, cannot be discarded merely on the ground of relationship, particularly when it is found consistent, confidence-inspiring, and supported by corroborative evidence on record.
ii.	It is a well-settled principle that, while as a general rule a conviction should not ordinarily rest solely upon a confession, there is equally no legal bar to basing a conviction on such confession alone if the Court is satisfied that it is true and voluntary.
iii.	It may be observed that situations of this nature frequently arise, where upon recovery of a weapon, a separate case under Section 15 AA is registered, and both challans are submitted before the learned Judicial Magistrate. The case triable by the Court of Sessions is rightly transmitted under Section 190 Cr.P.C., yet the case under the Arms Act often remains with the Magistrate. In such circumstances, when it appears to the learned Judicial Magistrate that the recovered weapon is the very weapon of offence in the principal case, judicial propriety would demand that instead of proceeding with the trial under Section 15 AA, the matter be transmitted to the learned Trial Court seized of the main offence.
iv.	Such a course would not only avoid conflicting findings, but would also enable the trial court, which has the complete picture before it, to properly assess the evidentiary value of the recovered weapon. Failure to adopt such a procedure, as is evident in the instant case, may result in procedural anomalies and evidentiary complications.
v.	We are, however, not placing any embargo upon the powers of the learned Judicial Magistrate, whose jurisdiction is statutory and well-defined; we merely observe that while taking cognizance of such matters, it would be desirable for the learned Magistrate to apply his judicial mind to the surrounding circumstances and, where appropriate, transmit the matter to the competent trial court to ensure the smooth and coherent administration of justice.

case_info: Cr.A No. 402-P of 2025 Sheraz Khan  Vs  The State
remarks: In this case the driver of the vehicle from whom a huge quantity was recovered from the secret cavities made in the vehicle was acquitted and the front-seater was prima facie held for the alleged recovery of the contraband as on the available record, the prosecution has been able to collect material, which speaks of the conscious knowledge of the front seater instead of the driver of the vehicle. As it was part of the record, that it was the front seater who introduced himself as the owner of the vehicle and then he also disclosed the presence of contraband in the secret cavities specially made in the said vehicle, whereas, the driver was merely found sitting on the driving seat, therefore, it was a case of exceptional circumstances, where the driver of the vehicle was found totally ignorant of the presence of the contraband and as such was acquitted, whereas, the front seater (since dead) was prima facie having the conscious knowledge of its presence.

case_info: W.P No. 466-A of 2025 Azim Khan etc  Vs  Govt of KPK
remarks:
(a) Subordinate legislation enjoys a strong presumption of constitutionality
and validity. It may be struck down only when it is established that the
rule-making authority acted beyond its statutory competence, or when
the delegated legislation is inconsistent with the parent Act or violates
fundamental rights. The vires of a rule or regulation may be challenged
only on grounds such as lack of legislative competence, mala fides, or
direct repugnancy to the purpose and scheme of the parent statute.
Judicial review cannot be invoked merely because the rule appears
harsh, unwise, or contrary to policy preferences; it can be declared ultra
vires only where it amounts to excessive delegation or is plainly
inconsistent with the constitutional or statutory framework.
(b) The mere fact that a law, bye-law or notification incidentally excludes
certain candidates who have crossed the age limit does not render it
discriminatory or arbitrary, as classification based on age has always
been recognized as reasonable for the purpose of recruitment in public
service.

case_info: Cr.A No. 183-M of 2024 Rashid Ali  Vs The State
remarks:
Court acquits the appellant, Rashid Ali, of charges of rape and abduction, while
simultaneously dismissing the connected appeal filed by the complainant against the
acquittal of seven other co-accused. The Court found that the prosecution failed to prove
its case beyond a reasonable doubt, as the evidence was riddled with fatal inconsistencies.
Critically, the Court established that the alleged victim was a major at the time of the
incident, her marriage (Nikah) to the appellant was legally solemnized and undisputed,
and the medical evidence did not support the claims of rape or forcible abduction. The
testimony of the victim and other witnesses was deemed untrustworthy due to dishonest
improvements and contradictions with the physical evidence. Applying the fundamental
principle of criminal law that any reasonable doubt must ensure to the benefit of the
accused, the Court set aside the convictions and upheld all acquittals.

case_info: W.P No. 992-M of 2023 Abdullah Vs Mst  Samiyat & three others
remarks:
Court has dismissed a writ petition filed by Abdullah, challenging the concurrent findings of the trial court and appellate court in a family dispute. The courts had granted dissolution of marriage, maintenance allowance, and marriage expenses to the respondent wife and her minor daughter. The petitioner husband claimed that the wife had voluntarily left the matrimonial home, but failed to provide credible evidence to support this claim. The courts held that the husband had failed to maintain the wife for a period exceeding two years, entitling her to dissolution of marriage and maintenance under the law.
The court also ruled that contracting a second marriage without prior permission of the Arbitration Council is a valid ground for dissolution of marriage under clause (ii A) of Section 2 of the Dissolution of Muslim Marriages Act, 1939. This decision was based on a Supreme Court ruling that the provision remains valid and enforceable, despite the repeal of Section 13 of the Muslim Family Laws Ordinance, 1961. The appellate court`s finding that this ground had ceased to exist was repelled by the Supreme Court, and the Peshawar High Court rectified the error. Ultimately, the writ petition was dismissed, and the concurrent findings of the trial court and appellate court were upheld.

case_info: Cr.R No. 47-A of 2025 Khalid  Vs  The State
remarks:
(a)Ordinarily, the date of birth of a person is determined on the basis of
documentary proof i.e. birth certificate, educational documents and National
Identity Card etc but when the date of birth is disputed and varies on all such
documents then the ossification test is the best way to determine a person`s age.
The ossification test is a test that determines age based on the \"degree of fusion
of bone\" by taking the x-ray of a few bones. In simple words, the ossification test
or osteogenesis is the process of the bone formation based on the fusion of joints
between birth and the age of twenty five years in an individual. Bone age is an
indicator of the skeletal and biological maturity of an individual which assists in
the determination of age. The ossification test varies slightly based on individual
characteristics such as climatic conditions where the person born and raised, dietic
values, hereditary differences etc.
(b) It is settled principle of law that if two views are possible from the evidence
adduced in the case then the view favourable to the accused is to be adopted.

case_info: Cr MBA No. 676-A of 2025 Imran Khan etc  Vs  The State etc
remarks:
a) Section 497 of the Cr.P.C. empowers the Court to grant or refuse bail in non-bailable offences, considering the nature of the accusation and available material. However, jurisdiction at the bail stage is limited and tentative; it does not extend to determining the substance of the charge. Under Sections 227 and 228 of the Cr.P.C., only the trial Court may alter or add any charge before judgment. Therefore, a bail Court cannot direct incorporation or deletion of any penal provision, as this would amount to exercising powers reserved for the trial Court. The direction issued by the learned Magistrate dated 05-09-2025, requiring incorporation of Section 365-A PPC at the bail stage, was thus without lawful authority and contrary to settled law.

b)The learned AAG relied on 2024 P.Cr.L.J 658 (Lahore High Court) and Criminal Bail Application No. 829 of 2025 (Sindh High Court) to justify the incorporation of Section 365-A PPC at the bail stage. With utmost respect, this Court disagrees with those views, as they conflict with the scheme of the Criminal Procedure Code and the binding precedent of the Hon’ble Supreme Court in Muhammad Yasin v.
