State (2016 SCMR 1740) in which the August Supreme Court has held that While deciding a bail application, the Court is not empowered to add, delete, or substitute any penal provision in the FIR; such powers can only be exercised by the trial Court at the time of framing charge or alteration of charge.Under Article 189 of the Constitution,
decisions of the Supreme Court are binding on all subordinate courts and High Courts; hence, any contrary interpretation cannot be followed. The objections raised by the learned AAG are therefore devoid of merit.

case_info: W.P No. 91-D of 2024 Shamsher Abbas  Vs  Mst Sajida Bibi and others
remarks:
Held:
(1) Family Laws—Kabin-nama—Undertaking forming part of marriage
contract—Transfer of Property Act, 1882—Scope—
Since the undertaking formed an integral part of the Kabin-nama, it
constituted a binding contractual obligation arising from the marriage
contract and was, therefore, enforceable under law. The stipulation
regarding transfer of a house and five tolas of gold ornaments in favour of
the wife created a valid and enforceable right, by virtue of which she
became the lawful owner of the said property, which fell within the ambit
of her personal property.
(2) Transfer of Property Act, 1882—“Actionable claim”—Meaning and
applicability—
The Kabin-nama executed between the parties was not an independent
civil contract so as to constitute an “actionable claim” under the Transfer
of Property Act, 1882.
(3) The Family Courts Act, 1964—Jurisdiction—
The wife’s claim for transfer of the house and five tolas of gold
ornaments, being a term of the marriage settlement and forming part of the
Kabin-nama, squarely fell within the purview of the Family Courts Act,
1964, and not under the Transfer of Property Act, 1882.
(4) The undertaking contained in the Kabin-nama was enforceable in law; it
created valid ownership rights in favour of the wife and attracted the
jurisdiction of the Family Court rather than the provisions of the Transfer
of Property Act, 1882.
Result. (W.P No. 91-D/2024 is dismissed, whereas. W.P No.158-
D/2025 stands allowed)

case_info: Cr.M.Q No. 09-A of 2025 Khalid Mehmood  Vs  Saqib Imtiaz
remarks:
(a) The jurisdiction of criminal courts is limited to matters of interim
custody and preservation of property. Questions of ownership or
title—being civil rights—cannot be conclusively adjudicated under
these provisions. The Superior Judiciary has consistently held that
the party producing the original registration documents of a vehicle
has a stronger prima facie claim to interim custody; however, such
custody does not amount to a declaration of ownership. The proper
forum for determining ownership is the civil court.
The Motor Vehicles Ordinance, 1965 reinforces this position by
recognizing the registration certificate as legal evidence of
ownership. While possession of original documents supports a
party’s prima facie entitlement, any rival claimant disputing that
ownership must seek redress before a civil court.
A civil court, exercising jurisdiction under Section 9 of the Civil
Procedure Code, 1908, is competent to entertain all civil disputes,
including suits for declaration of title, recovery of possession, and
cancellation of documents. It records evidence and issues a binding
judgment, conclusively settling the rights of rival claimants.
Conversely, criminal courts are not empowered to investigate or
determine questions of title. Their jurisdiction under Sections 516-A
and 517 of the Code of Criminal Procedure extends only to
determining entitlement to possession for interim purposes, not
ownership. The issue of title must be adjudicated exclusively by a
competent civil court. At the same time, this court is mindful that
interim custody of property ordered under the Cr.P.C is not
synonymous with final declaration of ownership.
(b) Once a Magistrate or Sessions Judge has signed a judgment,
the court becomes functus officio meaning that it exhaust its
jurisdiction over the matter. It can only correct clerical or
typographical errors but cannot recall, modify or redecide the case
on merits. The reason is rooted in public policy criminal proceedings
demand certainty and finality to avoid endless litigation and
miscarriage of justice. any attempt by a Magistrate to recall or
review his own order and order of the Revisional Court who
confirmed the order passed by the Magistrate amounts to acting
without jurisdiction. The proper recourse available to an aggrieved
person is by way of appeal or revision under the relevant provisions
of the criminal procedure code.

case_info: Cr.M No. 587-A of 2025 Idrees  Vs  The State and others
remarks:
(a)Delay in reporting a crime to the police—particularly one that affects a person’s honour
and reputation and may be viewed unsympathetically by society—can weigh heavily on the
victim and her family, discouraging an immediate approach to law-enforcement authorities.
Where such delay is plausibly explained, it becomes immaterial and inconsequential to the
prosecution’s case. In rape cases, victims and their families are often reluctant to promptly
report the offence due to the trauma suffered and the perceived shame or dishonour
associated with undergoing an invasive medical examination. Consequently, delay in
reporting a sexual assault is not considered substantially material, as repeatedly held by this
Court.
(b)The petition for the grant of bail must be decided on a tentative appraisal of the entire
material collected during investigation, both for and against the accused; a narrow reading
of the FIR alone is legally insufficient.
(c)Bail may be cancelled when:
1. The bail order is patently illegal, erroneous, factually incorrect, or causes a
miscarriage of justice.
2. The accused misuses the concession of bail.
3. The accused attempts to influence, pressure, or otherwise hamper prosecution
witnesses or evidence.
4. There is a likelihood that the accused may abscond.
5. The accused interferes with the investigation.
6. The accused commits a similar offence while on bail.
7. New facts or material emerge during investigation that tend to establish the
accused’s guilt.

case_info: W.P No. 736-A of 2023 Aksa Abdul Jalil  Vs  Government of KPK and others
remarks:
a). Promotion is neither a vested right nor any restriction or limitation could be imposed on the lawful power of the competent authority to create or alter the service structure or to provide higher/better qualifications for a higher post, on the ground that it would hinder a few persons’ promotion in some manner.
b). It is a well-settled principle that the power of judicial review does not equate to sitting as an appellate authority over the wisdom or merits of a policy, nor does it permit the Court to assume the role of an advisor to the executive on matters squarely within its policy-making domain. This judicial restraint is rooted in the principle of separation of powers. However, Court is duty-bound to intervene and is empowered to do so where a policy decision or executive action suffers from a fundamental flaw, such as a failure to adhere to mandated statutory procedures, a misdirection in law, an exercise of power for a purpose not intended by the statute, or a purported exercise of a power that the decision-making authority does not, in fact, possess. Intervention may also be warranted on the ground of irrationality, where the impugned policy or decision is so manifestly and outrageously unreasonable that no reasonable authority, properly directing itself to the law and the facts, could ever have arrived at it. Thus, while the Court steadfastly refrains from adjudging the “rightness” of a policy, it firmly retains its constitutional role as the guardian of legality and rationality, ensuring that the executive operates within the boundaries of the law.

case_info: Cr.R No. 312-P of 2023 Hizbullah  Vs  The State & another
remarks:
i.	 In order to meaningfully appreciate the essential aspects of the controversy, it is necessary to evaluate whether the learned trial Court, despite the aforesaid incriminating circumstances, exercised its sentencing discretion in accordance with settled principles of criminal jurisprudence, or whether such discretion was exercised in a manner warranting interference in revisional jurisdiction.
ii.	In such circumstances, this Court is required to carefully determine where the blame truly lies and to assess the repercussions of the conduct of the deceased in the context of the reaction of the respondent. This evaluation is essential to ascertain whether the act of the respondent attracts the normal penalty prescribed for the offence, or whether the attending circumstances disclose mitigating factors sufficient to justify the lesser sentence awarded by the learned trial Court.
iii.	However, the law does not require courts to judge human conduct in moments of extreme emotional upheaval by applying standards of detached calmness. The social milieu in which the respondent lived, the time of occurrence, and the shocking manner in which he found a naked man with his undressed daughter, both actively engaged in an act which no prudent man would tolerate, much less a father, are factors which cannot be ignored.
iv.	The law does not demand of a parent, placed in such an extraordinary and emotionally charged situation, a standard of detached and clinical reasoning. Human conduct, particularly when shaped by parental instinct, cultural values, and sudden shock, cannot be weighed in isolation from the circumstances that provoked it.

case_info: Cr.A No. 1058-P of 2024 Syed Tahir Hussain  Vs  Syed Shah Gul etc.
remarks:
i.	The Investigating Officer and the police official who effected the arrest, however, categorically stated that the appellant and certain co-accused were arrested in an injured condition, and their injuries were duly recorded. In these circumstances, the subsequent non-examination of the doctor is not of decisive significance. Had this been a case turning purely on the question of aggressor and aggressed, the medical officer’s testimony would have been essential.
ii.	When the statements of both parties are examined, particularly the report lodged by the appellant, no ambiguity remains that both sides were present at the place of occurrence at the relevant time, and that the incident occurred at spur of the moment.
iii.	Once the same set of witnesses was found unreliable regarding one group of accused, can their testimony be relied upon to fasten criminal liability upon the appellant. This Court has already held that the incident occurred spontaneously and that both parties were involved in a free fight. In such circumstances, the duty of the Court is to determine each individual’s liability separately.
iv.	The Trial Court, therefore, rightly held that the case against them was not proved. However, the Trial Court applied the principle of separating the grain from the chaff, when fixing liability upon the appellant. This Court finds no reason to invoke the principle of falsus in uno, falsus in omnibus, especially when the circumstances require that each accused be assessed individually.

case_info: C.R No. 122-M of 2024 Mst. Gul Badan & others  Vs  Public at large & others
remarks:
The donations received by a widow and children after a husband`s death aren`t
part of his estate (ترکہ). The court defined \'legacy\" based on Islamic principles,
stating it includes property owned at death or enforceable financial rights accrued
during lifetime. Since donations were given directly to the family, they weren`t
owned by the deceased and aren`t subject to inheritance laws. The court set
aside lower court decisions

case_info: F.A B No. 02-D of 2023 Babar Badshah and others  Vs  National Bank of Pakistan, Parao road DIKhan
remarks:
Held:
Financial Institutions (Recovery of Finances) Ordinance, 2001 (XLVI of
2001)
—--- Ss. 9, 10(3), (4), (5) — Limitation Act, 1908 (IX of 1908) — S. 5 —
Leave to Defend — Filing beyond prescribed period — Condonation of delay
— Maintainability — Scope.
(a) Financial Institutions (Recovery of Finances) Ordinance, 2001 —
Applicability of Limitation Act, 1908 —
The Financial Institutions (Recovery of Finances) Ordinance, 2001 is a
special statute providing a complete mechanism for recovery of finances. The
provisions of the Limitation Act, 1908 are inapplicable thereto, and any
application under Section 5 of the said Act for condonation of delay is a
nullity in the eyes of law.
(b) Leave to Defend – Form and content —
An application for leave to defend must be filed in the prescribed form of a
written statement in terms of Section 10(3) of the Ordinance. The requirement
is mandatory, ensuring expeditious disposal of banking suits. Failure to
comply with such formality renders the application incompetent.
(c) Non-compliance with Sections 10(3), (4), and (5) —
Where the defendant fails to disclose the required statement of account or
give valid reasons for non-compliance, the Banking Court is left with no
discretion but to proceed in accordance with the penal consequences
envisaged under the Ordinance.
(d) Appellate jurisdiction – New pleas —
Grounds not raised before the Banking Court cannot be urged for the first
time in appeal. New pleas at the appellate stage are beyond the scope of the
original proceedings and are not entertainable.
(e) Delay – Effect of non-compliance —
Non-observance of mandatory provisions of Sections 9 and 10 of the
Ordinance entails penal consequences, leaving no room for the Banking Court
to condone delay or to regularize procedural defects.
Held:
The decree passed by the learned Banking Court was well-reasoned and in
accordance with law. No illegality or infirmity was pointed out warranting
interference in the exercise of appellate jurisdiction. Both appeals were,
therefore, dismissed.

case_info: Cr.A No. 35-M of 2025 Mst. Sheema  Vs Jamal Nasir & others
remarks:
Court upholds the acquittal of two individuals charged with murder, dismissing the appeal
filed by the complainant. The Court found the prosecution`s case to be fundamentally
flawed and lacking credible evidence, as the murder occurred unseen during the night,
there were no reliable eyewitnesses, and the key piece of evidence—the complainant`s
identification of an accused by his voice—was deemed weak and unconvincing.
Furthermore, the Court affirmed the legal principle that an acquittal carries a \"double
presumption of innocence\" and that appellate court intervention is unwarranted unless the
lower court`s decision is perverse, which was not the case here, as the trial court correctly
applied Section 265-K of the Cr.P.C. to acquit the accused due to the groundless nature of
the charges.

case_info: B.A No. 521-M of 2025 Habib Ullah  Vs  The State
remarks:
1. High Court granted post-arrest bail to Habib Ullah, the petitioner, in a case
registered under Section 365-B PPC (kidnapping or abducting a woman for
forced marriage). The court noted that the abductee, Mst. Rubina Bibi, had
not charged the petitioner with abduction in her statement under Section 164
Cr.P.C. and had only alleged enticement. The court also observed that the
essential ingredients for constituting an offence under Section 365-B PPC
were not present, making the case one of further inquiry.
2. The concept of benefits of doubt at bail stage.

case_info: Cr.A No. 896-P of 2024 Muhammad Emran  Vs  Muhammad Afan & another
remarks:
I.	We are, therefore, not inclined to disbelieve the presence of the complainant at the scene, but we also recognize that he withheld essential details. Ordinarily, concealment of material facts by a witness may cast serious doubt on the prosecution case, sometimes even resulting in acquittal. But this case carries a distinguishing feature: the appellant himself has confessed to the crime. When both pieces of evidence, the statement of the complainant and the confession of the appellant, are read together, no ambiguity remains as to how the incident occurred.
II.	Such concurrence, however, cannot be stretched to absolve the appellant of liability. Instead, it provides a reliable basis for understanding the manner of occurrence. Thus, while the suppressed facts may narrow the prosecution case, the corroborated confession ensures that the appellant cannot claim acquittal on this ground.
III.	The statement therefore leaves no ambiguity regarding the manner in which the tragic event unfolded, as well as the role of the appellant therein. It is also significant that during cross-examination, the complainant himself admitted that the appellant had fired at the gate of the Hujra, and thereafter the deceased received a firearm injury. This account stands in harmony with the appellant’s confession. Taken together, both versions lend credence to the conclusion that the confessional statement is not only voluntary but also truthful, and that it reflects the real sequence of events.
IV.	The pivotal legal query which now engages the attention of this Court is whether, in taking such a specific plea, the burden of proof shifts upon the accused. There is no cavil with the proposition that the prosecution, in all circumstances, is duty bound to prove its case beyond shadow of doubt, and that a specific plea cannot, by itself, absolve it of this burden.
V.	The task before this Court is to ascertain whether the occurrence stemmed from sudden provocation, or whether it was an act born out of the apprehension of losing one’s life, which compelled the accused to fire. The circumstances, as narrated by the appellant, suggest that he was indeed overpowered, that he struggled to rescue himself, that he was chased, and that upon reaching outside the hujra, he fired. It may be that the doctrine of sudden provocation is not strictly attracted, yet this Court cannot turn a blind eye to the factual matrix.
VI.	When considered in this holistic perspective, no ambiguity is left that the learned trial court misdirected itself in law. True, that the appellant had the right of private defense, though he exceeded the same. Yet, the surrounding circumstances demonstrate beyond cavil that his sole intention was to rescue himself. Moreover, the firing being made at the closed door of the hujra further tilts in his favour, for it cannot be inferred with certainty that he intended to kill.

case_info: W.P No. 782-M of 2025 Deputy Director NHA  Vs  Ikramullah Khan & another
remarks:
Court has dismissed the writ petition filed by the Deputy Director, National Highway Authority, Malakand Division, challenging the trial court`s order directing the execution of a decree. The decree required the petitioner- department to reconstruct 500 feet of demolished drains and erect a retaining
wall for the protection of the road and adjoining land.
The court held that the petitioner-department`s objection petition in execution proceedings was misconceived, as it sought to defeat and nullify a valid decree on technical and factual grounds. The executing court is confined to enforcing the decree as it stands and lacks jurisdiction to re-open, vary or re- examine its merits.
The alleged impediments, such as utility installations and site topography, were administrative matters to be resolved by departmental coordination and could not render the decree inexecutable. The petitioner`s failure to challenge the original decree at the proper stage barred them from assailing it at the stage of execution.
The court relied on Section 47 of the Civil Procedure Code, 1908, and precedents to support its decision, dismissing the writ petition as being devoid of merits, with no order as to cost.

case_info: W.P No. 85-M of 2023 Bibi Hasina  Vs  Govt: of KPK & others
remarks:
Court has allowed the writ petition filed by Bibi Hasina, directing the respondents to appoint her to the post of Data Collection & Monitoring Assistant (DCMA) in District Chitral Upper. The petitioner was denied appointment due to being over-aged, despite securing first position in the merit list.
The court held that the petitioner is entitled to automatic age relaxation of up to ten years under the Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Initial Appointment to Civil Posts (Relaxation of Upper Age Limit) Rules, 2008. Rule 51 of the Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Education Monitoring Authority Employees (Terms and Conditions of Service) Rules, 2021, attracts the application of the Rules of 2008.
The respondents are directed to appoint the petitioner to the post, extending the benefit of automatic age relaxation. The court set aside the impugned appointment order issued in favor of respondent No. 7, Mst. Tashfeen Akram, as she has already resigned from the post.

case_info: W.P No. 1179-M of 2023 Farman Ali  Vs  Provincial Police Officer/ IGP at CPO peshawar
remarks:
Court has allowed the writ petition filed by Farman Ali, directing the respondents to reinstate him to his service as Special Police Constable. The petitioner`s services were terminated twice on allegations of bribery and other grounds.
The court held that the termination order was arbitrary and mala fide, as it was based on grounds not mentioned in the show-cause notice. The petitioner was not given a fair opportunity to defend himself against the allegations.
The court relied on Supreme Court judgments emphasizing the importance of procedural fairness and proportionality in disciplinary actions. The impugned termination order has been set aside, and the respondents are directed to reinstate the petitioner to his service.

case_info: Cr.A No. 25-D of 2024 with M.R No. 02-D of 2024 Mst. Razia Hanfi etc  Vs  The State etc.
remarks:
(Appeal against conviction)
(a) Criminal Procedure Code, 1898….Ss.302/311 PPC—re-appraisal of
evidence—Accused were directly charged for the murder of Mst. Saifoora Bibi, on the
allegation of committing blasphemy by the deceased. The occurrence took place at main gate
of the Maddrassa in broad day light and the report was promptly lodged against the accused,
hence, there was no element of substitution of exaggeration qua the accused.
(b) Criminal Procedure Code, 1898…Ss.302/311 PPC—re-appraisal of evidence—The
accused were arrested from their house soon after the occurrence and on their pointation,
recoveries of blood-stained veils and Chadar alongwith crime weapons were effected.
(c) Criminal Procedure Code, 1898…Ss.302/311 PPC—re-appraisal of evidence— On
following day of the occurrence, the accused confessed their guilt before the Judicial
Magistrate and their confessions were supported by the medical evidence, recoveries effected
from their house on their pointation, positive reports of FSL and DNA. The scientific findings
conclusively connected the appellants with the murder.
(d) Criminal Procedure Code, 1898.…Ss.302/311 PPC—re-appraisal of evidence—The
absence or weakness of motive did not dilute the strength of direct and corroborative evidence.
(Appeal was dismissed and the Murder Reference was answered in affirmative).

case_info: W.P No. 112-M of 2025 Ziarat Gul Gojar  Vs  Mian Zahoor Muhammad
remarks:
Court dismissed the writ petition filed by Ziarat Gul Gojar, upholding the ejectment order
and rent recovery decree passed by the Rent Controller and Appellate Court. The
petitioner had challenged the orders, claiming he was a tenant of Mst. Tasleem Bibi, not
the respondent, Mian Gul Zahoor Muhammad. However, the court found that the
respondent had established the landlord-tenant relationship and the petitioner's default in
payment of rent. The concurrent findings of the lower courts were based on a proper
appraisal of evidence and did not suffer from any jurisdictional defect or material
illegality. The court held that the petitioner had failed to establish his claim, and the
respondent`s evidence, including a rent agreement deed and receipts, was sufficient to
prove the landlord-tenant relationship.

case_info: Cr.A No. 743-P of 2025 Adam  Vs  Mst. Laiba and others
remarks:
I.	True it is that the offence is heinous, and equally true that it has brought social stigma upon the victims, but such considerations alone cannot determine the fate of the appellant, nor can they substitute for the required standard of proof.
II.	Mere detection of semen on swabs, without any semen grouping or DNA profiling, cannot safely be relied upon to establish the guilt of the appellant. The attending circumstances show that the learned trial Court has failed to appreciate these essential aspects, and appears to have been swayed by the gravity of the charge rather than the quality of the evidence.

case_info: Cr.A No. 109-A of 2021 Kashif Zaman  Vs  The State and others
remarks:
(a) The court held that when documentary evidence conflicts with oral testimony, the documentary evidence must prevail. Due to contradictions in the prosecution witnesses’ statements and the unexplained delay in reporting the incident, the complainant’s and eyewitnesses’ presence at the scene became doubtful. The prosecution’s failure to produce Mahfouz—who allegedly drove the injured to the hospital and received the dead body—invoked an adverse inference under Article 129(g) of the Qanun-e-Shahadat Order, 1984. Additionally, no blood was recovered from the vehicle or the complainant’s clothes, and such omissions and unnatural conduct further undermined the credibility of the prosecution’s version.
(b) In the present case, a clear conflict emerges between the ocular account and the medical evidence. While the prosecution witnesses have narrated a specific sequence of events regarding the manner in which injuries were inflicted, the medical examination does not support their version. The nature, number, and placement of the injuries described by the eyewitnesses are inconsistent with the findings recorded in the post-mortem report. This contradiction strikes at the root of the prosecution’s story and casts serious doubt on the veracity of the ocular testimony. As settled law dictates, when such inconsistency arises, the medical evidence—being scientific and impartial—prevails, thereby weakening the credibility of the witnesses and entitling the accused to the benefit of doubt.
(c) Before recording a confession in cases involving capital punishment, the Magistrate must strictly observe all mandatory legal precautions to ensure the statement is voluntary. The accused must be freed from any fear of the police, assured of safety, and granted adequate time for reflection between two formal warnings. All police personnel must be kept out of sight during the process. The Magistrate should
record the confession personally, or if dictated, ensure accuracy through an oath-bound court official. If the accused is illiterate or the statement is in another language, it must be read over and explained to him before certification under Section 364 Cr.P.C. Finally, the accused must be sent to judicial custody and not returned to police control, as doing so would compromise the voluntariness of the confession.
(d) In the present case, no test identification parade was conducted, which is a fundamental legal requirement to ensure the credibility of identification evidence. It is a settled principle that identification of an accused for the first time in court carries little or no evidentiary value, as it is inherently unsafe and open to manipulation. The purpose of a TIP is to test the memory and observation of witnesses under neutral conditions, free from police influence. Failure to hold such a parade deprives the court of reliable corroboration of the witness’s claim of having seen the accused at the time of the offence. Consequently, the in-court identification alone cannot safely be made the basis for conviction, particularly when the accused was previously unknown to the witnesses.
(e) It is well settled principle of law that prosecution is not bound to setup a motive in every case but once it is alleged, it becomes duty of prosecution to prove the same and otherwise to suffer consequences thereof.

case_info: Cr.A No. 751-P of 2024 Muhib Ullah  Vs  The State & another
remarks:
Likewise, although the complainant sustained injuries in the same transaction and was the one who reported the matter, the mere fact of being injured does not automatically guarantee that his testimony is truthful. His presence at the spot is not disputed, but it remains to be examined whether the incident took place in the manner and at the time alleged by the prosecution.
Nothing was brought on record to indicate which of the deceased or injured were specifically aimed at. In the absence of any clear explanation, the possibility cannot be ruled out that one of the victims might have been the intended target and, in attempting to attack that person, indiscriminate firing ensued, causing the death of innocent individuals and injuries to others.
if four accused were indiscriminately firing, as alleged, it is unlikely that this witness would have run towards the firing spot, rather than remaining inside to avoid danger. His presence at Point No. 6 in the site plan does not support his claim of witnessing the firing, and the circumstances indicate that he too arrived at the spot after the incident had taken place.
When the injured witnesses were closely related to the complainant, and the complainant was in a position to secure their attendance, their absence becomes significant. Since these witnesses received injuries in the same transaction, it was imperative for the prosecution to produce them to substantiate its case.
It is settled law that mere receipt of injuries by a witness is not, by itself, sufficient to hold his testimony as truthful in its entirety. The stamp of injuries may establish his presence at the scene of occurrence at the relevant time, but it cannot automatically guarantee the truthfulness or completeness of his version.
When the sole eyewitness account is in conflict with the complainant’s statement and the witnesses are found to have concealed material facts while implicating a larger number of persons than actually involved, such conduct casts serious doubt on the veracity of the prosecution’s case.
The prosecution also could not prove what common object, if any, was shared by the alleged unlawful assembly. When the common object is not proved, the liability of all accused for a collective act cannot be sustained. In our view, the prosecution has failed to prove motive. Where motive is the primary cause of the offence and is not proved, this failure can legitimately be taken into consideration in favour of the accused.

case_info: Cr.A No. 292-A of 2023 Muhammad Imran  Vs  The State & another
remarks:
(a) The prosecution evidence is consistent, confidence-inspiring and fully corroborated by medical and forensic chain. The confessional statement of the appellant provides direct evidence, while recoveries, circumstantial witnesses, and last-seen testimony provide further corroboration. The entire evidence forms a chain so complete that it leaves no room for doubt about the guilt of the appellant. The principle of benefit of doubt, though well-recognized, cannot be stretched to fanciful or speculative doubts. In the present case, no such reasonable doubt exists.
(b) Recovery on pointation is admissible under Article 40 of the Qanun-e-Shahadat Order, 1984, and has strong evidentiary worth. The recovery of crime weapon at the instance of accused, particularly when corroborated by medical evidence, constitutes a strong incriminating circumstance. Here, the sharp-edged weapon corresponds with the medical cause of death, providing direct nexus between appellant and offence.
(c) The appellant’s confession, recorded by a Judicial Magistrate under Section 164 Cr.P.C. on 07.03.2016, was found to be voluntary and in full compliance with legal formalities. The Magistrate confirmed that the statement was written in his own hand, sufficient time for reflection was given, and no coercion was used. The confession—signed and thumb-marked in both Urdu and English—contained detailed admissions of committing an unnatural offence, and murdering the child. Although the appellant later retracted the confession, the court held that such retraction does not affect its validity when the statement is voluntary and corroborated by other evidence, including recovery of the knife, medical findings, FSL report, and circumstantial links. The appellant’s claim of involuntariness was deemed unsubstantiated, as no proof of
coercion or bias was shown. Minor technical objections, such as a mistaken reference to “oath,” were considered immaterial since substantive compliance with law was achieved.
(d) In cases involving heinous offences such as the rape and murder of a four-year-old child, substitution—that is, replacing the accused person with another alleged perpetrator at a later stage of proceedings—is an extremely rare phenomenon. This is because such crimes typically generate strong and immediate investigative focus, involving medical, forensic, and eyewitness evidence that directly links the accused to
the offence. The gravity and brutality of the act attract heightened judicial scrutiny, leaving little room for mistaken identity or manipulation. Courts view substitution claims with great suspicion, as they are often raised as an afterthought to create doubt or delay justice. In offences of this nature, the prosecution’s case usually rests on  scientific evidence—such as DNA analysis, post-mortem findings, and recovery of
incriminating material—which objectively connect the offender to the crime scene. Therefore, unless there is compelling, credible, and independent evidence proving that the accused was falsely implicated, courts rarely accept the plea of substitution in such heinous crimes. The underlying principle is that the more shocking and grave the crime, the less plausible the theory of substitution becomes, given the careful and
evidence-based nature of such investigations.
(e) The Constitution of Islamic Republic of Pakistan, 1973, under articles 9 and 14, guarantee the right to life and dignity of every person, while the Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Child Protect and Welfare Act, 2010 imposes a binding duty upon the state to safeguard children from violence, exploitation, and abuse. In these circumstances the principle of justice and deterrence demand that the guilty be hold strictly accountable and dealt with the severest punishment prescribed by law. It is well to remember that criminal jurisprudence places the accused as a favourite child of law, but this protection is not a license to escape liability where guilt is established through cogent evidence.
(f) The reasons for the award of the death penalty far out weight the considerations for the award of lesser sentence. The tender age of the minor, the brutal and heinous nature of the crime and pre-mediation persuades us to agree with the sentence awarded by the learned trial Court. The deterrent aspect of the sentence cannot be lost sight of either as it was a crime of sodomy / Rape of minor, followed by murder. In such an eventuality, the normal sentence of death should be awarded and the Court should neither hesitate nor search for laboured pretexts to award a lesser sentence.

case_info: Cr.A No. 12-B of 2022 Shahi Marjan  Vs  Noor Ayaz and the State
remarks:
Criminal Procedure Code (V of 1898) --- S. 417 (2-A) --- Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Arms Act, 2013 --- Appeal against acquittal --- the right of appeal vest in Government and not in private individual --- Maintainability --- Scope. S. 417 (2-A) Cr.P.C confers a statutory right of appeal only upon a “complainant” in respect of cases instituted upon a private complaint under chapter XLV, Cr.P.C. --- Such right does not extend to state cases initiated on police report, particularly under special enactments like the Arms Act, which are cognizable by the police and prosecuted by the state --- In such cases, the exclusive right to file an appeal against acquittal vests in Provincial Government under S. 417 (1), Cr.P.C.--- Where the FIR had been lodged by the police under the Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Arms Act, 2013, the appeal filed by a private person against acquittal of the accused was incompetent and not maintainable --- Appeal dismissed in limine.
Held: Appeal by Private person against acquittal in a state case under Arms act not maintainable --- only Provincial Government competent to file Appeal.

case_info: W.P No. 835-M of 2025 Riaz Ali  Vs  Govt of KPK
remarks:
Court has delivered a judgment in a case involving Riaz Ali, a transport business owner, and the Government of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa. The court ruled in favor of the petitioner, declaring the demand for \"Adda fee\" by the Tehsil Municipal Administration (TMA) to be illegal and without lawful authority.
The TMA had demanded the fee from the petitioner`s buses, claiming it was authorized under Schedule-III of the Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Local Government Act, 2013. However, the court observed that the fee demanded is not covered by any of the items relied upon by the respondent/TMA in Schedule-III of the Act.
The court emphasized that a taxing statute must be construed strictly, and no tax can be imposed unless expressly authorized by law. The court cited various judgments, including those by the Supreme Court, to support its view that taxing provisions must be interpreted strictly and that local governments cannot charge for services unless authorized by law.
The court also noted that an identical controversy regarding the levy of tax/Adda fee on transporters had earlier come up for consideration before the court in a previous case, where it was held that the imposition of such fee was against the very spirit of Section 42(2) of the Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Local Government Act, 2013.
In light of the foregoing, the court allowed the petition and declared the impugned tax imposed upon the petitioner by the respondents to be illegal, without lawful authority, and of no legal effect.

case_info: W.P No. 1774-P of 2019 Gul Hassan etc  Vs  Federation of Pakistan
remarks: Orders passed by authorities established and working under Section 8,9 and 10 of the repealed Frontier Crime Regulation (FCR) before coming into force of 25th constitutional amendment, were past and close transactions, which could not be re-opened.

case_info: C.R No. 136-P of 2014 Pirzada Syed ul Amin  Vs  Haleem Gul & others
remarks:
115-CPC,—Judgment in rem-Where title and ownership of the transferors of the petitioner has duly been established by the relevant authority and subsequently validated by the orders of the superior Courts in various rounds of litigation, but with the same result, and the same has neither been assailed before the Apex Court nor it has been revisited so far either. And where, in the second round of litigation it has also culminated in favour of the transferor of the petitioner that has also attained finality right upto the Apex Court – In the circumstances, the judgments of the superior courts, to the extent of the controversy involved in the matter being finally determined and settled by the Apex Court, duly qualify the status of a Judgment in Rem. Any question arising during the course of subsequent litigation, but until & unless the aforesaid judgments & orders of the superior courts, which has already resolved the controversy are disturbed, had obtained the status of a judgment in rem, then, in such a situation, the same can only be given effect in accordance with the determination so made by the superior courts.
Order XXI Rule 36 CPC- Suit for Recovery of Immovable property- It is a settled law that a co-sharer in an un-divided immovable property can maintain a suit for ejectment against a possessor, in respect of the entire property, but the same cannot be considered as a proof of his denial of title of the other co-sharers, and any suit brought by him would be deemed to be for the benefit of all the co-sharers, and if in consequence of the said proceeding, the possession is acquired by the said co-sharer, then he would be holding the same on behalf of all co-sharers, which cannot be deemed to be adverse to the other co-sharers. The contention that the only option that can be resorted to for restoration of possession lies in filing of execution of the partition decree is also misconceived, because a decree for partition can only be executed, in real terms, against the co-owner, who is in possession of the portion of the shares allotted to the decree holder. However, as against another person, who occupies a land without any legal authority and also not in his capacity as a co-decree holder, then his such execution can only be entertained by giving symbolic possession of the property but if the decree holder wants to regain physical possession of the property against such person, the decree holder has to resort to filing a suit for possession, for the reason that his such request for physical possession against a person who is either tenant or entitled to occupy the same otherwise and is not bound by the decree, cannot be entertained in accordance with Order 21 Rule 36 CPC.
Order VI Rule 7 CPC- the rule of secundum allegata et probates, not only excludes the element of surprise, but also precludes the party from proving what has not been alleged or pleaded.
Gift- Validity- The objection to the validity of a gift on the ground of non delivery of possession can only be raised by either the donor or a person claiming through him, in a suit where the very validity of the gift transaction is the subject matter of the suit. Such an objection can neither be raised by a stranger to the gift transaction nor it can be entertained in a suit or proceeding, where the determination of the validity of the gift transaction itself is not a matter in issue, especially, when the donor or his legal representative has not put any challenge to the transaction of gift.

case_info: Cr.A No. 422-P of 2025 Sher Ali  Vs  The State.
remarks:
I.	These glaring contradictions regarding the complainant’s presence or absence, the duration of stay, and the circumstances of recovery strongly suggest that the prosecution witnesses were not speaking the truth.
II.	If indeed the accused was operating such a business, as alleged, one would expect substantial recovery from the premises as well. This inconsistency further undermines the prosecution’s narrative.
III.	The contradictions are not minor or peripheral; rather, they go to the root of the matter and demonstrate that the conduct of the complainant and the testimony of the prosecution witnesses cannot be relied upon.
IV.	It was argued that once, in prosecutions under the Act, the safe custody of the recovered contraband and its safe transmission to the office of the Chemical Examiner is proved, the role of the witnesses becomes secondary, and the documentary evidence would prevail. We are constrained to express surprise at such a submission. Under no circumstances can the principle of appreciation of evidence in cases under the Act be distinguished from that in ordinary criminal cases. To accept such a proposition would be to apply a principle alien to criminal jurisprudence. In our considered view, the credibility of the witnesses, whether in ordinary cases or under the Act, remains of foremost importance.

case_info: W.P No. 1132-M of 2023 Dawood Khan  Vs  Muhammad Fazil & others
remarks:
Writ petition filed by Dawood Khan, setting aside the orders of the lower courts that denied him the opportunity to cross-examine witnesses. The court held that the petitioner was denied a fundamental right to a fair trial, as the waiver by his counsel was unauthorized and premature.
The petitioner had filed a declaratory suit asserting his statutory and sharia- based right of inheritance in his deceased father`s estate. The lower courts had denied his request to cross-examine witnesses, citing an endorsement made by his counsel on the order sheet. However, the High Court found that
the waiver was not intentional, or informed, and that the petitioner had promptly disowned it.
The court emphasized that cross-examination is a cornerstone of adversarial adjudication and a fundamental right under Article 10-A of the Constitution. It also noted that Article 133 of the Qanun-e-Shahadat Order, 1984, allows the court to recall witnesses for further examination.
The court directed the trial court to recall the respondents' witnesses, record their examination-in-chief, and afford the petitioner a fair opportunity to cross-examine them, ensuring a just and fair trial.

case_info: R.F.A No. 39-M of 2022 Assistant Director NADRA at Rahim Abad  Vs Amir Gul
remarks:
Court has set aside the judgments of the Consumer Court, Swat, in two connected appeals, R.F.A No. 39-M/2022 and R.F.A No. 122-M/2022, involving disputes related to NADRA`s services. The court held that the Consumer Court lacks jurisdiction to entertain complaints regarding NADRA`s services, as CNIC is a statutory document issued under the NADRA Ordinance, 2000, and not a product or service under the Consumer Protection Act, 1997.
NADRA performs sovereign and administrative functions, not as a trader or service provider. The NADRA Ordinance, 2000, is a special law that prevails over the Consumer Protection Act, 1997, in this matter. The respondents are directed to resort to the statutory mechanism under the NADRA Ordinance, 2000, for redressal of their grievances.

case_info: B.B.A No. 506-M of 2025 Numan Ali  Vs  The State
remarks: Numan Ali`s pre-arrest bail has been granted by this court, in a case registered against him under Sections 302, 324, 147, 148, and 149 PPC. The court held that Numan Ali`s case is arguable for the purpose of grant of pre-arrest bail, considering the cross version of the incident and the fact that co-accused with similar roles have been released on post-arrest bail. The court also relied on the principle of consistency and the fact that sending Numan Ali to jail would amount to mockery of law, as he would be entitled to post- arrest bail soon after his arrest. The ad-interim bail already granted to Numan Ali was confirmed on the existing bail bonds.¹

case_info: W.P No. 14-C of 2022 Mohammad Hafizo Khan  Vs  Kabir Ullah & others
remarks:
Court dismissed the writ petition filed by Mohammad Hafizo Khan, challenging the
orders of the Senior Civil Judge and District Judge, Chitral Lower. The court held that the
petitioner failed to establish fraud or misrepresentation in obtaining the decree, and the
decree was passed by a court of competent jurisdiction. The petitioner had not been made
a party to the original suit and claimed the disputed path was his ancestral property.
However, the court found the petitioner's evidence lacking, and the commission report did
not mention the disputed path. The court concluded that the petitioner had not made out a
case for setting aside the decree under Section 12(2) CPC.

case_info: R.F.A No. 44-M of 2024 The Director Higher Education Colleges KPK  Vs  Syed Muhammad Khan & others
remarks:
Court dismissed the appeal filed by the Director Higher Education Colleges, Khyber
Pakhtunkhwa, challenging the enhancement of compensation for acquired land from Rs.703,792
to Rs.3,000,000 per Kanal. The court upheld the Referee Court`s decision, citing the land`s
proximity to the main road, commercial potential, and future prospects. The court also awarded
6% interest under Section 34 of the Land Acquisition Act, 1894, from the date of taking
possession till full payment.

case_info: W.P No. 157-D of 2023 Allah Nawaz Niazi and another  Vs  Mst Iram Firdous and others
remarks:
Held:
Muslim Family Law — Maintenance
Husband’s liability — Conditions
A husband is bound to maintain his wife so long as she remains
faithful to him and obeys his reasonable commands. He is not liable to
maintain a wife who, without lawful justification, leaves the matrimonial
home or refuses cohabitation, unless such conduct is justified by non-
payment of prompt dower or cruelty on the part of the husband.
Maintenance — Wife leaving matrimonial home Effect
Where evidence establishes that the wife voluntarily left the husband’s
house of her own accord and failed to discharge her matrimonial
obligations, she is not entitled to claim past or future maintenance.
Constitution of Pakistan, 1973 — Art. 199
Judicial review — Scope
Constitutional jurisdiction under Article 199 is confined to cases
involving misreading or non-reading of evidence, or findings based on
no evidence resulting in miscarriage of justice. The High Court cannot
reappraise evidence or interfere with concurrent findings of fact as a
substitute for appeal or revision.
Gold Ornaments 1-1/2
Burden of proof
Claims regarding gold ornaments weighing 1-1/2 must be established
through cogent and convincing evidence proving ownership. In
absence of such proof, the claim is legally unsustainable and liable to
be declined.

case_info: C.R No. 327-A of 2025 Jehangir Khan  Vs  N.B.P
remarks:
1. While deciding an application under Order VII rule 11 CPC, the Court is not to consider whether cause of action has been, or could be, established or not, rather it is merely the disclosure of cause of action which has to be considered.
2. It is clear from bare reading of section 8 of the Financial Institutions (Recovery of Finances) Ordinance, 2001 that it pertains to any amounts “written off, released or adjusted under any agreement, contract, or consent etc”. The amounts being sought to be recovered by the bank in these cases fall in none of the categories mentioned in the above quoted section 8 of the Financial Institutions (Recovery of Finances) Ordinance, 2001.
3. It is clear that only a ‘customer’ or a ‘financial institution’ may invoke the jurisdiction of the Banking Court under the Financial Institutions (Recovery of Finances) Ordinance, 2001 and that too only regarding a dispute pertaining to ‘finance’. ‘Financial Institution’ is defined section 2(a), ‘Customer’ in 2(c), ‘Finance’ in 2(d), while ‘Obligation’ in 2(e) of ‘the Ordinance, 2001’. An account holder is not necessarily a ‘customer’ under Section 2(c) of ‘the Ordinance, 2001’, because as per its definition given in ‘the Ordinance, 2001 ‘customer’ is a person to whom ‘finance’ (a loan, facility, or financial accommodation) has been extended by a financial institution. An account holder, in his capacity as such, does not fit this definition unless he has also availed of a financial facility from the bank. The definition of ‘Customer’ under Section 2(c) of ‘the Ordinance, 2001’ explicitly states that a ‘customer’ means a person to whom finance has been extended, while ‘Finance’ as per definition provided in section 2(d) of ‘the Ordinance, 2001, includes financial accommodation or facility provided by a financial institution, such as loans, leases, letters of credit, or other financial arrangements. Simply holding an account does not automatically make someone a ‘customer’ for the purposes of ‘the Ordinance, 2001’. The key is whether he has obtained a financial facility from the institution.

case_info: W.P No. 5813-P of 2024 Niaz Muhammad  Vs  Rangeen Khan etc.
remarks:
Article 199 of the Constitution of Pakistan, 1973.
Section 476, 195 of Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898.
i. Initiation of proceedings u/s 476 read with 195 Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898.
ii. Tampering and manipulation of Judicial record, abuse of judicial process.
iii. Discretionary powers conferred by Section 476 Cr.PC should be exercised
judiciously and not arbitrarily, to prevent the abuse of process of law.

case_info: WP No. 5104-P of 2025 Abdul Sattar   Vs  Govt. of KP through CS etc
remarks:
i.	It is by now well settled and consistent view of the superior Courts that it is the exclusive domain of the competent authority/government to make rules for raising the efficiency of the employees in particular and in the service in general. Reference can be made to the case of “Federal Public Service Commission through Chairman, Islamabad and another vs. Shiraz Manzoor and others [2023 SCMR 2087], Dr. Zulfiqar Ahmed Malik vs. Federation of Pakistan through Secretary Revenue Division, Chairman FBR Islamabad and others [2019 SCMR 1973]. The intention behind the amendment so introduced is for the betterment of service and is mainly aimedat having brilliant candidates shortlisted for their further appearance in the competitive examination. No malafide or ill-will can be attributed to the Government in the instant matter.
II.	There is yet another aspect of the case, as stated, the petitioners were having prior notice about the amendment in the minimum threshold much before the conduct of the written test, therefore, after their appearance and failure to get 50% passing marks in the test, their petition on such score alone amounts to waiver and acceptance on their part of the amended rules, as such, their such objection at this stage is hit by the principle of waiver.

case_info: C.R No. 256-A of 2016 Haji Muhammad Naeem  Vs  Malik Aman etc
remarks:
(a) In cases where an heir is deprived of his lawful share in inherited property, the cause of action to institute a suit arises when a co-sharer or legal heir in actual possession of the property either expressly denies, or clearly manifests an intention to deny, the claimant’s share therein. An actual denial of the rights of a co-sharer occurs when the other co-sharer performs a definite and overt act in repudiation of those rights—such as executing a fraudulent sale or gift deed.
The Apex Court has recently reaffirmed that the transfer of inherited property to a third party—whether by way of sale or gift—constitutes an actual denial of the rights of the other heirs. Conversely, a mere mutation or entry in the revenue record does not amount to such denial; it represents only a threatened or apprehended infringement of the claimant’s rights.
(b) It is now a well-settled principle of law that where a legal heir has been deprived of his or her rightful share in the legacy of a deceased predecessor, and after the attestation of the inheritance mutation remains alive for a considerable period without challenging such mutation—which adversely affects his or her rights—then, upon the heir’s death, his or her legal representatives have no locus standi to question that injuriously affecting mutation. This principle applies particularly where third-party rights have subsequently accrued in the property and the inheritance mutation remained unchallenged during the lifetime of the affected heir.

case_info: Cr.A No. 1708-P of 2023 Zaheer Ahmed & another  Vs  The State & another
remarks:
I.	The sequence of events, viewed in its entirety, leaves no room for ambiguity: the deceased departed in the company of the accused, they dined together, thereafter the accused returned alone, and the deceased never reappeared. When these essential pieces of evidence are examined in conjunction, the conclusion is irresistible that the prosecution has successfully connected the accused with the murders.
II.	It is settled law that a conviction may validly rest upon a truthful and confidence-inspiring confession, and in the present case, not only do the confessions inspire confidence, but they also find ample corroboration from the medical evidence confirming firearm injuries, from the recovery of empties at the spot, and from the recovery of the dead bodies from the river, while the testimony of witnesses remained consistent on material particulars.
III.	It is by now well entrenched that a retracted confession, if found voluntary and truthful, constitutes sufficient basis for conviction without offending the principles of safe administration of criminal justice.

case_info: C.R No. 735-M of 2024 Dilshad Alam & others  Vs  Yakmin Khan
remarks:
Civil revision petition filed by Dilshad Alam and others against Yakmin Khan. The case involves a dispute over a land sale transaction, where the respondent/plaintiff claimed that the petitioners/defendants had defaulted on paying the remaining sale consideration. The petitioners/defendants claimed that the entire sale consideration had been paid in lump sum, but failed to provide convincing evidence to support their stance.
The trial court dismissed the suit, but the appellate court allowed the appeal and remanded the matter to the trial court for fresh decision. The appellate court observed that the petitioners/defendants had failed to discharge the burden of proving payment of the sale consideration. The court noted that the defendant`s  claim of paying the entire amount in lump sum was inconsistent with their subsequent statements and actions.
The court emphasized the importance of determining possession over the suit land, which was a crucial aspect of the controversy. The trial court had failed to frame a specific issue regarding possession, which rendered its judgment deficient. The appellate court, therefore, correctly held that the trial court was
bound to examine this aspect and determine whether the transaction stood completed in all respects.
In light of the above, the court upheld the appellate court`s judgment and dismissed the revision petition, finding no illegality in the remand order. The court directed the trial court to examine the aspect of possession and determine whether the transaction stood completed in all respects, in order to secure a just and proper adjudication of the dispute.

case_info: F.A.O No. 05-A of 2017 M/S Horizone Paper Mills  Vs  SME Bank & others
remarks:
(a) Section 99 of the Code of Civil Procedure (C.P.C.) provides that no decree shall be reversed, nor any case remanded in appeal, on account of any error, defect, or irregularity in any proceedings in the suit, unless such error, defect, or irregularity has affected the merits of the case or the jurisdiction of the Court. Similarly, every irregularity in the exercise of jurisdiction by a lower Court does not warrant interference in revisional jurisdiction under Section 115(1)(c) of the C.P.C.; only a material irregularity—that is, one which affects the merits of the case or the jurisdiction of the Court—justifies such interference. Defects in signing or verifying pleadings, or in producing corporate resolutions, are merely procedural irregularities. Such omissions neither touch the merits of the case nor affect the jurisdiction of the forum and, as such, remain curable at any stage of the proceedings, even at the appellate or revisional stage.
(b) The procedural law serves as a framework for enforcing substantive rights and ensuring the orderly administration of justice, without creating or extinguishing those rights. The primary objective of procedural rules is to facilitate, not obstruct, the dispensation of justice. Cases should be decided on their merits rather than dismissed for minor technical lapses that cause no prejudice to either party. A fundamental distinction exists between jurisdictional defects, which go to the root of the matter and are incurable, and procedural irregularities, which concern matters of form and can be corrected at any stage. Section 99
of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, embodies this principle by prohibiting reversal or modification of decrees for errors not affecting the merits or jurisdiction. Courts should allow parties an opportunity to rectify such omissions rather than applying procedural law rigidly, as doing so would undermine the very purpose of justice.
(c) The wrongful sale of the appellant’s property without notice, without trial, and without lawful authority constitutes a violation of Article 10-A (Right to Fair Trial) and Article 24 (Right to Property) of the Constitution of the Islamic Republic of Pakistan, 1973. No person can be deprived of property except in accordance with law and after due process.

case_info: Cr.A No. 61-D of 2025 Ameer Khan  Vs  Nadeem Abbas Baloch
remarks:
(Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Consumer Protection Act, 1997)
1. Consumer-respondent purchased air conditioner from the appellant
which stopped cooling within first month of its use. Problem persisted
despite repair. Consumer demanded for replacement of the unit was
denied within the first year of warranty period. Consumer court
directed the appellant to replace the air conditioner with a new product
of the same description within a period of two weeks, subject to the
respondent paying transportation and installation charges. ---- Held ---
- The consumer made payment for a perfect and flawless product, not
to receive a defective or faulty item. Appellant was rightly directed to
replace the product as warranty period was still remaining.
2. Warranty defined: In the Chambers Concise Dictionary, “Warranty”
is defined as “an assurance of the quality of goods being sold, usually
with an acceptance of responsibility for repairs during an initial
period of use” or “an undertaking or assurance expressed or implied
in certain contract” while in Osborn’s Concise Law Dictionary
“warranty” means “A guarantee or assurance. An agreement with
reference to goods which are the subject of a contract of sale, but
collateral to the main purpose of such contract, the breach of which
gives rise to a claim for damages, but not to a right to reject the goods
and treat the contract as repudiated.”
3. Repair and Replacement Policy: Contention that there was no
replacement or return policy of the company, but rather it would repair
the faulty parts, it is important to note that the defects were reported
within the first year of the warranty when all warranties are still
active. Section 15 of the Act empowers the Consumer Court to issue
directions for the return of the price or charges paid by the
complainant if the assertions made in the complaint are proven true.
Therefore, consumer Court has the jurisdiction to order the return or
replacement or pass any other appropriate order if a faulty items or
product is supplied to the consumer.
(The Consumer Appeal was dismissed and the order of learned Judge Consumer Court was maintained.)

case_info: W.P No. 178-D of 2024 Tahir Bilal  Vs  Mst Asia Batool and others
remarks:
Held:
(1) Family Laws—Kabin-nama—Proof of execution—Appreciation of
evidence—Scope—
Respondent/wife produced cogent and convincing evidence in support of
the Kabin-nama. Although the petitioners alleged the document to be fake
and fabricated, they failed to substantiate their claim. The respondent
successfully produced the marginal witnesses and the scribe, whose
testimonies were consistent and corroborated the due execution and
genuineness of the Kabin-nama. The learned Courts below rightly
appreciated the evidence and correctly concluded that the Kabin-nama and
the fixation of dower therein stood duly proved. The plea of fabrication
raised by the petitioners remained unsubstantiated and was effectively
rebutted through credible and confidence-inspiring testimony. The
findings of the appellate Court regarding fixation and non-payment of
dower, dissolution of marriage on the basis of Khula, possession of the
suit house, and restitution of conjugal rights were borne out by the record
and disclosed no legal or factual infirmity warranting interference in
constitutional jurisdiction.
(2) Held—
Findings of the learned Courts below were based on proper appreciation
of evidence; the Kabin-nama stood duly proved; no illegality or perversity
found calling for interference in writ jurisdiction. (Both Writ Petitions
stand dismissed)

case_info: C.R No. 130-D of 2025 Muhammad Shoaib  Vs  Aminullah Khan
remarks:
(Civil Revision under Section 115 of the Code of Civil Procedure)
1. Recovery suit --- Defendant attended the court through his special
attorney as well as counsel but thereafter did not appear and was
placed ex-parte. Finally, an ex-parte decree was passed. Defendant
filed applications for setting aside ex-parte decree and condonation of
delay claiming applicability of residuary Article 181 of the Limitation
Act as defendant once appeared before the trial Court.--- Held --- The
date on which the ex-parte proceedings were initiated was a date of
hearing as it was scheduled for filing written statement. The defendant
was having the knowledge that a suit is pending against him. In such a
situation, the Article 164 of Limitation Act would be applicable
whereby period to file application of the nature is 30 days. No
plausible and days to day explanation was advanced for condonation
of the delay.
Relied --Honda Atlas Cars (Pakistan) Ltd. Vs. Honda Sarhad (Pvt.)
Ltd. and others (2005 SCMR 609), Rehman Weaving Factory (Regd.),
Bahawalnagar vs. Industrial Development Bank of Pakistan (PLD
1981 SC 21), Shahid Pervaiz alias Shahid Hameed vs. Muhammad
Ahmad Ameen (2006 SCMR 631), Secretary Education Department,
Government of N.-W.F.P., Peshawar and others Vs. Asfandiar Khan
(2008 SCMR 287), Government of N.W.F.P. through Collector, Land
Acquisition, Abbottabad and 3 others Vs. Mst. Surryia Begum (PLD
1995 Peshawar 53), Fazal Karim and 4 others Vs. Hussan Din and 8
others (2019 MLD 1082), Nisar Vs. Said Qamar (late) through LRs
and others (2023 MLD 1870)
(The revision petition filed by defendant was dismissed.)

case_info: C.R No. 701-P of 2024 Mukhtiar Ahmad & others  Vs  Parveen Begumj (widow) & others
remarks:
A Compromise decree, being a lawful expression of adjudicated rights based on mutual settlement, falls squarely within the definition of a decree. The law draws no distinction, in terms of excitability, between a decree passed after contest and one passed on compromise or consent. Both carry equal force and effect unless vitiated by fraud, misrepresentation, or lack of jurisdiction.
Judicial policy regards compromise decrees as valid and executable, encouraging amicable resolution of disputes and reducing the burden on courts. Rejecting this would undermine the sanctity of compromise and discourage settlements. Hence, the contention that a compromise decree is not executable has no merit.

case_info: RFA No. 67-P of 2024 Peshawar Development Authority through its Director General, Peshawar  Vs  M/S Al-Enterprises and another.
remarks:
i.	Where an objection petition against an award has been dismissed and the award of the Arbitrator (Umpire) has been declared as Rule of the Court through a composite order then in such eventuality as per mandate of Section 17 of The Arbitration Act, 1940 the objector / judgment-debtor has to challenge through filing of an independent appeal against that part of the judgment, whereby his objection petition has been dismissed or he has to take specific grounds in the memo of appeal filed against the whole judgment with respect to the dismissal of his objection petition.
ii.	The grounds as provided for challenging a decree passed on the basis of award for filing an appeal under Section 17 of The Act of 1940 is limited to two grounds, firstly that if the decree is in excess of the award and secondly that if the decree is not otherwise in accordance with the award, whereas under Section 39 of The Act of 1940, the decree may be challenged on more grounds as mentioned therein.
iii.	As per mandate of Section 30 of The Act of 1940, an award could be set aside where an Arbitrator or Umpire has misconducted himself or proceedings or if an award has been made after the issuance of an order by the court superseding the arbitration or if an award has been improperly procured.
iv.	Where the Arbitrator or Umpire has elaborately dilated upon, evaluated and analyzed all the materials produced by the parties and the same duly reflects in the award as well then such award could not be set aside by the court of law merely for the brevity of reasons, as the Arbitrator is the sole and final Judge of all questions both of law and facts.
v.	The domain and jurisdiction of a court of law in cases where award is under challenge is substantially restricted as the court can neither review an award nor entertain any question whether the Arbitrator has decided properly or not on a point of law and it is not open for the court to re-examine and re-appraise the evidence considered by the Arbitrator to hold that the conclusion reached by the Arbitrator is wrong or where two views are possible, the court cannot interfere with the award by adopting its own interpretation.

case_info: W.P No. 994-A of 2025 Lady Doctor Nargis  Vs  The State and others
W.P No. 1163-A of 2023 Said Qamar  Vs  Govt of KP
remarks:
(a) Every statute, it has been said, which takes away or impairs vested rights acquired under existing laws, or creates a new obligation, or imposes a new duty, or attaches a new disability in respect of transactions or considerations already past, must be presumed, out of respect to the Legislature, to be intended not to have a retrospective operation. An explanation in a statute ordinarily operates to clarify the law prospectively. However, retrospective liability is imposed when an explanation attributes a meaning to a substantive provision or expression whereby the burden, obligation or liability of a person is increased for a past period. Such retrospective impact is to be avoided unless the express language of the explanation warrants such an interpretation.
(b) It is well settled that pensionary benefits are a vested statutory right and not bounty or largesse. They cannot be curtailed, withdrawn, or altered to the detriment of a beneficiary by mere administrative circulars or later expediencies. The right of pension once sanctioned under valid rules, it assumes the character of accrued property within the meaning of Article 24 of the Constitution, which cannot be divested save in accordance with law.

case_info: C.R No. 1026-P of 2023 Govt: of KPK etc  Vs  Meer Ali Khan & others
remarks:
The wording of Sub-clause (b) explicitly signifies that it applies only where the appellate Court considers it necessary to require any document or evidence. In the present case, the impugned judgment and order of the learned appellate Court clearly demonstrate that no such necessity was felt by the Court to allow the production of additional evidence at the time of rendering its decision. Likewise, with regard to the expression \"substantial cause\" mentioned in Rule 27(1)(b) CPC, it is well-settled that each case must be examined in light of its own facts and circumstances. In the instant matter, the learned appellate Court rightly concluded that no such substantial cause existed to justify the recording of additional evidence.
• Rule 27(1) CPC places a clear restriction upon the parties to an appeal from producing additional evidence before the appellate Court. The exception provided under Clause (a) of the said Rule is evidently not attracted to the present case, as no such application was ever moved before the learned trial Court. Sub-clause (b) lays down a specific criterion under which an appellate Court may permit the production of additional evidence, namely, to enable it to pronounce judgment or for any other substantial cause. It is, however, the exclusive prerogative of the appellate Court to determine whether any such necessity exists or whether the matter can be decided on the available record. This provision, by no stretch of interpretation, confers upon any party a vested right to produce additional evidence as a matter of course.

case_info: W.P No. 987-A of 2023 Haji Muhammad Ishaq  Vs Syed Jameel Shah and others
remarks:
(a) It is a settled principle of law that in order to determine whether a cause of action exists or not, a specific issue must be framed during trial, and the parties must then be afforded the opportunity to lead evidence in support of or against the same. Order VII Rule 11 (a) CPC envisages rejection of a plaint only when, on its very face, it fails to disclose any cause of action. In other words, the existence of a cause of action must be ascertainable directly and clearly from the averments of the plaint itself without embarking
upon an inquiry requiring evidence. Thus, the test under this provision is whether the cause of action can be seen floating on the surface of the plaint.
(b) When a plaint discloses material allegations forming a legitimate basis for a claim, it cannot be rejected at the preliminary stage under Order VII Rule 11 of the Civil Procedure Code. If the dispute involves contested factual issues—such as questions of ownership or the validity of a legal document—those matters require a proper trial. The court must allow both parties to present evidence, frame issues, and reach a decision on the merits. It further notes that in this situation, there were conflicting claims regarding ownership of the property and the authenticity of a key document, both of which are factual matters unsuitable for summary rejection. Consequently, the appellate court erred in law by accepting a revision
petition that rejected the plaint without allowing a full adjudication, thereby committing a significant legal irregularity.

case_info: W.P No. 365-A of 2025 Shahbaz Khan  Vs  Mst Saba Sajid and others
remarks:
(a) The Nikah Nama is, in essence, a civil contract, the terms of which embody the mutual obligations agreed upon by the husband and wife. The placement of property or other stipulations in any particular column of the prescribed form cannot be regarded as conclusive in determining their true legal effect. What is material is the intention of the parties at the time of executing the marriage contract. Where a property is described in the form without any qualifying condition, the plain wording of the entry must prevail, and any subsequent interpretation inconsistent with the recorded terms cannot be accepted. Ambiguity arising from the drafting or filling of the form cannot be resolved against the wife unless it is shown that she was fully
informed, understood the implications of each entry, and gave free consent to such limitation. In interpreting the terms of a Nikah Nama, courts are required to ascertain the real intention of the parties by reading the contract as a whole, applying the ordinary meaning of its words, and giving effect to the principle of the bride’s free consent. Where doubt arises, it is a settled rule of construction that such ambiguity should not operate to the detriment of the wife, particularly in view of social and cultural tendencies that often curtail her freedom of negotiation. The judicial approach must therefore aim to protect the rights of the wife by ensuring that ambiguities are resolved in her favour and that no term is implied into the contract unless expressly provided for by the parties themselves.
(b) The concept of dower (mehr) forms an essential element of the marriage contract and is distinct from a gift or any other form of benefit. It is the exclusive right of the wife and may be stipulated in the form of cash or property. Whether prompt or deferred, it becomes her absolute ownership once agreed upon. The
significance of dower lies not only in its obligatory nature but also in its role as a safeguard of the wife’s financial security, equality, and dignity. Therefore, the headings of prescribed columns in the Nikah Nama cannot be treated as decisive in determining the scope of dower; rather, the true substance of the entry and the underlying intention of the parties must prevail.
(c) Under Islamic injunctions and statutory law, a father is bound to provide maintenance for his children—until the son attains majority and until the daughter’s marriage. The fixation of maintenance at Rs. 7,000 per month with a 10% annual increase is neither excessive nor arbitrary; it is a reasonable figure keeping in view prevailing inflationary trends. Similarly, maintenance of the wife during the subsistence of marriage is both a legal and moral obligation of the husband. It is well-settled that a wife’s entitlement to maintenance flows directly from the solemnization of a valid marriage and is neither contingent upon consummation nor dependent on rukhsati. From the moment a valid marital bond is created, the husband comes under a binding legal, moral, and constitutional duty to provide  financial support to his wife. This obligation is rooted not only in classical Islamic jurisprudence, wherein marriage is conceived as a solemn civil contract (‘aqd) founded upon mutual respect, compassion, and responsibility, but also in the higher objectives of Sharīʿah (Maqāṣid al-Sharīʿah), which emphasize dignity, justice, and protection of the family. To treat maintenance as a quid pro quo for obedience or physical submission distorts the very ethos of marriage envisioned under Islam and undermines the egalitarian spirit of the constitutional order.
(d) The High Court, while exercising constitutional jurisdiction under Article 199 of the Constitution, does not function as a court of appeal in family matters. It may only interfere where subordinate courts act without jurisdiction, in excess of jurisdiction, or in violation of the principles of natural justice. The High Court cannot reappraise evidence or substitute its own findings for those of the family or appellate courts. Its constitutional jurisdiction is supervisory in nature, confined to correcting jurisdictional or legal errors apparent on the face of the record.

case_info: C.R No. 03-A of 2023 Safdar Khan  Vs  Sajjad Khan
remarks:
(a)It is by now a well-settled and firmly entrenched principle of law that no litigant ought to suffer on account of any act, error, omission, or neglect on the part of the Court or its ministerial staff. The doctrine that \"Actus Curiae Neminem Gravabit\"  “an act of Court shall prejudice no one” is a salutary rule of law consistently upheld by superior Courts, premised upon the fundamental principle that justice must not be defeated or delayed because of procedural lapses attributable to the Court itself.
(b)It is universally acknowledged that the authority to pass a judgment carries with it a corresponding duty to complete the ancillary and consequential steps, including the preparation of a decree sheet. If, however, the Court or its office fails to discharge this statutory obligation, fairness and justice demand that a litigant
cannot be penalized for such omission. Under Order XX Rule 7 of the Code of Civil Procedure, no specific time limit exists for preparing or signing a decree after a judgment. The decree must bear the judgment’s date, but its preparation is a ministerial act handled by the court’s office. The Supreme Court has clarified that, for the purpose of limitation under Article 156 of the Limitation Act, the period for filing an appeal begins from the date the decree is signed, not from the date of judgment. This ensures litigants are not unfairly penalized for delays beyond their control, upholding the principle that “the law does not compel the doing of the impossible.”
Furthermore, procedural technicalities must not override substantive justice. Courts should prioritize fairness and equity over rigid formalism. Procedural rules are meant to aid justice, not obstruct it. Therefore, technical lapses that do not affect the essence of a case should not deprive parties of their lawful rights. The ultimate goal of the law is to promote justice, equity, and fair play, not to sacrifice them to
mere formalities.

case_info: C.R No. 117-D of 2023 Mst. Waziran Bibi  Vs  Khalil Ahmad etc
remarks:
—Civil Revision—Gift Mutations—Burden of Proof—Limitation—Fraud Allegation Not
Proved—Presumption of Truth Attached to Revenue Record—Mutation Proceedings Not
