Judicial—Held, where genuineness of gift mutations was challenged by legal heirs on ground of
fraud, burden lay on beneficiaries to prove actual transaction; however, when both parties adduced
evidence, case to be decided on preponderance of evidence—Petitioners neither appeared personally
nor produced any convincing proof of fraud—Attorney admitted execution of gift mutations by
predecessor—Revenue officials and attesting witnesses duly verified offer, acceptance and delivery
of possession—Donor remained alive for 21 years after first mutation and never challenged
same—Suit filed 23 years after first mutation was hopelessly barred by limitation—Presumption of
truth attached to duly incorporated mutations unrebutted—No illegality or material irregularity in
appellate Court’s judgment—Revisions dismissed.
[Muhammad Iqbal v. Mukhtar Ahmad through L.Rs (2008 SCMR 855) and Mst. Faheeman Begum
v. Islam-ud-Din (2023 SCMR 1402) referred.]

case_info: C.R No. 114-P of 2018 Shahid Wazir  Vs  Additional Chief Secretary & another
remarks:
i.	The court observed that a combined reading of Section 79 of the CPC and Article 174 of the Constitution makes it clear that a suit by or against the Federation or a Province must be instituted in the name of Pakistan or the respective Province. The court emphasized that this is a mandatory procedural requirement, and non-compliance renders the suit incompetent, preventing any relief from being granted.
ii.	The court considered whether a suit filed by the respondents in their official capacity, without impleading the Federal or Provincial Government, was competent under Section 79 CPC read with Article 174 of the Constitution. Referring to the apex court\'s decision in Nawabzada Mir Tariq Hussain Khan Magsi, the court noted that no suit can be instituted against a Provincial Government without including the Province as a party. The court emphasized that this procedural requirement is mandatory, and non-compliance renders the suit incompetent and unenforceable.

case_info: Tax Reference No. 49-P with CM No. 45-P of 2024 Rooman Khan  Vs  Additional Commissioner IR RTO Peshawar etc
remarks:
The question of distinction between “Consumer Goods” and “FMCG” has been raised in various judicial proceedings, particularly in matters pertaining to fiscal legislation and tax liability. While these terms are often used interchangeably in commercial circles, they carry distinct legal meanings with significant implications for the determination of tax rates under the Ordinance. This discourse aims to elucidate the legal distinction between these two categories with reference to statutory provisions and judicial precedents. It is pertinent to note that the exclusion of “durable goods” from the definition of FMCG was introduced through the Finance, 2017, thereby providing legislative clarity that goods with longer lifespan and utility are not to be classified as FMCG.

	The purpose of separately defining consumer goods as well as FMCG is to draw a clear distinction between the two sets of goods, i.e., consumer goods and FMCG; therefore, these terms should be given the meaning as intended by the lawmaker with regard to their use in the later part of the statute for determining the applicable rate of taxes. However, the law does not specify the species, category, nature, or character of goods that fall within the ambit of FMCG, evidently because the legislature could not feasibly enumerate an exhaustive list of such goods. In the event that such a complex question arises before the court, then the recourse should be made to the recognized tools of statutory interpretation.
	Based on the statutory framework and application of the three-part test:
i.	Storage batteries qualify as consumer goods as they are ultimately used by end-consumers for personal, family, or household purposes.

ii.	However, storage batteries DO NOT qualify as FMCG because:
o	They fail the velocity of trade test due to infrequent purchase cycles.
o	They are explicitly excluded as durable goods.
o	They do not meet the “daily demand” requirement.

iii.	Consequently, we hold that distributors of storage batteries should be subject to the standard minimum tax rate of 1.25% under S.No.4 of Division-IX of Part-I of the First Schedule to the Ordinance, rather than the preferential rate of 0.2% reserved for genuine FMCG. Accordingly, Tax Reference Nos.43-P/2025, 60-P/2025, 61-P/2025, and 62-P/2025 are answered in affirmative, whereas Tax References Nos.49-P/2024, 50-P/2024, 64-P/2024, 65-P/2024, 09-P/2025, 10-P/2025, 113-P/2025, and 152-P/2025 are answered in negative.

case_info: C.R No. 183-B of 2025 Syed Akbar Khan  Vs  Govt. of KP
remarks:
Writ Petition.
Civil Revision --- Constitution (Twenty-Fifth Amendment) Act, 2018 --- Frontier Crimes Regulations (FCR), repeal of --- FATA merger into Khyber Pakhtunkhwa --- Effect --- Maliki/Lungi Allowance --- Scope --- The petitioner’s suit for declaration and injunction claiming entitlement to Maliki/Lungi Allowance was dismissed by trial court on the ground of failure to prove entitlement, which findings was upheld by the appellate Court. Held, although the courts below did not advert to the most material legal aspect, i.e., repeal of FCR and abolition of Maliki/Lungi System after FATA merger, yet their ultimate conclusion in dismissing the claim did not warrant interference. The system of Maliki/Lungi allowance, being relic of the old tribal administrative structure, has lost all legal recognition after the constitutional amendment. However, if Government revives or reintroduces such a system through legislation or policy, aggrieved persons may approach the competent forum for redress.
--- Findings of Courts below maintained--- Revision Petition dismissed.

case_info: F.A.O No. 07-P of 2023 Syed Ul Abrar  Vs  Mst. Faheema etc.
remarks:
Articles 10-A, 199, 204 of the Constitution of Islamic Republic of Pakistan, 1973.
Sections 2,3,4,6 of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Enforcement of Women’s Property Rights Act,
2019.
Sections 5,7,9,10 of Protection against Harassment of Women at the Workplace Act,
2010.
Sections 3,5, 13,17 of Contempt of Court Ordinance, 2003.
Section 491 of Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898.
i. Powers and functions of Ombudsperson under Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Enforcement
of Women’s Property Rights Act, 2019.
ii. Sections 6 of the Act of 2019 empowers Ombudsperson to make reference of the
complaint to Civil Court where the complaint requires in-depth inquiry and detailed
recording of evidence.
iii. Contempt of Court and award of punishment by the Ombudsperson under Khyber
Pakhtunkhwa Enforcement of Women’s Property Rights Act, 2019.
iv. The power to punish for contempt is not expressly mentioned in the Act of 2019
ibid, however by virtue of section 3 of the Act, it has been vested with the same
powers in the ombudsperson under the Act of 2010 ibid. According to section 10
(vi) of the Protection against Harassment of Women at the Workplace Act, 2010,
the Ombudsperson shall have the same powers as the High Court has to punish
any person for its contempt. The powers so entrusted ought to be exercised
abiding by all the codal formalities, principles of natural justice and rights enshrined
in the Constitution.

case_info: W.P No. 241-P of 2025 Mujeeb Ur Rehman etc  Vs  Federation of Pakistan etc
remarks: Rule  12, 13 and 14 of the Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Births, Death , Marriages and Divorce or Dissolution of Marriages (Registration and Certification) Rules 2021, were held to be directory in nature rather than mandatory and its non-observance could not therefore be given the effect of refusal in registration of valid marriages between the parties. Same rules were also found to be having prospective operation not affecting the marriages that had taken place earlier.

case_info: BCA No. 51-B of 2025 Syed Musawar Abbas  Vs  Syed Tahir Abbas Bukhari etc
remarks:
The law is well settled that where Section 195 Cr.P.C. is attracted, the authority specifically mentioned therein alone is vested with jurisdiction to proceed, and initiation by any private person is barred to that extent. The mandate of Section 195(1)(c) Cr.P.C. unequivocally provides that only the Court or authority specified therein is competent to initiate proceedings in respect of such forgery, to the exclusion of any private person. No exceptional circumstances have been demonstrated to warrant cancellation of bail already granted, which appears tainted by mala fide on part of police and complainant. We have also been informed that the trial has been commenced in the court of Senior Civil Judge (Judicial), wherein the charge has been framed, in such an eventuality the court should not grant or cancel bail when the trial is in progress. As the trial is in progress, therefore, any findings over the merits of the case would pre-judice either party.
[BCA dismissed, pre-arrest bail allowed to the accused by the lower fora is maintained.]

case_info: BCA No. 48-B of 2025 Syed Musawar Abbas  Vs  Syed Tahir Abbas Bukhari etc
remarks:
When at the direction of this court, the counter version was recorded from the mouth of the accused/respondent Tahir Abbas subsequently that was cancelled by the investigating agency which has been challenged before this Court in writ petition, still pending. Hence, if two views are possible from the evidence then the view favourable to the accused is to be adopted.
With regard to the allegation of ulterior motive and mala fide intent, it is pertinent to note that the occurrence in question took place on 11.08.2024, whereupon the police promptly registered the F.I.R on the report of the complainant against the respondent. However, it is a matter of record that, for registration of his own version, the respondent had to approach this Court on two occasions, and ultimately his report was recorded only pursuant to the directions of this Court in contempt proceedings vide judgment dated 21.05.2025 in shape of Naqalmad. It also stands established from the record that there exists a history of numerous litigation between the parties, and several applications, both against and on behalf of the accused, are still sub-judice before different forums. In this backdrop, the element of ulterior motive and mala fide on the part of the complainant cannot be altogether ruled out.
Having regard to the site of the alleged injury on the shoulder of the complainant, the same squarely falls within the ambit of Section 337-N(2), P.P.C, wherein, in cases of hurt, the ordinary punishment prescribed is payment of Arsh and Daman. Sentence of imprisonment as Tazir may only be imposed if the offender is shown to be a previous convict, a habitual, hardened, desperate or dangerous criminal, or where the offence has been committed in the name or on the pretext of honour. In the present case, the prosecution has failed to bring on record any material to establish that the respondent falls within such categories
[BCA dismissed, pre-arrest bail allowed to the accused by the lower fora is maintained.]

case_info: W.P No. 2440-P of 2024 Iftikhar Hussain  Vs  Malik Fakhar Zaman and others
remarks:
Writ Petition
Held: Where administrative or revenue authorities act strictly in accordance with law, the civil courts have no jurisdiction; however, where they fail to do so or their orders are vitiated by mala fide or non-compliance with mandatory provisions, the civil courts are competent to interfere and strike down such orders. Order made in administrative capacity do not attract the provisions implicit in section 12(2) CPC but can validly be questioned before the Civil Court as the ordinary court of competent jurisdiction. This legal arrangement does not extinguish or dilute the parties\' right to challenge such order; rather, their right to seek redress in preserved under the fundamental principle of ubi jus ibi remedium.
Writ Petition stands disposed of in the above terms

case_info: Cr.R No. 26-D of 2025 Shah Barat and others  Vs  Riz Hussain and others
remarks:
Illegal Dispossession Act, 2005 (XLIII of 2005)—
—Ss. 7, 9—Criminal Procedure Code (V of 1898), Ss. 265-C & 265-D—
“During trial” — Meaning and scope — Interim relief under Section 7 can only be granted during trial —
Trial commences only after framing of charge and supply of complaint documents to the accused under
Sections 265-C & 265-D, Cr.P.C.— Where no charge had been framed and requisite copies had not been supplied, trial had not commenced — Order granting interim possession under Section 7, passed before commencement of trial, held without lawful authority and set aside — Trial Court directed to first decide question of maintainability and thereafter proceed afresh in accordance with law.
—Haq Nawaz and others v. The State and others (2000 SCMR 785) ref.
Held: Since the trial commences only after framing of charge and supply of documents under Sections
265-C & 265-D, Cr.P.C., the condition “during trial” appearing in Section 7 of the Illegal Dispossession Act, 2005, could not be deemed satisfied. As no charge had yet been framed and copies of the complaint had not been supplied, the trial had not begun; hence, the learned trial Court was not competent to grant interim relief under Section 7. The impugned order was, therefore, without lawful authority and liable to be set aside.
Result: Petition accepted.

case_info: W.P No. 4520-P of 2022 Collector of Custom and others  Vs  President of Pakistan and others
remarks:
It is a famous saying that “Power tends to corrupt and absolute power corrupts absolutely. Great men are almost always bad men, even when they exercise influence and not authority; still more when you superadd the tendency or the certainty of corruption by authority;. 1 When public officials exceed the lawful limits of their authority, the result is an injustice that shakes the foundations of rule of law. Montesquieu observed that “there is no greater tyranny than that which is perpetrated under the shield of the law and in the name of justice.” 2 In a nutshell, the public authorities in modern constitutional democratic states are no longer the absolute arbiters of power; they are bound by the rule of law and must respect the rights guaranteed under the Constitution and applicable laws. These authorities cannot violate these rights with impunity, as accountability mechanisms are in place to ensure that any infringement upon individual liberties is subject to scrutiny and redress. With this background, we approach the present matter. 
In our jurisprudence the above principles have been recognized by the superior courts within our own constitutional design. In the case of Supreme Court Bar Association (supra), Justice Athar Minallah (as he then was) in his separate note articulated that if officials transgress their lawful authority, courts will not hesitate to intervene. His lordship maintained that, Article 212 of the Constitution explicitly creates a constitutional remedy for cases involving the “transgression, wrongful exercise of authority, abuse of power, or reckless disregard for or neglect of public duties.” When a right is breached by the government or any person in the service of Pakistan, the aggrieved individual can seek damages against the officer responsible. This provision distinguishes itself by conferring the right to claim public law remedies for tortious acts committed by three categories of public authorities. It is a right that, regrettably, has not received adequate attention in the courts nor has it been invoked by victims of wrongful public power exercise. The absence of established special courts, as envisioned by the framers of the Constitution, does not negate this right. No right is without a remedy; even in the absence of a functional Service Tribunal, civil servants can seek recourse through other forums, including the High Courts. The right to claim remedies against the tortious acts of public authorities remains intact and cannot be denied simply because a special court has not been established. The jurisdiction of courts of plenary civil jurisdiction, and the High Courts in appropriate cases, is not barred until such a court is created. This constitutional right is vital for victims to vindicate their rights, hold public authorities accountable, and ensure that public powers are not abused. If effectively exercised, this right empowers citizens to monitor the misconduct of public authorities and the government, enabling them to seek compensation for wrongs suffered. The courts bear the responsibility to ensure that citizens can enforce this valuable right. The courts possess the discretion to design or create remedies to address constitutional violations and deter future tortious acts, including the potential for awarding exemplary or vindicatory damages as appropriate.

1. Lord Acton (John Emerick Edward Dalberg) in an 1887 letter to Bishop Mandell Creighton.
2. The Spirit of the Laws by Montesquieu.

case_info: W.P No. 139-A of 2018 Muhammad Rashid & others  Vs  Galiyat Development Authority & others
remarks:
The Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Public Property (Removal of Encroachment) Act, 1977 is a self-contained statute enacted to address the mischief of unauthorized occupation and encroachment upon public property. Its preamble declares the object of providing effective measures for the removal of encroachments and recovery of possession. Before its enactment, no special mechanism existed for the speedy removal of such encroachments, exposing the Government to prolonged litigation. The Act, therefore, provides both expeditious remedies for the State and procedural safeguards for individuals asserting lawful rights, reflecting a legislative intent to balance protection of public property with the preservation of due process.
It is a settled principle that exclusion of civil court jurisdiction cannot be presumed; it must be express or arise by necessary implication. In this Act, the legislature has explicitly structured a dual mechanism. Sections 3 and 4 empower the authorized officer to issue notices for removal of encroachments and, if necessary, enforce eviction, forming the executive framework for prompt administrative action.
Parallel to this, the Act establishes a judicial forum through Sections 12 and 13, creating a Tribunal with exclusive jurisdiction to determine: (i) whether a property is public property within the meaning of the Act, and (ii) whether any lease, license, or tenancy has been validly determined. Section 11 expressly bars the jurisdiction of civil courts in matters within the Tribunal’s competence, while Section 14 vests the
Tribunal with powers akin to those of a civil court—such as summoning witnesses, recording evidence, and issuing commissions. Proceedings before it are judicial in nature, and its decisions are final and binding.
In essence, the Act establishes a dual system: administrative action for immediate removal of encroachments, and judicial adjudication by the Tribunal for disputes concerning title or rights. The jurisdiction of civil courts stands expressly ousted, underscoring the legislature’s intent to ensure both effective protection of public property and fair adjudication of individual claims.

case_info: C.R No. 208-A of 2012 Mehboob Khan  Vs  Ali Asghar Khan
remarks:
(a) The failure of the respondent to establish the mandatory legal requirements of Talb-i-Muwathibat (immediate demand) and Talb-i-Ishhad (demand through notice) under the Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Pre-emption Act, 1987. The evidence presented showed contradictions regarding the time and details of the alleged “jumping demand,” as the plaint mentioned 3:00 p.m., while witnesses referred to 5:00 p.m., and none specified the year. Moreover, the informer who first conveyed the information of the sale was not produced as a witness, creating a serious evidentiary gap. Non-production of such a vital witness raises a presumption under Article 129(g) of the Qanun-e-Shahadat Order, 1984, that his testimony would have been unfavorable to the respondent. Similarly, the respondent failed to prove proper service of Talb-i-Ishhad—the postman, attesting witnesses, and the advocate who drafted the notice were not produced, nor was the acknowledgment due card presented. These omissions rendered the notice unproven in law. Given the strict statutory nature of pre- emption demands, such lapses are fatal. This court therefore held that the respondent had not fulfilled the mandatory requirements of law, and his claim of pre-emption was unsustainable.
(b) The above-mentioned principle applies in pre-emption suits as well, a pre- emptor claiming right of pre-emption, as a matter of prudence, ought to seek corroboration to satisfy reasonably the judicial mind of the Court that Talb-i- Mwathibat was made by him enabling him to make the other demand (Talb-i- Ishhad). In this case, failure to put the informer in the witness-box, seen from that angle as well, raises a logical presumption that appellant feared that the witness, if examined, could not stand the test of cross examination or that the witness would not support him or that his evidence would be against himNon- production of informer in a pre-emption suit is a sufficient ground for dismissal of the pre-emptor`s suit.
(c) It is settled law that in pre-emption cases, strict compliance with the statutory requirements of Talb-i-Muwathibat and Talb-i-Ishhad is mandatory, and any lapse therein is fatal to the claim. The burden lies squarely on the pre-emptor to prove that both demands were made in accordance with law. Failure to produce the best and direct evidence—such as the informer, witnesses, postman, or acknowledgment due card—creates a serious evidentiary gap and justifies dismissal of the suit. The courts have repeatedly held that non-production of such essential witnesses amounts to withholding of best evidence under Article 129(g) of the Qanun-e-Shahadat Order, 1984, warranting an adverse inference against the pre-emptor. Consequently, where the foundational requirements of Talb-i-Muwathibat or Talb-i-Ishhad are not strictly proved through credible and direct evidence, the claim of pre-emption cannot succeed in law.

case_info: F.A.O No. 159-P of 2022  Jubilee Life Insurance Company  Vs  Arshad Khan
remarks:
A.	In case of surrender of an insurance policy before achieving of the maturity period, the insurer would only be entitled to its surrender value, according to the Bancassurance Regulations 2015.
B.	When terms of a contract are reduced into writing and same remains admitted between the parties then oral evidence cannot be allowed to outweigh express terms of such agreement.

case_info: CM 12(2) CPC No. 70-D of 2025 Abdul Aziz  Vs  Mst Humaira Yasmeen & others
remarks:
(Petition under Section 12(2) of the Code of Civil Procedure)
1. Writ petition filed by petitioner against order of the learned Judge
Family Court, on the basis of a request of learned counsel for
petitioner, was dismissed as withdrawn. Petitioner challenged the
validity of withdrawal order by invoking provisions of Section 12(2)
CPC alleging fraud and misrepresentation on the ground that the
petition was withdrawn without obtaining his consent. ---Held--- The
petitioner had conferred the power of withdrawal to his counsel. The
consent of the petitioner would not make a difference in whether he
had instructed his counsels to act on his behalf or not. In the
Vakalatnama, there was no requirement or condition of prior
instruction or consent from the petitioner. Therefore, the actions taken
by the counsels on behalf of the petitioner are fully binding on him.
Since his authorised counsels were present before the court, he has
neither been misrepresented nor defrauded.
2. It is well-settled that an advocate has the authority to make statements
on behalf of their client, which are binding unless there is a restriction
in the Vakalatnama that limits the advocate’s authority to compromise
or abandon claims on behalf of the client. The advocate’s power in
conducting a case allows them to abandon all or part of the claim if
they believe it is advisable in the best interest of their client.
(The CM 12(2) CPC filed by petitioner was dismissed.)

case_info: C.R No. 631-P of 2025 with CM No. 932-P of 2025 Muhammad Yousaf and others  Vs  Ajab Khan and others
remarks:
1.	The mortgagor at the time of redemption of his property shall be entitled of the improvement made by the mortgagee and as such he shall not be liable to pay the cost thereof with certain exceptions.
2.	A mortgagor would be liable to pay the cost of improvement to the mortgagee if:-
i. 	there is any contract between the mortgagor and mortgagee to pay by the mortgagor to the mortgagee;
ii.	such improvement was necessary to preserve the property from destruction;
iii.	such improvement was necessary to prevent the security from becoming insufficient; and
iv. if such improvement was made in compliance with the lawful order
of any public authority.
3.	A plaintiffs) has / have to prove all those facts which he / she / they has/ have pleaded in his/her/their plaint and in case of success, he / she / they will be entitled for the grant of decree. Similarly, defendants) is/are also bound to prove all those facts which he / she / they has / have taken in their written statement.
4.	Civil cases are decided on the basis of principle of preponderance of evidence and they are to be decided in favour of the party who has succeeded to tilt the scale in its favour.
5.	Concurrent findings of facts could only be set aside by the High Court in its revisional jurisdiction u/s 115 CPC if the same suffer from misreading or non-reading of evidence or if while recording such findings the fora below have committed illegalities or irregularities.

case_info: W.P No. 729-B of 2020 Hizbullah Khan  Vs  Government of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa through Secretary Education, etc
remarks:
Writ Petition.
Held: Service--- Constitutional jurisdiction--- Quota for children of retired employees ---
Voluntary retirement --- Binding effect of Supreme Court pronouncements --- Petitioner sought appointment under quota for children of retired employees on basis of father’s retirement as Chowkidar --- Held, S&GAD policy dated 23.05.2000 reserves quota only for children of employees retiring on superannuation or invalidation; petitioner’s father had voluntarily retired, thus case not covered by policy ---. Moreover, in GPO Islamabad Vs Muhammad Jalal (PLD 2024 SC 1276), Supreme court struck down all rules/policies granting appointments to children/family members of employees on retirement or death in service as discriminatory and violative of Articles 3,4, 5(2), 18, 25(1) & 27of the Constitution --- Similarly, in Govt. of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa V. Tahir Mushtaq (2025 PLC (C.S) 34), Court held such quota offends constitutional mandate of equality, transparency, and merit --- Pronouncements of Supreme court under Article 189 are binding --- Petition found devoid of merit and dismissed.
(Writ Petition Dismissed)

case_info: Cr.A No. 66-B of 2025 Mst. Shahnaza Bibi  Vs  The state etc.
remarks:
A video recording, when duly authenticated and presented in accordance with law, possesses evidentiary worth, as it has the capacity to visually depict the occurrence, the individuals involved, and the sequence of events, thereby offering a more complete picture than oral testimony alone. In the instant matter, during cross-examination, the complainant (PW-4) candidly admitted that a CCTV camera was installed in the shop of one Shah Niaz, a grocer, and Asif (PW_5) also conceded that the said camera clearly captured the complainant party. Despite this admitted presence, the investigating officer failed to produce the recording at the relevant stage; nevertheless, the same was subsequently placed on record on the application of the accused party. The essential issue for judicial determination thus remains whether the recording is genuine, free from manipulation or editing, and whether its quality in any manner diminishes its probative value.
[Criminal appeal dismissed, acquittal of the accused by the lower fora is maintained.]

case_info: WP No. 559-P of 2024 Muhammad Zubair Khan etc  Vs  Govt. of KP through Chief Secretary, Peshawar
remarks:
Establishment of special Tribunals under the Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Public Property (Removal of Encroachment) Act, 1977 and its jurisdiction.
The Act, 1977 provides for the establishment of special Tribunals by the Government through notification in the official Gazette. Each Tribunal is constituted for a specified territorial area. Section 13 of the Act, 1977 vests exclusive jurisdiction in the Tribunal to adjudicate specific disputes. The law provides that where a question arises regarding, whether a particular property is public property or not, or whether a lease or licence in respect of such property has been legally determined, it is the Tribunal alone that has the authority to decide such matters. Ordinary civil courts have no jurisdiction in such matters and leaving the Tribunal as the sole competent authority to decide these issues.
Definition of public property and unauthorized occupants.
(iii) “Public property”, under section 2(h) of the Act of 1977, means a building, land, place, or premises which vests in, or is in the possession or under the management or control of Government, Local Council, autonomous body, public sector Company, or such other authority, while “unauthorized occupant”, under Section 2(j) of the Act 1977, means a person who has made encroachment on, or is in occupation of, any public property without the express permission or authority of a competent authority. In the instant case, respondents Nos. 5 and 6 cannot be treated as unauthorized occupants, as the revenue record, the allotment order and rent-paying documents confirmed that possession of the respondents No. 5 and 6 is lawful on the basis of lease. Thus, no anti-encroachment proceedings could be initiated against them.
Locus standi to challenge judgment of Tribunal in public interest cases.
(iii) This petition is not pro bono Público, as both petitioners seek to advance personal causes rather than public interest. Petitioner No. 1, already exonerated in departmental proceedings and facing a pending damages suit, cannot invoke this jurisdiction merely on account of having served as Deputy Commissioner, since such matters lie within the competence of his successor in office. Petitioner No. 2, transferred from Bannu to Mardan Division and himself a defendant in the damages suit, likewise lacks locus standi. The legality of the Tribunal judgment may be questioned only by those presently holding office or otherwise directly aggrieved, and not by the present petitioners. The petitioners, being public functionaries, had a nexus with the property to protect the interest of the State. In accordance with law, they contested the suit through their written statement. However, once they were transferred, they could not question the judgment of the Tribunal for the simple reason that they are public servants and the proceedings before the Tribunal were not in consonance with law.

case_info: W.P No. 480-A of 2025 Muhammad Babar  Vs  Commissioner Hazara Division
remarks:
(b)Under Rule 18 of the Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Residential Accommodation at Districts (Procedure for Allotment) Rules, 2018, an allottee, who is transferred out of the district, may, subject to fulfillment of certain conditions, retain the Government residential accommodation for a limited period. The scheme of the Rule clearly provides that the allottee may retain such accommodation for one year on payment of normal rent and, thereafter, for an additional year on payment of market rent. However, this right of retention is not absolute; it is hedged with two conditions of significant importance: firstly, that the allottee has not been provided or has not availed Government accommodation at his new place of posting; and secondly, that the family members of the allottee continue to reside with him in the accommodation so
retained. These conditions are aimed at ensuring that Government residences, being a scarce public resource, are utilized only by those genuinely entitled and not kept locked or misused in violation of the Rules.
(b)Rule 35 of the said Rules specifically provides a statutory mechanism of redress. It stipulates that a public office holder or an allottee, as the case may be, who feels aggrieved of any decision or order passed by the competent authority, whether the Estate Officer or the Deputy Commissioner, may file an appeal before the Deputy Commissioner or the Commissioner, depending upon the circumstances. Similarly, against the order of first appellate authority the allottee or the public office holder, as the case may be, may file second appeal under Rule 36 of the ibid Rules. The said provisions of law are reflective of the legislative intent that such matters should, in the first instance, be dealt with by the departmental appellate
forums created under the Rules, rather than by invoking constitutional jurisdiction directly. The petitioner, therefore, had an adequate, efficacious, and alternate remedy available under the law but chose to bypass the same without offering any justification for not availing the statutory remedy.

case_info: W.P No. 2470-P of 2022 Habib Ur Rehman etc  Vs  Senior Member Board of Revenue Khyber Pakhtunkhwa etc
remarks:
A.	Appointment of Naib Tehsildar, against a number of posts of Sub-Registrar, for performing functions under the Registration Act 1908, were held to be illegal, ultra vires and resultantly set aside.
B.	Petitioners in the writ petition were not aggrieved of any final order affecting their terms and conditions of service, therefore they could not have invoked the Jurisdiction of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Service Tribunal and their writ petition was maintainable

case_info: C.R No. 736-P of 2025 Muhammad Sareer  Vs  Muhammad Ameer and others.
remarks:
(i)	The scope, extent and applicability of section 4, of The Muslim Family Laws Ordinance, 1961 fully discussed and analyzed.
(ii)	In the event of death of any son or daughter of the propositus before the opening of succession, the children of such son or daughter who are living at the time of opening of the succession shall be entitled for such share in the legacy, which is to be equivalent to the share of such son or daughter, who would have received, if lived.
(iii)	Under the recent jurisprudence, great grandchildren who were not living at the time of death of propositus would not be entitled for the share in the legacy as section 4 of The Muslim Family Laws Ordinance, 1961, is restricted only to the living grandchildren of the propositus at the time of his death.
(iv)	Though Section 4 of The Muslim Family Laws Ordinance, 1961 has declared unislamic by the Federal Shariat Court in Allah Rakha case (PLD 2000 Federal Shariat Court, Page 1), however, since against the said judgment, a number of appeals are pending before the Shariat Appellate Bench of the Supreme Court, therefore, in view of proviso to clause (2) of Article 203-D, of The Constitution of Islamic Republic of Pakistan, the said decision of the Federal Shariat Court shall remain ineffective during the pendency of these appeals and till the decision over the same.
(v)	Where a party being a defendant has not agitated any factual plea and has relied only on a legal plea then in such case, recording of evidence would not be required as a legal controversy could be decided without recording of evidence and through a summary judgment.
(vi)	The High Court in its revisional jurisdiction could only interfere in the concurrent finding of the two courts below if they are either the result of misreading or non-reading of evidence or if the same suffer from any jurisdictional defect or any material irregularity.

case_info: W.P No. 2423-P of 2024 Umar Farooq  Vs  Arshad Malook
remarks:
1.	A tenant could only be evicted from the rented premises, if the landlord as per the mandate and requirements of Section 13 of the Urban Rent Restriction Ordinance, 1959, apply to the Rent Controller and as against this such eviction order of the tenant could not be passed in a petition filed by the tenant against the landlord, as there is no provision in the Urban Rent Restriction Ordinance to enable the landlord to take the plea of eviction of the tenant in his written statement.
2.	When Section 13 of the West Pakistan Urban Rent Restriction
Ordinance, requires from the landlord to apply for the eviction on any ground mentioned therein, then such eviction could not be ordered through any other mode as when the law prescribes a thing to be done in a particular manner following a particular procedure then it shall be done in the same manner following the provisions of law without deviating from the prescribed procedure.
3.	When once disputed questions of law and facts are raised in the pleadings or applications and its apply then courts of law are bound to provide an opportunity to both the parties to substantiate/ established their respective pleas and as such any short cut procedure would be against the principle of natural justice.
4.	The High Court could set-aside the orders and judgments of Special Courts/ Special Tribunals, if they have improperly exercise their jurisdiction or if their judgments are the result of illegal exercise of jurisdiction vested in them or where such fora lack jurisdiction or if there has been a serious mis-reading or mis-appreication of the evidence on the part of such Special Tribunals/Courts or if they have wrongly apply the statutory law or any principle or rule of law as laid down by the superior Court.

case_info: Cr.A No. 1673-P of 2023 Kausar  Vs  The State etc
remarks:
i.	It is of particular significance that the deceased lost her life inside the house of her husband, yet neither the husband, nor the other inmates of the house made any effort to shift her to the hospital or to report the matter to the police. Instead, it was the co-villagers, accompanied by the complainant, who shifted the dead body to the hospital. Had the incident not occurred in the manner alleged by the complainant, and had it truly been a case of suicide, the natural conduct of the appellant and his family would have been to report the matter to the authorities and to arrange for immediate medical assistance. Their failure to do so, coupled with their indifference in not accompanying the dead body, constitutes a strong incriminating circumstance. These circumstances, taken cumulatively, weigh heavily upon the judicial mind of this Court and point unmistakably towards the involvement of the appellant in the commission of the offence.
ii.	More alarming is the conduct of the appellant himself, as he did not remain present at the house after the occurrence, nor did he participate in the funeral ceremony of his own wife, instead, he chose to abscond. Such conduct is inconsistent with innocence. If the husband was truly innocent, the natural course of conduct would have been to remain with the family, to arrange immediate medical assistance, to inform the authorities, and to attend the funeral. His deliberate absence and subsequent abscondence provide a strong incriminating circumstance.
iii.	This Court, therefore, cannot ignore the subsequent conduct of the appellant, both at the time of occurrence and thereafter. When viewed in this backdrop, the subsequent conduct of the appellant provides a strong circumstance connecting him with the offence, and in our considered view, the trial Court was justified in holding him responsible.
iv.	It is true that there is no direct evidence with regard to motive; however, it is equally true that such like disputes usually arise within the domestic sphere, and incidents of this nature rarely take place in the presence of outsiders. The prosecution has, therefore, succeeded m bringing home the guilt against the accused beyond reasonable doubt.

case_info: Cr.M (BCA) No. 476-D of 2024 Ahmad  Vs  The State etc
remarks:
(Criminal Misc: Bail Cancellation Application)
(a) Sections 376/34 P.P.C read with Section 53 of The Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Child Protection and Welfare Act, 2010 (Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Act No.XIII of 2010)—Criminal Procedure Code, 1898..S. 497(5)—Allegations against the accused/respondents, as per FIR were that on 30.7.2024 at 03:00 p.m (Peshen Wela), they committed sodomy with the complainant. Though the respondents were directly charged for the commission of offence, however, the medico legal report of the victim/complainant did not support the allegation of sodomy as contained in the FIR. The FSL report regarding swab also did not support the prosecution version.
(b) Criminal Procedure Code, 1898—By virtue of amendment made in the Code through Act No.XLIV of 2016, Section 164-B was inserted whereby in cases under Sections 376, 377 or 378-B of PPC or attempt thereto, the Deoxyribonucleic Acid (DNA) testing was made mandatory, if practicable, from the victim and accused and sending of the same to the Forensic Laboratory, however, no such exercise was carried out to bring on record sufficient material, connecting the accused/respondent with the offence.
(c) Criminal Procedure Code, 1898…S.497(5) —The principles for the grant of bail and cancellation thereof are quite different from each other. Once bail is granted by a competent Court of law then strong and exceptional grounds in the shape of misuse of the concession of bail and tampering with the prosecution evidence are required for the cancellation of bail.
(Petition was dismissed in the circumstances).

case_info: Cr.A No. 414-B of 2023 Muhammad Umar  Vs  The State etc
remarks:
Penal Code (XLV of 1860)---
----Ss. 302, 324, 34---Qatl-i-amd, attempt to commit qatl-i-amd, armed with deadly weapon, ---
Appreciation of evidence---Benefit of doubt--- On 13.03.2015 at about 09:30 hours complainant along
with his brother Ismail and cousin Gul Marjan were busy in repairing roof of the room of his house when
from vacant plot outside of their house, Muhammad and Noor Janan sons of Sher Gul, co-villagers shouted at them to come out of the house. The complainant party raised their heads and saw both the accused standing with deadly weapons. The accused Noor Janan commanded his brother Muhammad to kill them, and both the accused started firing at the complainant party with the intention to commit their murder. From the firing of accused Muhammad, Gul Marjan got hit and fell down while complainant and his brother put their heads down and escaped unhurt luckily.--- If we see the statement of Doctor/PW-05, who has stated that there was one entry wound of firearm on the medial side of left side with base of nose size 1/3x1/3 inch cavity deep with another exit wound of fire arm on the back of head with brain matter coming out size 4x5 inch, which reflects that the entry wound was corresponding to a large exit wound on the back of head, which shows otherwise through and through. Here the medical evidence contradicts the ocular account for the simple reason that the accused both in number have been shown at points No.4 and 5 while on the other hand the deceased and eye witnesses have been shown at rooftop, where they were busy in mud plaster of the rooftop--- Apart from this aspect of the case when the deceased and the eye witnesses were working and were busy in mud plaster over the rooftop and were visible to them then there was no need to call upon them by the accused party for the reason that they wanted to kill them. Even otherwise it is illogical when the accused have been shown in an open plot covered by a boundary wall to have come to the place of occurrence for discussion over women dispute then they would certainly come to the main door side which is reflected from the record towards western side of the site plan indicating the house of the complainant then there was no need for the accused to have come to the open plot and have asked the deceased and eye witnesses to come out which otherwise reflects about the genuineness of the prosecution story---Apart from this fact when the prime target of the accused was to kill the deceased then astonishingly how there was shown the number of bullet marks in the site plan at points “B” and “C”----Now coming to the other aspect of the case, in this case it is the version of the complainant that they were busy in mud plaster of the complainant’s house then admittedly for mud plaster the meson use to plaster the said rooftop with the help of Girmala (wooden trowel), which has neither been recovered from the place of Wardat nor has been handed over by the complainant to the investigating officer------Appeal was allowed and accused were acquitted, in circumstances.

case_info: Cr.M BA No. 385-B of 2025 Rizwana Bibi  Vs  The State
remarks:
Bail Petition.
Code of criminal procedure, 1898---Bail---Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Control of narcotics substances act, 2019--- safe custody and transmission off samples--- search warrant--- unauthorized police officer--- female accused--- further inquiry--- recovery allegedly effected on 03.08.2025; samples dispatched to FSL after seventy two- hours limit prescribed under rule 4 (2) of the Control of Narcotics substances (Government analyst ) rules,2001--- Magistrate had authorized ASHO to conduct search, but SHO, without authority, entered lady accused’s house, arrested her, and failed to return warrant or produce recovered narcotics before Court--- Action in blatant violation of Court’s mandate; prosecution yet to explain or justify recovery through unauthorized officer--- Prosecution’s reliance on cursory mobile phone video, without placing on record any still photographs extracted therefrom, to be assessed at trial--- Petitioner a woman with no prior conviction; investigation complete, no further custody required--- Held, case called for further inquiry under Section 497 (2) Cr.P.C.--- Bail granted.
(Petition Alloowed)

case_info: C.R No. 1071-P of 2012 Murad Ali  Vs  Shah Jehan & others
remarks:
(i)	The nature, purpose and stage of proceedings of a preliminary decree and final decree, fully discussed and elaborated upon.
(ii)	In a preliminary decree the suit is not completely disposed of but through the same certain rights or liabilities of the parties are declared leaving further proceedings to be carried out at the stage of the final decree.
(iii)	While hearing an appeal against the preliminary decree, the appeal court has to restrict itself to the legality and propriety of such preliminary decree and it cannot take an exercise which ought to be taken by the trial court at the stage of proceedings of the final decree.

case_info: W.P No. 1034-A of 2025 Ejaz  Vs Pir Khitab Shah
remarks:
(a) Under Order XIII Rule 1 of the Code of Civil Procedure (CPC), it is incumbent upon the parties to produce all documentary evidence in their possession or power at the first hearing of the suit. However, it is a well-settled principle that the term “first hearing” refers to the stage when the court proceeds to frame issues after the filing of the written statement by the defendant, as provided under Order X Rule 1 CPC.
The CPC prescribes distinct stages at which parties are required or permitted to produce documents relied upon by them. The first stage arises at the time of filing the plaint, as per Order VII Rule 14 CPC, in respect of documents relied upon by the plaintiff; and for the defendant, at the time of filing the written statement.
If a party fails to avail this initial opportunity, it may proceed under Order XI Rule 1 CPC to deliver interrogatories when it believes that the opposing party possesses documents relevant to its case. Further, under Order XI Rule 12 CPC, a party may apply to the court for an order compelling the other side to disclose all documents in their possession relating to the matters in question.
(b) Under Order XIV Rule 4 CPC, the court may, if necessary for the proper framing of issues, compel the production of relevant documents. Although late production of documents is generally discouraged in the interest of procedural efficiency, such documents may still be accepted when their authenticity is not disputed—for example, in the case of genuine public records.
The underlying purpose of Order XIII Rules 1 and 2 CPC is to prevent procedural surprises, ensure transparency, exclude forged or fabricated documents, and expedite the trial process. Non-production of documents at the initial stage is not necessarily fatal to a party’s case, as the court retains discretion to permit production of documentary evidence at a later stage during the trial.
However, a party seeking to produce documents belatedly must satisfy the court that there exists “good cause” for such delayed production—such as the necessity of the document for an effective adjudication or the just determination of the issues in dispute.

case_info: Cr.A No. 22-D of 2025 Hasan Yar Barki  Vs  Haseeb Ullah Khan
remarks:
(Appeal under the Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Consumer Protection Act, 1997)
(a) Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Consumer Protection Act, 1997—Appellant was
convicted on the basis of plea of guilt—Held...There was no evidence or any other
proof to suggest that the appellant was selling High-Speed Diesel or was dealing in
excluded petroleum or non-dangerous petroleum.
(b) The Petroleum Act, 1934—S..17—The Director Consumer Protection Council or
his delegate is not a Testing Office, nor is Inspection Officer under Rule 54 of
Pakistan Oil (Refining, Blending, Transportation, Storage and Marketing) Rules,
2016, to get the sample and test it through any testing facility or laboratory. Director
Consumer Protection or his delegate also lacked the authority to enter and check the
storage of non-dangerous petroleum in the Oil Agency, particularly, when the
appellant was legally competent to store 2000 liters Class-B (non-dangerous)
petroleum as well as excluded petroleum without license.
(b) Pakistan Oil (Refining, Blending, Transportation, Storage and Marketing)
Rules, 2016—Rule 54—Only the Deputy Commissioner was having powers to get the
sample and test it through any testing facility or laboratory.
(c) OGRA Ordinance 2002 and Pakistan Oil (Refining, Blending,
Transportation, Storage and Marketing), Rules, 2016—Rule 54 recognizes the
Deputy Commissioner or any other person authorized in writing by the Authority, and
all members of the Authority to be the Inspection Officer. Such powers were not
conferred upon the Consumer Protection Council. The OGRA Ordinance and Rules
framed thereunder, being special enactment and latest law, shall prevail over the
provisions of the Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Consumer Protection Act, 1997.
(The consumer appeal was allowed in the circumstances).

case_info: W.P No. 45-M of 2025 Said Muhammad and others  Vs  Yar Muhammad and others
remarks:
Court has allowed a writ petition filed by Said Muhammad and others, setting aside the orders of the Additional Sessions Judge-II, Bajaur, and the Senior Civil Judge/Judicial Magistrate, Bajaur. The court directed the trial court to implead and hear the petitioners as respondents in proceedings under Section 145 of the Code of Criminal Procedure (Cr.P.C.). The case involves a dispute over possession of a property, and the trial court had dismissed the petitioners' application for impleadment.
The High Court held that the trial court erred in dismissing the petitioner`s application, as the statutory scheme and precedents favor inclusion and adjudication, not exclusion. The court emphasized the importance of notice to all interested parties, citing sub-sections (3) and (5) of Section 145 Cr.P.C. The object of Section 145 Cr.P.C. is to prevent breaches of peace by determining the factum of actual possession, independent of title.
The court noted that the petitioners have specifically pleaded that the proceedings have commenced behind their back, and they have produced partition deeds and assert that they had entrusted the disputed land to respondents No. 7-9 for protection. In these circumstances, the trial court ought to have permitted their joinder so the limited issue of actual possession could be tested. The High Court directed the trial court to implead and hear the petitioners, confining the inquiry to the factum of actual possession.

case_info: W.P No. 522-A of 2022 Mian Khan  Vs  The State and others
remarks:
(a) For the purpose of conducting an investigation under Section 174 of the Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898 (“the Code”), the police officer concerned is empowered, under Section 175 of the Code, to summon any person through a written order. This authority extends to requiring the attendance of any individual acquainted with the facts and circumstances of the case. Persons so summoned are legally bound
to answer all relevant questions put to them, except those answers which may tend to incriminate them in respect of any criminal offence.
However, where the case does not disclose the commission of a cognizable offence attracting the application of Section 170 of the Code, the persons summoned are not required to appear before a Court. Section 170 Cr.P.C. pertains to situations where, upon sufficient evidence being collected during investigation, the accused is forwarded to the Magistrate empowered to take cognizance of the offence on a police report.
In addition to the powers conferred upon the police under Section 174, the law further authorizes a Magistrate, under Section 176 of the Code, to conduct an independent inquiry into the cause of death. This inquiry by the Magistrate is supplemental to the investigation undertaken by the police. The Magistrate’s
jurisdiction extends to all cases where a person dies or disappears under suspicious circumstances, or where there is reason to believe that an offence has been committed. For this purpose, the Magistrate is required to record evidence and may, where necessary, direct a post-mortem examination to determine the true cause of death. Moreover, if the Magistrate considers it expedient in the interests of justice to examine a body that has already been interred, the law empowers the Magistrate to order the exhumation of the body for medical examination. This power is of great significance as it ensures that the actual cause of death may be ascertained, even after burial, whenever circumstances so demand.
(b) The scope of Section 174 of the Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, is narrow and specific. The provision primarily prescribes the procedure to be followed by a police officer upon receiving information regarding the unnatural or suspicious death of a person. When an unidentified or unnatural death is reported, the police officer is duty-bound to immediately inform the Magistrate empowered to hold an
inquest and to conduct a preliminary inquiry to ascertain the apparent cause of death.
Upon completion of this inquiry, the police officer must prepare an inquest report setting out his observations and findings, including the condition of the body, the nature and location of any injuries, and all relevant circumstances surrounding the death. The section further delineates the requirements for preparing the inquest report, which must clearly indicate the apparent cause of death.
It is crucial to emphasize that Section 174 Cr.P.C. does not empower the police to trace, identify, or apprehend any accused person. Its purpose is limited to determining, at a preliminary stage, the prima facie cause of death through a fact- finding exercise. The duty imposed upon the police officer under this provision is, therefore, a special statutory obligation that arises only in cases of unnatural or suspicious deaths. Accordingly, the application of Section 174 Cr.P.C. is confined to such cases where the police officer, upon receiving information, deems it necessary to hold an inquiry for the preparation of an inquest report, which is then submitted to the competent Magistrate for appropriate action under the law.
Thus, while limited in scope, Section 174 Cr.P.C. embodies the fundamental objective of criminal procedure—to ensure a fair, transparent, and credible preliminary investigation into deaths occurring under unnatural or suspicious circumstances, thereby enabling the Magistrate to proceed further in accordance
with law.

case_info: W.P No. 4827-P of 2025 Mst. Lal Pari etc  Vs  Federation of Pakistan etc.
remarks: Federal Government has devolved powers under section 19 of Citizenship Act, 1951 to Director General NADRA and therefore, application for cancellation of ACC or POR cards should now be moved to designated Service Delivery Centers of NADRA where it should be processed in accordance with directions given in the Judgment

case_info: C.R No. 221-M of 2024 Govt. of KP Vs  Rozimand Khan & others
remarks:
Court has dismissed a writ petition filed by the Government of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa, challenging the maintainability of a suit filed by private respondents regarding royalty rights in a protected forest. The private respondents are claiming ownership and royalty rights in certain Khasra numbers, which are partly notified as protected forest.
The government argued that the civil court's jurisdiction is barred under Section 92 of the Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Forest Ordinance, 2002. However, the court held that the bar of jurisdiction doesn`t apply, as the dispute is between private parties regarding royalty rights, not challenging the protected forest notification. The court distinguished the case from a previous apex court judgment, which dealt with challenges to protected forest notifications. The impugned judgments/orders of the lower courts were upheld, and the writ petition was dismissed.

case_info: W.P No. 1710-A of 2023 Mst. Shaheen Gohar  Vs  Muhammad Humayun & others
remarks:
(a) When an attorney or advocate commits fraud against a client, the only appropriate remedy available to the aggrieved person is to institute a suit against the attorney for damages or recovery of the amount defrauded. Other parties cannot be dragged into litigation merely because the advocate has allegedly committed fraud with someone.
(b) Once a judgment debtor has availed one legal remedy against an order striking out his defence, he cannot be permitted to pursue another, as doing so would amount to an abuse of the process of the court and lead to multiplicity of proceedings.
(c) It is a well-established principle of law that while examining allegations of fraud, misrepresentation, or lack of jurisdiction raised in an application under Section 12(2) of the Code of Civil Procedure (CPC), the court is not bound in every case to frame issues or record evidence in strict conformity with the procedure for suits. The matter rests on the court’s discretion, depending on the nature and seriousness of the allegations. The court may determine whether framing of issues and recording of evidence are necessary for just adjudication.
In the present case, the petitioner’s main contention was that summons were not served on their predecessor-in-interest due to an incorrect address. However, neither a copy of the plaint nor the agreement forming the basis of the specific performance suit has been placed on record to substantiate this claim. There exists a clear distinction between Order IX Rule 13 and Section 12(2) CPC. Under Order IX Rule 13, a defendant may seek to set aside an ex parte decree if the court is satisfied that the summons were not duly served or that he was prevented by sufficient cause from appearing when the suit was called for hearing. However, the rule further provides that an ex parte decree shall not be set aside merely for an irregularity in service of summons if the defendant had sufficient knowledge of the
proceedings to appear and defend.
Conversely, under Section 12(2) CPC, a person may challenge the validity of a judgment, decree, or order on grounds of fraud, misrepresentation, or want of jurisdiction by filing an application—rather than a separate suit—before the court that passed the impugned order.
The term “person” used in this section must be interpreted broadly to include any individual adversely affected by the decree, judgment, or order, whether or not such person was a party to the original proceedings.
(d) It is a settled principle of law that the doctrine of res judicata applies equally to applications. Therefore, where an earlier application under Section 12(2) CPC has been dismissed, a subsequent application on the same grounds would be barred by res judicata.

case_info: WP No. 5799-P of 2025 Rai Haider Ali Khan  Vs  Federation of Pakistan through Secty. Ministry of Parliamentary Affairs Govt. of Pakistan
remarks:
i. Whenever a lis/petition is presented to a Court of Law then it is its fundamental duty to first ascertain that as to whether it has the jurisdiction to entertain the same as the legal authority of a Court of Law to entertain and adjudicate upon a controversy is directly linked with the question of jurisdiction of that Court as a Court of Law possesses only that jurisdiction, which has specifically and clearly been conferred upon it by the Constitution/Law.

ii. As per the provisions of The Constitution as well as of The High Courts (Establishment) Order of 1970, one High Court cannot interfere in the territory domain of another High Court and each High Court of the country shall have powers and authority in respect of their territories, for which they are established, whereas any authority functioning at Islamabad in connection with the affairs of Federation are the subjects to the jurisdiction of Islamabad High Court.

iii. While determining its jurisdiction by a High Court in its Constitutional Jurisdiction under Article 199 of The Constitution, it has to see that as to whether any act/order has been made or any proceedings have been taken or as to whether any actionable cause of action wholly or partly has accrued to the petitioner within its territorial limits or as to whether the respondents are stationed/resident in its limits or not and in case of its affirmative answer on any of the above eventuality, the High Court could entertain a writ petition.

iv. A High Court of Province can also assume jurisdiction on the basis of concept of concurrent jurisdiction, if an authority at Islamabad has passed an order with respect to the affairs of Federation and when the said order has any effect in that Province and thus, in such state of affair, the Islamabad High Court or the High Court of the concerned Province shall have jurisdiction to entertain a petition.

v. A Court of Law cannot assume jurisdiction even with the consent and wishes of the parties if it otherwise lacks it.

vi. The Supreme Court of Pakistan has shown an immense displeasure over the conduct of parties who approaches various High Courts for redressal of their grievances without ascertaining the proper High Court for the same and thus it has directed to the High Courts of the country that while entertaining a petition under Article 199 of The Constitution of Islamic Republic of Pakistan, 1973, it has to see the dominant object of filing of the writ petition. It has also to see that if the ultimate relief sought relates to an act done or proceedings taken within the jurisdiction of a particular High Court, then no other High Court in the country could assume and exercise jurisdiction in such controversy.

case_info: Cr. Misc BA No. 374-D of 2025  Rizwan  Vs  The State & others
remarks:
(Bail Petition)
1. Accused/petitioners charged for the murder of a male and female on account of honour killing under Section 302/311 PPC. Legal heirs of both the deceased persons did not charge the petitioner nor they allowed the police to conduct their post-mortem. ---- held ----- Killing of a human being with the consent of his/her legal heirs, can neither be a license for murder nor aloof from the law and even extremely un- Islamic. The Holy Quran says that murdering a person is like murdering the whole of mankind. Islam does not permit parents to kill their child. There is no doubt that Islam grants the right of Qisas and Diyat, or the option to accept compensation for murder, to the legal heirs of a murdered person. However, this does not mean that legal heirs have the right to order the death of an innocent person under the guise of honour, or to forgive the killer.
2. The honour killings, associated with the concept of “Izzat” (honour), is also a form of Fasad-fil-Arz as defined under section ‘299ee’ and thereby this offence has been excluded from the Clause (c) of Section 302 PPC, and as referred above, according to Section 311 PPC the Court has a prerogative to award imprisonment for life to a person, being accused of honour killing even though, against whom the right of Qisas has been waived or compounded.
3. Killing anyone, regardless of their gender or perceived wrongdoing, is not permitted in the legal system of Pakistan. There should be no special treatment for a killer, even if they try to justify their actions as honour killings. Such acts are a clear indication of ignorance of Islamic principles, Quranic teachings, and the country’s laws. In Islam, honor killing is not a legitimate form of punishment for any offense. It is actually considered a major sin and goes against Islamic teachings. Taking the life of another human being in the name of honour has no justification, as all human life is sacred in Islam.
4. Although the post-mortem has not been conducted on the bodies of deceased male and female, but this cannot be a ground for the grant of bail, as in the case of honour killing where all relatives and loved ones of deceased person and lady are on the same page with accused persons. In such a situation it is the duty of State to ensure the safety of every citizen and uphold their constitutional rights qua Article 9 read with Article 8(1) of the Constitution of Islamic Republic of Pakistan, 1973.
(Bail was refused in the circumstances)

case_info: Cr.A No. 110-M of 2022 Nihar Ali  Vs  Zeeshan & another
remarks:
Court has dismissed the criminal appeal filed by Nihar Ali against the acquittal of Zeeshan, who was accused of stabbing Nihar Ali's brother, Uzair Ali. The court held that the prosecution`s case
was full of doubts and contradictions, including:
a) Lack of CDR proof to verify the complainant`s claim of being informed by mobile phone.
b) Contradictions in the statements of witnesses regarding eyewitnesses and hospital. accompaniment.
c) Delay in lodging the FIR despite the injured being present in the hospital.
d) No independent witnesses were associated with the recovery memo.
e) The confessional statement of the accused was not voluntary and was recorded in an utter state of fear and inducement.
f) The weapon of offence was not sent for expert analysis.
g) The doctor`s statement was not recorded, and the inquest report bore no official seal.
h) There existed a cross-version case, which weakened the prosecution`s case.

case_info: Cr.M.B.A No. 2314-P of 2025 Shahibzada Noor Ul Amin  Vs  The State and another
remarks:
i.	Where two or more accused are charged for an offence with an identical role, then, an order of acquittal of an accused in such case could not be considered as exclusive ground for the grant of bail, when such subsequent accused has also remained absconder for noticeable period and when the elements of an intentional and willful abscondence is also spelling out from the record, as releasing such subsequently arrested person on bail on the ground of acquittal of co-accused would amount to give him a premium of his abscondence.
ii.	There is no cavil that bail cannot be refused merely on the ground of abscondence, however, if on the available record there are other reasonable grounds to connect the accused person with the commission of an offence then an abscondence too could be considered as an additional ground for the refusal of bail.

case_info: Cr.A No. 165-A of 2024 Saif Ur Rehman  Vs  Federation of Pakistan
remarks: Section 11-EE of the Anti-Terrorism Act, 1997, makes it abundantly clear that certain prerequisites must be fulfilled before the name of a person can be included in the list prepared under the Fourth Schedule of the Act. These include the existence of concrete material and cogent reasons, establishing prima facie that the person is an activist, office- bearer, member of a proscribed organization, or linked with an organization suspected of involvement in terrorism or sectarianism. Furthermore, the material or information placed before the Government must be of such quality that it would satisfy a reasonable person that the entry in the Fourth Schedule is justified. Above all, the competent authority is required to establish the legality and validity of the order.

case_info: Cr.M.B.A No. 2460-P of 2025 Jamal Shah  Vs  Mst Qais Begum and another
remarks:
i.	When a case is one of cross version, one lodged by one party and other by another party and when the time of occurrence, the place of occurrence and the parties are the same and when it is not spelling out from the record that as to who was the aggressor and who was aggressed upon, then such like cases are covered under the concept of further inquiry and benefit of it is to be extended to both set of accused.
ii.	When once a Court of law has extended benefit of a case being of cross version to one set of accused then such benefit cannot be refused to another set of accused.
iii.	When once it is established that the case is one of cross version and it is not spelling out from the record that as to who was the aggressor and who was aggressed upon, then the number of casualties on either side as well as the role of an individual in such occurrence become irrelevant.

case_info: Cr.A No. 362-A of 2023 Tahir Mehmood  Vs  The State
remarks:
(a) There is no apparent motive or rationale to suggest any false implication or substitution of the real offenders. It is wholly against reason and the prudence of an ordinary mind to believe that the complainant would exonerate or omit the actual culprits responsible for the brutal murder of his father, uncle, and brother, along with causing injuries to himself and his surviving brother, and instead falsely implicate innocent persons without any cogent basis or prior enmity. In cases involving multiple fatalities
within a family, the natural course of human conduct is to seek justice by identifying and naming the actual assailants. Hence, the possibility of substitution of accused persons is effectively excluded, and the credibility of the prosecution’s case is further reinforced by the facts and circumstances, particularly the immediate and consistent reporting of the incident.
(b) The medical evidence, including the postmortem reports of the deceased and the medico-legal certificates of the injured witnesses, fully corroborates the prosecution’s case. It is in complete harmony with the established motive and lends strong support to the unimpeachable ocular account furnished by the eyewitnesses. The consistency between the medical findings and the ocular testimony rules out any possibility of fabrication or false implication. Such corroboration not only strengthens the veracity of the prosecution’s version but also provides independent assurance regarding the manner of occurrence and
the role attributed to the accused. Therefore, the medical evidence, when read in conjunction with the trustworthy ocular account, constitutes sufficient material to sustain the conviction of the accused beyond reasonable doubt.
(c) There is no justification to disbelieve or discard the testimony of injured eyewitnesses merely on account of their relationship with the deceased, particularly when their presence at the scene of occurrence is conclusively established by the injuries sustained by them and further corroborated by medical and forensic evidence. Even otherwise, It is a settled principle of law that the testimony of an injured witness is considered to be of higher probative value, as such a witness would not ordinarily let the real culprits go unpunished and falsely implicate innocent persons, especially when they themselves have suffered harm in the same incident.
(d) An unproved or unverified Call Data Record (CDR) carries no evidentiary value and cannot be relied upon to discredit the prosecution’s case. In the absence of ownership certificates establishing a clear nexus between the SIM numbers and the accused or other relevant individuals, CDRs alone cannot be treated as conclusive or reliable evidence of a person’s presence or absence at a particular location. Mere production of CDRs, without proper verification of SIM ownership or examination of the deponents of supporting affidavits, lacks probative force and is inadmissible in law. Consequently, such omissions render the objection raised by the appellants meritless and insufficient to dislodge the presumption of truth attached to the prosecution’s case, which stands fortified by ocular, medical, and forensic evidence.

case_info: Cr. M. (BA) No. 379-D of 2025 Sial Muhammad alias Siyal  Vs  The State etc
remarks:
(Criminal Misc: Bail Petition)
(a) Criminal Procedure Code, 1898.—bail grant of—medical ground—According to report of the Standing Medical Board, the accused/petitioner was suffering from Multi Drug Resistant TB and Hepatitis-C. Both the diseases were epidemic which could affect health of other patients/prisoners in jail hospital, while there was no facility of isolation of the petitioner in the jail premises.
(b) The International precedents are binding on the State(s) which are signatory to the Conventions/Treaties in relation to which International Judicial Forums deliver decisions. These precedents can also be taken as guideline even by the non-signatory States.
(c) Under Article 3 of the European Convention on Human Rights, the State, must ensure that a person is detained in conditions which are compatible with respect for his human dignity, that the manner and method of the execution of the measure do not subject him to distress or hardship of an intensity exceeding the unavoidable level of suffering inherent in detention and that, given the practical demands of imprisonment, his health and well-being are adequately secured by, among other things, providing him with requisite medical assistance.
(Petition was allowed and the accused/petitioner was granted bail in the circumstances)

case_info: C.R No. 106-D of 2025 Muhammad Nazir  Vs  Amir Muhammad
remarks:
(Civil Revision under Section 115 of the Code of Civil Procedure)
1. Recovery suit under order XXXVII CPC--- conditional leave granted to defendant and thereafter he failed to appear before the court and also did not obey the condition, hence, placed ex-parte. Afterwards,
during recording of plaintiff’s evidence, defendant filed application seeking permission to cross-examine the plaintiff’s witnesses, which was dismissed by the trial court. --- Held ---- If the defendant fails to
obtain leave to appear and defend or failed to fulfil the condition, it will be treated that he has no defense and that situation will have to be equated with a situation where the averments in the plaint are deemed
to have been admitted in view the terminology used in Rule 2 of Order 37 CPC “the allegations in the plaint shall be deemed to be admitted, and the plaintiff shall be entitled to a decree”. Hence, the case is no longer open to the defendant as cross examining a witness amounts to raising defenses.
2. Notwithstanding the word shall used in Rule 2 of Order 37 CPC, the court may proceed to record evidence of plaintiff, after the leave to defend the suit is refused to defendant or he failed to fulfil the
condition, in order to ascertain the genuineness of the negotiable instrument. There is no restraint in the said rule that the Court should not proceed with the trial for recording evidence of plaintiff. Fixing
the case for plaintiff’s evidence, instead of outright decree, does not make the trial redundant.
3. All laws are mandatory in the sense they impose the duty to obey on those who come within its purview, but it does not follow that every departure from such law shall taint the proceedings with a fatal
blemish. In the Rule 2(2), the absence of provision for consequence in case of non-compliance with the procedure would indicate its directory nature despite use of word \"shall\" as, the word “shall” is not
determinative of the fact whether this proviso is mandatory.
(The revision petition filed by defendant was dismissed.)

case_info: C.R No. 293-A of 2012 Mst. Khursheed & others  Vs  Mst. Chan Bibi and others
remarks:
(a) In order to understand the concept of declaratory relief as envisaged under Section 42 of the Specific Relief Act, it is necessary to determine whether a person seeking declaration can file a suit for a declaration which may appear negative circumstantially but is positive by implication of its outcome. Such relief is directly associated with the legal right, character, or status of the person seeking declaration from the competent court of law. Section 42 of the Specific Relief Act must necessarily be referred to for a clear understanding of the concept and intent of the legislature. The phraseology of Section 42 does not indicate that a negative form of declaration cannot be sought. At times, a negative declaration is in substance the assertion of a positive nullification in terms of the relief claimed by a person regarding his legal right, character, or status. Such a composite form of declaration can legitimately be sought under Section 42 of the Specific Relief Act. This court also held that where in plaint, relief sought for is in negative form of declaration its refusal is not justified, as there is no absolute bar contained anywhere in law.
(b) To obtain relief of declaration, the plaintiff must establish :
•	That, at the time of filing the suit, he is entitled to a legal character or a legal right to property.
•	That the defendant has denied, or is interested in denying, the plaintiff’s legal character or title.
•	That the declaration sought pertains to the plaintiff’s entitlement to legal character or a right to property.
•	That the plaintiff is not in a position to claim further relief beyond a bare declaration of his title.
•	That the legal character or right must be real and recognized by law; no suit involving abstract, imaginary, or hypothetical rights is competent under Section 42.
•	That the relief of declaration must relate to the plaintiff’s own rights or entitlements, and not merely to the rights of another person.
Thus, keeping the above parameters in view, it is consistent with the mandate of Section 42 of the Specific Relief Act that a negative declaration may be maintainable. However, the appropriateness of such relief varies from case to case. In suitable circumstances, courts are not barred from issuing negative declarations, as the essential concern of the court is with legal character, right, or status—the form of the declaration is immaterial.

case_info: C.R No. 40-A of 2025 Saleem Ullah  Vs  Mst. Bibi Jana and others
remarks:
(a) The term “formal defect” has not been defined anywhere in the Code of Civil Procedure (C.P.C.). Therefore, recourse must be taken to its ordinary meaning as found in legal dictionaries. According to Black’s Law Dictionary (5th Edition, p. 376), a formal defect is described as:
