“The want or absence of some legal requisite; a deficiency, imperfection, or insufficiency. It refers to the lack of something necessary for completeness or perfection—an absence of an element essential to the proper use or purpose for which a thing is intended.” From the above meaning of the word defect, it can conveniently be understood, with reference to a suit framed under the C.P.C., that if the plaint is not arranged in accordance with Order VI, Rules 1 to 4, 14, 15 or Order VII, Rules 1 to 7, C.P.C., it would be deemed to suffer from a defect. However, such defect alone is not sufficient to justify withdrawal of the suit unless it is shown that, by reason of a formal defect, the suit is likely to fail. This distinction implies that the defect must be apparent rather than latent. An apparent defect is visible on the face of the record, such as procedural irregularities in the form or arrangement of the plaint, and does not require detailed inquiry. In contrast, a substantial or latent defect goes to the merits of the case and can only be discovered after recording evidence. Therefore, permission under the C.P.C. can only be granted to withdraw a suit in order to cure an apparent or formal defect which, if left unaddressed, may lead to its failure. Withdrawal cannot be allowed where the defect is latent and affects the substantive rights or merits of the case.
(b) It is also settled law that in case of inconsistency of the judgment and decree between the appellate Court and the trial Court, the High Court shall see that as findings of which of the Court are based on proper appraisal of evidence and as such in this case after analyzing and evaluating the evidence on file, the findings recorded by the appellate Court are correct and are the result of proper appreciation of evidence.

case_info: W.P No. 599-M of 2025 Waheed Ullah  Vs  Sher Afzal & others
remarks:
High Court has dismissed a writ petition filed by Waheed Ullah and others, upholding the jurisdiction of the Rent Controller to entertain proceedings for ejectment of tenants from rented shops situated in Degai Market, Sakhakot, Tehsil Dargai. The court held that the Rent Controller has jurisdiction to entertain proceedings under the West Pakistan Urban Rent Restriction Ordinance, 1959, as the area where the shops are situated is administered by a municipal body and declared as urban.
The court relied on the definition of “municipality” and held that any area placed under the control of a local government body performing municipal functions qualifies as a municipality. The court noted that the suit property falls within the administrative control of Tehsil Municipal Administration (TMA) Dargai, and municipal services are being delivered in that area. A notification issued by the Provincial Government, declaring the area where the shops are situated as an urban area, further supported the court`s decision.
The court also upheld the revisional court`s dismissal of the petition on the ground of maintainability, as the Ordinance contains no provision for revision and the Code of Civil Procedure is inapplicable to rent proceedings. As a result, the Rent Controller can proceed with the ejectment petition filed by the landlord, and the court directed the Rent Controller to decide the ejectment petition in light of the directions previously issued by the court.

case_info: C.R No. 258-M of 2025 Behrawar Khan  Vs  Govt of KPK
remarks:
The petitioner, Behrawar Khan, claimed that he and others donated land for a government school with the understanding that they would receive jobs in return. However, the government failed to fulfill this commitment and instead built a boundary wall around the land. The petitioner filed a suit seeking compensation for the land.
The court observed that the government cannot acquire land without compensation, citing Article 24 of the Constitution, which protects property rights. The court held that the government must initiate acquisition proceedings and pay compensation to the landowners. The court also directed the government to conduct a survey and measurement of the acquired land to determine the
compensation amount.
The court`s decision emphasizes the importance of protecting property rights and ensuring fair compensation for land acquisition. The government is now required to initiate acquisition proceedings and pay compensation to the landowners in accordance with the law. The court`s judgment provides relief to the petitioner and other landowners who were denied compensation for their land.
In its judgment, the court also referenced a letter from the District Education Officer, which mentioned that the land was acquired from the petitioner and others, with a total measurement of 600 Gaams. The court directed the government to consider this letter as evidence of the land acquisition and to proceed accordingly.

case_info: Q.P No. 40-M of 2024 Muhammad Qamar Ud Din  Vs  ZIa Ud Din
remarks:
Court has delivered a judgment in a case involving a dispute over land possession between two parties. The dispute dates back to 2020 when the respondents filed an application with the SHO, seeking legal action against the petitioners for allegedly raising a wall on the disputed land. The Magistrate passed a preliminary order attaching the disputed property and directing both parties to file written statements.
The court observed that Section 145 of the CrPC empowers the Magistrate to restore lawful possession to a party that has been unlawfully dispossessed. The Magistrate`s role under Section 145 does not end with the initial restoration of possession but includes ensuring that the restored possession remains intact
until displaced through lawful process.
The petitioners argued that the Magistrate became functus officio after restoring possession to the respondents on April 8, 2022, and lacked jurisdiction to entertain a subsequent application for restoration of possession. However, the court held that the Magistrate's duty under Section 145(6) is to protect the possession of the party declared entitled, and the petitioner`s; claim of ownership or superior right to possession can be adjudicated by a civil court.
The court allowed the petition, set aside the impugned orders, and remanded the matter to the Executive Magistrate to afford the petitioners a fair opportunity of hearing and decide the case strictly in accordance with law. The Magistrate is directed to conclude the proceedings within two months.

case_info: C.R No. 69-A of 2010 Qazil Abdul Malik  Vs  Qazi Atta Ur Rehman
remarks:
(a) The phrase “better management” in relation to agricultural land exchange cannot be confined to rigid rules, but certain guiding purposes may be identified. An exchange may be justified where it enables more effective control and use of the land for agricultural activities; brings land within a common or sole irrigation system with improved water channelization; prevents harmful effects of surplus or unwanted water discharge from adjoining fields; consolidates scattered parcels into a compact block, allowing uniform irrigation and cropping; provides access to a path or road connecting the farm with markets; or safeguards standing crops from damage by animals or other external factors. These considerations are not exhaustive and may vary depending on local conditions, the parties involved, and the nature of the land, but the overriding objective must remain the achievement of better agricultural results through the exchange.
(b) The foremost step to be taken in order to bring home a suit for pre-emption is the demand of Talab-e-Muwathibat, which according to Section 13 of the Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Pre-emption Act, 1987, means immediate demand by the pre-emptor in the sitting or Majlis in which he/she has come to know of the sale by declaring his/her intention to exercise the right of pre-emption. The word ‘immediate’ used in the Section ibid signifies greater importance in so far as a slight delay in its performance might entail greater loss by crumbling down the whole exercise to the ground.
(c) The plaintiffs failed to mention the names of the requisite witnesses in the plaint. The right of pre-emption is considered a weak right, as it seeks to divest another who has lawfully acquired property for valuable consideration. Accordingly, the statutory requirements of talbs must be strictly complied with, and any deviation, however minor, defeats the claim. In this case, the omission is therefore fatal to the plaintiffs’ claim of pre-emption.

case_info: Cr.A No. 99-M of 2025 Ijaz Ali  Vs  The State & others
remarks: Court partially allowed the criminal appeal of Ijaz Ali, who was convicted for his role in a murder and assault. While the court upheld his conviction for the armed assault and injuries caused to two women, Mst. Shama Bibi and Mst. Shamim Bibi, it set aside his life sentence for murder. The court found that the prosecution failed to prove he shared a \"common intention\" with his absconding co-accused, Naseer, who was solely responsible for the fatal shooting of Bakhti Rahman. The testimonies of the injured eyewitnesses were deemed credible and consistent, establishing Ijaz Ali`s presence and his role in the non- fatal attacks. Consequently, his sentences for assault under various sections of the PPC remain intact and will run concurrently, but his life imprisonment for murder was overturned. The connected criminal revision filed by the complainant for sentence enhancement was dismissed as infructuous

case_info: W.P No. 428-M of 2025 Amjid Ali  Vs  Mst. Hina
remarks: Court has dismissed Amjad Ali's writ petition challenging the judgment and decree of the Additional District Judge, which partially allowed his wife's appeal. The court ruled that Amjad Ali`s wife is entitled to dowry articles or their market value of Rs. 400,000, as they are deemed her property regardless of who purchased them. She is also entitled to a monthly maintenance allowance of Rs. 10,000 from May 2023 until she remains his wife, stopping if she shifts to his house. Additionally, Amjad Ali is granted restitution of conjugal rights, provided he offers a separate house with facilities matching his financial status. The court did not find Amjad Ali guilty of cruelty, so marriage dissolution was not granted. Furthermore, his second marriage during the suit entitled his first wife to maintenance.

case_info: C.R No. 225-M of 2022 Govt. of KP through Secty. C& W Dept. etc   Vs Umar Ali etc
remarks:
i. A plaint can only be rejected under Order VII Rule 11 C.P.C, if it does not disclose any cause of action on its face, and as such defect must be discernible solely from plain reading of the plaint itself, without reference to the defence taken by the defendants.

ii. The distinction between “no cause of action” and “non-disclosure of cause of action” is not merely semantic but jurisdictionally decisive. The former is a matter of defence to be adjudicated at trial whereas the latter, if present on the face of the plaint, alone warrants rejection.
 
iii. The invocation Order VII Rule 11 C.P.C must be exercised with judicial circumspection and only in clear cut cases where the plaint is manifestly devoid of legal foundation. Premature termination of proceedings at the threshold stage, absent such manifest defect, not only offends the principles of natural justice but also undermines the plaintiff’s vested right to have claim adjudicated upon its merits through due process of law.

case_info: Review Petition No. 07-B of 2025 Mst. Sultan Zarina and others  Vs  Govt of KP and others
remarks: Review --- Scope of--- Section 114 read with Order XLVII, Rule 1 , C.P.C --- Review lies only where an error is apparent on face of record or discovery of new and important matter not within knowledge despite due diligence --- Dissatisfaction with judgment or attempt to re-argue decided points not ground for review--- Regularization of contractual employees --- No vested right accrues unless appointment made pursuant to advertisement and prescribed recruitment procedure. --- Conditional recommendation without sanctioned post creates no enforceable right --- length of service alone does not confer entitlement --- petition dismissed in limine.

case_info: Cr. MBA No. 2065-P of 2025 Khaista Akbar  Vs  State
remarks:
Apart from this fact the complainant has been shown at point No. 2, while accused/petitioner has been shown at point No. 4 with the distance in between 2 and 4 is 244 paces, so, the identification from such a distance is a question which makes the case of prosecution is of further probe. \"K. Krishnamurthi in his book [Police Powers & Duties/ has observed, which is as under

\"The time, light and opportunity, that the witness had, the range, distance and view from which the witness saw the accused during the occurrence to see whether the identification was genuine. Dealing with the normal identifying capacity of persons, Dr. Hans Gross in his celebrated work \"Criminal Investigation\" (page 123) states: \"Presuming the eyesight to be normal and the light good, one is able in broad day light to recognize

(a) Persons whom one knows very well at a distance of from 50 to 90 yards; when there particular and very characteristic signs 110 yards; in exceptional cases up to 165 yards

(b) Persons one does not know very well and has not often seen from 28 to 33 yards

(c) People one has seen only once 16 yards.

case_info: Cr. MBA No. 2231-P of 2025 Khalid  Vs  The State
remarks:
From the perusal of the record it reveal that Junaid was Juvenile & Saeed was given the role of abetment. The role of the present accused/ petitioner is different from others accused who have been granted bail by this Hon\'ble Court, in Cr. Mise.BA No. 1328-P/2025 titled Junaid & other Vs State..

Perusal of FIR reveals that respondent No. 2 Mst. Nargis, mother of deceased Hussain herself deposed that,

\" It was accused Khalid son of Javed r/o Sheikh Abad Rajjar who had invited the deceased to his village Sheikh Abad, where his dead body was bound.

case_info: RFA No. 01-B of 2021 Rizwan Ullah Shah  Vs The Collector/DC, Karak
remarks:
Regular First Appeal.
Land Acquisition Act, 1894 --- Compensation --- Appointment of local commission --- Mandatory compliance with Section 31 and 34 of Land Acquisition Act, 1894 (as amended in Khyber Pakhtunkhwa) --- Failure of Referee court to secure deposit of compensation --- compound interest under section 34 --- Appeal under Section 54 of Land acquisition Act, 1894 r/w Section 96 CPC against dismissal of reference for enhancement of compensation --- Held, Referee court erred in failing to appoint local commission under order XXVI Rule 9 CPC and section 75 CPC to ascertain true market value of acquired land; such omission deprived court of essential aid for fair determination of compensation --- Deposit of awarded compensation in Referee court is mandatory under section 31; non-compliance prejudices landowners and delays investment under sections 32-33 --- Under Khyber Pakhtunkhwa amendments to section 28 and 34, Collector is bound to deposit excess compensation as determined by the court, interest at 6% remains compensatory in nature and payable until actual deposit, as reaffirmed in Nawabzada Abdul Qadir Khan (2023 SCMR 950), Nasreen Zohra (2022 SCMR 890) and NHA vs Mazhar Siddique (2023 SCMR 493) --- Findings of Referee Court set aside; matter remanded to District Judge Karak for fresh decision on merits after appointment of local commission. Appeal allowed, case remanded.

case_info: W. P. No. 1361-A of 2024 Muhammad Afzal  Vs  NAB through Chairman etc
remarks: Purchase of property already frozen u/s 12 of the NAB Ordinance, 1999, would be void u/s 23 of the Ordinance, 1999, and create no title in favour of a third party. No question of issuance of a notice of caution in respect of such transaction is necessary. The question of lack of knowledge pleaded by such third party, being factual in nature, could not be resolved in constitutional jurisdiction. Such transaction would not offend Articles 23 and 24 of the Constitution.

case_info: CR. No. 768-P of 2025 University of Peshawar through Controller of examination  Vs  Mst. Iqra & 03 others
remarks:
Summary Disposal of suit

Consequences of interim order if the suit was dismissed as withdrawn.

The concept of summary disposal of suits within the framework of civil litigation is encapsulated in Order XV-A of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908. This provision authorizes the Court to dispose of a suit at the first hearing, provided the parties are not in dispute over any material question of law or fact, or if the defence is found to be frivolous, vexatious, or illusory. The issue before this Court is whether the suit of the plaintiffs/respondents can be disposed of under the provisions of Order XV-A, or whether the case necessitates the recording of evidence. Since the petitioners have unequivocally contested the allegations in the plaint, manifests that the parties are at issue, thereby excluding the applicability of Order XV, Rule 1, or Order XV-A, Rule 1, CPC. Furthermore, the plaintiffs\' continued eligibility to sit for examinations and attend classes in subsequent semesters was contingent upon the outcome of Suit No. 345/1, which was not decided in their favour. Additionally, the plaintiffs have neither challenged the relevant regulations nor demonstrated any legal basis entitling them to continue their studies, particularly considering their failure to achieve the requisite GPA in their first semester. Furthermore, the plaintiffs have failed to provide any legal or factual basis to support their claim for the determination of their suit within the framework of Section 42 of the Specific Relief Act, 1877. The law mandates that where the lis does not demonstrate a violation of law, rules, or regulations, and fails to disclose a cause of action, it is the duty of the courts to prevent any further progress in the matter. Reference may be made to the case of Misree Khan versus Abdul Ghafoor (PLD 2025 SC 24), Ahmed Ali Talpur versus Sub Registrar, Latifabad Hyderabad (PLD 2025 SC 325).

case_info: WP No. 1974-P of 2006 Muhammad Nazeer (Deceased) through LRs   Vs  Master Naveed etc
remarks:
Application for ejectment of tenant.
It is a settled proposition of law that an application for ejectment is maintainable only at the instance of a person who qualifies either as the landlord of the tenant ie, within the broad definition of landlord, which encompasses entitlement to seek ejectment on the basis of the landlord-tenant relationship, or as the owner of the property in question. Where the applicant fails to establish himself as the landlord, and equally cannot prove his ownership of the premises, the application is not maintainable. Conversely, if the right of ejectment is asserted based on ownership, the applicant must first have his title adjudicated and established before the court of competent jurisdiction, namely the civil court. Only upon such adjudication and proof of ownership can his claim for ejectment be entertained under the rent laws. It is further well-settled that to qualify as landlord, ownership of the property is not a sine qua non; what is required is entitlement, for the time being, to receive rent from the tenant. In the instant case, even such entitlement could not be established.

case_info: Cr. Misc BA No.319-B of 2025 Mati Ullah  Vs  Amin Ullah, etc
remarks:
Bail Petition
Criminal Procedure—Bail—Further inquiry—Offences under Sections 352, 337-A(ii), and 336 PPC—Held, Section 352 PPC is bailable; other offences do not fall within the prohibitory clause of Section 497 Cr.P.C.—Section 336 PPC prescribes punishment with imprisonment upto 10 years as alternate, when, after consultation with doctor the punishment of Qisas is not executable—Section 337N (2) PPC lays down that in all cases of hurt under Chapter XVI of the Pakistan Penal Code, 1860, the Court may, in addition to Arsh, impose a sentence of Ta’zir only when the accused is a previous convict, or is found to be a habitual, hardened, desperate, or dangerous criminal, or when the offence has been committed in the name or on the pretext of honour—No material on record suggesting petitioner fits any such category—Occurrence stemmed from a sudden local dispute involving a rooster; injury caused with back of a sickle and not repeated—Possibility of sentence under Ta’zir to be determined at trial—Case required further inquiry under Section 497(2) Cr.P.C.—Bail granted.
(Petition allowed)

case_info: WP No. 392-M of 2023 Mst. Nasreen   Vs  Gohar Ali etc
remarks: In family matters, issue of recovery of the dowry articles, can be decided in favor of a wife on the basis of her oral evidence as per the list of dowry articles. Even if she has failed to produce purchase receipts of the dowry articles.

case_info: B.A No. 421-M of 2025 Aurangzeb  Vs  State & another
remarks:
Bail application of Aurangzeb, who is accused of murdering his father and causing firearm injury to his sister-in-law. The petitioner has been in custody for over two years, and the trial has not been concluded despite a previous direction from the court to do so within five months.
The court has dismissed the bail application, citing that the petitioner is a hardened, desperate, or dangerous criminal, and the offence is grave and serious. The court has held that the gravity of the offence and the petitioner`s character make him ineligible for bail. The petitioner`s case falls within the prohibitory limb of Section 497(1) Cr.P.C, which makes it challenging to grant bail.
The court has emphasized the importance of expeditious trial and has directed the trial court to conclude the trial within three months. The court`s decision is based on the facts and circumstances of the case, including the nature of the offence and the petitioner`s alleged role in it.

case_info: R.F.A No. 156-M of 2025 Habib Ullah  Vs  Aman Ullah Khan
remarks:
•	The objection raised by a judgment debtor that he has made payment of the decretal amount outside the Court, without documentary proof as required under Order XXI Rule 1 C.P.C., cannot be entertained merely on the basis of his oral assertion.
•	In a money decree passed on the basis of a compromise/consent of the parties, wherein the judgment-debtor has specifically undertaken to make payment within a specified period, the provisions of Order XX Rule 11 C.P.C. cannot be invoked by the judgment debtor after defaulting on his obligation without any justified reason. He cannot seek indulgence contrary to the spirit and mandate of the compromise.
•	An order passed during execution proceedings u/s 47 C.P.C. does not constitute a decree as defined u/s 2(2) C.P.C., and as such is not appealable u/s 96 C.P.C.; rather, an appeal u/s 104 C.P.C. would be maintainable against such an order.

case_info: Cr. R No. 145-P of 2025 Feroz Khan    Vs  The State
remarks:
A witness hostile, there is no specific provision in the Qanun-e-Shahadat Order, 1984, however, a provision has been provided in Article 150, under which, a party calling the witness, may question his/her own witness.
Now, the question is whether such a witness can be declared hostile, once his cross-examination has been conducted by the opposite party. The Qanun-e-Shahadat Order is also silent on this score, however, as per practice, the stage to declare the witness hostile, is during his examination- in-chief where it can be alleged that the witness is deviating from his previous statement or is suppressing the truth or is otherwise un-cooperative. In this respect, the superior court has settled the issue that if a witness in examination-in-chief makes a statement adverse to the interest of the party calling him as witness, the court can exercise the discretion under Article 150 of the Qanun-e-Shahadat Order, 1984, however, once the cross-examination is recorded, there is no concept of declaring a witness as hostile.
At the final stage of adjudication, the trial court is required to evaluate the evidence in its totality by appreciating the cumulative effect of the evidence led during the trial.

case_info: Review Petition No. 13-M of 2025 in W.P No. 222-M of 2023 Fazal Hanan and another Vs  Government of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa through Secretary E&S Education and others
remarks: The jurisdictional contours governing the scope of a review petition are well-settled and have been constantly delineated by the superior Courts. A review is not an appeal in disguise, nor does it afford a party a second opportunity to re-argue a matter already adjudicated on merits. The review jurisdiction, as encapsulated u/s 114 read with order XLVII Rule 1 of Civil Procedure Code, 1908, is exercisable only upon the discovery of a new and important matter or evidence which, despite the exercise of due diligence, was not within the knowledge of or could not be produced by applicant at the time the decree or order was passed, or upon demonstration of a manifest error apparent on the face of record. It does not extend to reappreciation of facts or reconsidered of legal grounds already examined. The Courts have repeatedly emphasized that even an erroneous decision, if rendered after due adjudication, does not ipso facto furnish a ground for review unless it leads to miscarriage of justice that is manifest and self- evident from the record itself. Thus, the power of review is to be invoked with judicial circumspection and strict adherence to the settled legal parameters, lest it undermine the finality and sanctity of judicial pronouncement.

case_info: W.P No. 848-A of 2020 Muhammad Zaifat  Vs  Tariq Nawaz Khan & others
remarks:
(a) The primary issue is whether the petitioner qualifies as a \"landlord\" under section 2(c) of the Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Urban Rent Restriction Ordinance, 1959. The statutory definition of \"landlord\" is broad, encompassing not only those who actually receive rent but also those entitled to receive it, even if rent has never been collected. This includes owners, co-owners, attorneys, estate managers, trustees, and other persons lawfully vested with such entitlement.
(b) It is a well-settled principle of law that when a landlord, or a duly authorized attorney acting on the landlord’s behalf, requires the rented premises for bona fide personal use—whether for residence or for business—the tenant is under a legal obligation to vacate and hand over possession. A tenant’s refusal to comply renders him liable to ejectment under the applicable rent laws.
(c) Courts are bound to decide cases on their merits and should not allow technicalities to frustrate substantive rights.
(d) It is equally a settled rule of tenancy law that oral tenancies, unless expressly agreed otherwise, are deemed to be monthly tenancies.
(e) It is further well established that an attorney, or a subsequent purchaser who lawfully acquires ownership of the premises, is fully competent to institute ejectment proceedings on the ground of bona fide personal requirement.

case_info: C.R No. 125-P of 2024 Mst. Islam Bibi and others  Vs  Ali Ahmad and others
remarks:
Held:
Limitation Act, 1908
Application for setting aside ex parte decree — Period of limitation. 
The Limitation Act, 1908 being a special statute exclusively governs limitation for suits, appeals, and applications. An application for setting aside an ex parte decree is governed by the specific provision prescribing limitation of thirty days, and not by the residuary Article providing a longer period. 
Limitation Act, 1908 - Art. 164
Specific application – Applicability
An application seeking setting aside of an ex parte decree is a specific application falling squarely within the ambit of Article 164 of the Limitation Act, 1908.Recourse to Article 181 (residuary provision) is not permissible where a specific Article squarely covers the field. 
Limitation - Accrual of vested right
Upon expiry of the prescribed period of limitation, a valuable and vested right accrues in favour of the opposite party, which cannot be taken away lightly. Limitation is not a mere technicality but a substantive law creating rights and obligations. 
Appellate Court - Error of law
Holding that limitation for filing an application to set aside an ex parte decree is three years, contrary to the express provisions of the Limitation Act, 1908, constitutes a gross error of law warranting interference

case_info: W.P No. 821-M of 2018 Niamat Ali Khan  Vs  Provincial Housing Authority of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa through its Director General and others
remarks:
•	A higher qualification in the relevant field does not disqualify a candidate from applying for a position that requires a lesser qualification, denying such a candidate eligibility solely on the ground of being ‘over qualified’ would be arbitrary to law and discriminatory.
•	As Regulation No. 19(f)(ii) of the Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Public Service Commission Regulations, 2017 clearly prescribes the required qualifications, therefore, no legislative gap known as casus omissus, exists to warrant judicial intervention.

case_info: Cr.A No. 211-M of 2024 Akbar Khan  Vs  The State and another
remarks:
•	Without first-hand knowledge, hearsay evidence, without independent corroboration, cannot be made basis for the conviction of the accused.
•	Medical evidence affirming the unnatural death of the deceased alone is not sufficient to convict the accused unless it is supported by confidence-inspiring direct or strong circumstantial evidence.
•	Once an accused is acquitted by a competent Court after a full-fledged trial, the presumption of innocence is no longer theoretical, it becomes a judicially recognized and fortified presumption.

case_info: Cr.A No. 26-B of 2025 Habib Ullah Shah  Vs  The State etc
remarks:
Criminal Appeal—Under Sections 302/324/34 P.P.C —Qatl-e-Amd—Ocular account—Solitary
witness—Presence of eyewitness doubtful—Unexplained delay in FIR—Conflict between ocular version
and medical evidence—Contradictory statements—Motive not established—Unwitnessed
occurrence—Benefit of doubt.
Appellant convicted by trial Court under Sections 302(b)/324 PPC. Prosecution case rested solely on the
complainant (PW-10) as eyewitness. Held, conviction on solitary testimony requires exceptional caution;
prosecution failed to establish presence of eyewitness at relevant time—material contradictions between
FIR, testimony of PW-10 and PW-6 (identifier), and documentary evidence including post-mortem timings,
inquest report sequence, and transportation of the deceased. Unexplained delay of 1 hour 15 minutes in
reporting the incident, despite short distance and availability of transport, indicated deliberation. Ocular
account inconsistent with medical evidence: firearm entry wound on back contradicting assertion that firing
was frontal; trajectory of bullet (downward to upward) inconsistent with alleged positions. Motive weak,
improbable, and directed more towards complainant than deceased. Collection of eight empties did not
corroborate PW-10’s presence. Overall circumstances created serious doubt as to witnessing of
occurrence—reliance placed on Mst. Saddan Bibi v. Muhammad Amir (2005 SCMR 1128); Muhammad Ali
v. The State (2015 SCMR 137); Ali Sher v. The State (2008 SCMR 707). Prosecution failed to prove case
beyond reasonable doubt. Appeal allowed; conviction and sentence set aside; appellant acquitted and
released. Connected criminal revision petitions for enhancement dismissed as infructuous.

case_info: Cr.R No. 51-B of 2025 Muhammad Sudais  Vs  The State etc
remarks:
Petitioner being involved in a criminal case vide F.I.R No.27 dated 22.01.2024 u/s 302/34 P.P.C, Police Station, Latamber, District Karak is on bail as is evident from the order sheet dated 11.06.2025, and he has submitted an application for his exemption through counsel from court appearance on the ground of serious threat to his life and security while attending court proceedings on each and every date, but the request of petitioner did not find favour with the learned trial court, and turned down the same vide the impugned order dated 30.06.2025 mainly on the ground that the petitioner did not attach or refer to any F.I.R, police complaint, or protective request in order to substantiate the alleged threats and that he had to be physically present in the court proceedings.
Even section 353 Cr.P.C, empowers the Court of Sessions to grant exemption even in a single accused case, hence, section 353 Cr.P.C is reproduced as under:-
353. Evidence to be taken in presence of accused. Except as otherwise expressly provided, all evidence taken under1 [Chapters XX, XXI, XXII and XXIIA] shall be taken in the presence of the accused, or, when his personal attendance is dispensed with, in presence of his pleader.
The record reveals that charge against the petitioner has been framed on 18.05.2024 and 04 PWs have also been examined in the case till date. Since there is a serious threat to the life of petitioner, thus, his non-appearance should have been condoned by granting him exemption from his personal appearance particularly when he is represented by his counsel before the court, after obtaining a valid Wakalatnama furnished in his favour.
Thus, what to think of exemption of the accused, even charge can be framed in absence of the accused through his counsel, therefore, in view of the above, the instant petition is accepted and the impugned order dated 30.06.2025 rendered by the learned trial court is hereby set-aside, the desired exemption is granted to the petitioner from his personal appearance before the court as prayed for while petitioner will be represented by his duly appointed counsel.
[Cr.R allowed, and the order of lower fora declining exemption from personal appearance to accused is set-aside]

case_info: Cr. MBA No. 317-B of 2025 Muhammad Saleem  Vs  The State
remarks:
Held: Petitioner was not named in the F.I.R and was implicated later on the basis of statement of
a witness not cited as eye-witness; said statement was recorded with unexplained delay of 07
days---Petitioner had allegedly threatened juvenile accused Adnan with death to compel him to
commit the murder, however, Adnan did not confess to the crime and police investigation did
not yield any incriminating evidence connecting petitioner with the offence---CDR alone,
without concrete corroborative evidence, was held insufficient to establish guilt---No recovery
effected from petitioner--- Case against petitioner called for further inquiry-Bail granted.

case_info: B.A No. 1887-P of 2025 Mina Gul  Vs  The state
remarks:
The issue of videography of the recovery has been discussed.
This Court is fully alive to the evolving contours of evidentiary standards in criminal jurisprudence
particularly with regard to the use of videographic recordings in cases involving the alleged recovery of
narcotic substances or other contraband items. It is trite law that videographic evidence, when duly
authenticated and shown to be free from tampering or manipulation can constitute a vital component of
the evidentiary matrix. In this regard, reference may appropriately be made to the ‘Silent Witness’
doctrine, a recognized evidentiary principle, which permits the admissibility of video footage,
photographic evidence or other digital recordings without the necessity of oral testimony from a
sponsoring witness provided that the integrity, chain of custody and genuineness of the recording can be
established through reliable technical and forensic means.
This Court is also mindful of certain recent judgments wherein the issue of disputed videographic
evidence in the form of memory cards or USB drives was considered at the bail stage. Specifically, in
Cr.M(BA)No.1745-P/2025 decided on 13.06.2025 and Cr.M(BA)No.1828-P/2025, decided on 25.06.2025,
this Court having considered the submissions of the learned counsel for the accused-petitioners in view of the peculiar facts and circumstances therein, directed that the relevant memory cards and USB drives,
alleged to contain videographic evidence of the recovery process be requisitioned and played in open
court. But, certainly, it cannot be construed a precedent to be followed in each and every case. It is a
settled principle of criminal jurisprudence that each bail application must be determined on the basis of
its own peculiar facts and circumstances. The adjudication of bail is a judicial function not to be
undertaken mechanically but through the careful application of the judicial mind to the factual matrix of
the case at hand. No rigid formula exists for the grant or refusal of bail. The ultimate determination must
rest on the merits of each individual case evaluated in its own context through a comprehensive and
contextual analysis of the record. Moreover, there is a distinction between cases where videography is
available and those where it is not. In cases without videography, the matters shall be decided on its own
merits in accordance with the peculiar facts and circumstances, and, where videography is available, the
cases shall also be decided on its own merits. Anyhow, Courts must exercise their discretion judiciously
and with vigilance ensuring that miscarriage of justice is avoided, whether by granting bail inappropriately
in serious cases or by unjustifiably denying bail where it is legally warranted.
However, after receiving valuable assistance from the learned counsel representing the parties and
particularly the learned Amicus Curiae, this Court has arrived to an irresistible conclusion that
notwithstanding the potential evidentiary significance of videographic recordings in recovery proceedings, such recordings may only be treated as corroborative or circumstantial evidence to be duly assessed during the course of trial. The playing or presentation of such videographic material at the bail stage is not permissible and certainly falls outside the scope of the limited judicial assessment undertaken
in bail proceedings. It is a settled proposition in criminal jurisprudence that at the bail stage, the Court
must refrain from embarking upon a detailed or microscopic evaluation of the evidence. Accordingly, this
Court is of the considered view that requests for the presentation or playing of videographic material in
open court during the bail stage are legally untenable. Court while seizing of the bail matter, at best, may
take into consideration the forensic report issued by a laboratory duly designated and accredited for such
purposes solely for the purpose of forming a tentative view regarding the authenticity, veracity and
genuineness of the disputed videographic material. Such consideration, however, must be confined to
assessing whether the forensic findings prima facie support or otherwise the claim of tampering,
fabrication or manipulation of the recording. It bears emphasis that a conclusive determination regarding
the admissibility and evidentiary value of such videographic evidence can only be made by the learned
trial Court during the course of the trial. As such, it is the bounden duty of all seizing officers and
investigating officers to ensure that where videographic recordings are made of the recovery proceedings, the same are duly subjected to forensic examination by a competent authority and the reports thereof shall be placed on record verifying the authenticity, continuity and integrity of the videographic material. The submission of such forensic reports forms an essential part of the investigative process and serves to safeguard the evidentiary sanctity of digital recordings.
Anyhow, on a tentative assessment, the material available on record clearly reflects that the
accused-petitioner was apprehended red-handed at the spot and a substantial quantity of narcotic
substance viz. 5000 grams of ‘Heroin’ was recovered from the vehicle which he was found driving at the
relevant time, in respect of which, the FSL report is also in the affirmative. Apart from that, keeping in
view the nature and quantity of the narcotics involved, it appears highly improbable that such huge
quantity or a large amount of contraband could have been falsely planted against the accused-petitioner,
that too, when he did not plausibly dispute his presence on the spot nor could show that he was not in
conscious knowledge of the concealment of the contraband in the vehicle. Being so, this court does not
feel persuaded to grant him the concession of bail as he being prima facie linked with the commission of
the offence. Moreover, individuals engaged in the trafficking, transportation and distribution of narcotic
substances are not merely violating statutory provisions but are, in fact, perpetrating heinous crimes
against society at large. Such activities have devastating consequences, particularly for the youth, the
most vulnerable and impressionable segment of the population, who are often the primary targets of
drug proliferation and abuse. In view of the grave and far-reaching consequences of such offences, this
Court is of the firm view that cases of narcotics require careful examination and appreciation at all stages.
Copy of this order be endorsed to the worthy Inspector General of Police, Khyber Pakhtunkhwa,
the worthy CCPO, Peshawar, all the worthy Regional Police Officers as well as all the District Police
Officers of their respective Regions and Districts shall ensure the strict compliance of the ibid
directions, as specifically highlighted in the later part of Para-9.
For the reasons discussed above, this bail petition being without substance is dismissed.

case_info: Cr.MBA No. 246-B of 2025 Kifayat Ullah Khan  Vs  Waheed ullah etc
remarks:
Bail Petition.
Code of Criminal Procedures, 1898--- Bail--- Statutory Ground--- Obstruction of trial---
Misconduct by accused before trial court--- Petitioner sought post-arrest bail on statutory ground
of delay, having remained in custody for more than two years --- Held, delay in conclusion of
trial was not solely attributable to prosecution; petitioner repeatedly obstructed trial by failing to
produce counsel, misbehaving with and threatening the Presiding Officer, and instructing
defense counsel not to participate in proceedings; petitioner also sought stay of trial on pretext of
complaint against trial judge, resulting in case transfer; such conduct showed deliberate attempts
to delay trial --- Petitioner disentitled to statutory relief under section 497 Cr.P.C.; petition
dismissed --- Trial court directed to proceed on day-to-day basis, ensure cross-examination of
remaining witness without fail, and appoint state funded counsel if defense counsel again fails to
appear --- Petitioner to be given one last opportunity to appoint counsel, failing which, Court
may proceed with the case and decide the matter relying on the existing/available evidence on
record.
(Bail refused)

case_info: W.P No.1272-P of 2022 Arbab Arshad  Vs  National Bank of Pakistan
remarks:
1.	(A) 	The core issue for determination before this Court is whether the punishment awarded i.e., dismissal from service is legally sustainable and proportionate to the misconduct, particularly in view of the allegations regarding unauthorized absence and the issuance of dishonoured cheques.
2.	(B) 	It is a settled principle of service jurisprudence that punishment must be commensurate with the gravity of the misconduct established. While unauthorized absence and financial irregularities may warrant disciplinary action, it is incumbent upon the competent authority to ensure that:
i.	The charges are clearly and properly framed;
ii.	The accused is afforded a meaningful and effective opportunity to defend himself;
iii.	The punishment imposed is not excessive, arbitrary, or disproportionate to the misconduct proved.
3.	(C)	While this Court does not overlook the petitioner\'s admitted prolonged absence from duty, the central concern lies in the quantum of punishment awarded. In the present case, the penalty of dismissal from service appears grossly disproportionate to the nature and gravity of the alleged misconduct, thereby offending the well established doctrine of proportionality.
4.	The role of the judiciary in reviewing disciplinary actions is to ensure that civil servants are not subjected to excessive or arbitrary penalties. Judicial review serves to protect procedural fairness and uphold the rule of law, without unduly encroaching upon executive discretion. A key principle m this review is proportionality, the punishment must bear a rational nexus to the offense committed and must not be unduly harsh. Courts also apply the principle of reasonableness, which requires that disciplinary decisions be based on cogent and objective evidence, and not on conjecture, arbitrariness, or extraneous considerations. The Court is not to act as an appellate authority over factual determinations made by competent disciplinary bodies. Interference is warranted only m cases where illegality, procedural impropriety, mala fide intent, or manifest unreasonableness is established.

case_info: Cr.A No. 130-A of 2022 Mian Gul  Vs  The State
remarks:
(a) The mere mention of the number of shots fired or the specific types of weapons used during cross-examination, when elicited through questions posed by the defense, does not amount to a material improvement or contradiction. These clarifications were drawn out by the defense itself and were not volunteered in conflict with earlier statements. It is a well-settled principle that not every elaboration made in court constitutes a contradiction or an improvement, particularly when such details are furnished in response to targeted cross-examination. Moreover, it is not necessary for the First Information Report (FIR) to contain exhaustive particulars such as the exact number of shots fired or the precise weapons used; the FIR is not intended to serve as an encyclopedia of the prosecution’s case but merely to set the criminal law in motion. Such variations in minor details, such as the exact duration of the firing, the number of shots fired, or who fired at whom, are not unusual and cannot be deemed fatal to the prosecution`s case. These are natural discrepancies that are expected due to the passage of time, trauma of the incident, and normal lapse of human memory. It is important to note that the witnesses were examined before the trial court after a considerable delay of approximately one and a half years from the date of the occurrence. In such circumstances, it would be unrealistic to expect the witnesses to recall every minute detail of the incident with mathematical precision.
(b) In the absence of any move by the State, the appellate court was empowered under the law only to accept or reject the appeal, or to reduce the sentence imposed by the learned trial court. The appellate court was clearly not vested with suo motu authority or jurisdiction to enhance the sentence in an appeal filed by the convict/appellant, particularly without issuing prior notice and affording a fair opportunity of hearing.

case_info: W.P No. 1109-M of 2024 Said Fareen  Vs  The State
remarks:
•	When materials collected during the course of investigation do not sufficiently connect the accused with the commission of the alleged offence then the accused can be discharged, however, such discharge does not deprive the complainant of his remedy rather section 200 Cr.P.C. affords him a viable recourse through the filing of a private complaint.
•	In the absence of any compelling evidence of bias, mala fide, procedural irregularity, or miscarriage of justice, mere dissatisfaction of the petitioner does not justify the transfer of investigation from one jurisdiction to another.
•	The primary procedural safeguards available to an accused during investigation are set out in section 161 Cr.P.C. governing statements to the police, and section 342 Cr.P.C., empowering the Court to examine the accused during trial. This framework, consistently upheld by the superior judiciary, ensures fair process. The accused/ petitioner failed to record his statement u/s 161 Cr.P.C., indicating non-cooperation with the investigation, nevertheless, at trial, he retains the right u/s 340(2) Cr.P.C. to produce defence evidence, including witnesses and his own sworn statement; thus, he is not without remedy.

case_info: Cr.A No. 69-M of 2024 Aqal Zeb  Vs  The State& others
remarks:
Aqal Zeb`s appeal against his conviction and sentence for murdering Noor Muhammad has been dismissed by this Court. The court upheld the trial court`s decision, citing overwhelming evidence against Aqal Zeb, including a dying declaration, eyewitness account, and circumstantial evidence.
The prosecution`s case relied on Noor Muhammad`s dying declaration, which implicated Aqal Zeb as the perpetrator. This was corroborated by Farman Ali, Noor Muhammad`s cousin, who testified against Aqal Zeb. Additionally, Aqal Zeb was arrested with a .30- bore pistol, and the FSL report matched the crime empty recovered from the spot.
The court rejected Aqal Zeb`s arguments, including the claim that the single shot fired was a mitigating circumstance. It emphasized that targeting a vital area like the chest demonstrates lethal intent. As a result, Aqal Zeb`s death sentence, along with a compensation of Rs. 1,000,000 to the deceased`s legal heirs, has been upheld. He was also convicted under Section 15 AA and sentenced to three years RI with a fine of Rs. 20,000.

case_info: W.P No. 91-M of 2025 Sheraz Sikander  Vs  SHO, PS Mingora & others
remarks: Court has directed the District Police Officer, Swat, to issue a Police Clearance Certificate to Sheraz Sikandar, the petitioner, without mentioning any adverse remarks related to FIR No. 1078 dated 26.10.2022. This decision was made after the petitioner was acquitted of the charges in the case, based on a compromise between the parties.

case_info: WP No. 6063-P of 2024 Abdul Sattar Khan Adv  Vs  Govt of Kp etc
remarks:
i. This Court would have considered the request of the petitioner favourable, had it been permissible under the law. However, we are constrained to apply the settled principle of law, namely the Doctrine of Election, which carries both significance and purpose. Any deviation therefrom would not only undermine its underlying rationale but would also amount to circumventing established legal norms.

ii. The Doctrine of Election is not a novel proposition. It is a time-honored principle, deeply embedded in the jurisprudence of English Common Law, and draws its strength from the maxims of equity, estoppel, and judicial efficiency. Historically, equity courts discouraged litigants from engaging in \"forum shopping\" a practice whereby a party attempts to try its luck before multiple forums in pursuit of a favourable decision. The evolution of the doctrine was primarily to curb such abuse of process, to prevent conflicting decisions, and to uphold the sanctity and dignity of judicial forums.

iii. Here, the petitioner himself approached the Pakistan Bar Council, a forum competent under the law to redress the grievance in question, and his appeal is admittedly sub judice before the said authority. Once the petitioner elected to pursue his remedy before the Pakistan Bar Council for the same relief, the maintainability of a subsequent application under the Right to Information Act, 2013, followed by the instant constitutional petition under Article 199, becomes highly questionable.

case_info: W.P No.306-B of 2025 Shah Nawaz Khan (lte) through his legal heirs Aziz-Ur-Rehman  Vs  Gul Khand (Late) through his legal heirs Akber.
remarks:
A pivotal point raised in the arguments is how Suit No. 70/1 was decreed on the
very date of its institution. The record reflects that the suit was filed and disposed of
through a compromise decree on 20.02.1970. In this regard, Order V Rule 1 of the C.P.C.
is explicit: issuance of summons is not mandatory if the defendant appears at the time of
filing and admits the plaintiff’s claim. Hence, the decree passed on the same date is
legally permissible under the said provision.
The petitioners did not deny that their father passed away in 2022, shortly before
filing the application under Section 12(2) C.P.C. Therefore, it raises the question as to
why he did not challenge the decrees during his lifetime. Petitioners lack locus-standi to
assail the decrees unchallenged by their deceased father himself during his life span.
Now, so far as the contention of learned counsel for petitioner that the trial court
has dismissed the application without framing of issues and recording of evidence,
suffice it to say that in every application filed U/S 12(2) of the Code of Civil Procedure,
1908 it is not requirement of law for the court to frame issue and record evidence unless
it appears that the application does require recording of evidence.

case_info: W.P No. 107-P of 2025 Naeem Asghar & another  Vs  Provincial Govt.& others
remarks:
The term maladministration, in its broader legal context, refers to inefficient, unjust, or improper administration. It encompasses conduct such as bias, neglect, inattention, delay, incompetence, arbitrariness, or the violation of legal or procedural requirements by a public authority or official in the discharge of their official duties. It includes any act or omission that falls short of the standards of fair, lawful, and transparent governance.
Admittedly, a dispute concerning the title and possession of the land in question was/is sub judice before a court of competent jurisdiction. During the pendency of those proceedings, and prior to the issuance of the impugned direction by the Ombudsman, an interim order was passed. Relying upon that judicial restraint, the Executive Engineer declined the application for Tarmeem Warbandi. In this factual and legal context, it is manifest that the said action of the Executive Engineer cannot, by any stretch of legal reasoning, be termed as maladministration. The order passed was neither perverse, arbitrary nor unreasonable. It did not reflect any unjust, biased, oppressive, or discriminatory intent.
As to the question of jurisdiction, whether the Ombudsman can direct an authority functioning under the Canal and Drainage Act, 1873, to process a case of Warbandi, it is pertinent to examine the scope and nature of such authority. In this regard, Section 69 of the Act merits consideration. It provides that any officer empowered under the Act to conduct an inquiry may exercise all such powers relating to the summoning and examination of witnesses as are conferred upon civil courts under the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 (Act V of 1908). Furthermore, it stipulates that every such inquiry shall be deemed a judicial proceeding. A plain reading of Section 69 clearly demonstrates that proceedings conducted under the Canal and Drainage Act, 1873, by the designated authority are to be treated as judicial in nature, and the authority itself performs functions akin to those of a court.
Given that the authority acting under the Canal and Drainage Act, 1873, exercises powers of a judicial nature, it falls outside the definition of an \"Agency\" within the meaning of Section 2(a). Accordingly, the Ombudsman lacks jurisdiction to issue directions to such an authority, as doing so would amount to interference with a judicial process, an area explicitly excluded from the Ombudsman\'s mandate.
Therefore, the objection regarding maintainability of the petition on the basis of Section 31 of the Act is misconceived. Once it is established that the Ombudsman acted outside the scope of his jurisdiction under Section 9, the existence of an alternate remedy under the Order does not preclude this Court from exerc1smg its constitutional powers.

case_info: RFA No. 270-P of 2023 Sohrab Khan  Vs  Muhammad Tayyab
remarks:
The controversy arising in the present appeal hinges upon two material questions: firstly, whether the appellant/defendant had extended an offer to the respondent/plaintiff to take oath in support of his claim, and secondly, whether the appellant/defendant, after having made such an offer and upon the matter being decided in consequence thereof, could subsequently resile from the same.

* It appears from the record that a wakalatnama was filed on behalf of the appellant in favour of Mr. Sher Muhammad, Advocate, before the learned trial Court on 19.06.2023. The record further reveals that the application for leave to defend had earlier been submitted by the appellant himself on 08.06.2023. Today, the appellant has appeared in person before this Court, and upon being questioned regarding his offer, the recording of his statement, and the affixation of his thumb impression thereon, he candidly admitted all these facts. The suit, therefore, was decided in accordance with the mode voluntarily adopted by the appellant, which squarely attracts the doctrine of election.

* Moreover, it is not the case of the appellant that no such offer was made to the plaintiff for decision of the suit on oath, or that he himself was absent from the Court on the relevant date. On the contrary, the facts pertaining to the offer extended by the appellant, its acceptance by the plaintiff, the recording of their statements, and the consequential decision of the trial Court, are admitted and undisputed. These proceedings, to which a presumption of truth is attached, cannot now be questioned or disowned either by the plaintiff or by the appellant.

* In addition, proceedings undertaken under the provisions of the Oath Act, 1877, are to be conducted strictly in accordance with the terms mutually agreed upon by the parties. Once it stands established on the record that the parties consented to certain terms and conditions for the adjudication of the lis, neither party can thereafter be permitted to approbate and reprobate, having already taken a decisive step because of their mutual agreement.

case_info: Cr.A No. 78-A of 2025 Doctor Faqir Nawaz  Vs  Aurangzeb etc
remarks:
(a) A plain reading of Section 489-F, P.P.C. makes it evident that the provision does not apply to every case of dishonour of cheque. To constitute the offence under this section, the following essential ingredients must be established:
(i) the cheque was duly issued;
(ii) it was issued with dishonest intent;
(iii) it was issued in discharge of a loan or fulfillment of an obligation; and
(iv) it was dishonoured on presentation.
The absence of even a single element takes the case outside the ambit of Section 489-F, P.P.C.
(b) Section 84(1) of the Negotiable Instruments Act, 1881 provides that a cheque must be presented for payment within a reasonable time. Under Section 84(2), what constitutes “reasonable time” is to be determined with reference to the nature of the instrument, commercial usage, banking practice, and the facts of each case. Generally, a cheque presented beyond six months from its date is treated as a stale cheque.
Section 138 of the Indian Negotiable Instruments Act, 1881, though distinct from Section 489-F, P.P.C., is relevant for comparison. It recognizes the concept of a stale cheque and stipulates that a cheque must be valid at the time of presentation. Clause (a) of Section 138 requires that a cheque be presented to the drawee bank within nine months from its date or within its validity period, whichever is earlier, for valid initiation of criminal proceedings.
In line with this principle, under banking regulations, no bank is obliged to honour a stale cheque. Consequently, a cheque presented after the validity period is liable to be returned unpaid, even where no other defect exists.

case_info: W.P No. 97-M of 2024 Dr. Waqar Un Nisa Faizi  Vs  Government of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa through Chief Secretary & others
remarks: Selection Board or Selection Committee constituted for the purpose of recruitment for a certain post has the prerogative at its credit to select a candidate it thinks fit and suitable or reject whom it considers unsuitable and unsatisfactory. The purpose of the constitution of a board or committee and its intent is to search for the best amongst the contenders and when a board or committee consider none amongst the candidates to be the one it is searching for, then cancelling the whole process in the best interest of an institution is the vested authority and powers conferred on it which cannot be questioned before a court of law. The members of the board / committee at the relevant time are the best judges for a person before them during the process of interview and the court is not presumed to interfere in it.

case_info: C.R. No. 689-P of 2011 Asadullah etc  Vs  Abdul Ghafoor Khan
remarks:
The competent forum was never approached for incorporation of the alleged gift deed into the revenue record. Neither the particulars nor a copy of the said gift deed has been placed on file, which prevents this Court from ascertaining the precise amendment being sought
No order of any competent authority or Court existed in favour of Umrah Khan to validate the incorporation of the entry in the revenue record which is questioned by the petitioner
The reduction of the share of petitioners\' possession from 3 Kanals 7 Marlas to 1 Kanal 7 Marla has also been effected without sanction of any competent authority or decree of a Court of law.

case_info: Cr.A No. 89-M of 2024 Muhammad Zaman  Vs  State and another
remarks:
The testimony of a witness related to the deceased cannot be brushed aside merely on the ground of relationship if his presence on the spot at the relevant time is otherwise established by the prosecution through strong, reliable and convincing evidence.
Judicial confession of an accused if found to be in consonance with other evidence on the record especially the ocular account and the defence fails to establish that it was obtained through persuasion, promise, torture, coercion or undue influence, can be treated as a true and voluntary confession.
The purpose of lodging an FIR is simply to set the law into motion, therefore, any fact not mentioned therein but subsequently proved by the prosecution during trial through confidence-inspiring evidence cannot be termed as a dishonest improvement

case_info: W.P No.120-M of 2025 Abdul Azeem  Vs  District Magistrate/Deputy Commissioner & others
remarks:
Court, has set aside an order by the District Magistrate/Deputy Commissioner of Khar Bajaur to detain Abdul Azeem for one month under Section 3 of the West Pakistan Maintenance of Public Order Ordinance, 1959 (MPO).
The District Magistrate ordered Abdul Azeem`s detention for allegedly disrupting the peaceful atmosphere by blocking a public pathway in village Toor Mian Kalay. Abdul Azeem argued the pathway isn`t for general public use, and a civil suit (No. 131/1 of 2023) regarding the pathway is pending in a civil court.
The Court found insufficient grounds for detention under Section 3 of MPO. The court emphasized that detention requires objective satisfaction of the executive based on credible evidence.
No justifiable or specific allegations were disclosed against Abdul Azeem for detention under MPO.
The court cited constitutional guarantees of liberty and the need for sufficient material to curtail a person`s liberty.

case_info: W.P No.4423-P of 2025 Pakistan Muslim Leaque (N) through its President, Khyber Pakhtunkhwa  Vs  The Election Commission of Pakistan through its Secretary and others
remarks:
To sum up and conclude the scheme governing the election and allocation of reserved seats to political parties that have secured general seats in the National or Provincial Assemblies, it is evident that such reserved seats are allocated to political parties in proportion to the general seats won, following the declaration of final results by ECP under Section 98 of the Act of 2017. Furthermore, in view of the proviso to Article 106 of the Constitution and sub-rule (6) of Rule 92 of the Rules of 2017, any independent candidate who has won a general seat and whose name has been notified as such under Section 98, but joins a political party within three days of such notification, shall be counted towards the credit of that political party for the purpose of allocation of reserved seats.
If the assertion of the ECP regarding the imposition of a cut-off date of 22.02.2024 is accepted, despite the absence of any legal or rational foundation, it would not only be inconsistent with the statutory and constitutional scheme governing the allocation of reserved seats for women and non-Muslims, but would also render nugatory the express mandate of the proviso to Article 106 of the Constitution and sub-rule (6) of Rule 92 of the Rules of 2017. It is by now well-established that the Constitution is a living and evolving document; its provisions must be interpreted dynamically and purposively so as to give effect to the constitutional intent. An interpretation that renders any part of the Constitution otiose or redundant cannot be presumed to have been the intention of its framers.Aam Log Itehad and another vs. The Election Commission of Pakistan and others (PLD 2022 SC 39) and Suo Motu Case No.8 of 2018 and Civil Misc. Application No.649-L of 2018 (PLD 2019 SC 201).
The only legally relevant date for determining a party\'s strength for the purpose of allocation of reserved seats is the date on which such allocation or election actually takes place. Therefore, any independent candidate who has joined a political party within the prescribed time prior to that date must be counted towards the total number of general seats held by that party. A cut-off date fixed earlier than this point of reference not only lacks legal foundation, butalso disrupts the proportional representation framework laid down in the Constitution and the Act of 2017. Such administrative action by the ECP is, therefore, contrary to the law and cannot be sustained.

case_info: B.A No. 371-M of 2025 Aftab-ud-Din  Vs  The State & others
remarks: Court granted bail to Aftab-ud-Din in case FIR No. 31 dated 20.02.2022. The complainant, Shahab-ud-Din, reported that unknown accused fired upon his sister-in-law (Mst. Naureen Bibi) and brother (Aftab-ud-Din), with Aftab-ud-Din having had a love marriage with Mst. Naureen. Aftab-ud-Din was arrested on 07.3.2022 and has been in jail for over 3 years and 7 months. He was initially convicted but appealed, leading to a de-novo trial. Bail was granted due to detention beyond the 2-year period for trial conclusion as per section 497 Cr.P.C., no delay attributed to the petitioner, and Aftab-ud-Din not being a hardened criminal. Aftab-ud-Din will be released on bail with bonds of Rs. 100,000 and two sureties of the same amount, with sureties being local, reliable, and men of means.

case_info: W.P No. 1634-A of 2023 Mst. Maqbool Illahi  Vs  Mst. Khaiber un Nisa & other
remarks:
(a) It is a well-settled principle of law that in matters relating to the custody of a minor, the paramount consideration is always the welfare of the child. A mother is naturally endowed with love and affection for her children, whether male or female, and this affection has no substitute. Particularly in the case of a daughter, the mother’s company and guidance play a vital role in preparing her to shoulder future responsibilities. For this reason, Muhammadan Law recognizes the mother’s preferential right of custody of infant children, as reflected in Para 352 of Mulla’s Principles of Muhammadan Law.
It is true that a mother ordinarily loses this preferential right upon contracting a second marriage with a person not related to the child within the prohibited degree, thereby making him a stranger to the child. In such circumstances, the right of custody (Hizanat) generally vests in the father. However, this is not an absolute rule. The courts in Pakistan have consistently held that departure from this principle is permissible where exceptional circumstances justify it. Ultimately, the controlling factor is not the technical right of either parent but the welfare of the minor. Thus, even in cases of the mother’s remarriage, if the court finds that the welfare of the child still lies in her custody, preference may rightly be given to the mother.
(b) Where a subordinate court commits an error of law in deciding a matter, such error assumes the character of a jurisdictional defect, for a court is vested only with the authority to decide a case in accordance with law. A decision rendered contrary to law is therefore liable to be quashed under the constitutional jurisdiction, being an act in excess of lawful authority, as contemplated by Article 4 of the Constitution. In such circumstances, the case becomes proper for interference by the High Court in exercise of its constitutional jurisdiction under Article 199.

case_info: W.P No. 04-A of 2018 Shakeel Ahmad and another  Vs  Mst. Noshaba & others
remarks:
(a) It is a well-settled principle of law that where a legal heir, directly aggrieved by a wrongfully recorded inheritance mutation, fails to assail it during his or her lifetime, such prolonged inaction operates to bar the subsequent legal heirs from questioning the mutation. This bar rests on the doctrines of estoppel, abandonment of claim, and extinguishment of the cause of action.
(b) Where a suit is ex facie barred by limitation, the recording of evidence is unnecessary, and the Court is fully empowered to reject the plaint at the very threshold and when the primary relief of declaration is time-barred, then any consequential relief irrespective of whether it may appear to be within the prescribed limitation would be of no legal consequence because time-barred declaratory claims cannot be revived through the guise of consequential relief.
(c) The object of the powers conferred upon the trial Court under Order VII, Rule 11, C.P.C. is that the Courts must put an end to the litigation at the very initial stage when on account of some legal impediments full-fledged trial will be a futile exercise.
(d) The plaintiffs contend that each subsequent entry in the record of rights (Jamabandi) provided them with a fresh cause of action, thereby keeping their suit within limitation. While in certain cases a wrongful mutation or adverse entry may amount only to an apprehended denial of rights—thus giving rise to a fresh cause of action—the position differs where the affected parties had actual knowledge of the disputed entries but failed to challenge them during their lifetime. Once there is an unequivocal denial of rights and the limitation period expires without recourse to legal action, the claim becomes barred.
Subsequent repetition of the same adverse entry in later revenue records does not revive or extend the limitation period. Settled law holds that limitation runs from the date of knowledge, and once extinguished, the right cannot be reopened through later entries.

case_info: W.P No. 835-M of 2024 Sajjad Khan and others  Vs  Government of KPK
remarks:
The Court allowed writ petitions filed by landowners in Swat, setting aside the impugned orders that dismissed their objections to the land acquisition for the Swat Expressway Phase-II project. The court ruled that the petitioner`s objections cannot be dismissed solely on the grounds of limitation, as they were not served with notices under Section 12(2) of the Land Acquisition Act, 1894. The court emphasized the importance of fair compensation, citing Article 24 of the Constitution, which mandates that no property shall be compulsorily acquired or taken possession of save for a public purpose and with compensation.
The court noted that other landowner`s objections were entertained and sent to the Referee Judge for decision on merit, and therefore, the petitioner`s objections should also be considered similarly to avoid conflicting decisions and promote consistency and fairness. The court`s decision enables the petitioners to have their objections considered and decided on merit. If the compensation amount is enhanced in pending references, the petitioners will also be entitled to the same enhanced compensation. The petitioners will have the opportunity to participate in the process of computing due compensation, ensuring their rights are protected under Article 10-A of the Constitution.

case_info: W.P No. 1700-P of 2022 Hashim Khan through LRs  Vs  Saad Ullah Khan through LRs
remarks:
Section 24 & 25 of KHYBER PAKHTUNKHWA PRE-EMPTION ACT 1987.
• The Pre-emption Act, 1987 prescribes two distinct timelines. Under Section 24(1), deposit of one-third of the pre-emption amount is mandatory and non-compliance entails dismissal of the suit. However, the present controversy pertains to Section 25 of the Act, which, as interpreted by the Hon\'ble Supreme Court, is directory in nature. There is nothing on record to suggest that the pre-emptor was informed of the exact amount payable under the decree at the time of pronouncement of judgment. The specific amount of Rs. 25,80,446.35 became known to the decree-holder only upon receipt of the attested copies on 09.12.2019, pursuant to application No. 8282 dated 31.10.2019. In the case of Rahamdal Khan v. Ihsan Ullah & others (2022 CLC 2106), after considering various precedents including 2013 MLD 360, 1999 SCMR 342, 1991 SCMR 2149, 1986 SCMR 849, PLD 2006 SC 140, and 2005 SCMR 1664, this Court held that trial courts ordinarily do not issue a short written order at the time of pronouncement that specifies the exact preemption amount and related directions. Consequently, the Registrar of this Court was directed to circulate a copy of the said judgment among the District Judges for further dissemination to Civil Judges of the province.
• While the decree was announced on 31.10.2019, attested copies were provided only on
09.12.2019. In the absence of a written short order specifying the amount and conditions, the pre-emptor could not reasonably be expected to comply with the direction prior to receiving the attested copies. Learned counsel for the petitioner rightly contended that no short order was issued by the trial court. Therefore, in the absence of any evidence to the contrary, the trial court appropriately allowed the application for extension. Though section 25 of the Act does provide the period of 30 days but without any penal consequence for deposit of the remaining amount and has consistently been held to be directory by the Superior Courts.

case_info: WP. No. 4256-P of 2025 Syed Bahadar Shah  Vs  Syed Aamir Shah etc
remarks:
(a) Under the mandate of Order VII Rule 11, a plaint can be rejected only if it does not disclose a cause of action. The concepts of disclosing a cause of action and having a cause of action are distinct. A plaint may be rejected when it fails to disclose any cause of action, whereas the question of having a cause of action necessarily involves an inference that can only be drawn after recording evidence.

(b) The absence of a cause of action in fact is different from a plaint that discloses no cause of action on its face. Whether the plaintiff actually has a cause of action is a matter that can only be determined on the basis of evidence. Rejection under Order VII Rule 11, C.P.C., is permissible only when the plaint fails to disclose a cause of action.

case_info: R.F.A No. 254-P of 2019 Shah Hussain  Vs  Shafiq Ur Rehman
remarks:
i)	The party to a suit cannot be allowed to lead evidence beyond its pleading and if led the same could not be considered in its favor, as it is settled that a party has to alleged a facts) and then it has to be allowed to prove the same.
ii)	The production of a document, proof of execution of documents and proof of contents of a document are distinct phenomenon and a beneficiary of a document could only be succeeded to get a favorable decree, if he succeeded to prove all of them.
iii)	There is no cavil that u/s 118 of The Negotiable instrument Act, 1881, a negotiable instrument has a statutory presumptions to the effect that the same has been made/ drawn for a valid consideration, however, such presumptions is rebuttable and if the defendants succeed to establish that no negotiable instrument was drawn or that there exist no considerations for issuance of the same then, the said
statutory presumptions would automatically lose its significance and as such in such eventuality, the plaintiff would not be entitled for the decree on the basis of a negotiable instrument.
iv)	Where in a suit filed on the basis of a negotiable instrument and a direct trustworthy and confidence inspiring evidence is brought on record especially, from the statements of the marginal witnesses of the said negotiable instrument then in such state of affairs too, the plaintiff would not be entitled to get a favorable decree on the basis of the said negotiable instrument.

case_info: C.R No. 700-P of 2025 Fayaz Khan & others  Vs  Zar Wali & others
remarks:
(i)	The suit filed by a co-owner in the nature of declaration or seeking possession with respect to a \"Shamilaat land\" could not be maintainable, claiming an exclusive ownership or possession and the proper course for such an aggrieved co-owner is to file a suit/ application for official partition as
\"Shamilaat Land\" belongs to the village propriety body and every original owner of the \"Moza\" is to be considered as co-sharer/ co-owner in the same and as such none of an individual/ co-owner could claim an exclusive ownership, title or possession over a specific portion of the \"Shamilaat Land\".
(ii)	If a co-sharer/ co-sharer of \"Shamilaat Land\" is an aggrieved for having less possession of such land as against the other co-sharers, then in such situation too, such co-sharer would not be entitled to file a suit for possession rather the proper course for him is to file a suit for official partition.
(iii)	Any co-sharer/ co-owner, who is aggrieved of any entries in the \"Khasra Girdawari\" or any report submitted by the revenue authority in this regard could not file a suit before the Civil Court and the proper course for such an aggrieved person is to approach to the revenue authority, established under The Land Revenue Act, 1967. As entries in the \"Khasra Girdawari\" are made twice in a year on the basis of spot inspection and reports so submitted by the revenue officials.
(iv)	For maintaining a suit, the plaintiff has to show that he has an actionable cause of action and any suit filed before time qua the maturity of the cause of action would be not competent.
(v)	Civil cases are decided on the basis of preponderance of evidence and when any party who succeeds to slightly tilts the scale in his favour would be entitled for the grant of desired relief.
(vi)	In case of concurrent findings of facts and law, the scope, extent and domain of the High Court in revisional jurisdiction is fully discussed and dilated upon.

case_info: W.P No. 6258-P of 2024 M/S GHC International Pvt. Ltd. (Engineers & Contractors) Peshawar  Vs  Tariq Saeed
remarks: Legal set-off, under Order VIII Rule 6 CPC, applies to ascertained sums within the court\'s jurisdiction, while equitable set-off, rooted in fairness and equity, covers closely connected cross-demands even if unascertained. Order XX Rule 19 serves as a statutory acknowledgment of the remedy of equitable set-off. However, both require that the claim falls within the court\'s pecuniary jurisdiction.
