turning to another aspect of the present case, admittedly, the mutation in question was attested way back in the year 1936 while on one hand the legal heirs of the original owners who were minors at the time of attestation of mutation did not question the mutation after attaining the age of majority in their life time, and on the other hand, predecessors of petitioners kept mum for a considerable long period of time consisting is about 88/89 years, thus, the petitioners are stopped u/a 114 of the Qanun-e-Shahadat Order, 1984.
The limitation Act, 1908 provide a period of limitation for instituting a declaratory suit is six years, while the mutation in question was attested on 16.11.1936. The petitioners filed the instant suit challenging the mutation in question with a considerable unexplained delay of about 80 years, hence, the suit of petitioners is miserably hit by law of limitation. The Hon`ble Supreme Court of Pakistan in plethora of judgments held the limitation a substantive law that extinguishes the right to sue if the suit is not filed within prescribed time.
[Civil Revision petition dismissed in liminie and the concurrent findings of dismissal of suit of petitioners by the lower fora maintained.]

case_info: W.P No. 3797-P of 2025 Ihsaq Ahmad Khan  Vs  Muhammad Pervez Khan and others
remarks:
i.	A 12(2) petition would only be maintainable and proceedable if the petitioner succeeded to establish that at the time of obtaining a decree, the decree holder had committed any fraud or misrepresentation or that the Court was lacking jurisdiction.
ii.	Any decree which has been passed on the basis of a concessional statement or consent or in terms of compromise then such decree could not be challenged in 12(2) petition, however, if such decree is passed on the basis of a void agreement between the parties then the same could validly be challenged in a 12(2) petition.
iii.	In the present case, the petitioner had not challenged the instant decree
on the basis of any fraud or misrepresentation but only on the ground of want of jurisdiction, however, the record would show that in this case, firstly, the petitioner had never raised any such objection during the proceedings, secondly. the petitioner had personally appeared before the trial Court and was also represented by duly appointed legal practitioner and has recorded his no objection over the grant of a decree in favour of his brother/decree-holder and thirdly, the principle of estopel would also debars the petitioner to maintain the instant petition as if such objection is allowed to prevail at this belated stage then the same would erode the very confidence of the litigants over the judicial system.
iv.	There is no cavil to hold that a decree which was granted by a Court having no jurisdiction could validly be challenged in 12(2) petition, however, in the instant case, on one hand such objection has never been raised by the petitioner and on the other, on the available record, the Civil Court was having jurisdiction to entertain the instant suit and to pass the impugned decree.

case_info: Cr.A No.86-A of 2023 Amanullah  Vs The State
remarks:
(a) The doctrine of res gestae has been explained in Article 19 of the Qanun-e-Shahadat Order, 1984. The said doctrine of res gestae is based upon the assumption that statements of witnesses that constitute part of the res gestae are attributed a certain degree of reliability because they are contemporaneous making them admissible by virtue of their nature and strength of their connection with a particular event and their ability to explain it comprehensively.
(b) Where the eyewitnesses were inmates of the house, their presence at the place of occurrence was deemed natural and expected. In such circumstances, their testimony cannot be discredited merely on the basis of their relationship with the deceased or injured. The law recognizes that in domestic incidents, particularly those occurring within the household premises, the presence of family members or household inmates is normal and does not, by itself, cast suspicion on their credibility as eyewitnesses. Their evidence, if otherwise consistent and trustworthy, carries full evidentiary value.

case_info: WP No. 6637-P of 2018 Dr. Muhammad Younas Khan etc  Vs  Abdul Hanan etc
remarks: In view of the law laid down by the Apex Court in the case of Qazalbash Waqf (supra), the decisive steps have already been taken in the matter, and the remaining step ie. the transfer of surplus land measuring 781 PIUs in favour of the Provincial Government, is a self-executory provision. Hence, the present case is not hit by the law laid down by the Apex Court in the case of Qazalbash Waqf (supra). The remaining surplus land measuring 781 PIUs is thus to be transferred in favour of the Provincial Government in terms of MLR-115 as well as the Act of 1977. Therefore, the impugned order passed by the Member-II, Board of Revenue, Khyber Pakhtunkhwa, is not open to any exception. Resultantly, W.P No.6637-P/2018 filed by Dr. Muhammad Younas Khan stands dismissed.

case_info: R.F.A No. 21-A of 2011 M Nazir  Vs  RDFC
remarks:
Execution of a decree by sale of immovable property involves strict procedural safeguards. These safeguards are not mere formalities; they are substantive protections against arbitrary deprivation of property. Under Order XXI Rule 66 of the CPC, the court is mandated to issue a sale proclamation prior to the auction, detailing the property, its value, any encumbrances, and to issue notice to all persons having an interest in the property. The proclamation must be settled after hearing both the decree-holder and the judgment-debtor, as well as any other party claiming an interest.
The provision of Order XXI Rule 89 CPC allows a judgment-debtor or an interested person to have the sale set aside by depositing the sale price plus 5% of the purchase money as compensation within 30 days of the sale. However, this presupposes knowledge of the sale. The appellant, having received no notice or proclamation, nevertheless deposited the abovementioned sale price before the executing court.
Order XXI Rule 90 CPC is the cornerstone of this case. It enables any person whose interest is affected by the sale to move an application to set aside the auction on the grounds of material irregularity or fraud in publishing or conducting the sale, provided that substantial injury has occurred.
Order XXI Rule 91 CPC protects auction purchasers when it turns out, post-sale, that the judgment-debtor had no saleable interest. However, this does not protect the sale itself; it merely entitles such purchaser to a refund or damages from the decree-holder who misrepresented the facts. The sale remains null and void as against the true owner.
Order XXI Rule 92 CPC states that a sale must not be confirmed if there is an application pending under Rule 90 CPC. In the present case, no such application was filed prior to confirmation only because the petitioner was never notified or made aware of the proceedings. This procedural failure renders the confirmation itself contrary to law.
Once a sale is set aside, Order XXI Rule 93 CPC mandates a refund to the auction purchaser. Order XXI Rule 94 CPC clarifies that title only passes upon confirmation and issuance of the sale certificate. Where confirmation is invalid due to a jurisdictional error or procedural fraud, no title ever validly passes to the purchaser.
The wrongful sale of the appellant’s property—without notice, without trial, and without lawful authority—constitutes a violation of Article 10-A (Right to Fair Trial) and Article 24 (Right to Property) of the Constitution of the Islamic Republic of Pakistan, 1973. A person cannot be deprived of property except in accordance with law and after due process.

case_info: C.R No. 694-P of 2024 M/s SNK Enterprises, CNG Filling Station through Wajid Khan Khattak  Vs Managing Director, SNGPL and others
remarks:
i.	A complete mechanism for the redressal of the grievance of a consumer, the powers and functions of the Authority established under The Oil and Gas Regulatory Authority Ordinance, 2002, and its delegatee have been provided with respect to the matters duly highlighted in section 6 of the ibid Ordinance therein and as such in case of the grievance of the consumer it has to file an appeal with the Authority instead of approaching to any other forum and in case
of his failure to get a favourable order from the Appellate Authority then another remedy of review before the Federal Government has also been provided.
ii.	In case of any grievance of a consumer falling under section 6 of the Ordinance, 2002, against the delegatee (SNGPL) or the Authority (established under section 3 of the Ordinance), the Civil Court shall have no jurisdiction to entertain a suit as under section 43 of the Ordinance, 2002, the said Ordinance has been given overriding effect over all the other laws and in case of an order issued by the Authority or its delegatee (SNGPL) then an aggrieved person thereof would require to file an appeal before the Authority if his grievance is covered by section 6 of the said Ordinance.
iii.	Where special Authorities/Tribunals/forums have been established/
provided by law to deal with the technical matters and which require expert opinions/findings, then in such matters, the proper course for an aggrieved person is to firstly approach such forums/Authorities instead of approaching a Civil Court.
iv.	Any question of law set by the Apex Court is binding on this Court as well as on all other Courts including public functionaries as per mandate of Article 189 of The Constitution of Pakistan and as in a case of grievance of a consumer against the Authority/delegatee established under the Ordinance, 2002, the Apex Court in the case of Lamar Zaman has debarred the jurisdiction of the Civil Court, therefore, this Court has to follow the said dicta by directing the petitioner to approach the Authority established under the Ordinance, 2002.

case_info: CR No. 448-P of 2019 Abid Khan  Vs  The MD Regional Director & Headmistress City School Mardan Campus
remarks:
(a) Award of damages in a particular case include general or special damages. In case of grant of general damages for breach of contract the plaintiff or claimant had to prove that there had been a contractual relationship in existence between the parties and that the contract had been breached beside proving justification for grating of the damages claimed.

(b) In awarding general damages a rule of thumb normally follow however discretion lies with the court to calculate the amount of damages keeping in view circumstances of a particular case.

case_info: B.A No. 1796-P of 2025 Mudeer  Vs  The State
remarks:
Non-appearance of prosecution witnesses has been discussed.
On the preceding date of hearing, this Court, keeping in view the fact that all the police / official
witnesses were showing reluctance to appear before the learned trial court for the purpose of recording
their statements, had passed the following directions:-
Yesterday, keeping in view the controversy involved in the matter, this court had issued the following
directions:-
This court, after examining the entire record coupled with order sheets of the learned trial court has
observed with a great degree of concern that the prosecution witnesses, who happen to be the police
officials, are found reluctant rather adamant to appear before the learned trial court to record their
statements and in this regard even the learned trial court has been found helpless and handicapped to
procure their attendance notwithstanding NBWs were issued against them time and again and lastly on
04.06.2025, when the learned trial court while adjourning the case for 18.06.2025 made directions for
the issuance of fresh NBWs of arrest against the absent PWs viz. Serial No.2, 4, 5, 9, 10 of the calendar of witnesses. As such, this court deems it appropriate to issue direction to the worthy District Police Officer, Nowshera to positively execute the NBWs of arrest against the ibid PWs and they shall be produced before this court tomorrow i.e. 13 th instant. The office is directed to endorse copy of this order sheet to the learned Advocate General, worthy Inspector General of Police, worthy D.I.G, Mardan Division and worthy District Police Officer, Nowshera for information. The worthy D.I.G, Mardan Division be also put
on notice for tomorrow i.e. 13 th instant to let this court knows that why the PWs have failed to appear
before the learned trial court in the case in which huge quantity of narcotics is involved. Adjourned.
Pursuant to such directions, the Regional Police Officer Mardan and District Police Officer Nowshera
while executing the NBWs of arrest against the Prosecution witnesses namely, M/s Rauf Khan, DSP (CTD), Nowshera, Muhammad Salim Khan, SHO / SI / OII City Charsadda, Shakoor, Constable No.1118,
Nowshera, Sahil Constable No.448 Nowshera (Lower Court Hangu) and Ali Said LHC No.522, Nowshera
produced them before the court. With respect to non-appearance of the five official witnesses before the
learned trial court despite repeated summons and NBWs of arrest for recording their statements, neither
the RPO Mardan nor the DPO Nowshera could give any plausible explanation to such extent and the
reasons, so put-forth by the worthy RPO Mardan about the professional engagements of the official
witnesses not seeming to be reasonable, hence, cannot be appreciated. This court itself has thoroughly
gone through the entire record and found that all the aforesaid official witnesses remained adamant
towards the court’s directions insofar as their non-appearance for recording their statements is
concerned. Anyhow, this court cannot shut its eye from such conduct of the prosecution witnesses, who
are indeed police officials, hence, both, the Regional Police Officer Mardan and the District Police Officer
Nowshera are directed to lodge / confine all the aforesaid official / prosecution witnesses in judicial
lockup till 18.06.2025 and produce them before the learned trial court on the next date of hearing, which
is 18.06.2025, for recording their statements. A compliance report about their confinement shall be
shared with the court by the worthy Regional Police Officer Mardan by tomorrow through the worthy
Registrar of this court. The learned trial court is however directed to ensure the conclusion of the
prosecution evidence on the same date. A compliance report about the proceedings before the learned
trial court shall be submitted to the Additional Registrar (Judicial) of this court. Adjourned to 20 th instant
for further proceedings.
Before parting with the order, this court has observed with a degree of concern that the normally
cases are not concluded / decided within the statutory periods, so provided by the statutes / law only
because the prosecution is always found helpless to ensure the production of its witnesses, therefore, the
learned Advocate General is directed to convene a meeting with the worthy Inspector General of Police
Khyber Pakhtunkhwa, the worthy Director General Prosecution, worthy CCPO, Peshawar, all the worthy
Regional Police Officers of their respective regions and find some via media / mechanism for the smooth
and swift conclusion of trials before the learned trial courts within the stipulated statutory periods, so
provided by the Statutes / Law. The needful be done within thirty working days. Complete and
comprehensive report to such extent be submitted through the Additional Registrar (Judicial) of this court
for the perusal of the Bench in its Chamber. A copy of this order sheet be endorsed to all of them for
compliance.
In compliance thereof, all the official witnesses duly appeared and recorded their statements before
the learned trial court yesterday (19.06.2025). As a result, the trial proceedings in the instant case have
been effectively concluded to that extent, which is evident from the report, so furnished by the learned
trial court pursuant to the ibid directions of this court, available on file, and now the matter is fixed before
the learned trial court for 24.06.2024 for the purpose of recording the statement of the accused under the
relevant provisions of law. This development reflects a positive step toward the expeditious disposal of
the case in accordance with law.
The learned Advocate General also apprised the court that pursuant to ibid directions of this court, he
has written a letter dated 16.06.2023 to all the stakeholders (copy placed on file) and a proper meeting in
line with guidelines of this court would be convened shortly. He is directed to furnish minutes of the
meeting / progress report through the Additional Registrar (Judicial) of this court for the perusal of the
Bench in its Chamber.
This Court has noticed with a great degree of concern that in a good number of cases, trials have
remained pending well beyond the statutory period, so prescribed by the law. Such delays not only erode
public confidence in the administration of justice but also stand in direct contravention of the
fundamental principles of fair trial and timely adjudication, which form the bedrock of our legal system.
The failure to conclude trials within the prescribed period adversely affects the rights of all stakeholders,
particularly the accused persons, who are presumed innocent until proven guilty, and the victims, who
await closure and redress.
At this juncture, this court must say that, despite the existence of a well-structured framework for the
expeditious disposal of cases, as provided in the National Judicial Policy, 2009, still the rate of disposal of
criminal cases remains discouragingly low. In many instances, the proceedings continue to progress at an
unacceptably slow pace, akin to a snail’s crawl, thereby defeating the very spirit of timely justice and
undermining public confidence in the efficiency of the criminal justice system. Relation portion of the
guidelines of the National Judicial Policy, 2009 is reproduced herein below for ease of reference:-
10) All criminal cases punishable with imprisonment from 7 years and above including death cases shall be decided within a period of 1 year.
Chapter XX and XXII-A of the Code of Criminal Procedure 1898 prescribe detailed procedure for trial of
cases by Magistrate and the Court of Sessions to ensure fair trial for the accused. Since this procedure
takes longer time, therefore to finalize the proceedings, the following measures should be adopted to
cut short the delays:
(a) On receipt of Challan, the court shall immediately fix the case and issue production warrants/notice.
(b) When the accused is brought or appears before the court he should be provided with copies of statements and relevant documents as provided under section 241C and 265C Cr.P.C and be directed to ensure presence of his Counsel on the next date of hearing enabling the court to commence the trial.
(c) Under section 173 Cr.P.C, it is the duty of the concerned SHO / Investigating Officer to produce witnesses and case property before the court during trial. Therefore, the court shall take all necessary measures to bind the SHO / IOs to procure evidence on the fixed date.
(d) For ensuring production of witnesses, the IOs should provide land line telephone number, cellular phone number, e-mail address to the court / prosecutor for timely service.
(e) All efforts should be made to produce witnesses and the case property on the first date of hearing.
(f) If no case is made out or there is no probability of accused being convicted, the accused should be acquitted of the charge under Section 249-A or 265- K Cr.P.C, as the case may be.
(g) The court shall not grant unnecessary adjournments and if possible should proceed with the case on day to-day basis.
(h) The court shall take care that only relevant and admissible evidence is recorded.
(i) The District and Sessions Judges should hold meetings with the jail authorities to ensure the production of UTPs on the date of hearing to avoid delays on account of non-production of prisoners.
(j) The court should take strict action against the parties or witnesses causing deliberate delays in proceedings.
(k) The judgments should be based on well founded reasons and acumen so that it not only resolve the disputes but also lessen the prospects of future litigation.
(l) Delay in disposal of criminal cases is mostly due to the non-cooperation of relevant stakeholders of justice sector namely, lawyers, police and prison authorities; therefore, the court should ensure that they may fulfill their legal obligations to minimize delays and expedite trials.
The purpose and intent behind the aforesaid reproduction was to underscore and draw the attention
of all the stakeholders viz. subordinate courts, members of the legal fraternity, officers / officials of justice
sector and the litigants to the prevailing issues insofar as non-disposal of cases within the stipulated
period is concerned. It serves as a reminder of their shared responsibility in ensuring the timely
dispensation of justice and to highlight the need for greater diligence, commitment, and adherence to
judicial norms and timelines. All the courts, subordinate to the High Court, across the province are
supposed to ensure strict compliance with the guidelines laid down in the National Judicial Policy, 2009
because adherence to these directives is essential for securing the timely and effective disposal of
criminal cases within the prescribed periods.
Reverting to the main case, since the trial has been successfully concluded of course pursuant to the
directions of this court and presently the matter is fixed for 24 th instant for recording the statement of
accused in terms of section 342 CrPC, whereafter, the case would be ripe for final arguments and it is
anticipated that the matter will be decided within one or two hearings at most, therefore, this court
understands that grievance of the accused-petitioner qua non-conclusion of his trial has been adequately
redressed. This petition having become infructuous is thus disposed of.

case_info: Cr.A No. 454-P of 2018 State  Vs  Dawood Jan
remarks:
Sections 265-D & 265-K CrPC have been discussed.
Before moving ahead, it would be worthwhile to reproduce herein below the provision of section
265-D Cr.PC for ease of reference:-
S.265-D. When charge is to be framed. If, after perusing the police report or, as the case may
be, the complaint, and all other documents and statements filed by the prosecution, the Court is
of opinion that there is ground for proceeding with the trial of the accused it shall frame in
writing a charge against the accused.
Section 265-K, CrPC also requires verbatim reproduction, which reads as under:-
265-K. Power of Court to acquit accused at any stage. Nothing in this Chapter shall be
deemed to prevent a Court from acquitting an accused at any stage of the case; if, after
hearing the prosecutor and the accused and for reasons to be recorded, it considers that
there is no probability of the accused being convicted of any offence.
In our criminal justice system, the provisions encapsulated under Chapter XXII-A of the Code of
Criminal Procedure, 1898 are mandatory in nature. This Chapter delineates the procedural framework
that is required to be followed by the Courts in order to ensure a fair, just and impartial trial not only for
the accused but also for the prosecution and the complainant. As such, compliance with the said
provisions in their true letter and spirit is not merely desirable but obligatory. Amongst the various
provisions contained in Chapter XXII-A, Section 265-D assumes significant importance. This provision casts a statutory duty upon the Trial Court to frame a charge against the accused, where appropriate. The charge constitutes a concise and precise formulation of the allegations levelled against an accused and it forms the foundation of a criminal trial. Section 265-D mandates that before the charge is framed, the trial Court must, at a minimum, consider the following material placed before it by the prosecution:-
the First Information Report (FIR);
the police report under Section 173 Cr.PC;
all accompanying documents; and
the statements of the witnesses.
The purpose of such scrutiny is to enable the Court to determine whether it has the jurisdiction to
take cognizance of the matter and whether prima facie reasonable grounds exist for proceeding with the
trial of the accused. Only when the Court is satisfied that it is competent to take cognizance and that the
material on record discloses sufficient grounds to proceed, the charge should be framed. The object and
purpose of this procedural requirement is to initiate criminal proceedings judiciously and lawfully while
ensuring that the accused is made aware of the specific nature of the allegations and the offences
attributed to him. This knowledge is a fundamental right of the accused enabling him to effectively
defend himself and to avoid any element of prejudice or miscarriage of justice. On the other hand, if,
upon a comprehensive consideration of all the material available on record, the Court arrives at the
conclusion that it either lacks jurisdiction or that no sufficient grounds exist for proceeding with the trial,
it is incumbent upon the Court to abstain from framing a charge. Proceeding otherwise would amount to
a futile, vexatious or purposeless prosecution and would constitute an abuse of the process of law and
the judicial system.
The Code of Criminal Procedure confers inherent jurisdiction upon the Trial Courts under Sections
249-A and 265-K, as the case may be, to acquit any or all accused persons at any stage of the judicial
proceedings subject to the recording of reasons and after affording an opportunity of hearing to all
concerned parties. The expression ‘at any stage’ as employed in both provisions is of broad amplitude and includes the stages prior to or subsequent to the framing of charge or even after the recording of partial evidence during the course of trial. This inherent power may be exercised by the Court in circumstances where:-
the material on record is insufficient to establish the ingredients of any cognizable offence;
although a charge is framed, it appears to the Court to be groundless or frivolous and continuation of
the prosecution would result in an abuse of the process of law; or
upon an overall assessment of the case, there exists no realistic probability of the conviction of the
accused even after a full-fledged trial.
Accordingly, if such circumstances exist warranting the exercise of powers under Sections 249-A
or 265-K Cr.PC, the Trial Court is duty-bound to invoke these provisions provided that the parties are
granted a fair opportunity of hearing prior to the passing of any such order. The object and purpose of
conferring such powers upon the Courts are manifold:-
to prevent abuse of the process of the Court;
to safeguard the sanctity and integrity of the criminal justice system;
to protect an accused person from the trauma and harassment of a malicious, vexatious or baseless prosecution; and
to secure the ends of justice.
The exercise of such inherent power, though discretionary in form, is mandatory in effect wherever
the factual and legal circumstances justify its invocation. Any failure on the part of the Court to exercise
this power in an appropriate case would constitute a violation of the substantive provisions of law and
would prejudice the rights of the accuse, rendering the continuation of proceedings illegal and contrary to
the principles of criminal dispensation of justice. However, the exercise of this power requires to be
undertaken with utmost caution and upon a careful and judicious application of mind to the peculiar facts
and circumstances of a case.
Adverting to the merits of the case. No doubt, the complainant / SDO, through his report, has alleged
that the accused-respondent was involved in the unauthorized and direct connection with the LT
electricity line. However, a perusal of the said report reveals that it does not disclose any specific location,
address or identifiable premises where the alleged illegal connection was established. Furthermore, there
is no material on record to substantiate that the premises in question, if any, was owned, possessed or
under the control of the accused-respondent at the relevant time. In the absence of such essential
particulars, the allegation remains vague and uncorroborated thereby failing to establish a prima facie
nexus between the accused-respondent and the alleged offence. Moreover, it is noteworthy that no
private or independent witness was associated with the alleged proceedings including the purported
recovery of five yards of electric wire from the spot. The entire operation appears to have been
conducted solely in the presence of official witnesses thereby casting serious doubt on the transparency
and credibility of the proceedings. This concern is further compounded by the fact that the record is
completely silent as to whether any prior permission and authorization was obtained from the competent
authority for conducting such an operation purportedly aimed at recovering arrears or detecting
unauthorized usage. This procedural lapse becomes even more significant in light of the fact that the
source of information which allegedly implicated the accused-respondent in the unauthorized connection
as well as the alleged loss caused to the public exchequer has not been disclosed and remains shrouded in mystery. In the absence of such foundational details and procedural safeguards, the entire proceedings appear to suffer from serious legal infirmities thereby undermining their evidentiary value and raising a legitimate apprehension of mala fide or arbitrary action. All the aforesaid factors were duly and properly appreciated by the learned Trial Court in their true legal and factual perspective culminating in the acquittal of the accused-respondent under Section 265-K CrPC through a well-reasoned judgment dated 25.11.2017. The said judgment, in the circumstances, is both legally sound and factually justified
inasmuch as the material produced by the prosecution was wholly insufficient to establish the guilt of the
accused-respondent. The Trial Court rightly concluded that even if the statements of the prosecution
witnesses had been recorded, no reasonable probability existed of securing a conviction, given the lack of cogent evidence linking the accused-respondent to the alleged offence. The invocation of section 265-K Cr.PC in such a case was, therefore, not only appropriate but also necessary to prevent abuse of the process of law and to uphold the ends of justice in the given circumstances. Upon being confronted with the aforementioned factual and legal position, the learned AAG was unable to controvert or dislodge the same by any cogent argument or reference to the record. Viewed in this legal and factual backdrop, this Court is of the considered opinion that the impugned judgment of acquittal cannot, by any stretch of
reasoning, be termed as arbitrary, capricious or perverse so as to warrant interference in the exercise of
appellate jurisdiction. It is a settled principle of criminal jurisprudence that if, upon evaluation and
examination of the material on record, the Court entertains even a single reasonable doubt as to the guilt
of the accused, such doubt must be resolved in favour of the accused thereby entitling him to acquittal.
Accordingly, this appeal being without any merit and substance is dismissed.

case_info: T.A No. 34-P of 2025 Shabir  Vs  The State & pthers
remarks:
Articles 10 & 10-A of the Constitution have been discussed.
At the previous hearing, it was brought to the Court’s attention that due to the influence allegedly
exercised by the accused respondents, a notice dated 10.05.2025, now part of the record, was issued by
DBA Mardan, directing all advocates to abstain from representing the petitioner complainant in the
present case. Such a directive strikes at the core of legal ethics and compromises the independence of the Bar. More gravely, it amounts to an obstruction of justice by effectively depriving the petitioner of his
fundamental right to legal representation. This development raises profound concerns regarding the
fairness and integrity of the ongoing judicial process and constitutes a direct affront to the constitutional
guarantee of a fair trial under Article 10A of the Constitution. The provision enshrines not merely
procedural safeguards but ensures that every individual, whether complainant or accused, is entitled to
be heard before an impartial, competent and independent forum and to secure legal counsel of one’s
own choosing, free from fear, coercion or undue interference.
Given the foregoing concerns and the gravity of the situation, particularly where fundamental rights
of an individual were at stake, this Court found it necessary to summon the personal appearance of the
President and General Secretary of the DBA, Mardan, as well as representatives of the Khyber
Pakhtunkhwa Bar Council from District Mardan, including the Vice Chairman and Chairman Executive
Committee. The objective was to ascertain the factual matrix and institutional position, especially in the
context of Article 10-A of the Constitution. In compliance, M/s. Amin-ur-Rehman Yousafzai and Ashfaq
Ahmad Daudzai, Advocates, President and General Secretary of the Peshawar High Court Bar Association (“PHCBA”) respectively; Syed Mubashir Shah, Advocate, Member of the Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Bar Council; Fida Gul, Advocate, Former President of the PHCBA; and Syed Sajid Shah, Secretary of the Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Bar Council, appeared before the Court.
At the very outset of the proceedings, it was brought to the notice of the Court that the alleged
notice dated 10.05.2025 has since been withdrawn. In this regard, a certificate titled “To Whom It May
Concern” has been produced and placed on the record. Indeed, this role of the Bar Association qua
withdrawal of the impugned notice is commendable in the peculiar facts and circumstances of the case.
Be that as it may, this Court recognizes that the incorporation of the right to fair trial and due process
as an independent and enforceable fundamental right in terms of Article 10-A of the Constitution
underscores its paramount constitutional significance. This provision elevates the principles of fairness,
impartiality, and procedural justice to the status of constitutionally protected guarantees, thereby placing
a positive and binding obligation upon the State and the judiciary to ensure their meaningful and effective
enforcement. Like all other fundamental rights enshrined in the Constitution, Article 10-A must be
interpreted in a liberal, purposive, and progressive manner, aligned with the evolving standards of justice
and the rule of law. Courts are, therefore, duty-bound to zealously protect and uphold this right, ensuring
that no person is denied access to justice or subjected to arbitrariness in legal proceedings. Reference
may be made to Muhammad Yousaf v. Province of Sindh and others (2024 SCMR 1689) & Chairman NAB through P.G., Accountability v. Nasar Ullah and 5 others (PLD 2022 SC 497).
It is equally important to note that the right to fair trial, as guaranteed under Article 10-A, is not confined to the formal conduct of judicial proceedings alone. Rather, it encompasses broader and substantive safeguards, including the right to be heard before a competent, independent, and impartial tribunal, and the unimpeded right of a party, whether complainant or accused, to engage legal counsel of their own choice. This right must be exercised free from coercion, intimidation, obstruction, or undue influence. This constitutional protection is reinforced by international human rights law. Article 10 of the Universal Declaration of Human Rights of 1948 (UDHR) provides that “everyone is entitled in full equality to a fair and public hearing by an independent and impartial tribunal.” Likewise, Article 14(1) and (3) of the International Covenant on Civil and Political Rights of 1966 (ICCPR), to which Pakistan is a State party, affirm the right to a fair and public hearing, the right to legal assistance of one’s own choosing, and the right to equality before the courts.
Any act whether by public authority, private individuals, or professional bodies that restricts or
discourages access to legal representation, undermines the independence of the legal profession, or
compromises the neutrality of the adjudicating forum constitutes a serious violation of these
constitutional and international guarantees. Such interference not only prejudices the rights of an
individual litigant but also strikes at the core of constitutional justice, eroding public trust in the legal
system and rendering the entire adjudicatory process vulnerable to miscarriage of justice. It is, therefore,
incumbent upon the Bar Associations and the courts to vigilantly guard against such violations and to
enforce Article 10-A with the full force of constitutional and international mandate, ensuring that justice
is not only done but is manifestly and unambiguously seen to be done fairly, impartially and consistently
regardless of the circumstances within the mandate of the Constitution.
It must be emphatically stated that Bar associations are not mere professional collectives; they are
vital constitutional institutions integral to the administration of justice and the safeguarding of the rule of
law. They serve as custodians of legal ethics, defenders of judicial independence, and facilitators of equal
access to justice. Throughout history, both in Pakistan and abroad, bar associations have stepped into the breach when constitutionalism was under threat or when individual rights were suppressed. In Pakistan’s own recent past, the 2007 Lawyers’ Movement stands as a monumental testament to this legacy. When General Pervez Musharraf imposed a state of emergency and unlawfully sacked the Chief Justice of Pakistan, it was the legal fraternity, spearheaded by the Pakistan Bar Council, provincial bar councils, and bar associations nationwide, that galvanized mass democratic resistance. Thousands of lawyers marched, faced arrests, and endured police brutality in defense of judicial independence and constitutional supremacy. The sacrifices made by members of the legal community reflect the nobility and
indispensability of the Bar in preserving the rule of law. Their commitment culminated in the reinstatement of deposed judges and reaffirmed the sanctity of the Constitution, marked as a defining chapter in Pakistan’s democratic evolution.
In addition to, Bar Associations in Pakistan have also made vital contributions through legal advocacy
in landmark constitutional cases. Their involvement in shaping the contours of constitutional
jurisprudence is evident from their lead role in seminal decisions such as Sindh High Court Bar
Association v. Federation of Pakistan (PLD 2009 SC 879), where the Supreme Court invalidated the
Provisional Constitution Order of 2007 and reaffirmed judicial independence, and District Bar
Association, Rawalpindi v. Federation of Pakistan (PLD 2015 SC 401), where foundational questions
regarding constitutional amendments and the basic structure were adjudicated. These interventions
highlight that in moments of constitutional crisis, it is often the Bar Associations that first steps forward to
defend the rule of law and safeguard fundamental rights, thereby reaffirming its institutional significance
in Pakistan’s democratic architecture.
Viewed in this spectrum, any act by an individual or a bar association that restricts or discourages
lawyers from representing litigants, especially vulnerable complainants or accused, is not only legally
indefensible but morally dissonant with the Bar’s historic mission. The issuance of a directive, as in the
present case, instructing lawyers not to appear on behalf of the petitioner complainant, runs counter to
the foundational principles of legal representation, access to justice, and independence of the legal
profession. Internationally, bar associations such as the American Bar Association, the International Bar
Association, and the Law Society of England and Wales have consistently advocated for the protection of
these core rights. They have opposed repressive regimes, supported persecuted lawyers, and denounced efforts to curtail fair representation.
In this regard, the International Bar Association’s Standards for the Independence of the Legal
Profession (1990) provide authoritative guidance. Most crucially, Principle 18 outlines the core
responsibilities of lawyers’ associations, including upholding justice without fear or favor, maintaining
professional integrity and ethics, defending the independence of the judiciary, and ensuring access to
justice and legal representation for all, particularly the vulnerable. It also mandates that bar associations
promote fair hearings before competent, independent tribunals and contribute to public education and
legal reform. These principles collectively affirm that a bar’s duty transcends organizational interests. It is
fundamentally a constitutional obligation. Similarly, in a neighbouring jurisdiction, the Supreme Court of
India in the case of R. Muthukrishnan v. The Registrar General of the High Court of Judicature at Madras
(AIR 2019 SC 849), while elaborating on the role of Bar Associations, observed as follows:
It is basically the lawyers who bring the cause to the Court are supposed to protect the rights of
individuals of equality and freedom as constitutionally envisaged and to ensure the country is governed
by the Rule of law. Considering the significance of the Bar in maintaining the Rule of law, right to be 
treated equally and enforcement of various other fundamental rights, and to ensure that various
institutions work within their parameters, its independence becomes imperative and cannot be
compromised. The lawyers are supposed to be fearless and independent in the protection of rights of
litigants. What lawyers are supposed to protect, is the legal system and procedure of law of deciding the
cases.
In light of the foregoing, any directive by a bar association that seeks to restrain or inhibit legal
representation, particularly for vulnerable complainants or accused persons, constitutes a grave breach of the ethical and constitutional duties incumbent upon the legal profession. Such conduct undermines the principle of equal access to justice, compromises the independence of the legal profession, and violates internationally recognized standards. The right of every individual to be represented by counsel of their own choosing, without intimidation or obstruction, is a foundational component of the right to fair trial. It is therefore imperative that bar associations, especially at the district level, remain vigilant against any institutional or individual overreach that erodes these guarantees. Any deviation from this obligation not only affects the rights of the litigants but also tarnishes the credibility of the legal system and diminishes the Bar’s constitutional stature as an enabler of justice and the rule of law.
Reverting back to the main issue, since the impugned notice has been withdrawn, as noted above,
this Court is of the view that the core grievance stands amicably resolved and no further proceedings are
warranted at this stage. Accordingly, this petition is disposed of in the above terms.
The Additional Registrar (Judicial) is directed to transmit / circulate copies of this Judgment to the
Chairman, Vice Chairman, Chairman Executive Committee of the Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Bar Council,
Presidents of the Peshawar High Court Bar Associations both at Principal seat and its Benches, and
Presidents of the District & Tehsil Bar Associations of the Province for information.

case_info: R.F.A No. 37-M of 2022 Nawabzada Muhammad Khaisro Khan  VS  Mst. Aneesa Bibi and others
remarks: Mere issuance of notices to the applicant during the acquisition process is insufficient to establish any legal right or ownership over the acquired land. Neither there is any evidence on record or annexed with his impleadment application to show that he had received any compensation for the acquired land nor he has ever objected to or questioned the acquisition proceedings when his name was dropped even after initial participation in the process.

case_info: Cr.A No. 260-M of 2024 Noor Muhammad  VS State & another
remarks:
Criminal appeals filed by Noor Muhammad against the acquittal of Zahid Islam, who was accused of murdering his sister-in-law and her alleged paramour. The court upheld the trial court`s decision, citing lack of evidence and inconsistencies in the prosecution`s case.
The incident occurred on March 1, 2023, at midnight, but the FIR was lodged at 6:15 am, with no explanation for the delay. The prosecution failed to provide direct evidence, and the FIR lodged by the accused cannot be used as evidence against him. The recovery of the weapon of offence has no
value, as the prosecution failed to prove its case.
The court emphasized that an acquitted accused earns double presumption of innocence, and it is slow to interfere with such judgments unless they are perverse or against evidence. The prosecution failed to discharge its initial burden, and the trial court rightly extended the benefit of doubt to the accused.

case_info: Cr.A No. 245-P of 2025 Wahab Khan  Vs  Dawat Jan and another
remarks:
i.	Where the prosecution has been able to prove its case to the hilt then the Court of law should not hesitate in awarding the permissible punishment provided thereunder.
ii.	Where an offence is punishable for a specific punishment then the Court of law while exercising its discretion in the matter of grant of a specific punishment has to keep in mind that as to whether any mitigating or extenuating circumstances are present in the case for awarding a lesser punishment.
iii.	While exercising its discretion, the Court of law has to consider the mode and manner of offence, the nature of offence and the evidence so brought on record by the prosecution in this regard and the same has to be appreciated in its true letter and spirit so as to achieve the ultimate purpose of awarding punishment i.e. retributive and deterrence and any leniency in awarding the punishment as against the record would defeat the very object of penal law of upholding justice and social order in the society.
iv.	While exercising its discretion in award of punishment, the Court of law has to keep in mind that the proposed punishment would create an atmosphere of deterrence in the people who have inclination towards the said crime and it shall also keep in mind that the punishment would help in discouraging of the offence as well as in the reformation of the convict and any leniency in this regard would destroy peace, tranquility and harmony of the society.
v.	Where the prosecution has been able to prove its case to the hilt then any leniency in the award of punishment would on one hand jeopardize the society and on the other hand the same would amount to frustrate the very object and purpose of penal law.
vi.	Earlier the discretion of the Court in awarding punishments was to be regulated by the dicta laid down by the superior courts, however, recently through an Act No. XXV of 2021 namely, The Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Sentencing Act, 2021 has been enacted which also provides a yardstick to help the Courts of law in awarding the sentence, therefore, all Courts of law have now to adhere to the aforesaid law strictly which awarding punishment.
vii.	When once the prosecution has been able to prove its case then a culprit would not only be liable to serve the imprisonment but he has also to pay the fine amount and would also be liable to pay the compensation amount to the victim of the case as per mandate of section 544-A Cr.P.C.

case_info: W.P No.815-A of 2022 Pir Asfandyar  Vs  Govt of KP
remarks:
(a) When some of the selected candidates do not join the service, such posts remain vacant, and it is imperative for the department to consider the remaining candidates for appointment against said posts. Such posts cannot be kept vacant until the next recruitment process if some of the selected candidates are still available on the waiting list.
(b) The principle of natural justice is another name for commonsense justice. Rules of natural justice are not codified canons, but principles ingrained in the human conscience. Natural justice is the administration of justice in a commonsense, liberal manner. Justice is based substantially on natural ideas and human values. The administration of justice should be free from narrow and restricted considerations commonly associated with a formulated law involving linguistic technicalities and grammatical niceties. It is the substance of justice that must determine its form. The expressions “natural justice” and “legal justice” do not represent a watertight classification. It is the substance of justice that must be secured. Whenever legal justice fails to achieve this solemn purpose, natural justice is called in aid. Natural justice relieves legal justice from unnecessary technicalities, grammatical pedantry, or logical prevarication; it supplies the omissions of a formulated law.
(c) When the foundational or basic order is without lawful authority and void ab initio, any subsequent actions, proceedings, or superstructures built upon such an order automatically collapse, having no legal sanctity or enforceability in the eyes of the law.

case_info: C.R No. 97-B of 2022 Kareem Ullah and another  Vs  Mst Imran Shahzadi and another
remarks: Civil Revision---Maintainability---Alternative remedies---Doctrine of Election of remedy---Section 12(2) & 115, C.P.C.---Where petitioners had earlier challenged the same judgment and decree through an application under Section 12(2), C.P.C., and that application was dismissed, they could not subsequently or simultaneously file a revision petition under Section 115, C.P.C., challenging the same order---Pursuing parallel remedies for identical reliefs is not permissible---Doctrine of Election of remedy and principle of Res Judicata attracted-Revision petition dismissed.

case_info: Cr.A No.1006-P of 2024 Haris  Vs  State etc
remarks:
i.	Where a conviction is recorded and the Court refrains from awarding the maximum sentence, it must provide cogent reasons for such leniency. Conversely, the imposition of the death penalty must also be well-founded, based on a fair appreciation of the evidence and surrounding circumstances.
ii.	While it is true that the witnesses are closely related to the deceased, it is equally true that they share the same degree of relationship with the appellant. On one hand, the deceased was their sister; on the other, the appellant is their real nephew. Hence, the possibility of false implication merely on account of relationship appears highly unlikely.
iii.	While injuries alone do not render a witness truthful, in this case, the surrounding circumstances, social status, and the character of the witnesses, being parda-nasheen, elderly, and illiterate, enhance their credibility.
iv.	It is pertinent to note that while medical expertise in the area may be limited, such factors alone should not negate the reliability of the report. Courts must evaluate the broader context in which an injured individual is received and examined, and whether a statement was obtained promptly.
v.	Although the deceased suffered injuries on vital parts of her body, the record and testimonies of the witnesses affirm that she remained conscious and oriented in time and space. No substantial challenge was raised during cross-examination to dispute her ability to give a statement. The consistency between the statements of the scribe and the doctor lends credibility to the dying declaration, and this Court finds no justification to exclude it from consideration.
vi.	While it is true that a dying declaration is generally considered a weaker form of evidence and must be corroborated by independent testimony, this requirement stands fulfilled in the present case. The Court must assess whether the declarant was in a fit state of mind and whether the declaration was duly recorded. In light of the medical and eyewitness evidence, this Court finds that the deceased was indeed conscious and oriented at the time her statement was recorded.
vii.	In a case where a real aunt, grievously injured, unequivocally accuses her own nephew, and where other eyewitnesses, being close blood relatives, corroborate the sequence of events without deviation, the presumption of truth gains considerable strength. It is implausible that such close kin would fabricate a false narrative to substitute the appellant for another assailant.
viii.	It is in this chilling context that we find ourselves unable to take a lenient view. The brutal nature of the offence, the vulnerability of the victims, and the betrayal of familial bonds collectively mark the appellant as one whose actions are not merely criminal but morally abhorrent.

case_info: RFA  No.149-B of 2020 Muhammad Jamil and others Vs. N.H.A & Others
remarks:
Land Acquisition---Compensation---Determination of market value---Scope of Sections 23, 28 & 34of Land Acquisition Act, 1894 (as amended by KPK)---Power of Referee Court and High Court to enhance compensation beyond claim---Principle---Held, Court may award compensation exceeding amount claimed in reference if justified by evidence---Rejection of earlier Local Commission report attained finality---subsequent Local Commission assessed land value at Rs. 600,000/- per Kanal based on commercial potential and location---Referee Court erred by reverting to earlier rejected report---Although Section 28 of the Land Acquisition Act, 1894, has been amended by the Province of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa, the amendment did not delete the provision regarding payment of 6 % simple interest---Under the amended law, the Collector remains bound to pay 6% interest on the amount of compensation determined by the Court which exceeds the amount already deposited--- Section 34 of the Act still holds the field and continues to apply---The language employed in the amended Section 34 is mandatory in nature, obligating the Collector to pay compensation along with interest, to be calculated from the date of taking possession until final payment or deposit of the excess amount is made---Interest under Section 34 is compensatory, mandatory, and not  “riba\" in the Islamic sense---High Court fixed compensation at Rs. 600,000/- per Kanal with l5% compulsory acquisition charges and 6% simple interest on enhanced amount-Appeals of landowners allowed,
NHA\'s appeals dismissed\'

case_info: WP No. 551-A of 2025 Saradr Mubashir   Vs  Ambreen Bibi
remarks:
(a) Under Islamic jurisprudence, a father is legally, socially, and morally obligated to provide adequate maintenance for his children, ensuring a standard of living that is respectable and in accordance with the family’s status. This duty is enshrined in Islamic principles and codified under Section 370 of Mulla’s Muhammadan Law. The responsibility to maintain continues until the son attains the age of majority, and, in the case of a daughter, until she enters into marriage. Section 369 of Mulla’s Muhammadan Law defines “maintenance” as including essential provisions such as food, clothing (raiment), and lodging. The term “maintenance” must be interpreted broadly to encompass all aspects necessary for the holistic development and well-being of a minor. This includes not only basic necessities but also expenditures required for the child’s social, physical, mental, and educational growth, all of which must be considered in light of the family’s social standing and contemporary societal norms. Importantly, such obligations are to be fulfilled in proportion to the father’s financial capacity and available means.

(b) The Family Courts Act, 1964 does not provide the right of second appeal to any party to the proceedings. The legislature intended to place a full stop on family litigation once it was decided by the appellate court. However, the High Courts routinely exercise their extraordinary jurisdiction under Article 199 of the Constitution of the Islamic Republic of Pakistan, 1973 as a substitute for appeal or revision, and, more often than not, the purpose of the statute — i.e., expeditious disposal of cases — is thereby compromised and defeated.

case_info: Cr.A No. 125-P of 2025 Haider Ali Vs The State
remarks:
(i)	The driver having the charge of vehicle is supposed to have knowledge with regard to contents and articles being transported in it.
(ii)	Co-accused at the relevant time was found seated in the front seat of the vehicle and noting incriminating was recovered from the personal possession.
(iii)	Appellant filed appeal for the return of vehicle. Who is neither the registered owner of the vehicle nor its bonafide purchaser.

case_info: Cr.A No. 1111-P of 2024 Arshid etc Vs  The State etc
remarks:
i.	There is no denial to this fact that the tragic incident led to the tragic death of the deceased, where one was the husband and the other his wife, but there is no denial to this fact as well that mere heinousness of an offence would hardly be a circumstance for holding an accused responsible, rather under all circumstances the Courts of law must look for independent corroboration and the prosecution must bring on record trust-worthy and confidence-inspiring evidence. If on one hand the unfortunate spouses lost their lives, then on the other four (04) real brothers are charged, so to reach to a just conclusion extra care is needed, so that the liabilities could be fixed against those who are responsible.
ii.	The improvements are dishonest and we are confident in holding that the same has damaged the case of the prosecution. The witnesses failed to establish their presence on the spot. Had they been present and had they been fired upon, there was hardly an occasion for them to escape unhurt, as the accused were armed with deadly weapons.
iii.	On one hand the witnesses remained inconsistent on material aspects of the case, whereas on the other they failed to establish their presence on the spot at the time of occurrence, so this Court is inclined to hold that the incident went unwitnessed and the report was made after attendance of the complainant was procured.

case_info: Cr.A No. 89-A of 2024 Waqas etc  Vs  The State
remarks:
(a) When there is contradiction in the statements of eyewitnesses and site plan, the same are not safe for reliance for the purpose of conviction.
(b) When cause of death of the deceased as per post mortem report is single shot firearm injury, possibility of false implication of two accused who are real brothers inter-se cannot be ruled out when motive is shown as previous hostilities.
(c) Where crime empties recovered from the spot at the time of inspection by the Investigation Officer, are sent to the Forensic Science Laboratory after the arrest of the accused or together with the crime weapon recovered after a considerable delay without justifiable reason, the positive report of the said Laboratory loses its evidentiary value and the same cannot be relied upon for the purpose of conviction.
(d) Where hostilities followed by litigations exist between the parties, the same may drive one of the parties to enmesh its rival in a false case or the latter to do away with the former, therefore, such a motive can be considered a double-edged weapon, hence, merely on the basis of such motive, an accused cannot be held responsible for commission of offence.
(e) It is not essential that there should be many circumstances creating doubts, even a single circumstance creating reasonable doubt in the prosecution’s case is sufficient to extend the benefit
of doubt to the accused as it is the cardinal principle of criminal administration of justice that it is better that ten guilty persons escape than that one innocent suffer.

case_info: C.R No. 317-P of 2021 Ameer Zali Khan  Vs  Alam Khan and others
remarks:
i.	For maintaining a suit being filed in a representative capacity, a plaintiff is required to file an application under Order I Rule 8 CPC for seeking permission to file the said suit in its representative capacity and such permission is to be granted by the Court through a specific and explicit order.
ii.	In order to file a suit in representative capacity, a plaintiff is required to append the list of the interested persons on whose behalf the suit is being filed and then such interested persons/owners are to be served through notices or at least through publication of an advertisement and in case of absence of the aforesaid exercise by the trial Court and in absence of any specific order qua the grant of permission to file the suit in representative capacity then such suit could
not be treated as a suit filed in representative capacity by such plaintiff.
iii.	In case of absence of any permission by the trial Court to file the suit in representative capacity, a co-owner/individual cannot maintain a suit for declaration or possession with respect to Shamilat land, as a co-owner/co-sharer of Shamilat land cannot, in his personal capacity can maintain a suit for declaration or possession.
iv.	A suit for declaration, issuance of permanent injunction or suit for dispossession in respect of a Shamilat land could not be filed by an individual/co-owner, and the proper course for an aggrieved co-owner with respect to Shamilat land is to file a suit for official partition.
v.	An excess of possession of a co-owner in Shamilat land could not be challenged by the co-owner of Shamilat land unless and until such Shamilat land is officially partitioned and thereafter every co-owner is handed over
possession proportionate to his original ownership in the said mouza.
vi.	Civil cases are decided on the basis of preponderance of evidence, and any party who succeeds to tilt the scale in his favour would be entitled for the relief claimed.
vii.	The scope, extent and domain of revisional jurisdiction of the High Court in cases of concurrent findings has duly been dilated and highlighted.

case_info: Cr. Misc B.A No. 1847-P of 2025 Yad Gul  Vs  State
remarks: Admittedly and as is evident from the record in the present case chars weighing 1024 grams was recovered. But it is noted with great concern that a person on seeing the police party still have the contrabands in trouser fold, why he did not threw away just to save his skin? Furthermore, in the absence of Forensic Science Laboratory report, how it would be determined that the recovered contraband was charas or otherwise? These are big question marks, and resolution thereof could be made during the trial. There is no confession or admission on the part of accused/ petitioner.

case_info: Cr. Misc B.A No. 1576-P of 2025 Mustafa  Vs  State
remarks: Accused/Petitioner habitual offender offence for which accused/ petitioner charged does not hit prohibitory clause of Sec. 497 CrPC. Not entitled to bail.

case_info: W.P No.891-M of 2024 Mst. Shamaa  Vs  Said Amin Shah
remarks:
Custody dispute between the petitioner (mother) and the respondent (father) over their two minor children. The father had filed a petition under Section 25 of the Guardian and Wards Act, 1890, seeking custody of the minors, which was granted by the trial court and upheld by the appellate court.
1. The welfare of the minors is the paramount consideration in cases of custody of a minor, and it includes physical, emotional, educational, moral, and psychological aspects.
2. The mother`s remarriage to a stranger (the brother of her previous husband) has introduced strained familial relations and criminal allegations, which affects the overall well-being of the minors.
3. The father, being in the military service, has demonstrated the ability to provide a secure and stable environment to his children.

case_info: W.P No.807-A of 2022 Mst. Aziz Jan Vs  M. Fiaz etc
remarks:
(a)Averments in the plaint must be read as a whole to determine whether they disclose a cause of action or whether the suit is barred under any law, including a bar created due to the lapse of the limitation period. At the stage of exercising power under Order VII, Rule 11, C.P.C., if the averments in the plaint ex facie do not disclose a cause of action, or if on a plain reading the suit appears to be barred under any law, the plaint can be rejected.
(b)Limitation was not merely a technicality. Court could not allow the law of limitation to be bypassed. Court would not come to the rescue of persons who slept over their rights. Under section 3 of the Limitation Act, 1908, it was the bounden duty of every court to take notice of the question of limitation even if not raised in defense by the contesting party. Incompetent, frivolous and time barred suits are liable to be buried from their inception and even in such circumstances it is duty of the court, without there being an application to invoke the provisions of O. VII, R.11, C.P.C., so that the other may not be dragged in such litigation and to save the precious time of the court.
(c)The High Courts must not exercise their constitutional jurisdiction to interfere with the discretion exercised by lower courts unless the same suffers from jurisdictional, factual, or legal errors. In other words, such interference is justified only where the impugned order has been passed without jurisdiction, is based on misreading or non-reading of evidence, or is otherwise not in accordance with the law. If none of these errors is present, the High Courts must refrain from interfering with the findings of the lower courts merely because they would have reached a different conclusion on the controversy.

case_info: Cr. A No. 87-M of 2023 Alauddin Khan   Vs  Manager Faisal Bank
remarks:
The appellant challenged an order from the Consumer Court that had dismissed his complaint against the bank for lacking jurisdiction. The dispute arose when the bank refused to allow the appellant to withdraw $92,000 from his US Dollar account in US currency, instead converting and transferring the amount to his Pakistani Rupee account without his consent. The Consumer Court had dismissed the complaint, ruling that the matter fell exclusively under the jurisdiction of the Banking Court pursuant to Section 7(4) of the Financial Institutions (Recovery of Finances) Ordinance, 2001, a federal law which it held prevailed over the provincial Consumer Protection Act.

On appeal, the High Court conducted a comparative analysis of the two statutes. It noted that the Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Consumer Protection Act explicitly defines \"services\" in Section 2(n) to include banking and financing, thereby covering the appellant\'s grievance about deficient service. In contrast, the Financial Institutions Ordinance, particularly Section 9, is primarily concerned with matters of loan recovery, default, and financial obligations between banks and customers—elements entirely absent in this case, which pertained solely to the terms of operating a foreign currency account. The High Court found that the Consumer Court had misconstrued the applicable laws; the Ordinance did not oust the Consumer Court\'s jurisdiction over a standard service deficiency claim unrelated to financial recovery. Consequently, the appeal was allowed, the Consumer Court\'s order was set aside, and the complaint was restored for a fresh merits-based hearing, with a directive for the Consumer Court to conclude the matter within 90 days.

case_info: Cr.A No. 456-P of 2024 Muhammad Khan  Vs  The state etc
remarks:
Criminal Appeal
Held:	Principal- A man may lie, but circumstances rarely do- the universal standard is that circumstantial evidence must lead to a singular, inescapable conclusion of guilt, excluding all other possibilities. The convergence of telecommunication records, geo-location, and recovery from possession eliminates the likelihood of mere coincidence. In the circumstances of the case, the Call Data Record (CDR) data speaks volumes, forming a strong and unbroken chain of circumstantial evidence that establishes the accused`s connection with the planning and execution of the offence. For every offence, the mens rea and actus rea needs to be independently proved against each accused, if there are more than one. Of the element of motive and intention is lacking against either of the accused, that accused must be given the benefit of such.
Cr.A No. 456-P/2024 is dismissed, Cr.A No. 536-P/2024 is allowed while Cr.R No. 161-P/2024 stands infructuous)

case_info: Cr.A No. 42-M of 2025 Mian Rahman Badshah  Vs  The State & others
remarks:
Criminal appeal under section 8 of the Illegal Dispossession Act, 2005.
The appellant had filed a complaint alleging dispossession by respondent’s No. 2 to 6 from a property. The trial court acquitted the respondents using Section 265-K Cr.P.C. There was a previous civil suit regarding the property between the appellant and the respondent`s predecessor, which was decided against the appellant. PW-1 (Said Badshah) and PW-2 (Badshah Mian, the appellant`s brother) testified about the civil litigation concerning the property. The court found zero possibility of conviction based on the evidence and upheld the acquittal.

case_info: CM No. 762-P of 2023 in FAO No. 159/2022 Jubilee Life Insurance Company Pvt Ltd  Vs  Arshad Khan
remarks: A defect in signing, verifying or presenting a pleading or memorandum of appeal or revision would not affect merits of a case or jurisdiction of a court and same should be treated as curable irregularity of proceedings.

case_info: W.P No.825-B of 2025 Muhammad Jamshed Khan and others  Vs  National Bank of Pakistan and others
remarks:
Constitution of Pakistan (1973), Article 199 –
Regularization – Petitioners were engaged through third-party contractors for services at National
Bank of Pakistan – Appointment letters issued by private outsourcing companies – No evidence of
direct appointment or absorption by the Bank – Mere long service or continuous performance of
duties at Bank premises does not create employment relationship with principal employer –
Regularization cannot be claimed as a right unless specifically provided by statute – Writ not
maintainable in disputes involving contractual arrangements with third-party service providers –
Petitioners at liberty to seek remedy under applicable labour laws –
Held, employment relationship must be established before invoking writ jurisdiction – Disputed
questions of fact and private contractual issues are not triable under Article 199 –
Petitions dismissed.
Cases referred:
1.Vice Chancellor, Bacha Khan University Charsadda v. Tanveer Ahmad and others (2022 PLC (C.S.) 85)
2.Government of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa v. Sher Aman and others (2022 SCMR 406)
3.Sui Southern Gas Company Ltd. v. Zeeshan Usmani and others (2022 PLC (C.S.) 424)
4.Province of Punjab v. Prof. Dr. Javed Iqbal and others (2022 SCMR 897)
5.National Bank of Pakistan v. Sohaib Iftikhar (C.P. No.425-L/2014, decided on 20.6.2018)
6.National Bank of Pakistan v. Kashif Salah-ud-Din (C.A. Nos.1340–1342/2014, decided on 3.3.2016)
Held: All writ petitions dismissed as not maintainable. Petitioners may approach appropriate labour forum.

case_info: W.P No.14-D of 2025 Samdani  Vs  Mst Zahida Bibi and another
remarks:
Held:
(1) The trial court failed to apply the correct legal standard by treating khula as conditional upon a subsequent talaq by the husband.
(2) The punitive measures directed against the petitioner, in the absence of legal justification, are ultra vires and arbitrary.
(3) It is settled law that once a decree for khula is passed by the competent court, the marriage stands dissolved and there remains no legal necessity to compel the husband to pronounce talaq, whether orally or in writing. The imposition of penal consequences i.e civil imprisonment and financial penalties are also beyond the statutory mandate conferred upon Family Courts under the relevant laws, unless explicitly sanctioned by legislative provisions or through binding judicial precedents.

(4) A judge is duty-bound to decide cases strictly within the ambit of authority conferred by law. Any exercise of power beyond the statutory framework amounts to judicial overreach, which is antithetical to the rule of law and undermines the integrity of the judicial process. When a court steps outside its jurisdiction or renders a decision in contradiction to established legal principles, such a decision is void ab initio—a nullity in the eyes of law.

(5) The judicial role is not one of personal discretion or subjective interpretation; rather, it entails interpreting and applying the law objectively, in line with binding precedent and statutory mandates. A judgment must reflect a logical and lawful reasoning process, not personal beliefs or considerations. Findings that fall outside the legal framework not only compromise the validity of the judgment but also
erode public confidence in the justice system.

(6) Accordingly, this petition is partially allowed to the extent of upholding the decree for dissolution of marriage on the basis of khula. However, the remaining portion of the impugned order, whereby unwarranted conditions were imposed upon the petitioner by the learned Civil Judge-I, Paharpur, D.I.Khan—including directives for pronouncement of written talaq, civil imprisonment, and imposition of daily compensation—is hereby declared to be erroneous, illegal, and without any lawful authority or statutory backing, hence, set aside. Consequently, this petition stands partly allowed in the above terms.

case_info: CM 12(2) No. 37-P of 2023 in C.R No. 562-P of 2010 Muhammad Amir Shehzad and others  Vs  Ahmad Rasool
remarks:
i.	Against an exparte judgment and decree an aggrieved person has the following four remedies;
 	a. to file an application under Order IX Rule 13 CPC; or
 	b. to file a review petition under Section 114 CPC read with Order XLVII • CPC;         or
 	c.to file an appeal under Section 96 CPC; or
 	d. to file a petition under Section 12(2) CPC

however, if a party opts for any of the aforesaid remedy then it has to prove the legal requirement for maintaining the selected-cum-availed remedy.
ii.	If an aggrieved person of an exparte decree has opted for filing a 12(2) petition thereagainst then it has to prove that the said decree has been obtained by the decree-holder on the basis of fraud, misrepresentation or that the said decree suffer from jurisdictional defect. The aggrieved person has also to meet the question of limitation provided for availing the said remedy.
iii.	Any allegation of fraud on behalf of a co-judgment-debtor / third party could not be considered as a ground for maintaining a 12(2) petition.
iv.	Earlier question of limitation was to be treated and considered as mixed question of law and facts, however, after the decision of the larger bench of the Apex Court in the case of Khushi Muhammad through L.Rs. and others vs. Mst. Fazal Bibi and others\" reported as PLD 2016 Supreme Court 872 the question of limitation is no more a question of fact and the same is to be considered and taken as question of law as the law of limitation ensure the certainty in the affairs of the men, to bring repose confidence in the court proceedings / decision as well as to bring an end to litigations after a certain period.

case_info: C.R No. 279-A of 2013 Muhammad Gulfam  Vs  M. Ayaz Khan
remarks:
(a)	An agreement to sell an immovable property squarely falls within the purview of the provisions of Article 17(2) of the Qanun-e-Shahadat, 1984, and must be compulsorily attested by two witnesses. This is a sine qua non for the validity of the agreement. If a document is required by law to be attested, it shall not be used as evidence until at least two attesting witnesses have been called for the purpose of proving its execution, provided they are alive, subject to the process of the Court, and capable of giving evidence. For the purposes of proving such an agreement, it is mandatory that two attesting witnesses be examined by the party, as required under Article 79 of the Qanun-e-Shahadat, 1984. The scribe of an agreement to sell immovable property is not a substitute for an attesting witness and does not legally qualify as such. Therefore, while his evidence may have a supportive value, it is neither in line with the mandate of law nor does it satisfy the requirements of Article 79 of the Qanun-e-Shahadat, 1984.
(b)	It is settled law that the plaintiffs could not be allowed to draw any benefits from the lapses or lacunas in the case of the defendants, and it is entirely for the plaintiff to prove their case independently as required under Article 117 of the Qanun-e- Shahadat Order, 1984. It is only thereafter that the burden would shift to the defendant to prove his own stance as required under Articles 118 and 119. The aforesaid burden of proof on the part of the plaintiff is enrooted in Article 117 of the Qanun-e-Shahadat Order, 1984, which declares that any person who desires a Court to issue a judgment in his favour has to prove all those facts which he has pleaded in his plaint. The evidence and record must reveal that the plaintiff has proved the existence of all those facts on which the plaint is based. The term “burden of proof” entails the burden of substantiating a case. The meaning of onus probandi is that if no evidence is produced by the party on whom the burden is cast, then such issue must be found against him. Lawsuits are determined on the preponderance or weighing of the scale of probabilities, in which the Court has to see which party has succeeded in proving its case and discharging the onus of proof. This must be scrutinized as a whole, together with contradictions, discrepancies, or inconsistencies, to enable the Court to detach the truth from falsehood. An endeavour should be made, in terms of the well-known metaphor, to “separate the grain from the chaff,” which connotes and obligates the Court to scrutinize and evaluate the evidence recorded in the lis judiciously and cautiously, in order to distinguish falsehood from truth, and to judge the quality—not the quantity—of the evidence.

case_info: RFA No. 25-M of 2017 Dr. Muhammad Ismail  Vs  Govt. of KP
remarks: High Court set aside the compensation rates fixed by both the initial Land Acquisition Collector and the various Referee Courts. Applying the constitutional principle of \"gold for gold,\" it held that the prime commercial land must be compensated at a uniform rate of Rs. 3,000 per square foot, along with mandatory additional charges and interest, to truly reflect its market and potential value at the time of acquisition. The government\'s appeals were dismissed.

case_info: W.P No.181-A of 2023 Javed Iqbal  Vs  Mst. Nosheen Rani etc
remarks:
(a) Where the lower fora make an error of law in deciding a matter, it becomes a jurisdictional issue, since such fora are only vested with the jurisdiction to decide a matter correctly.
