Hence, it must comply with regulatory conditions as specifically provided under Section 41(1) of the Ordinance.
4. A tractor-trolley combination, especially when used for commercial transport purposes, qualifies as a transport vehicle and necessitates compliance with the law regarding permits and vehicle fitness certificates. Utilizing such vehicles without a valid route permit and compliance with fitness standards is unlawful, and in that case these cannot operate on the public road.
(The constitutional petition was disposed of with the direction to authorities for proper adherence to law relating to mines and minerals and motor vehicles)

case_info: W.P No. 1382-A of 2021 Muhammad Irshad & Co... Vs  Secty. Govt of KPK C&W Department
remarks:
(a)	While making an award a Rule of the Court, the Court is not supposed to act mechanically, like a post office merely affixing its seal. Instead, it must examine the award; and if it finds patent illegality on the face of it, the Court may remit the award—or any matter referred to the arbitrator—for reconsideration, or set it aside. An arbitrator cannot act arbitrarily, capriciously, irrationally, or independently of the contract. A deliberate departure from, or conscious disregard of, the contract not only reflects a disregard of authority or misconduct but may also amount to mala fide action, thereby vitiating the award. In such circumstances, the Court may, on its own motion, modify or set aside the award if the facts and the demands of justice so require.
(b)	When an Award of arbitrator(s) is set aside by the Court of law and the matter is remitted/sent to arbitrator(s) for re-consideration of certain matter(s) pertaining to the agreement between the parties, the authority of arbitrator(s) is confined only for such reconsideration and not to give any findings on the admissibility or the applicability of any clause(s) of the agreement. Any findings to that extent would be beyond the scope of reference and must be discarded.
(c)	An Arbitrator cannot interpret a document in a manner that replaces the express terms of that document with his own views. It is not the function of the arbitrator to be influenced by personal imagination or experience in finding faults with respect to certain clauses of the contract between the parties. Rather, they should construe the contract as it is and ensure their interpretation is in conformity with the terms of reference, both in substance and spirit. If it is established that the arbitrator has traversed beyond the scope of his authority, or the terms of reference, award submitted by him would lose its legal sanction.

case_info: W.P No. 858-D of 2018 Dera Press Club  Vs  Govt. of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa etc
remarks:
(Writ Petition under Article 199 of the Constitution of Pakistan)
1. Petitioners have challenged the vires of Section 43(2) of the Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Press, Newspapers, News Agencies and Books Registration Act, 2013, by means whereof fee Rs.20,000/- was levied on the annual renewal of the registration of daily newspaper, on the ground that they being local newspaper having low circulation are required to be exempted from the said annual fee. --- Held--- according to the pre-amble and other provisions of the act there is no generic difference between the newspapers with regard to their circulation either on district level or nationwide, and also there is no restraint upon any of the newspapers to circulate at any place within the province or country. Similarly, there is no categorization of daily newspapers by virtue of its circulation, linguistics or number of pages. Besides, provincial assembly is fully competent to promulgate any law not enumerated in the Federal Legislative List. The legislative competence of the provincial assembly can only be circumscribed by express prohibition contained in the Constitution itself and unless and until there is any provision in the Constitution expressly prohibiting legislation on the subject either absolutely or conditionally, there is no fetter or limitation on the plenary powers.
2. Principles and guidelines to strike down a statute defined.
3. The function of legislation is the exclusive prerogative of the legislature. The wisdom of the legislature to promulgate a law and to achieve a particular object and purpose cannot be questioned and, therefore, it is presumed that laws have been legally, validly and constitutionally promulgated on the basis of its competence. The courts have no jurisdiction or power to rewrite the laws and the Constitution. The promulgated laws or its provisions cannot be struck down lightly and it is the duty of the courts to make every possible effort to reconcile the statute to the Constitution and to strike it down
when it becomes impossible to do so.
(The writ petition was dismissed in the circumstances).

case_info: Cr.A No. 878-M of 2024 Zahid Ullah  Vs  Muhamamd Ismail
remarks:
The Court has dismissed a criminal appeal filed by Zahid Ullah, challenging his conviction and sentence for murder and attempted murder. The appellant was convicted and sentenced to life imprisonment for murdering Masam Khan and attempting to murder Muhammad Ismail, his brother.
The prosecution`s case was based on eyewitness testimony from Muhammad Ismail (PW-9) and Kashif (PW-10), who claimed that the appellant opened fire on them after being reprimanded for making noise in the Baitak of their house.
The court held that:
1. The eyewitness testimony of PW-9 and PW-10 was confidence-inspiring and remained unshaken during cross-examination.
2. The medical evidence supported the prosecution`s case, with injuries on the bodies of the deceased and injured consistent with the eyewitness account.
3. The appellant`s motive for committing the crime was established as ill-will due to the reprimand.
4. The circumstantial evidence, including blood-stained earth and crime empties, supported the prosecution`s case.
The court ultimately upheld the conviction and sentence of the appellant, dismissing the appeal. Additionally, a connected criminal revision for enhancement of sentence was also dismissed.

case_info: Cr.A No. 474-P of 2024 Imtiaz Akhtar Vs  The State
remarks:
The Court has dismissed a criminal appeal filed by Imtiaz Akhtar, challenging his conviction and sentence for possession and transportation of narcotics. The appellant was convicted and sentenced to life imprisonment with a fine of Rs. 500,000 under Section 9(d) of the Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Control of Narcotic Substances Act, 2019.
The prosecution`s case was based on a tip-off received by the Anti-Narcotics Force (ANF) about the appellant`s plan to smuggle a large quantity of narcotics from Khyber Pakhtunkhwa to Punjab. The ANF officials conducted surveillance and stopped the Appellant`s vehicle, recovering 20 kilograms of charas and 12 kilograms of opium.
The court held that:
1. The prosecution had established the safe transmission and safe custody of the samples and contraband from the spot to the police station and from the police station to the Forensic Science Laboratory (FSL).
2. The testimony of the official witnesses, including the complainant and the marginal witness to the recovery memo, was confidence-inspiring and remained unshaken during cross-examination.
3. The FSL report confirmed the presence of charas and opium in the samples.
The court ultimately upheld the conviction and sentence of the appellant, dismissing the appeal.

case_info: W.P No. 509-B of 2025 Dr. Jamil Anwar  Vs  The Govt of KP Through Chief Secretary, KP Peshawar etc.
remarks:
Undoubtedly, the criminal cases are decided on the basis of material collected by the investigating officer during the course of investigation presented as evidence at the trial. In instant case, bare perusal of the contents of F.I.R lodged by the respondents, disclose commission of cognizable offence. The same prompted the local police to register the F.I.R in question. This court under its constitutional jurisdiction has no power to evaluate the material collected during the investigation. Hence, this Court would refrain from giving any finding on the merits of the case, lest it may prejudice the case of either party. The legal and factual issues raised in the present petition can be taken by the petitioner in the first instance before the Investigating Officer, and then, before the trial Court.
The FIR in question couldn’t be considered a result of malafide and ulterior motives. This Court, in its constitutional jurisdiction, cannot probe deeply into the merits of the case to outrightly conclude that the petitioner had no connection with aforesaid matter. Such a fact can only be seen after the prosecution has been given the opportunity to produce evidence and the defence has had the chance to cross-examine the witnesses. It is only after recording pro and contra evidence that a court seized of the matter would be able to render proper findings on the merits. Significantly, the law regarding the jurisdiction of this Court under Article 199 of the Constitution in matters involving disputed questions of fact is quite clear. It has been held frequently that the High Court cannot act as an investigator, nor can it control the investigation or any proceedings pending before a competent Court of law.
This Court of-course has the powers to quash FIR, but in exceptional circumstances, i.e., where the allegations made in the FIR, on the face of it, do not constitute a cognizable offence, or the allegations made in the FIR are false or its reflects that an attempt has been made to convert the civil liability into a criminal case, or where there is an express legal bar in any of the provisions of the Code or the concerned Act, under which a criminal proceeding is instituted.
[Writ Petition dismissed.]

case_info: Cr.A No. 337-M of 2024 Abdullah  Vs  The State
remarks:
Sections of law; - 302, 311, 148, 149, 338-C PPC
Issue with regard to remand of a case to learned trial Court for de-novo trial and framing if a distinct charge U/S 302 PPC instead of section 338-C PPC, in an eventuality, when accused person has been charged for dual murders of deceased lady as well as her unborn child by placing reliance on several verdicts of superior Courts, in respect of matter-in-issue, fully discussed.

case_info: W.P No. 5016-P of 2024 with IR  Abu Bakar Ali Shinwari  Vs  Maqsood Perviz & others
remarks:
Ejectment Petition under section 13 of Rent Restriction Ordinance1959.
Application for impleadment in the ejectment petition under order 1 rule 9&10 CPC.
It is an established law that Rent Controller cannot decide the question of title as to whether the vendor of the petitioner was the owner from whom he allegedly derives title of the rented premises and, thereafter, had entered into any agreement of tenancy with Malik Javed because these matters are pending adjudication before the Civil Court and even an application under Section 12(21 CPC is also subjudice. Learned Rent Controller has got limited jurisdiction to determine the relationship of landlord and tenant and ancillary matters thereto, determination of the ejectment of the tenants and fair rent. The petitioner may have purchased the property, but based on such purchase, neither could he resist the application for ejectment of the tenant nor his presence before the Rent Controller is indispensable. The petitioner may get determine his right of ownership not only against respondents No. I to 3, but also prove the validity, authenticity, and correctness of the deed through which he has allegedly purchased the rented premises, but before the court of ultimate jurisdiction and the Rent Controller cannot enter into any such dispu0e because of his limited jurisdiction. The tenant has already admitted his relationship with respondents No. I to 3 being their tenant, thus, on establishment of the grounds alleged by respondent No. I to 3 the tenant will have to surrender the possession of the rented premises to the landlord and thereafter, he or anyone including petitioner could file a suit for determination of his right before the civil court or through pending cases he may establish his title.

case_info: Cr.A No. 1164-P of 2023 Muhammad Akbar  Vs  The state etc
remarks:
True that the eyewitnesses are the close relatives of the deceased, but equally true that mere relationship would hardly be a circumstance for excluding the statements of the witnesses, who claimed to have seen the incident. If the defence would succeed in convincing regarding the malafide of the witnesses and if the record would suggest the interest of the witnesses to falsely implicate, then their relationship with the deceased would have damaged the case of the prosecution, but as their presence on the spot is supported by the investigating officer, and their identification before the police and before the doctor, has further confirmed their presence on the spot and in the hospital, at the time of occurrence and at the time of report.

We are not in happy mood to accept that the witnesses let off the real culprits by substituting an innocent person. Keeping in view the relationship between the appellant and the deceased
And keeping in view the relationship of the witnesses with the deceased, this Court is not inclined to accept that a real father would let off a real culprit and would substitute an innocent person for the killing.

Once this Court comes to the conclusion that the matter was promptly reported, accused was arrested near from the spot and the weapon of offence was recovered from his possession went positive, then little is left for hold the accused innocent.

To kill is the most abhorrent activity, but the cause that persuades would determine the punishment. It was the sudden death of his sister that shocked him to the core. It was his obsession which persuaded him to kill. He was obsessed and so was overtaken by the unholy thoughts which in turn pushed him to kill. It is his troubled mind that persuades us to interfere. So the instant criminal appeal is partially allowed and the sentence of death awarded to the appellant under section 302(b) PPC is reduced to that of imprisonment for life, whereas the remaining portion of the judgment is left undisturbed. The Murder Reference No.l7 /2023 is answered in NEGATIVE.

case_info: W.P No.1340-A of 2019 Tauqir Ahmad  Vs  Registrar, PHC and others
remarks:
(a)	Review is a discretionary relief and serves as an alternative to the primary remedy. A litigant may either challenge the judgment or order of the High Court before the Supreme Court, or, if the prescribed grounds exist, choose to seek a review before the same Court. However, even where valid grounds for review are established, it remains within the discretion of the Court to grant such relief. Therefore, a review is not a matter of right, even where the criteria for it are met.
(b)	The right to seek a review is not an inherent or absolute right. While a party may apply for a review of a Court’s decision, it is for the Court to decide whether such review is warranted. Furthermore, the requirement to deposit a security amount as a pre-condition for filing a review petition does not curtail the right to seek enforcement of any fundamental right.
(c)	The impugned Rule does not impose a fee or cost at the time of filing a review petition. Rather, it mandates a refundable security deposit, which is to be returned to the petitioner upon the success of the review. If the petition fails after due contest, the amount is awarded to the opposite party as minimal compensation for being subjected to further litigation. Thus, a litigant with genuine and sufficient grounds for review need not be deterred by the security requirement, as recovery is likely upon success. On the other hand, the requirement acts as a deterrent against frivolous or vexatious litigation, compensating the Court and the opposing party for unwarranted proceedings. Such a measure cannot be said to infringe any constitutional or legal right.
(d)	Frivolous review applications burden both the judiciary and the parties who have already secured a favorable outcome. Imposing costs on such applications is a globally accepted judicial practice aimed at discouraging misuse of the review jurisdiction, ensuring finality in litigation, and protecting judicial resources from abuse by habitual or chronic litigants.
(e)	The rules framed by the High Court in exercise of its powers under Article 202 of the Constitution are not subject to writ jurisdiction under Article 199. A writ petition cannot lie against a High Court, as the term “person” under Article 199(5) explicitly excludes the High Court. Permitting such a challenge would lead to an anomalous situation where judges are required to show cause before themselves, thereby violating the principle of judicial comity. Hence, any rule formulated collectively by the judges of the High Court is immune from challenge through a writ under Article 199.

case_info: RFA No. 363-P of 2022 Bashir Khan  Vs  Inam Ullah
remarks:
(a) Application for setting aside exparte decree granted under Order XXXVII Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 is made under rule 4 of order XXXVII CPC and not under Order 13 rule 4 CPC.
(b) In an application filed under rule 4 of order XXXVII CPC a party has to demonstrate that Special circumstances are made out for setting aside the exparte decree otherwise said decree cannot be set aside for the only reasons that the application has been filed within time.

case_info: FAO No. 117-P of 2024 M/s Black Bricks Builders & Developers Pvt. Ltd.  Vs  M/s Abasin Builders & Developers etc.
remarks:
(a)	When arbitration clause was admitted in the agreement and both parties were ready and willing to refer the matter to Arbitration then the Court should have adopted the mechanism provided in section 21 of Arbitration Act, 1940 and should have referred the matter for arbitration itself.
(b)	While opting to available one of the two legal options the court should always lean in favour of such an option which facilitate a citizen in exercising his fundamental right.

case_info: W.P No. 1062-M of 2024 Abdul Khaliq  VS Dr. Liaqat Ali Khan and others
remarks:
(A) As regards the bar contained in Article 247(7) ibid and notwithstanding its repeal, the superior courts have repeatedly held that such barring clause is to be very strictly construed and in cases where an error of jurisdiction or striking violation of law was floating on surface of the record, then such orders have consistently been held to be amenable to judicial review both before the High Courts as well as the Supreme Courts of Pakistan. Even otherwise, by the time when this writ petition was filed and is being heard, Art. 247 has since been omitted and is no more therein in the constitutional scheme governing matters relating to erstwhile Tribal Areas. This particular deletion / amendment in the Constitution may not affect the remedies and avenues which were available to litigating parties right on the date of filing of suit but that does not mean that the omitted barring clauses affecting jurisdiction of constitutional court would still survive. Once Art: 246 and 247 have been taken away from the letter of the Constitution, then bar of jurisdiction under Art. 247 (7) has also been removed. And writ petition in hand could be maintained, provided grounds of challenge come within the ambit of judicial review.
(B) A High Court while exercising powers of judicial review under Article 199 of the Constitution could not substitute the findings of facts arrived at by competent forum and Tribunal in routine as a court of appeal. Powers of judicial review conferred upon the High Court under 199 of the Constitution are very limited to the extent of correcting jurisdictional defects and patent violation of law arising out of the judgments impugned before it. Reappraisal of evidence or record and possibility of another conclusion per se are no valid grounds to question the judgments of competent forum as well as Tribunals / courts. In such cases, relator has to establish that the challenge brought before the High Court was on the ground of some jurisdictional defect and / or patent violation of law. Based on that, the preliminary objection raised by the respondents’ side is overruled being untenable.
(C) The above review jurisdiction is not made available to the courts as of routine to upset its earlier findings upon rehearing of the case and such powers are not for reopening of the past and closed matters unless errors or mistakes as aforesaid are floating on the surface of record. There is an abundance of case-law wherein the powers of a court to review its previous judgments has been dialed upon and from those judgments of the superior courts, following principles are deducible;
 	i. 	When there is a discovery of new fact or evidence, or when some mistake or error apparent on the face of record is found, then the power of review may be exercised.
 	ii. 	Law provides three grounds for review such as: (1) discovery of new and important matter or evidence which, after the exercise of due diligence, was not within knowledge of, or could not be produced by, the party seeking review at the time when the decree was passed or order made; (2) some mistake or error apparent on the face of the record. The third ground for review, i.e. “any other sufficient reason” is always held to be read as ejusdem generis in the context of two preceding grounds.
 	iii. 	What is meant by an error apparent on the fact of record is that it must be self- evident floating on the surface and not requiring elaborate discussion or process of ratiocination.

case_info: FAO No. 08-A of 2018 Zafar Ejazi & others  Vs  Malik Muhammad Ishaq
remarks:
The suit filed by the plaintiffs for recovery of damages was decreed ex- parte and the appellants herein moved an application for setting aside of said decree on the ground that their personal service was not effected during trial nor their correct address was mentioned in the plaint or summons. The learned trial Court dismissed such application where-against appeal in hand was filed…. Held
The defendants should have been served in person or through their agent empowered to receive/accept the service. In case the defendant was found not present on the given address, nor there was any authorized agent, then service could be made through male member of the defendant`s family, residing at the same address. Once the proper service is effected upon defendant then the Court gets jurisdiction to dispose of the matter.
The basic object of service on defendant is to enable him to resist the claim, if so desired. In safe administration of justice the Process Servers play a pivotal role, as the reports/affidavits sworn by them on the back of notices become a source of make or break for the cases. Therefore, they should take the pain and gather the exact information, inquire about the parties and in case of incomplete service, they are bound to follow the matter more than once in order to obtain proper service of parties and should not wait for the fixed date of hearing and shall take the matter earlier much before the date fixed, so that proper and personal service of the parties could be obtained within time. The process servers are also required to note-down all these efforts in their reports with utmost care and professional sincerity.
It is also the responsibility of Presiding Officers to look into the service effected by the Process Server and if necessary summon the concerned officials in order to inquire about the authenticity and correctness of effected service and should keep vigilance on the concerned officials and their reports on the back of notices. In the instant case, the report of the Process Server reveals that he had personally served the process upon each of the defendant, however, the signature of the recipient on each summons is identical, which itself negates the version of the process server and, as such, the provisions of Order-5 Rule-15, C.P.C have been violated. Similarly, substituted service has not been done in compliance to the provisions of Order-5 Rule-20 of C.P.C nor, serving official has been examined. As no personal service effected in the instant matter, nor procedure provided for service under the law has been followed, therefore, ex parte proceedings later on culminated into ex parte decree have not been passed in line with law.

case_info: W.P No. 326-A of 2023 Mushtaq Ahmed & 02 others  Vs  Ali Haider & others
remarks:
Husband filed a suit against his wife for issuance of direction to her to lift her dowry articles along with prayer for custody of the minor. The wife in her written statement sought dissolution of marriage, recovery of dower and maintenance etc. The learned family Court partially decreed the suit for recovery of dower and maintenance etc. while the appellate court dissolved the marriage between the parties on the basis of Khula…. Held
the plaintiff, while bringing his suit before the learned Family Court, just prayed for a direction to the defendant No.2 (wife) to lift her dowry articles with further prayer for the custody of minor, however, he has not prayed for restitution of conjugal rights. Such plea of the plaintiff itself clarifies his intention that he was not willing to rehabilitate defendant No. 2. It has also been brought in evidence that the plaintiff failed to provide any maintenance to the defendant No. 2 during the period of desertion. No doubt, the defendant No. 2 failed to prove any sort of physical torture on the part of plaintiff, but when the plaintiff himself is not willing to keep matrimonial relations with the defendant No. 2, by rehabilitating her and providing her proper maintenance, the defendant No. 2 cannot be forced to rehabilitate with the plaintiff. Moreover, the evidence also suggests that the plaintiff has contracted second marriage after obtaining permission from the concerned union council, which is also suggestive of the fact that he was not serious towards keeping matrimonial relations with the defendant No.2. The defendant No. 2, on the other hand contended that she was not being provided proper care and attention by the plaintiff, that’s why she was not happy with him and that from the day first of marriage, the plaintiff was intending to contract second marriage, that’s why he used to ignore her and never provided any maintenance to her. Similarly, it also came in evidence that defendant No. 2 was ready to rehabilitate with the plaintiff at second portion of the house, but her request was not acceded to. Such conduct of the plaintiff, i.e. his non-serious attitude towards rehabilitation of defendant No.2, non provision of proper maintenance and continuously disregarding his wife by not providing her proper care etc. coupled with his consistent desire for second marriage and thereafter materializing the same, clearly amounts to cruelty and entitles the defendant No. 2 for dissolution of her marriage on the basis of cruelty instead of Khula.
For grant of a decree for dissolution of marriage on the ground of Khula, the foundational requirement under Muhammadan Law is the wife’s clear expression of her desire to terminate the marital relationship on grounds of her inability to live with the husband ‘within the limits of Allah.’ Therefore, unless such a request has been made and is supported by evidence showing hatred, aversion, or incompatibility, no decree of Khula can be lawfully passed, thus, the appeal court has erred in granting the decree of dissolution of marriage on the basis of Khula when the wife had not prayed for the same.

case_info: C.R No. 709-P of 2008 Muhammad Sajjad Zamil and another Vs  Irshad Zamil & 28 others
remarks:
Gift of property and proof thereof:
It is worth to mention that allegedly deed was executed on 10.05.1987, stamp papers were purchased on 08.04.1987 and deed was registered on 03.08.1987, witnesses of the deed Ex PW-4/l deposed that they being illiterate were unaware about the contents of the deed. There was nothing in the statements of special attorney for petitioners and marginal witnesses about the offer, acceptance and transfer of possession. Moreover, the marginal witnesses of the deed were Fazal Dad and Taj Muhammad, who appeared before the court, but the transferor was identified by Mushtaq Hussain councilor and Niqab Shah, none of them was produced before the court. The date of purchase of the stamp papers, execution of the deed and attestation of the registered deed is not of one and the same date. Stamp papers were purchased on 08.04.1987, allegedly the deed was executed on 10.05.1987 whereas the witnesses deposed that all the proceedings were conducted on one and the same day. Lack of the evidence about the basic ingredients of gift, non-production of identifier, difference of three dates, marginal witnesses being illiterate cold not depose about the contents of the deed before the court were the grounds, which were taken into consideration by the learned appeal court. In such an eventuality neither the validity of the gift deed could be proved by the petitioners, nor the execution of the documents has been established through direct, cogent and reliable evidence.

case_info: Cr.A No. 41-D of 2024 Jamal ud Din and another  Vs  The State
remarks:
Control of Narcotic Substances — Section 9(d) — Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Control of Narcotics

case_info: R.F.A No. 03-B/2023 Shahid Imran  Vs Muhammad Khurshid
remarks:
As per Article 159 of the Limitation Act, 1908, read with Order XXXVII Rules 1 and 2 C.P.C., the period prescribed for submission of an application for leave to defend the suit under summary procedure referred to in section l28(2)(f) or under order XXXVII of the CPC, 1908 is ten days from the date of service of summons. In the present case, the appellant appeared before the trial Court on 22.03.2022, and ex-parte proceedings against him were set aside on 07.06.2022. However, the application for leave to appear and to defend the suit was submitted belatedly on 07.07.2022. exceeding one month from the date of ex-parte proceedings were set aside without an application for condonation the delay. The scheme of Order XXXVII C.P.C. is distinct: it permits a plaintiff to obtain a quick judgment in cases based on negotiable instruments orwritten contracts unless the defendant secures leave to defend within the stipulated time. The right to defend is not automatic but conditional on disclosure of a sufficient cause. In absence of timely leave, the allegations in the plaint are deemed admitted, and the plaintiff becomes entitled to a decree.
The appellant, despite appearing before the trial Court, failed to comply with the mandatory period for seeking leave to appear and to defend the suit and offered no legal justification or request for condonation of delay. Therefore, the trial Court was fully justified in striking off his defence and decreeing the suit.

case_info: Cr.A No. 195-M of 2021 Managing Director SNGPL & 05 others  Vs  Fakhar Ul Islam
remarks:
1.	The appellants, in an attempt to justify the impugned demand, have relied on the testimony of Mian Mirwes Saeed, Incharge SNGPL Malakand Division (DW-2), who claimed that the disputed amount was assessed under the head of “fault volume charges” for the period from 12.02.2017 to 29.11.2017, based on a laboratory analysis of the plaintiff’s gas meter. He produced documents including the MIR report, calculation sheet, final bill and history sheet (Ex.DW-2/1 to Ex.DW-2/4). However, several critical shortcomings were revealed during cross-examination. No vigilance team had visited the plaintiff`s premises prior to the meter replacement to detect any fault. The meter had been regularly read by the designated meter reader namely Ayub Khan (DW-3) during the relevant period, who unequivocally stated that no fault was ever observed in the meter and that all bills were duly issued and paid. Moreover, the meter was replaced without issuing prior notice to the plaintiff, and no meter replacement pro forma, bearing the plaintiff`s or the SNGPL employee`s signatures, as required by departmental procedure, was ever produced in court. The laboratory report, which was the main basis of the appellants’ claim, was not presented before the court in its original form. It was neither certified nor verified and the witness who submitted it in court was not the person who had prepared or signed the report. The record also shows that the meter was replaced on 29.11. 2017 but the same was received by the laboratory on 20.02.2018. It has not been explained that where and in whose custody the meter had been kept during the intervening period nor it has been explained that why the meter was dispatched to laboratory with such an inordinate delay. Likewise, it cannot be ascertained that the meter had been sealed right at the time of its removal from the premises, hence manipulation thereof cannot be ruled out in the circumstances. Additionally, the person who transmitted the meter to the laboratory was neither named nor produced before the court. These procedural lapses have rendered the laboratory report legally unreliable and deprived it of any evidentiary value.
 2. Legally, the learned trial court has correctly concluded that the entire billing based on the unverified laboratory report, in the absence of procedural compliance, was unjustified and without legal sanction. The foundational principle of due process in utility regulation demands that the consumer must be put on notice prior to adverse billing actions, particularly when the charges pertain to retrospective fault volume assessments. In this case, not only was there no prior notification of any alleged defect or disconnection, but the evidence also confirmed consistent usage and billing without objection throughout the relevant period. The claim of joint business activity between the plaintiff and defendant No.1 was also unsubstantiated, as there was no evidence on record to prove continuity of the business after 15.06.2017, when the premises were admittedly handed over back to the plaintiff and used merely as a go down. The uncontroverted narration of defendant No.1 that he had ceased business activity since December 2016 further negates the assertion of continued commercial usage. Accordingly, the trial court has rightly disbelieved the appellant`s version and held that the disputed bill was assessed in violation of procedural safeguards, based on inadmissible evidence, and without establishing the plaintiff’s liability. Thus, the findings recorded by the learned trial court merit no interference.

case_info: Cr.Misc BCA No. 30-B of 2023 Samiullah Khan etc  Vs  Najib Ullah alias Najju, etc
remarks:
BCA
Held: (i) The issue of granting or refusing bail has been extensively addressed and clarified by the superior courts through various judgments and precedents. There is now a judicial consensus that in order to seek bail on medical ground, the accused must establish that the illness is life threatening; that its treatment is not possible within the jail premises; that continued detention would pose a serious risk to the life of the accused; and that the disease is contagious or transmissible in nature. In the absence of these conditions, bail cannot be granted on medical ground.
(ii) In this context, the record was thoroughly examined to determine whether the accused/ respondent’s case met the established parameters for bail as laid down by superior Courts. The Court found that the record did not show the injuries sustained by the accused/respondent to be life threatening, or their treatment being not possible within the jail premises, and his detention would pose a serious threat to his life. The Anti- Terrorism Court (ATC) released Najib Ullah, alias Najju, on bail based on an inconclusive so-called medical report and personal observations that his left eye had been removed and his right eye was still under treatment and more particularly that he required a stick and also human support to attend Court, indicating incomplete recovery. The trial Court’s record shows that after his release on bail due to medical reasons, the accused Najib Ullah, alias Najju, consistently attended the Anti-Terrorism Court (ATC) proceedings, suggesting he was in good health and stable.
(iii) The record shows that the accused’s bail application was dismissed on its merits by the Anti-Terrorism
Court (ATC) on January 24, 2024. Despite the initial dismissal, the accused filed another application for bail on medical grounds. The trial Court considered an inconclusive medical report from the Medical Officer of Central Jail, Bannu, dated May 20, 2022, and granted bail to the accused based on medical grounds, relying on personal observations instead of referring the accused to a Medical Board for a thorough evaluation. Legally speaking, granting bail on medical ground based on personal observation rather than thorough medical evaluations or expert opinions contradicts established legal principles and is unwarranted.
(iv) The argument that the accused hasn`t misused bail is irrelevant in this case since bail was granted on medical grounds, not on merits.
(BCA allowed)

case_info: W.P No. 5340-P/2024 Madad khan  Vs  Mst. Samiya Nisar and others
remarks: This Court understands that welfare of the child is paramount consideration in any decision regarding custody or visitation rights, however, at the same time, cannot ignore that non-custodial parent has an inherent right to maintain relationship with his/her children. This right is crucial for the emotional and psychological development of the child. The superior Courts of this country have consistently held that denying visitation rights can lead to emotional deprivation and parental alienation syndrome, where the child may develop negative feelings towards the non-custodial parent due to lack of interaction. As stated above, the welfare of the minor is the primary consideration when determining visitation rights. Courts must evaluate the potential impact of visitation on the child\'s well-being, especially if there are concerns about the child\'s mental health or safety. Thus, in such circumstances, Courts often establish structured visitation schedules to ensure that the non-custodial parent can spend meaningful time with the child and for this purpose, visitation may be arranged on weekends, during holidays or special occasions with specific times for pickups and drop-off. In cases, there are concerns about the child\'s safety or well-being, Courts may impose conditions on visitation. This could include supervised visits, the presence of a mental health professional or the requirement for the non-custodial parent to provide surety bonds to ensure the child\'s safe return.

case_info: Cr.A No. 402-B of 2023 Said Raheem  Vs  The State etc.
remarks:
Perusal of site plan there are two room towards the western side attached with the bathroom, At point No.1 deceased has been shown while at point No.2 the complainant/PW-7 while point No.3 is the place of appellant Said Rahim and point-4 shows the place of appellant Ibrahim Shah. Now it is in the evidence that the appellant entered through the entrance which has no proper gat. No doubt when the firing was made allegedly by the appellant from point No.3 and appellant at point No.4, there are no bullet marks behind the wall at point No.1 & 2. It has also be reported from the mouth of complainant that his life was also attempted but ineffectively it means that he was also in the firing of appellant Said Rahim and Ibrahim. It is also in the evidence of PW-7 complainant that he took shelter from the volley of shots in the old baitak of Hazrat Ali Shah which is attached with the baitak in which the alleged occurrence has taken
place. The place which has been shown as old baitak of Hazrat Ali Shah its gate opened towards the Northern side and this old baitak has no gate towards the new baitaik in which the alleged occurrence has taken place, which fact falsify the version of the complainant/PW- 7 that he took shelter from the volley of shots in the old baitak. Moreover, nor the complainant has shown a specific place in the old baitak nor the investigating officer had tried or asked the complainant to show the place specifically where he took shelter.

case_info: C.R No. 516-P of 2020 Asliyat Khan etc Vs  Mst. Saeeda and others
remarks:
> Determination of Parentage of a child- Establishment of pertenage- \"The fact that any person was born during the continuance of a valid marriage between his mother and any man, or within two hundred and eighty days after its dissolution, the mother remaining unmarried, shall be conclusive proof that he is the legitimate son of that man, unless it can be shown that the parties to the marriage had no access to each other at any time when he could have been begotten.\"
> Limitation- Inheritance- Undeibly the impugned inheritance mutation No.1707 was entered and attested on 08.10.1999 whereas, the petitioners have brought their suit on 24.02.2016 i.c. after more than 17 years without giving any convincing justification for not seeking the relief at the earliest.

case_info: W.P No. 6625-P of 2018 Provincial Government of KP  Vs  Malik Qasim Khan etc
remarks: (a) Constitution of Islamic Republic of Pakistan-Article 199 -- Khyber Pakhtunkhwa, Public Property (Removal of Encroachment) Act, 1977-S (2)(h)-S-3-Notice of removal of encroachment-challenge to-Contention of the respondents before the Tribunal was that they are owners in possession of the suit property through allotments and sale agreements and have obtained NOC from the concerned authorities for construction of Model Town hence notice for removal of encroachment is wrong, illegal and ineffective upon their rights-Record reveals that the respondents are joint owners/co-owners in the suit property alongwith the Provincial Government-held that in case of joint ownership, a co-owner cannot be dispossessed, without having recourse to partition and though the respondents are in possession of land in access to their entitlement but they can not be held to be illegal occupant within the meaning of S.2(h) of the Act of 1977 hence such co-owner cannot be served with a notice under S.3 of the Act, as they hold the suit property not as encroachers but co-owners/co-sharers and they can only be evicted from the suit property/access land by approaching the competent forum for partition.

case_info: Tax Reference No. 13-A of 2016 M/S Mir "A" Bakers and Sweets  Vs  Director Intelligence& Investigation and others
remarks:
Exercise of jurisdiction by the Officer(s) of Directorate General of Intelligence & Investigation, Peshawar without limits as to area, persons or class of persons under SRO-776(1) of 2011 was violative of section 30 of Sales Tax Act, 1990.
It is settled law that an audit must be conducted under Section 25 of the Sales Tax Act, 1990. A ‘desk audit’ or mere ‘analysis of data’ is not a substitute for a proper audit under law. While such analysis may generate information that could lead to a proper audit under Section 25, it cannot itself be the basis for imposition of tax liability.

case_info: Cr.A No. 375-M of 2023 Muhammad Jalal VS The State.
remarks:
The circumstances are strikingly similar, wherein appellant aftab-ud-Din has been convicted and sentenced under Section 338-C PPC instead of Section 302 PPC for causing the death of fetus. In such scenario, we see no reason to depart from the view already taken by this Court in Wahidullah case (2021 PCrLJ 1706).
In view of the above, the instant as well as the connected appeal are allowed, the impugned judgment dated. 22.11.2022 is set aside and the case is remanded back to the learned trial court for do novo trial after framing of distinct charges against the appellant/accused facing trial under section 302 PPC   (instead of section 338-C PPC) for murders of both Mst. Noreen bib as well as unborn child.

case_info: Sales Tax Reference No. 25/2024 M/s National Logistic Cell (NLC).  Vs  The Additional Collector, Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Revenue Authority and others
remarks:
In the matters of tax, the apex court held in case of Commissioner Inland Revenue Karachi that while interpreting fiscal statutes, the court looks to what is clearly said and there is no room for any intendment nor is there any equity about a tax. There is no presumption as to tax and nothing was to be read in or implied and one could only look fairly at the language used. In the present situation, the words \'or otherwise\' clearly indicate that default surcharge can be imposed even if the default is not wilful. The addition of the phrase \'or otherwise\' after the word \'wilfully\' removes any ambiguity and makes it clear that whether the default is wilful or due to any other reason, default surcharge can still be imposed.
The petitioner relied upon a judgment of the Sindh High Court in the case of Commissioner Inland Revenue, Karachi. However, the facts of that case are distinguishable from the present case. In that matter, the Sindh High Court addressed the issue of default surcharge under sections 161 and 205 of the Income Tax Ordinance, 2001. The petitioner also relied on a judgment of the Lahore High Court in the case of United Sugar Mills Ltd, but the facts of that case are also distinguishable from the instant case, as the matter under adjudication was not related to default surcharge. The Lahore High Court addressed issues under the Sales Tax Act, 1990, particularly concerning the proof of payment, the demand for additional tax, and penalties on advances received by the petitioner.
On the other hand, the Islamabad High Court in the case of M/S Attock Refinery Limited, held that default surcharge under section 34 of the Sales Tax Act is automatically applicable in cases of non-payment of tax, regardless of whether the non-payment was willful or inadvertent.

case_info: Cr.A No. 209-M of 2024 Javeed Khan  Vs  The State
remarks:
The applicability of Section 311, P.P.C. to the present case, under which the learned trial court has awarded life imprisonment to the appellant, it is pertinent to first reproduce the said provision, which was last amended through the Criminal Law (Amendment) (Offences in the Name of or on Pretext of Honour) Act, 2016:
311. Ta`zir after waiver or compounding of right of qisas in qatl-i-amd.-
Where all walis do not waive or compound the right of qisas, or if the principle of fasad-fil-arz is attracted, the court may, having regard to the facts and circumstances of the case, punish an offender against whom the right of qisas has been waived or compounded with death or imprisonment of life or imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to fourteen years as ta`zir:
Provided that if the offence has been committed in the name or on the pretext of honour, the punishment shall be imprisonment for life.
A plain reading of Section 311 PPC makes it abundantly clear that a court may award punishment under this provision in cases where the principle of fasad-fil- arz is attracted but only in situations where the right of Qisas has been waived or where a compromise has been effected in relation to a sentence awarded as Ta’zir. In the present case, however, neither was the punishment awarded as Qisas, nor has there been any waiver or compounding of the offence by the legal heirs. Moreover, no compromise under Section 345 Cr.P.C. has been effected in respect of the punishment of Ta’zir awarded under Section 302(b) PPC. In this context, the learned trial court’s decision to award punishment under Section 311 PPC apparently on the assumption that the circumstances attracted the principle of fasad-fil-arz is legally untenable and misconceived. The essential preconditions for the applicability of Section 311 PPC namely the existence of a Qisas-based sentence or a compromise in relation to Ta’zir were simply not met. Therefore, the reliance on fasad-fil-arz to justify sentencing under Section 311 PPC in the absence of these statutory triggers is wholly misplaced. Even otherwise, if the essential preconditions for attracting the provision of Section 311 PPC are satisfied, the court cannot award punishment under Section 311 PPC when no clear evidence is available to constitute the element of fasad-fil-arz.

case_info: W.P No. 90-M of 2024 Ihsan Khan  Vs  Govt of KPK
remarks:
1. The court relies on key precedents to support its decision. In Government of the Punjab vs. Mubarik Ali Khan (PLD 1993 SC 375), the Supreme Court applied the “job description test” to grant Secretariat Allowance to Lahore High Court employees, noting their similar functions to employees of the Federal Shariat Court and Supreme Court. Additionally, cases like Rehman Medical College vs. Wafa Gul (2022 SCMR 36) and Employees of the Pakistan Law Commission vs. Ministry of Works (1994 SCMR 1548) emphasize principles of equal pay and treatment under the law, highlighting that discrimination without reasonable classification violates Article 25 of the Constitution.
2. Support staff in the police department, play a crucial role in enabling senior officials to perform their duties effectively. Given this, the court finds it reasonable and plausible for the petitioners to be entitled to Risk Allowance. Drawing an analogy with the Judicial Allowance case, where support staff and judges are treated as one class without distinction, the court directed that similar treatment should be extended to the petitioners.

case_info: C.M No. 12(2) No.24-P of 2024 in W.P No. 762/2011 Sadiq Ali and others  Vs M.D PTET & other
remarks:
Section 12(2) CPC:
In civil litigation, Section 12(2) of the Civil Procedure Code (CPC) provides an extraordinary remedy to aggrieved parties who believe that a judgment decree or order has been obtained through fraud, misrepresentation, or want of jurisdiction. The provision stands as a safeguard against miscarriage of justice and upholds the sanctity of legal proceedings by ensuring that the courts are not misled into delivering unjust decisions. This section, therefore, bars the institution of a separate suit and mandate s that the proper forum for challenging such a decision is the same court that passed it. Section 12(2) is not a substitute for appeal of revision. It is a collateral remedy meant to address only specific grievances: i.e. Fraud: which is a deliberate deception to secure an unjust advantage, Misrepresentation such as false representation that misleads the court and Want of Jurisdiction as a judgment rendered by a court that lacked legal authority.
It is settled law that Section 12(2) cannot be invoked merely on the ground that a plea or issue raised during trial was not considered or decided, unless such omission was the result of fraud
or misrepresentation. In the case o[ applicants, the plea about the status of the respondents (petitioners of writ petitions) was raised, replied, details provided by them (employees) and the court has decided the lis, the petition there against failed, moreover, the applicants could not prove the factum of alleged fraud & misrepresentation through the contents of applications or during arguments.
(i)Whether a person who has contested the lis submitted a reply, and was afforded an opportunity of being heard-where after the lis was decided on merits can subsequently challenge the judgment under Section 12(2) C.P.C.?
ii) Whether, in deciding the writ petitions during the earlier round of litigation. The respondents/petitioners committed any fraud concealed material facts, or misrepresented events in relation to the proceedings before this Court?
iii) Whether a order can be challenged through application under Section 12 C.P.C - if a plea taken during the trial was not specification addressed or decided in the final determination?
Section 12(2) cannot be invoked merely on the sounds that a plea or issue raised during trial was not considered or decided. Unless such omission wasthe result of fraud or misrepresentation.
Failure to consider a legal does not constitute fraud or misrepresentation unless the opposing party actively concealed facts or manipulated the proceedings.
Application under Section 12(2) is not a substitute for an appeal or review, even where the court has ignored a material plea or evidence.

case_info: Cr.A No. 358-B of 2023 Imran Khan  Vs  The state etc
remarks:
The bare reading of the statement of complainant (PW-7), who is the sole eye witness of the incident and brother of the deceased. This witness when asked as to what is his profession, he stated in his cross-examination that "I am serving in Live Stock Department since 03 years and at the time of occurrence my deceased brother Iftikhar Ali Shah was residing in his separate house. He also admitted it correct that after the death of my deceased brother Gohar Ali Shah, an F.I.R was lodged
against us in respect of forcibly taking accused Imran Khan." It was for the prosecution to have produced some evidence that on the day of occurrence as the same had taken place at 10:30 a.m, on 20.05.2019, at such time the complainant (PW-7) is supposed to have been at his office of Live Stock Department. In rebuttal, he did not bring anything on record that on the day, date and time of incident he was not in office rather was present at the eventful time with his deceased brother.
Moreover, the deceased and the complainant (PW-7) were residing in separate houses as admitted in the above narration and it is the stance of complainant in the F.I.R that he on the date of incident were going back to their village after purchase of house hold commodities with his brother when they were stopped by the accused party and no sooner they deboarded from the motorbike the accused and his co-accused started firing at them. Persual of the site-plan Ex.PB shows that accused has been shown at Point No.4, co-accused at Point No.5, the deceased at Point No.1 and complainant at Point No2. It is part of record that on the day of incident one passerby Noor Zali Khan has also received Fire-arm injuries which led to his death, has been shown at Point No.3 and he after hitting had fallen at Point No.3-A. The site-plan also reveals as has been said by the complainant (PW-7) that he on the day of occurrence after purchasing the house hold articles and commodities from Bannu Bazar, were going towards their house, whereas, the site-plan show Bannu city towards Northern side and the village of complainant towards Southern side, and if it is considered that in this country left hand drive is allowed so in the F.I.R the deceased and accused alongwith complainant has been shown towards right side of the road which suggest otherwise that the occurrence has not been taken place in the mode and manner as alleged by the prosecution.
It does not appeal to a prudent mind that complainant (PW-7) has been shown at Point No.2 closed to the Point No.1, the place of the deceased. Now if one see Point No.3 which is also closed to the deceased towards East side of the spot and he has received Fire-arm injuries at the hands of the appellant and absconding co-accused. The bare perusal shows that he, the complainant is absolutely is within the firing range of accused at Point No.4 and 5, the point of the appellant and absconding
co-accused, thus, by not receiving even a scratch on his body reflects his absence on the spot. Had the complainant been present at the relevant time he might have not been spared in order to depose against the accused.
Another significant aspect of the case is that it has not been disclosed by the complainant, but the investigating officer while preparing the site-plan the place of the hitting of passerby Noor Zali Khan has been shown at Point No.3, as per the foot notes, who fall and succumbed to his injuries at Point-3A which speaks otherwise to the testimony of the complainant (PW-7), as there is no trail of blood in between Points No.3 and 3A and that he did not record such a version in his report. Moreover, we have also noticed from the F.I.R wherein the alleged occurrence has taken place at 10:30 a.m while the same was reported at 11:30 a.m. If we see and peruse the PM report of the deceased Noor Zali Khan the same clear cut reveals that the dead body was examined by the doctor at the hospital on the same day at 10:30 a.m, while that of deceased Iftikhar Ali Shah at 10:35
a.m, thus, it has been confirmed that on one hand as per murasila the complainant (PW-7) has recorded his report of the occurrence at 11:20 a.m while on the other hand the doctor had examined the dead bodies of both the deceased at 10:30 a.m and 10:35 a.m respectively, 50 minutes before lodging of the report which eliminates the presence of the complainant at the relevant time of incident with the deceased.
Two accused have been charged by the complainant for the commission of the offence. The investigating officer when visited the spot, collected 04 crime empties of .30 bore near from the places of both the accused on the spot. The F.S.L report Ex.PK/2 shows that the same were fired from one and the same .30 bore weapon which suggests the commission of offence by a single accused.
The motive advanced was stated to be the previous blood feud, but the Investigating Officer did not bring on record copies of the criminal cases between the parties. If we admit that it was the blood feud between the parties which led to the death of the deceased, in that eventuality the complainant would have also been the prime target if he was present on the spot. There is no cavil to the proposition that once the prosecution alleges a motive then it is under the bounden duty to prove the same, failing which it is the prosecution to suffer and the present case is no exception.
[Criminal appeal allowed, conviction of the accused by the lower fora is set-aside.]

case_info: C.R No.692-P of 2015 Mst. Shamim etc Vs  Mst.Mumtaz Begum and others
remarks: When findings of civil court, on question of fact, are at variance with the findings of an appellate Court then revisional court should give preference to the findings of appellate forum.

case_info: Cr.A No. 1681-P of 2023 Khan Muhammad  Vs  Shafi ullah etc
remarks:
(a) Corroboration is only a rule of caution and not a rule of law. If the eye-witness account is found reliable and trustworthy, then there is hardly any need to look for corroboration.
(b) When witnesses are examined after years of the occurrence, a photographic narration of the incident cannot be expected from them. The creeping in of minor contradictions in the testimony of prosecution witnesses, with the passage of time, is natural and can easily be ignored. Merely on the basis of such contradictions, the statement of a prosecution witness cannot be discarded if it is corroborated by other incriminating material.
(c) It is a well-recognized principle of criminal justice that relationship by itself is no ground to discard or discredit the testimony of eye-witnesses, unless they are shown to be partisan or interested witnesses. In the absence of any ulterior motive or animus for the false implication of an accused, the confidence-inspiring testimony of an eye-witness—whose presence with the deceased at the time and place of occurrence is established—cannot be discarded merely due to his relationship with the deceased.
(d) While dealing with murder cases falling within the ambit of Section 302, P.P.C., the Trial Court must evaluate whether the act was committed in furtherance of common intention or based on individual liability, and apply Section 302(b) or 302(c), P.P.C., accordingly. The Court must give a definite finding in this regard. Any judgment concluding that the offence under Section 302(b), P.P.C., was committed in furtherance of common intention or common object, but imposing the sentence on the basis of individual liability, would be contrary to the intent and spirit of the law. However, if the Court concludes that common intention or common object is not established, then each accused shall be dealt with under Section 302(c), P.P.C., according to their individual role and the severity of the allegations, and sentenced accordingly, using the Court’s discretionary powers.
(e) The provision of Section 302, P.P.C. provides punishment for the commission of Qatl-i-amd. The punishment for Qatl-i-amd is categorized under clauses (a), (b), and (c). Section 302(a), P.P.C., reflects the punishment under the Islamic system by way of Qisas. The word “Qisas” means return of evil for evil; it denotes retaliation. Another synonymous term is “retribution,” which refers to punishment inflicted in return for wrongdoing, stressing the administration of strict justice. The application of Section 302(a), P.P.C., provides for equal retaliation—putting the offender to death in the same manner in which he caused the death of the victim.
However, certain legal requirements must be fulfilled for the application of Section 302(a), P.P.C. The legislature has specifically laid down that the initiation of proceedings under this section is subject to the qualifying prerequisites of Section 304, P.P.C., and a voluntary and true confession regarding the commission of the offence, in accordance with the postulates of Article 17 of the Qanun-e-Shahadat Order, 1984. Section 302(a), P.P.C., is reserved for the most serious cases of premeditated murder. The Court must be satisfied beyond doubt that the accused acted with full intent and purpose to take the life of another.
(f) Section 302(c) PPC addresses scenarios where death has occurred, but the evidence does not conclusively establish premeditated intent. This clause is invoked when the killing results from sudden provocation, an emotional reaction, or lacks a clear motive. It is also applicable where contradictions exist between medical and ocular evidence, where a non-lethal weapon is used, or when only a
single injury is inflicted. Key indicators for the application of Section 302(c) PPC are:
(i) Single injury or lack of repetition – A single blow, especially without targeting vital parts, weakens the case for intentional killing.
(ii) Sudden quarrel or heat of passion – A death resulting from a spontaneous clash, without planning, supports mitigation.
(iii) Non-lethal weapon usage – When the weapon used is not ordinarily intended to kill, such as a stick or rod, the court may infer lack of intent.
(iv)Weak prosecution evidence – Doubts arising from inconsistencies between the medical report and the ocular account benefit the accused.
(v) Absence of motive – Where motive is unclear or absent, premeditation becomes difficult to prove.
Section 302(c) PPC is rooted in the principle of proportionality. It ensures justice without excess by differentiating between intentional murder and killings resulting from circumstances beyond cold deliberation.

case_info: QP No. 13-B of 2024 Feroz Khan  Vs  The State etc
remarks:
Quashment Petition
Held: (i) The grounds for declining the petitioner’s plea as considered by both subordinate Courts, were twofold. Firstly, the petitioner was not the last possessor of the recovered money. Secondly, no identification parade was conducted to ascertain whether the recovered banknotes were indeed the same as those allegedly robbed from the petitioner / complainant.
(ii) As far as the first ground is concerned, suffice it to say that the instant case is one of robbery. The petitioner / complainant has specifically mentioned in the FIR that he was robbed of his money. The record confirms that the recovery was made on the pointation of the accused. In such circumstances, the observations that the petitioner / complainant was not the last possessor are without basis / unfounded.
(iii) As regards the second ground, suffice it to say that the petitioner / complainant was robbed of the cash amount, which he had freshly withdrawn from the bank. It is understandable that the complainant could not recall the details of each banknote, especially when the amount involved was a substantial sum of Rs.26,00,000/-. Therefore, even if an identification parade had been conducted, it would not have served any useful purpose insofar as the plea of the petitioner for interim custody of the recovered money is concerned.
(iv) Be that as it may, the petitioner is the complainant in the instant case, and as per the record, the recovered amount is shown to be part of the robbed money. While the guilt of the accused on the basis of the recovery is yet to be adjudged at the trial, the petitioner / complainant has succeeded in tentatively establishing his entitlement to the interim custody of the recovered amount.
(Petition allowed)

case_info: WP No. 5260-P of 2024 Mst. Musarat   Vs  The Govt of KP through CS
remarks: Widowed or divorced daughters would fall in the definition of family, for the purpose of pension rules for lifetime or till re-marriage.

case_info: Cr.A No. 626-P of 2024 Stana dar  Vs  M. Ayeen etc
remarks:
(a) An injured witness—someone who has sustained injuries during the incident—holds a unique and significant position in legal proceedings. Their testimony is often considered highly reliable for several reasons. First, their injuries serve as tangible evidence of their presence at the scene, lending credibility to their account. Second, being directly affected by the incident, such a witness is generally less likely to have a motive to falsely implicate another person.
(b) While appreciating the evidence, the Court must avoid attaching undue importance to minor discrepancies. Discrepancies that do not affect the core or salient features of the prosecution`s case should be disregarded. The accused cannot claim an advantage based solely on such insignificant inconsistencies. If excessive importance is given to minor contradictions, it would become nearly impossible to secure convictions. Minor discrepancies in the statements of eyewitnesses are often the result of the passage of time and natural human error, and can therefore be overlooked in the interest of fair administration of justice.
(c) According to the provisions of Section 337-N(2), P.P.C., a punishment of imprisonment by way of Ta`zir can only be awarded if the convict is a “previous convict, habitual, hardened, desperate or dangerous criminal,” or if the offence has been committed in the name or on the pretext of honour. In cases relating to the causing of hurt, unless the criteria laid out in Section 337-N(2), P.P.C. are met, the accused cannot be awarded a sentence of imprisonment by way of Ta`zir. The non- obstante clause in subsection (2) gives it overriding effect over all other provisions related to hurt under Chapter XVI of the P.P.C. As it stands, Section 337-N(2), P.P.C. contemplates that in all cases of hurt, the default punishment is payment of Arsh or Daman. Factors to be considered for awarding Ta`zir punishment include the facts and circumstances of the case, the nature of the injury or hurt caused, the weapon used, and whether the act was committed in a brutal or shocking manner that outrages public conscience or disturbs societal harmony.

case_info: W.P No.2784-P of 2024 M/s Noor Jannast Shah Proprietor  Vs Director General (KP) NAB etc.
remarks: When an Act is not found falling in the definition of \"offences\" for the purpose of NAB Ordinance, then NAB Authorities would be completely precluded from enquiring or investigating said mater.

case_info: C.R No. 370-P of 2025 Zeeshan Ahmad  Vs  Shukat Ali
remarks: “A co-sharer cannot be allowed to alter the nature of the joint land unless it is partitioned.”

case_info: Cr.A No. 58-M of 2023 Javaid  Vs  Sher Zaman & others
remarks:
(a) Scope and extent of Illegal Dispossession Act, 2005.
(b) Act of 2005 envisages two distinct and different provisions i.e. quasi-criminal and quasi-civil, therefore, proceedings under this Act could not be termed to that of exclusively criminal in nature, in view of certain powers, such as, attachment of property and to pass an order as an interim arrangement to evict an accused from disputed property and hand it over to aggrieved party i.e. complainant.
(c) Act of 2005 provides for two eventualities i.e. firstly: - learned trial Court U/S 8 (1) of Act can restore possession to an aggrieved person i.e. complainant without awarding punishment of imprisonment to an accused person and secondly: - learned trial Court U/S 6 of Act as an interim arrangement can evict a trespasser or an illegal occupier from disputed property and hand it over to complainant. Even upon conclusion of trial, nominated accused can be provided punishment up to ten years U/S 3 of Act and if a Court comes to a conclusion that an owner or occupier of property has illegally been dispossessed, then in such eventuality, trial Court can render direction to nominated accused to restore possession of a property to an owner or occupier, as the case may be.

case_info: Cr.Misc(B.A) No. 611-P of 2025 Abdur Rahman alias Bilawal  Vs  The State and another
remarks:
1.	In light of Article 46 of the Qanun-e-Shahadat Order, 1984 no specific form or forum is required for a dying declaration.
2.	The postmortem report, particularly the timeline regarding the arrival of the deceased at the hospital in an injured condition and the subsequent time of death, lends ample corroboration to the prosecution version. It supports the assertion that the deceased had made a conscious and voluntary statement identifying the petitioner as the assailant while enroute to the hospital.
3.	In this case, the initial report (Murasila), which directly implicates the present petitioner on the basis of the deceased\'s dying declaration, constitutes a material piece of evidence, hence cannot be summarily discarded or treated as inconsequential at this stage.
4.	The presence of the petitioner at the scene of the incident is prima facie established in light his own report, which is purportedly the cross version of the FIR lodged by complainant Bilal.
5.	Although the cross-versions of a case may be considered for granting bail to accused but the rule is not universally applicable as it is settled law that mere existence of a cross-version cannot • alone be considered a sufficient ground to grant bail to an accused.
6.	Clause (c) of Section 10(7) of the Act ibid lays certain limitations for exercising the discretion. According to said provision, if the Court would come to the conclusion that reasonable grounds were available to believe that such child was involved in an offence which in its opinion was serious, heinous, gruesome, brutal, sensational in character or shocking to public morality or he was a previous convict of offence punishable with death or imprisonment for life, then he would not be entitled to that concession.
7.	It is a well-settled principle that, in bail matters, the application of the rule of consistency requires the Court to carefully examine the nature, extent and specificity of the role attributed to each accused. Parity is not to be deduced as a matter of course but only where the allegations and circumstances are materially similar.

case_info: Cr. Misc (B.A) No. 509-P, 513-P,514-P & 517-P of 2025 Mudasir  Vs  The State
remarks:
Keeping in view that the vehicle was being driven by petitioner Syed Noman Ali Shah and was under his direct and immediate control, his claim of lack of conscious knowledge regarding the presence of opium concealed in the door panel, as well as his purported ignorance of the other petitioners\' possession of chars, appears to be devoid of plausible justification at this stage.
The collective recovery of narcotics from the same vehicle, and the manner in which the drugs were found in close proximity to each petitioner, reasonably suggests a joint enterprise aimed at the transportation of narcotics. Such a coordinated arrangement, particularly involving bulk of contraband, cannot be readily disbelieved or dismissed as implausible in a tentative assessment at the bail stage.
Furthermore, there is no material on record indicating any prior enmity, mala fide or ill-will on the part of the Seizing Officer or any member of the ANF that could suggest false implication.

case_info: Cr.A No. 304-M of 2023 Wali Rahman  Vs  State & others
remarks:
(a) Evidentiary value of circumstantial evidence, in an eventuality, where an occurrence is not only a nocturnal-one but it also berefts of ocular or direct account.
(b) Delayed plea of alibi has no worth and value in eyes of law, especially, when during the course of trial an accused person could not put-forward any evidence in his own defence within the meaning of section 340 (2) Cr.P.C.
(c) Testimony of an injured eyewitness is required to be adjudged with judicious and independent mind in light of attending circumstances of each and every case.
(d) When an occurrence took place without any planning or premeditation on part of an accused person, then benefit of such an eventuality can be extended to accused in terms of reduction of his/her sentence.
(e) Distinction between punishment awarded U/S section 311 PPC in terms of Ta’zir or Qisas.

case_info: W.P No. 4701-P of 2024 Amjad Zaman Afridi  Vs Government of KPK
remarks:
A careful analysis of the case-law noted above clearly gives it to understand that no doubt a right of appeal is not an inherent or vested right but is a creation of statute. Legislature is quite competent to lay different conditions when is proposes to confer upon a party such right to challenge an order before a higher forum/fora like for instance requirement to pay court fee; of sending pre-filing notice to the other side etc. But if incase the conditions so imposed are such that these are too onerous and cumbersome so as to render the very right of appeal as illusory, redundant or arduous, then obviously the same are not to be given effect to by the courts. Such clogs introduced have consistently been considered so as to curtail the right of appeal and render the same nugatory; and are taken as they run in direct conflict of the letter of Art\' 4, 9 and 10-A of the Constitution.
It needs hardly to reiterate that the Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Payment of Wages Act, 2019 is a beneficial legislation promulgated for the larger benefit of worker / labour class of employees but while providing a right of appeal to one litigating side, a balance has to be maintained for both parties and undue preference and tilt towards the employee by relegating the employer or any other party in the manner that it has to necessarily deposit the whole sum adjudged against before it could have a recourse to appeal is too onerous and arduous as it renders the very purpose of appeal just illusory, ineffective and sham\' Besides, it is also too lop-sided and discriminatory against one set of litigating party that it cannot withstand the test of Art. 25 0f the constitution of Pakistan. It can therefore be safely concluded that the impugned proviso to section 17(1) (a) of the Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Payment of Wages Act, 2013 is ultra vires the Constitution of Pakistan with particular\' reference to Art. 4, 9, l0-A and25 thereof. Such being a case the courts / tribunals shall not give effect to such impugned provisions6 and the appeals filed under section 17 ibid are to be dealt with, entertained and decided on merits without requiring a party to deposit the sum adjudged in the order appealed against.

case_info: W.P No. 1329-M of 2023 Mst. Maria Kabir  Vs  Israr Ahmad & others
remarks:
Sections of law;- Article 199 of Constitution of Pakistan, 1973
(a) Conditions mentioned in Nikah-Nama in the form of dower must be complied with, especially when same stand proved during the course of family proceedings.
(b) Husband is bound to maintain his wife when marriage-tie between spouses is still in existence and there is willingness on part of plaintiff to rehabilitates with her husband.
(c) Scope and extent of High Court in writ jurisdiction.
(d) Concurrent findings of Courts below.

case_info: W.P No. 1136-P of 2024 Sahibzada Abdul Latif Vs Federation of Pakistan
remarks:
1.	It is a settled principle of law that when it comes to imposition of major penalty and that too of removal from service on the charge of misconduct, then the accusation are to be proved beyond reasonable doubt and the conclusion of guilt based upon inferences and hearsay is not permissible.
2.	To dismiss a civil servant or for that matter, any employee on a charge of taking bribe or misconduct is as serious a matter as convicting a person for a crime because his whole career is ruined. Therefore, the order of dismissal must be based on some evidence.

case_info: Cr.A No. 248-M of 2022 Said Naik Amal Shah  Vs  The State
remarks: It is settled principle that when a witness is declared as hostile, the trial Court must examine the totality of his evidence with great caution. The record reveals that the prosecution had requested the trial Court to declare PW-17 and PW-18 as hostile witnesses during the cross-examination of Abdul Samad Shah (PW-18) when cross-examination on the complainant (PW-17) had already been completed. A party can ask for declaring the witness as hostile, when the opposite party has not started cross examination but once cross examination starts or commence then such request could not be entertained.

case_info: W.P No. 3329-P of 2024 Javed Akhtar  Vs  Government of KPK and others
remarks:
Likewise, general rule making powers of government deriving their force and validity from section 22 of the Act are available with the government but for carrying out the purposes of the Act and not to destroy the same. Besides, it is noted that sub-section 3 of section 9 of the Act was specific provision providing qualification for the office in question with particularity and thus cannot be controlled by general provision empowering government to frame rules. It is by now a settled principle of law that general provisions in a statute do not control specific provisions and so general rule making powers provided under section 22 would not control and affect upon specific provisions of section 9(3) of the Act. Reliance in this respect is placed on the judgments of Superior Court in the case \"A. Qutubuddin Khan vs. CHEC Millwala Dredging Co. (Pvt.) Limited\" reported in 2014 SCMR 1268, \"Golden Oraphies (Pvt.) Ltd. And 12 others vs. Director of Vigilance, Central Excise, Customs and Sales Tax and others\" reported in 1993 SCMR 1635 and \"Al-Jehad Trust through Raeesul- Mujahidin Habib Al-Wahabul Khairi, Advocate Supreme Court and another vs. Federation of Pakistan and others\" reported as PLD 1997 Supreme Court 84.
In addition thereto, it also noted that advertisement for the post in question was made in September 2020, whereas the Rules relied upon by the respondents for ousting the petitioner from the run have been framed and notified on 17.04.2024. And so in that context too, these rules cannot apply to already concluded recruitment processes. Reliance m this regard is placed on the judgments of Superior Court m the case \"Controller General of Accounts, Government of Pakistan, Islamabad and others vs. Abdul Waheed and others reported in 2023 SCMR 111, \"Water and Power Development Authority, Lahore through Chairman and others vs. Haji Abdul Aziz and others\" reported in 2012 SCMR 965, \"Sindh Revenue Board through Secretary Government of Sindh vs. Messrs Quick Food Industries (Pvt.)
