9(c)/11(b) K.P CNSA, 2019/15 AA, PS Yarik, D.I. Khan – FIR alleged recovery of 400 g ice, 200 g charas
and unlicensed 30 bore pistol with 6 rounds – Investigation complete, no previous criminal record, FSL

The State (2022 SCMR 1555), Fahad Hussain v. The State (2023 SCMR 364), Shabir Ahmed v. The State (2023 YLR Note 35) – Bail allowed subject to furnishing bonds of Rs.2,00,000/- with two sureties.

case_info: Cr.A No. 367-B of 2023 Tayyab Khan and another  Vs  The State etc.
remarks:
Criminal Appeal
Qatl-i-Amd—Appreciation of evidence—Cross-versions—Benefit of doubt—Appeal under section 410
Cr.P.C. against conviction under sections 302(b), 324 & 337-F(iv) PPC—Held, complainant’s presence at spot established by medical evidence but testimony suffered from material contradictions with site-plan; absence of blood at alleged points of fall undermined prosecution version—Separate FIRs (Nos. 657, 658 & 659 of 2018) lodged by rival parties disclosed same date, time, parties and proximate venues (separated by only 60–65 paces), thus constituting cross versions—Prosecution suppressed material facts including injury to appellant and death of Gulbaz Khan; failed to establish that appellants were aggressors—Motive of forcing house rental also unproved—Medical and FSL reports confirm firearm use and multiplicity of assailants but could not link appellants specifically to fatal shots—In cross-cases, burden lies on prosecution to prove aggressor role beyond reasonable doubt; failure entitles accused to benefit of doubt—Consistent jurisprudence requires even a single doubt to suffice for acquittal (Muhammad Imtiaz Bai v. State 2024 SCMR 1191; Mst. Asia Bibi v. State PLD 2019 SC 64)—Convictions set aside; appellants acquitted and released.
(Appeal allowed)

case_info: RFA No. 285-P of 2024 Muhammad Farid and another  Vs  Hassan Pari Nisar and others

case_info: Cr.A No. 193-P of 2024 Muhammad Ijaz etc Vs  The state etc
remarks:
The matter does not end here, rather this Court is anxious to note that why the statement of the eyewitness I.e. Muhammad Rangeen was not recorded and that why he was not produced before the learned trial Court. We are shocked to see that he was abandoned as unnecessary. The attitude of the prosecution, more particularly, the prosecutor has disturbed the judicial mind of this Court, as he could not explain that how a witness who claimed himself to be the eyewitness, was thought unnecessary, we cannot ignore the importance of the witness as the deceased was his real brother.
Right from the beginning till the end we noticed that the injured eyewitness was reluctant to join the investigation and even he was reluctant to support the report of the complainant and when he put his appearance before the Court, during the trial, he denied to have identified the accused and even he did not charge the accused by name. In his cross-examination he explained that as the accused/appellants were not known to him, so he could not mention their names, but at the same time he did not charge the accused for the injuries caused to him and for the death of the deceased. If such were the circumstances, then it was essential for the investigating officer to have arranged for test identification parade and in absence of the same the statement of the
complainant cannot be taken into consideration.
The contradictions between the statements of the scribe and the doctor has created dents in the prosecution case and has questioned the credibility of the scribe, who though, is an important witness, yet he failed to understand the seriousness of the matter. He wanted to favour the prosecution, so he lied, ignoring the fact that the doctor did confirm his consciousness.
As the complainant admitted his charge in the murder of the father of the appellant Ijaz and also that of his brother, so when the accused were armed, then there was hardly an occasion for the complainant and the eyewitness to escape unhurt, rather the accused would have easily targeted the witnesses alongwith the deceased.
In our understanding when the witnesses is unrelated to the complainant and to the deceased, if he does not turn up to depose in the Courts of law, then adverse inference should not be taken, as the witness was not under the influence of the complainant or of anyone from the deceased side, once the prosecution is having no influence over the witness and once the witness is having no relationship with the deceased, if he is not produced and if he would not appear to record his statement, then the prosecution should not suffer because of his none appearance, as the witness was beyond the influence of the complainant and the prosecution, but once a witness is closely related to the deceased, then it can be presumed that because of his close relationship the witness must appear before the Court as the prosecution had the influence to produce, and if is not produced, then the Court is bound to take an adverse inference.

case_info: Cr.A No. 239-M of 2023 Gul Rahman & another  Vs state & another
remarks:
Sections of law;- 302, 337-A (i), 337-A (ii), 337-A (iii), 337-L(ii),34 PPC
(a) Natural conduct of eyewitnesses. Neither any dishonest improvements appear in their statements nor the story advanced by them appears to be unnatural or concocted-one.
(b) Presence of injured PWs on the spot of occurrence is established in view of stamp of injuries on
their persons.
(c) Relationship by itself is no ground to discard testimony of an interested witness.
(d) Existence of cross-version is no ground for innocence of an accused person.
(e) Benefit of doubt.

case_info: C.R No. 600-A of 2019 Niaz Muhammad Khan & others  Vs  Faiz Muhammad Khan & others
remarks:
A declaratory suit was decreed by the learned trial Court and appeal filed there against was dismissed by the learned appellate Court. During execution proceedings, the petitioners filed application under Section 12(2) CPC to the effect that they being necessary party, were not impleaded in the suit, therefore, the decree was based on misrepresentation and fraud. Learned Civil Court tried and accepted the application under Section 12(2) CPC, however, the learned Revisional Court held that the application under Section 12(2) CPC was not maintainable before the Civil Court, rather same was required to have been filed before the appellate Court, as such ordered return of application to the learned Civil Court. The petitioners then filed fresh application under Section 12(2) CPC before the learned appellate Court. The learned appellate Court dismissed the application on the ground of limitation.
Held;
	In the case in hand, the petitioners had been pursuing their application under Section 12(2) CPC before the wrong forum, therefore, they are required to fulfil the conditions provided in sub-Section (2) of Section 14 of the Act, i.e. that such proceedings were prosecuted in good faith. If the petitioners succeed to meet out such requirement of sub-Section (2) of Section 14, they would be entitled to get benefit and in case of their failure, such benefit could not be extended to them.
	No doubt, the petitioner preferred fresh application, instead of filing the returned memo of petition, however, the returned memo of petition was annexed with the subsequent fresh petition and the fresh petition was in continuation of the former application.
	No lapses were found on behalf of the petitioners, while pursuing their application before the wrong forum, i.e. the Court of learned Civil Judge-VI, Mansehra. The application under Section 12(2) CPC was entertained and decided by the learned Civil Judge-VI, Mansehra. Upon decision of revision petition by the revisional Court, the learned Civil Judge-VI, ordered return of petition under Section 12(2) CPC on 22.12.016 and the petitioners filed application under Section 12(2) CPC before the learned Additional District Judge-II, Mansehra on 23.12.2016, which was well within time. The time consumed in pursuing the application before the learned Civil Judge-VI, Mansehra, was required to have been excluded, because the petitioners were able to establish that the proceedings were being pursued before the learned Civil Judge-VI, Mansehra in good faith and secondly, they could not be made to suffer due to act of the Court, because in view of the findings rendered above, it was also the duty of the Court to have returned the petition to the petitioners for its presentation before the Court, having jurisdiction in the matter, while the learned Civil Judge-VI, Mansehra, instead of returning the petition, entertained and decided the same.
	Revision Petition allowed.

case_info: W.P No. 1738-P of 2020 Khair Muhammad etc  Vs  Hayat Khan etc

Article 10-A right to fair trial.

case_info: B.C.A No. 67-M of 2024 Muhammad Qayum  Vs  The State another
remarks:
(a) A High Court can evaluate & assess grounds furnished by learned trial Court qua grant of bail to an accused person under 3 rd proviso of section 497 Cr.P.C in view of conclusion of delay in his/her trial.
(b) Those dates of hearing cannot be counted in total tally of an accused person, wherein his learned counsel was though not in attendance, however, on those particular dates of hearing, prosecution could not produce their complete set of witnesses and no progress could otherwise be made in a case even if learned counsel representing an accused person would have been present.

case_info: C.R No. 178-D of 2024 Shah Jahan and others  Vs  Mst. Haseena Bibi and others
remarks:
Held: (1) Admittedly, the respondent No.1 is the only daughter of the deceased Ghulam Yasin
while he divorced his wife during his lifetime. The Shia Law of Inheritance divide heirs into two
groups, namely, (1) heirs by consanguinity, that is, blood relations, and (2) heirs by marriage, that is,
husband and wife. Heirs by consanguinity are divided into three classes and each class is sub-
divided into two sections.
(2) Of three classes of heirs, first excludes the second from inheritance and the second excludes
third. In the present case, the respondent No.1, being heir of first class excludes the petitioners, who
are the heirs of third class.
(3) It is settled principle of Islamic Law, that nearer in degree excludes the remoter. The petitioners
are not only distant kindred of Ghulam Yasin but also remoter in degree.
(Civil revision was dismissed).

case_info: W.P No. 101-B of 2024 Munawar Khan etc  VS Sheikh Fazal ur Rehman etc
remarks: The bare perusal of Article 163 of Qanun-e-Shahadat Order, 1984 reflects that an initiative has to be taken by the petitioner who\'s first stance is to offer himself for oath in support of his lis where after at his request the Court has to call upon the other side to refute the said statement of the petitioner on oath and the Court is bound to pass any order in the light of the said statement of the parties. Under the Oath Act, 1873, the party desirous of decision on oath ask other party to take oath whereas under Article 163 of Qanun-e-Shahadat, the party having desire to decide the matter on oath put himself to task by offering to take oath first and then on his request the court calls upon the other party to refute the said statement on oath. Both the provisions of Oath Act, 1873 and Article 163 of Qanun-e-Shahadat are opposite to each other. In present case, the plaintiff/respondent did not make an offer for administering special oath as provided in Article 163 of Qanun-e-shahadat Order, 1984 but offered the petitioners/defendants to take oath, which clearly manifest that respondents/plaintiffs offer was under Oath Act, 1873 and not under Article 163 of Qanun-e-Shahadat. The mentioning of wrong nomenclature will not change the complexion or nature of the application and the Court has to see the pith and substance of the application rather than nomenclature of the application.

case_info: W.P No. 5673-P of 2024 Mst Naseem Akhtar  Vs  Mst Shairoon
remarks:
Even recognizing that this dower is categorized as an unpaid debt recoverable from the legacy of the deceased, the legitimacy of suit brought against one set of legal heirs, while excluding other being necessary and proper party remains in question.
In the absence of all legal heirs of deceased, being necessary party as previously stated, the suit in its current form is bad, therefore the ex-parte decree issued against the petitioners is even otherwise not legally sustainable.

case_info: FAO No. 01-A of 2025 Director General (Headquarters) Civil Works Organization, Rawalpindi  Vs  Syed Wali Shah
remarks:
Order 27, Rule 8-A of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, states that in certain cases, no security deposit is required from the Government or a public officer when they are sued for actions taken in their official capacity. Essentially, this rule exempts the Government from providing security in specific legal proceedings against it. This means that when the Government or a public officer is sued for official acts, they are not required to furnish a security deposit as per the usual provisions of Order XLI of the Civil Procedure Code, 1908. However, this exemption applies only when the lawsuit pertains to actions performed in an official capacity.
On the other hand, Order 21, Rule 23-A of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, states that if a judgment debtor wishes to object to the execution of a decree, they must first deposit the decretal amount in court or provide security for its payment. This means that a judgment debtor cannot simply file an objection without first offering financial assurance to the court. This rule is considered mandatory, meaning the court cannot entertain an objection unless the security requirement is met.

case_info: C.R No. 547-M of 2012 R/O Kota through Representative Maqam Khan etc  Vs  Ahmad through LRs & others
remarks:
(a) If the judgment reflects a thorough consideration of the parties\' arguments, evidence, and applicable law, then the mere omission of separately framed points of determination is treated as a procedural lapse rather than a substantive error. The focus is on whether justice has been served and whether the reasoning of the court is clear enough for the appellate court to understand the basis of the decision. In essence, as long as the judgment shows a proper application of judicial mind, addresses all material issues, and provides adequate reasoning, the absence of formally framed points of determination does not render the decision defective.
(b) In the civil dispensation of justice, courts are to adjudicate the lis on the standard of the preponderance of probability of the evidence produced by the parties. The decision of the court would tilt in favor of the party having the preponderance of evidence. As for the burden of proving a fact, it gains importance and relevance only when no evidence is led by the concerned party, or when the Court is unable to make a decision one way or the other based on the evidence available on record.
(c) The High Court\'s jurisdiction under Section 115 of the C.P.C. is limited in cases of concurrent findings by competent courts. It can only intervene if there are serious legal flaws, such as insufficient evidence, misreading or ignoring material evidence, reliance on inadmissible evidence, abuse of jurisdiction, or perverse conclusions. Mere disagreement with factual findings or reappraisal of evidence does not justify interference unless there is gross misreading or overlooking of crucial evidence.
(d) It is settled law that a strong presumption of truth and correctness is attached to the Revenue Record (the record of rights), especially to the first-ever settlement record, which carries the highest degree of authenticity. Very strong evidence is required to rebut this presumption, as a document carrying a presumption of truth cannot be disregarded without convincing and cogent evidence. The burden of proof rests on the party challenging the entries.
(e) The sale deed must be assessed based on the legal and customary practices in force at the time of its execution. Before the State of Swat merged into Pakistan in 1969, there was no formal revenue record system or application of the Registration Act, 1908. However, sale transactions were written and registered with the local Tehsildar. Land was identified based on surrounding properties rather than khasra numbers. In this case, the petitioner’s sale deed was registered on 16-09-1965. A Record Keeper testified and presented the relevant register entry. As per the law then applicable, such an entry was sufficient to prove the transaction. The sale deed also clearly identified the property by describing the surrounding ownerships.
(f) Sarai land refers to property historically reserved for public rest houses established for travelers, typically governed under statutory or customary law as public utility land, and often treated as inalienable without express government sanction. Dautari land denotes immovable property distributed among co-sharers through customary familial partition, granting each holder an individualized share recognized under local riwaj. Ancestral land comprises property inherited through successive generations under Islamic inheritance law or tribal custom, in which all legal heirs retain undivided co- ownership unless partitioned. Dhakarakh land pertains to mountainous or rocky terrain, generally uncultivable, utilized for grazing or firewood, and often held jointly under customary communal rights. Sadeen land refers to barren or sloped land, typically rain-fed and agriculturally marginal, held under traditional ownership with limited productive use. Shamilati land constitutes communal land owned collectively by co-sharers within a revenue estate, commonly including forests, pastures, or hills, and remains indivisible unless legally partitioned.

case_info: C.R No. 454-P of 2024 with CM No. 789 & 961-P of 2024 PESCO and others  Vs  Dr. Kashmala Orakzai

It is also settled that once discretion is exercised by the Court qua condonation of delay, the High Court or, as the case may be, the Supreme Court would interfere in very rare cases and in exceptional circumstances.

case_info: Cr.A No. 188-A of 2023 State  Vs  Ishfaq
remarks:
1. Where the Seizing Officer despite having prior information about narcotics neither conducts test purchase from the accused nor makes any efforts in this regard, such recovery is not free of doubt.
2. If the Seizing Officer gets information about narcotics in a dwelling house, then he/she by virtue of section 27 of The Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Control of Narcotic Substances Act, 2019 was required to obtain warrant for searching of that house otherwise to show valid reason(s) for not doing so and if he fails to do so, the same would create about conduct of the Seizing Officer.
3. If sample of the recovered contraband is not sent to the Forensic Science Laboratory for analysis within prescribed time i.e. 72 hours, then report thereof even if is found positive becomes inconsequential.

case_info: Cr.A No. 240-B of 2023 Qismat Ullah  Vs  The state etc
remarks:
Criminal Appeal 
Held: Pakistan Penal Code, 1860---Section 302(b)---Section 203---Qanun-e-Shahadat Order, 1984---Articles 40 and 122---Circumstantial Evidence---Recovery of weapon of offence on pointation of accused---Forensic confirmation---Evidential burden on accused---False exculpatory plea---Murder of wife inside bedroom--- Appellant convicted for qatl-i-amd of his wife and for causing disappearance of evidence by staging false story of attack by unknown thieves---FIR lodged by appellant alleging unknown assailants fired through closed windows, causing death of his wife and injury to him---Investigation revealed recovery of .30 bore pistol on appellant’s pointation---Crime empties secured from room and courtyard matched with recovered weapon as per FSL report---Medical evidence showed single through and through firearm injury on head--- Pillow, mattress and bed bore bullet marks inconsistent with version of firing from outside---Presence of crime empty on bed and matching of all empties recovered from inside and outside the room with recovered pistol falsified defence version---Deceased died instantaneously, contradicting appellant’s plea that she succumbed en route to hospital---Prosecution case based on circumstantial evidence forming continuous chain connecting appellant with commission of offence---Held, though no direct evidence available, circumstantial evidence was well-knit and excluded every hypothesis of innocence---Recovery of weapon pursuant to disclosure constituted admissible discovery under Article 40 Qanun-e-Shahadat Order---Once prosecution established incriminating circumstances, evidential burden shifted upon accused under Article 122 to explain death occurring in his sole company---Appellant failed to offer plausible explanation and took self-contradictory plea regarding alleged involvement of third person---False defence and failure to explain material facts provided additional link in chain of circumstances---Conviction under Sections 302(b) and 203 PPC upheld---As regards revision for enhancement, motive remained unproved and occurrence followed altercation, negating premeditation---Life imprisonment held justified in view of settled principles---
Criminal appeal and criminal revision petition dismissed.

case_info: Cr.MBA NO. 583-D of 2024 Khuda-e-Dad  Vs  The state and another
remarks: Bail petition dismissed; FIR No.210 dated 30.6.2023, sections 302, 324, 337-A(ii)(iii), 337-F(v), 148, 149 PPC read with section 15 Arms Act; recovery of 7.62 bore rifle and cartridges from accused; accused prima facie connected with offence; petitioner not entitled to bail.

case_info: C.R No. 14-C of 2024 Muhammad Shah  Vs  Zahoor-ud-Din and others
remarks: An application for final decree is maintainable when there is preliminary decree, however, in the present case there was no decree at all rather the suit was simply withdrawn by the plaintiff. Thus, the order dated 21:10.2013 is not executable under Order XXI read with Section 47 of the Civil Procedure Code for the reasons that the said order neither falls within the domain of an executable decree nor it can be further proceeded with on any ground. In other words, the order of the learned trial Court simply dismissing the suit as withdrawn lacks the final decision on the matters in dispute. Similarly, the said order does not outline the obligations of the parties nor it can be enforced through legal means.

case_info: Cr.A No. 399-M of 2023 Sahib Zaman  Vs  The State & another
remarks:
(a) Delayed recording of statements under sections 161 as well as 164 Cr.P.C and its effect on prosecution’s case.
(b) Mere stamp of injuries on person of an injured PW is not a sole criterion for his/her presence on the venue of crime.
(c) Contradiction in-between star witnesses of prosecution is detrimental for its case.
(d) If prosecution fails to produce an important and material witness without any reason then an adverse inference under Article 129 (g) of Qanun-e-Shahadat Order, 1984 could be drawn in terms that had such a PW been produced, he would have not supported a case of prosecution.
(e) Negative FSL report is of no worth for prosecution ‘s case.
(f) When ocular-account fails, then circumstantial or medical evidence alone would be no worth in the capacity of substantive evidence.
(g) Failure to prove motive would have effect on the prosecution’s case.
(h) Benefit of doubt.

case_info: Cr.A No. 142-B of 2023 Abdullah Mir & another  Vs  The State etc
remarks:
Appeal against Conviction.
(a) Criminal Procedure Code, 1898.S.302, 324,34 P.P.C----benefit of doubt--- Testimony of related witness could be relied upon for sustaining conviction on a capital charge, however, same is to be scrutinized with great care and caution----Deceased was son of the complainant who allegedly witnessed the occurrence---deposition of the complainant was sufficient to vitiate the prosecution case, as his testimony was not reliable.
(b) The matter was reported with an in ordinate delay, hence, element of consultation and deliberations could not be ruled out.
(c) The medical evidence belied the prosecution story as there was contradiction between the time of occurrence and the time of death of the deceased as per the statement of concerned medical officer, who conducted autopsy on the dead body, which casted doubt regarding the mode and manner of the occurrence.
(Appeal was allowed and appellants were acquitted in the circumstances).

case_info: C.R No. 29-C/2024 Rahmat Wali and others  Vs  Residents of Munoor Garam Chashma, Chitral Lower through representative Muhammad Rahim and others
remarks:
Section(s) of law: Section 115 CPC.
1. It is well-settled principle of law that the party who wants to withdraw his suit with the permission to file a fresh one, must show that there is a formal defect in the suit, which could result in failure of the same, if such defect was removed or rectified by the amendment of the plaint, permission to file a fresh suit could not be granted.
2. The provisions of Order XXIII Rules 1 and 2 CPC fully discussed.
3. When all the legal proceedings have been completed and only arguments on the main case are  required to decide the same, at that very belated stage, withdrawal of a suit with permission to bring a fresh one, could not be granted.

case_info: F.A.O No. 5-M of 2025 Government of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa through Secretary Local Government Elections & Rural Development  Department, Peshawar and others  Vs  Nasir Hayat and anothers
remarks:
Section(s) of law: Section 104/XLIII read with 82 CPC.
1. Sections 79 to 82 of the Code of Civil Procedure are procedural in nature, which do not create any substantive right or liability.
2. The main object of Section 82 CPC is to give time to the Government or the Public Officer, at the highest level to consider the matter involved, and in case the suit has already been instituted to determine whether to defend the same or to contest the claim.
3. The provisions of procedural law as to the technicality must be liberally interpreted.
4. Where the original decree did not grant a specific time to comply and the three months period has since passed, the Government generally cannot re-assert Section 82 CPC to stall execution proceedings indefinitely, even if the Provincial Government tries to argue that it was not giving “reasonable opportunity of Section 82 CPC”.
5. According to Order XXI Rule 115 CPC, all the objections shall be raised at the first instance and no objection will be entertained afterwards.

case_info: RFA No. 42-A of 2025 Shah Faisal etc  Vs  Mst. Zainib etc
remarks:
1. The Preamble of The Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Enforcement of Women\'s Property Rights Act, 2019 reads that the same is enacted to protect and secure the rights of ownership of women in the property owned by them and not to judicially adjudicate and ascertain title/ownership of a party to immoveable property particularly when the same is already pending adjudication before a civil Court.
2. Section 7 of The Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Enforcement of Women\'s Property Rights Act, 2019provides two different procedures dealing with two types of matters i.e. the matter which requires further probe or investigation [under subsection (2)] and the matter which does not require any detailed probe, investigation or recording of evidence [under subsection (3)]
3. Though under subsection (2) of section 7 of The Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Enforcement of Women\'s Property Rights Act, 2019words \"if the matter requires further probe or investigation occur, however, the fact remains that words \"or recording of evidence\" are omitted by the legislature, for the reason that recording of pro and contra evidence in proceedings pending adjudication before a civil Court qua determining the title/ ownership of a party to immoveable property, is the sole domain of civil Court. Subsection (6) of section 7 of ibid Act reads that the Ombudsperson upon receiving the report under sub-section (2), may further conduct such summary inquiry and call for such record as he may deem fit. As such, this subsection also does not confer mandate upon the Ombudsperson with respect to recording of pro and contra evidence in proceedings pending before a civil Court.
4. Where civil suits) regarding the subject matter before Ombudsman/ ombudsperson,is/are pending before the civil Court which requires detailed probe, investigation or recording of evidence, then the recommendations under subsection (3)of section 7 of The Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Enforcement of Women\'s Property Rights Act, 2019are not warranted and thus subsection (4) of section 7 of the ibid Act which deals with subsection (3) also becomes irrelevant in this case.
5. Where language of the Statute is clear and unambiguous, the law enunciated therein should be interpreted by assigning it the plain and ordinary meaning.
6. When property which is subject matter in civil suit(s) pending adjudication in civil court also includes constructed house/rooms etc then the entitlement/ ownership of the suit property cannot be determined without going through the mechanism as provided under the relevant laws i.e. the West Pakistan Land Revenue Act, 1967 or the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, as the case may be, for no such mechanism has been provided in The Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Enforcement of Women\'s Property Rights Act, 2019for the determination of the share of each co-sharer/co-owner in the joint property, and any order Ombudsperson passes in this regard holding complainant entitled for such property, on one hand, would not only be a parallel judicial order despite that Office of Ombudsman is neither a Court nor a judicial tribunal within the scope of Article 175 of The Constitution of Islamic Republic of Pakistan while on the other it would be in conflict with Article 175(2) of The Constitution.
7. Where civil suits) regarding property which includes constructed rooms and is also the subject matter before Ombudsman/ombudsperson, is/are  pending before the civil Court which requires detailed probe, investigation or recording of evidence, the only mandate left with the Ombudsperson was under subsection (5) of section 7 of The Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Enforcement of Women\'s Property Rights Act, 2019 to advise the complainant to pursue the proceedings in the Court of law and terminate the complaint.

case_info: Cr.A No. 89-A of 2018 Mst. Safeen Akhtar  Vs  Ehtisham and others
remarks:
1. Where preliminary inquiry/investigation is started and conducted before the registration of case, therefore, element of deliberations on the part of prosecution cannot be ruled out and in that eventuality such investigation cannot be safely relied upon for the purpose of conviction.
2. Where ocular account furnished by eyewitnesses is not trustworthy and confidence inspiring and is not supported by any medical evidence and the occurrence has not been proved in the mode and manner as alleged in the Crime Report then such ocular account cannot be relied upon. 
3. There is no cavil with the proposition that merely because a witness has stamp of injury on his/her person is not per-se tantamount to a stamp of credence on his/her testimony.
4. If eyes of the deceased are open at the time of preparation of inquest-report then presence of eyewitnesses being close relatives of the deceased becomes doubtful.
5. Prosecution is not bound to set a motive, however, when once it opted to set a motive then it has to be proved and in case of its failure to do so, benefit thereof has to be given to the accused.
6. Mere abscondance cannot be made a ground for the conviction of an accused as disappearance of a person after the occurrence is but natural if he is involved in a criminal case rightly or wrongly.
7. For giving benefit to an accused, it is not essential that there should be many circumstances creating doubts, even a single doubt is sufficient to extend its benefit to an accused person.

case_info: Cr.A No. 261-A of 2023 The State  Vs  Ashiq
remarks:
1. Where statements of prosecution witness are not trustworthy and confidence inspiring being not consistent rather material contradictions exist therein on material points of the prosecution case, prosecution case cannot be proved beyond shadow of reasonable doubt.
2. If name of Seizing Officer does not tally with the abbreviation / monogram of seal affixed on the parcels, it casts a reasonable doubt of his presence on the spot as it is the mandate of the relevant rules that the Seizing Officer shall affix the seal/monogram carrying the abbreviation of his own name.
3. For giving benefit to an accused, it is not essential that there should be many circumstances creating doubts, even a single doubt is sufficient to extend its benefit to an accused person.
4. Once an accused succeeds to earn an acquittal order then double presumption is attached to his innocence and as such while hearing an appeal against acquittal, the appellate Court cannot disturb, or reverse or set-aside such an acquittal order unless the same is found to be perverse, shocking, artificial and over and above suffering from jurisdictional defect or non-reading of evidence or misreading of evidence.

case_info: C.R No. 183-M of 2016 Muhammad Shoaib  Vs  Mst Hussan Zadgai and others
remarks: The purchase of land and the improvements made by the respondents in the disputed house have been established by them but there is no evidence on the record to suggest that the petitioner/plaintiff had ever raised any objection regarding the mentioned developments in the disputed area. The doctrine of acquiescence is an equitable doctrine which applies when a party having right stands by and sees another dealing in a manner inconsistent with that right, while the act is in progress and after the violation is completed, he cannot afterwards complain. In literal sense, the term ‘acquiescence’ means silent assent, tacit consent, concurrence, or acceptance, which denotes a conduct that indicates an intention of a party to abandon an equitable right and also denotes the conduct from which another party will be justified in inferring such an intention. Acquiescence may be either direct with full knowledge and express probation, or indirect where a person having the right to set aside the action stands by and sees another dealing in a manner inconsistent with that right and inspite of the infringement takes no action mirroring acceptance. However, acquiescence will not apply if laps oaf time is of no importance or consequence.

case_info: C.R No. 115-M of 2021 Sher Ali Khan  Vs  Fazal Akbar and others
remarks:
1. Strong presumption of truth is attached to a registered document and it is an established law that the registered sale deed(s) stands on higher pedestal as against any other deeds/documents.
2. When a basic document is not proved, then the whole structure built upon it would automatically collapse.
3. In revisional jurisdiction, the High Court cannot disturb or set aside the concurrent findings, on the facts, when the same do not suffer from any misreading or non-reading of evidence.

case_info: C.R No. 499-M of 2019 Gul Farid through LRs  Vs  Government of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa through Chief Secretary, Peshawar and others
remarks:
1. Presumption of truth is attached to the long-standing entries made in the record of a Revenue Estate and to dislodge/rebut such entries, very strong, convincing and trustworthy evidence is required.
2. Documentary evidence has to prevail over the oral/verbal assertions.
3. The purpose of the laws of limitation is to establish certainty in the affairs of men, to bring repose and to bring an end to litigation after a certain time period has expired from accrual of an actionable right.

case_info: Cr.Misc BA No.27-B of 2025 Asif alias Asif ullah  Vs  Saad Ullah Jan etc
remarks:
Bail Application
Held: i) As per the medical evidence, the deceased received a single firearm entry wound for which two persons have been charged with a generalized role. Whether the shot allegedly fired by the petitioner / accused proved effective or that of the co-accused emerges as a significant question to be thrashed out at the trial.
(ii) It is alleged that the deceased was simultaneously fired at by two persons with Kalashnikovs from a distance of around 10 to 14 feet. If this were indeed the case, the consequences would have been far more drastic. Nevertheless, as noted above, the deceased still sustained a single firearm entry wound.
(iii) It is now well settled that when an accused is otherwise entitled to bail, mere abscondence, not being conclusive proof of guilt, would not suffice to obstruct the way of his bail. (Bail allowed)

case_info: W.P No. 2750-P of 2024 Ali Azim Afridi Advocate & another  Vs  Federaton of Paksitan and others
remarks:
(Petition under Article 199 of the Constitution of Islamic Republic of Pakistan, 1973)
(a) Constitution of Islamic Republic of Pakistan, 1973- Article 199-The petitioners questioned Rule-3 of the Civil Service of Pakistan (Composition and Cadre) Rules, 1954 (Rules 1954\'), submitting that its provisions violate the fundamental rights enshrined in the Constitution of Islamic Republic of Pakistan. The petitioners also sought relief in the form of directions to the respondents to facilitate the petitioners/public citizenry and legal practitioners in the same manner as officers of the Armed Forces for appointments based on the recommendation of the Federal Public Service Commission (FPSC) through an interview and not otherwise-Held— Rule 3 of the \'Rules 1954\' amended through CSP (composition and cadre) amended Rules 2014 is in consonance with Article 241 of the Constitution of the Islamic Republic of Pakistan, 1973, as well as Sections 23 and 25 of the Civil Servants Act, 1973. It is also a settled principle under Section 21 of the General Clauses Act, 1897, that the power to make a rule includes the power to amend, vary, or rescind that rule.
(b) Constitution of Islamic Republic of Pakistan, 1973—Article 199—The exception created in Rule 3 of the Rules, 1954, through Rules, 2014, no doubt, applies only to officers of the Armed Forces. However, these officers join service after a competitive examination and specialized training, distinct from other standard appointment procedures.
(c) Constitution of Islamic Republic of Pakistan, 1973-—Article 199—The petitioners failed to substantiate their claims regarding the alleged inconsistency of the impugned rule with the constitutionally guaranteed rights. The terms \"intra vires\" and\"ultra vires\" are opposites. \"Ultra vires\" means acting within legal authority. If an act is done with proper legal power, it is \"intra vires\", but if done without authority, it is \"ultra vires\". If the vires of a law are challenged, the burden always rests upon the person making such challenge to show that the same was violative of any of the fundamental rights or the provisions of the Constitution and where more than one interpretation is possible, the Court must prefer the interpretation which favours the validity without attributing mala fide to the legislature.
(d) Constitution of Islamic Republic of Pakistan, 1973-Article 199-The petitioners failed to demonstrate the lack of legislative competence of the President of Pakistan in amending Rule 3 through SRO.88(1)/2014 dated 10.02.20214.
(Constitutional petition was dismissed of in the circumstances)

case_info: Cr.A No. 348-M of 2023 Imran Khan  Vs  State etc
remarks:
1. A chance witness is a one who should normally be where and when he professes to have been, then he is declared to be a highly interested witness and as such, his evidence needs strong corroboration, which in the instant case is missing.
2. In order to maintain conviction of a convict on a capital charge, like the one in hand, on the basis of testimony of chance witnesses, the Court had to see and required corroboration while relying on such evidence.
3. For bringing home the charge, the foremost duty of the prosecution is to establish the presence of the eyewitnesses and without establishing their presence, their evidence would be of no help to its case.
4. If any party withholds the best piece of evidence, then it can fairly be presumed that the party had some sinister motive behind it and the presumption under Article 129(g) of the Qanun-e-Shahadat Order can fairly be drawn to it.
5. For giving a benefit to the accused, it is not essential that there should be many grounds rather a single doubt if appealable to the prudent mind is sufficient for acquittal of an accused person.

case_info: Cr.A No. 07-A of 2025 The State  Vs  Najeeb Ullah
remarks:
S. 417(2) Cr.PC, 1898.
S.9(a), 11,14 KP Wildlife & Biodiversity (Protection, Preservation, Conservation and Management Act, 2015.
(i) Appeal u/s 417(2) Cr,PC against the order of acquittal in a complaint case is incompetent.
(ii) Appeal was dismissed being incompetent as well as barred by 125 days.

case_info: W.P No. 33-A of 2024 Husaifa Nawaz  Vs  Govt. of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa etc
remarks: The case revolves around the admission of Respondent No.10 (Muhammad Faizan) to a reserved backward area seat for Upper Tanawal in a medical college. Initially, Respondent No.10  had applied for admission from District Torghar for the 2023-2024 session but was categorized as \"OUT\" and failed to secure a seat. For the 2024-2025 session, Respondent No.10 reappeared in the MDCAT exam and successfully qualified on 22.09.2024.A Location Certificate, issued by the competent authority, confirmed that Respondent No.10 had completed his entire education from institutions located in District Mansehra, thus qualifying him as an \"IN\" candidate for Upper Tanawal. Before this, on 07.08.2024, the Deputy Commissioner of Torghar had issued an Abandonment Certificate, formally disassociating Respondent No.10  from Torghar. Respondent No.10  then filed a declaration on 21.08.2024, and subsequently, his Domicile Certificate for Mansehra was issued on 25.09.2024. His mother also submitted a declaration on 02.10.2024, with her Domicile Certificate issued on 12.11.2024.The court clarified that the MDCAT registration and the actual admission process are distinct stages. The requirements for MDCAT registration only included educational documents and identity documents, while domicile verification was required at the final admission stage, with the final submission deadline being 08.01.2025. Respondent No.10  fulfilled all necessary requirements before this date, making his admission legally valid.On the issue of Respondent No.10 improvement examination taken in Torghar, the court held that this did not affect his status, as his original educational and residential record consistently established his link with District Mansehra (Tehsil Darband). Supporting documents, including his parents\' Nikah Nama, CNIC, and property ownership documents, further confirmed that Respondent No.10  and his family had been long-time residents of Mansehra.Finally, the court addressed the maintainability of the constitutional petition, noting that the petitioner had alternate remedies available under the KMU Admission Committee’s appeals process and the PM&DC complaint mechanism. Citing Palwasha Sherani’s case (2024 MLD 623), the court reiterated that constitutional jurisdiction cannot be invoked when alternate, adequate remedies exist.

case_info: W.P No. 31-A of 2024 Sardar Faraz Hussain  Vs  The State
remarks:
(a)The Companies Act, 2017 was enacted to provide a special and expedited mechanism for resolving corporate disputes and related matters. It is a well-established principle that when both a special law and a general law apply to a situation, the provisions of the special law prevail over the general law.
(b)Section 477 of the Companies Act, 2017 sets out that offences listed in the Eighth Schedule can only be prosecuted upon a written complaint by the SECP (Securities and Exchange Commission of Pakistan), the registrar, or qualified members or creditors holding at least 5% of the issued share capital (or equivalent interest in case of creditors). For companies without share capital, any member or creditor eligible to file a winding-up petition can also file a complaint. This provision ensures that only authorized parties can initiate legal proceedings for specified corporate offences, preventing frivolous cases. Section 497 imposes penalties on directors, officers, or employees who wrongfully possess, withhold, or misuse company property. Such persons may face a fine up to one million rupees and may be ordered to return the property or gains derived from it. Failure to comply may lead to imprisonment up to three years and an additional fine up to five hundred thousand rupees. Together, these sections ensure corporate accountability while balancing protections against misuse of legal processes.
(c)It is established that Section 476 of the Companies Act, 2017, being a non-obstante clause, overrides general laws and provides a complete mechanism for taking cognizance of offences under the Act, including Section 497, which is listed in the Eighth Schedule. Therefore, offences under Section 497 must follow the procedure laid out in the Companies Act. Consequently, the lodging of an FIR by local police for such offences is beyond their authority and is legally invalid. In view of the above, the instant writ petition is allowed, and the impugned FIR registered under Sections 419, 420, 468, 471, 408, and 409 PPC, is hereby quashed, being without lawful authority and jurisdiction.

case_info: W.P No. 134-B of 2023 Shaukat Ali Shah  Vs  Noorma Gul etc
remarks:
Held: (i) As a rule, this Court exercises judicial restraint in interfering with ongoing investigations while adhering to the well embedded principle that the functions of the Judiciary and Investigating Agencies are complementary rather than overlapping. However, there are certain exceptions to this general principle, as the law does not impose an absolute bar on the jurisdiction of this Court to judicially review actions taken by Investigating Agencies under Article 199 of the Constitution. This Court may, in appropriate cases, quash an FIR where it is registered without lawful authority, suffers from jurisdictional defects, or constitutes an abuse of process. We are also cognizant of the fact that such power is not exercised in a casual manner, but only where the continuation of criminal proceedings would result in grave injustice or violate fundamental rights.
(ii) It is well settled that the quashment of an FIR falls within the extraordinary jurisdiction of this Court and is warranted only in exceptional cases. The Superior Courts have consistently held that an FIR may be quashed where it is based on mala fide, frivolous, or the result of an ulterior motive. Likewise, where an FIR discloses no cognizable offence, is barred by law, or is lodged by an authority lacking jurisdiction, the Court may intervene to prevent the misuse of criminal law.
(iii) There is now a judicial consensus that where legislative instruments or any provisions contained therein are in competition—one promulgated by the Federal legislature and the other by the Provincial legislature—the test to determine legislative supremacy or dominance is comparatively simple, and the privilege of overriding supremacy is conceded to the federal legislature under Article 143 of the Constitution.
(iv) When an offence concurrently falls within the respective Schedules to the West Pakistan Anti- Corruption Establishment Ordinance, 1961 and the Federal Investigation Agency Act, 1974, and such offence is alleged to have been committed in connection with the affairs of the federation, then, notwithstanding its commission within the province, the provincial Anti-Corruption Establishment shall have no authority to register an FIR in this regard, rather it shall be the FIA that is competent to inquire into and investigate such an offence under the 1974 Act.
(Writ petition allowed; FIR quashed)

case_info: W.P No. 244-B of 2024 Abdus Samad Khan  Vs  Government of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa through Secretary LG&RDD, Peshawar etc.
remarks:
Writ Petition
Held: The petitioner had multiple remedies available to him: either to institute a civil suit under the provisions of Section 42 read with 54 of the Specific Relief Act, 1877, or to petition this Court under Article 199 of the Constitution. The petitioner chose to avail himself of the remedy of filing a civil suit before the civil court for the same cause of action, which was dismissed. By electing to proceed before the civil court, the petitioner stood precluded under the doctrine of election from invoking the constitutional jurisdiction of this Court under Article 199 of the Constitution.
(Writ petition dismissed)

case_info: W.P No. 972-A of 2025 Muhammad Sultan  Vs  Govt of KPK etc
remarks:
(a) Where an agreement contains an arbitration clause, a writ petition is not maintainable. It is well settled that contractual rights, commitments, and obligations are to be enforced through courts of ordinary jurisdiction or by the agreed dispute resolution mechanism, and should not ordinarily be interfered with by the High Court while exercising its constitutional jurisdiction, particularly in matters arising from contractual obligations.
(b) When an alternative, adequate, and efficacious remedy is available to a litigant, he must pursue that remedy rather than invoke the constitutional jurisdiction of the High Court. Where the law provides a remedy before another authority fully competent to grant relief, the High Court’s indulgence to the contrary may undermine public confidence in such authorities, cast an undeserved reflection on their integrity and competence, and defeat the legislative intent.
(c) Constitutional jurisdiction under Article 199 of the Constitution of the Islamic Republic of Pakistan, 1973, cannot be exercised in matters involving disputed questions of fact requiring the recording of evidence. The scope of this jurisdiction is confined to issues free from factual controversies; therefore, the Court cannot interfere in matters where disputed questions of fact are involved.

case_info: Cr. M(BA) No. 51-A of 2025 Inayat Ullah  Vs  The State
remarks:
S. 497 Cr.PC, 1898.
S. 5 ES, 7/11-F(2), 11-OO ATA, 1997.
(i) Confessional statement recorded by SP CTD instead of concerned Judge cannot be used unless its voluntariness decided by the Trial Court.
(ii) Expert report not available to confirm that the recovered material was explosive substance.
(iii) No record either showing or involvement of accused in anti-state activities or to be a member of proscribed organization

case_info: Cr.A No. 150-A of 2024 Gulraiz  Vs  The State
remarks:
To constitute the offence of cheating and criminal breach of trust, the prosecution must establish the presence of fraudulent and dishonest intention (mens rea) and inducement at the time the act was committed. The burden lies on the prosecution to prima facie prove these essential ingredients before the court can take cognizance of the offence. Mere denial of a transaction or refusal to repay money does not, by itself, indicate criminal intent from the outset. An accused cannot be convicted based on presumptions alone.
To establish cheating under Section 420 PPC, the prosecution must demonstrate fraudulent or dishonest deception, which induces a person to deliver property or to act (or refrain from acting) in a way they would not have otherwise. Clear evidence of deception and intentional inducement is necessary; in the absence of these, the offence of cheating is not made out. Furthermore, mere financial loss without proven deception does not amount to cheating.

case_info: Cr.A No. 65-B of 2023 Liaqat Ali Khan  Vs  The State etc
remarks:
Criminal Appeal
Held: (i) True, related witnesses cannot be termed as interested witnesses under all circumstances, but equally true, the testimonies of witnesses who are not only closely related to the deceased, but also have a strong motive against an accused must be appreciated with great care and caution to avoid any miscarriage of justice.
(ii) In criminal jurisprudence, when a witness deliberately changes his testimony to strengthen the prosecution case is known as dishonest improvement. It has been unequivocally laid down by the Superior Courts of the land numerous times that where a witness makes a dishonest improvement in his statement, it loses its significance.
(iii) The prosecution version that the assailants simultaneously opened fire with Kalashnikovs from a distance of about five paces for about 4 / 5 minutes while the complainant and the eyewitness remained at their respective places without moving or taking any shelter, yet escaped unhurt, does not appeal to a prudent mind. It also does not appeal to a prudent mind that the real son of the complainant and the brother of the eyewitness was gunned down in their immediate presence, yet neither of them attempted to touch his dead body or render any assistance in shifting him to the hospital. Rather, they remained mere silent spectators for about 4 / 5 minutes until their co-villagers arrived for this purpose. The unnatural conduct of an eyewitness in criminal cases has repeatedly been considered a factor creating doubt in the prosecution case.
(iv) It is a well settled principle that an eyewitness failing to prove his presence at the spot would entitle the accused to the benefit of the doubt.
(v) The value and status of medical evidence are always confirmatory in nature with regard to the seat and kind of injuries and the weapon used. When direct or circumstantial evidence fails to establish the guilt of an accused, the medical evidence loses its weight for the simple reason that it cannot tell who caused those injuries to the victim.
(vi) Abscondence alone does not serve as a conclusive proof of guilt. While it can be considered as a corroborative piece of evidence, its weight is contingent on the prior establishment of an accused person’s guilt through compelling evidence. However, when direct evidence falls short, abscondence as a piece of evidence becomes inconsequential.
(Criminal appeal allowed; conviction set aside; appellant acquitted.)

case_info: Cr.A No. 257-P  of 2024 Manzoor Ali Vs  The State etc
remarks:
There is no cavil with the preposition that it is the quality and not the quantity which would be the determining factor. True that the discretion lies with the prosecution to produce the witnesses of its choice, but equally true that in case of selection, the prosecution would convince that why a particular witness was not produced when he was so relevant to the issue in hand.

The presence of the witnesses has further disturbed the judicial mind of this court, as the dead body was shifted to the police station, but none of the witnesses stood identifier and, none of the witnesses verified the report of the complainant.

Record is silent regarding the previous altercation between the father of the appellant and, that of the deceased, when the eye witness was examined, he explained that altercation took place in the presence of Laiq Zaman, his younger brother whereas, they were present in their fields. This witness did not admit that the altercation took place in his presence, so he is not the witness to the altercation and, he is not the witness to the motive, rather the person who could depose in respect of the altercation was Liaq Zaman, but neither he appeared before the investigating officer, nor he was produced before the learned trial court and, as such the altercation between the parties could not be proved.

Once this court comes to the conclusion that the witness was not telling the whole truth, then his relationship with the deceased can be taken into consideration and, it is because of his character and relationship that his testimony cannot be taken into consideration.

True that it is not necessary, in all cases, that the dead body should be identified by the complainant or the witnesses, but as the incident occurred away from the house of the deceased and, as the dead body was picked up from the spot, shifted to the Police Station, then it was essential for the witnesses to have identified the dead body before the Police at the time report and, before the doctor at the time of postmortem examination.

case_info: B.A No. 06-P of 2025 Abdur Rehman  Vs  The State and another
remarks: The accused/petitioner has directly been charged in the FIR for committing the murder of his wife inside his house. The version of the prosecution is supported by the statements of eyewitnesses especially Mst. Iqra Bibi (daughter of the accused/petitioner), recovery of crime empties from the spot, recovery of the crime weapon on pointation of the accused/petitioner and the medico-legal/postmortem report of the deceased, according to which she has received multiple firearm injuries on her body. Therefore, the petitioner could not make out a case for further inquiry into his guilt under sub-section (2) of Section 497, Cr.P.C.

case_info: B.A No. 33-M of 2025 Saifullah  Vs  The State and another
remarks: The petitioner was admittedly a juvenile being below the age of 18 years at the time of occurrence. According to Section 6(5) of the Juvenile Justice System Act, 2018, a juvenile shall be released on bail if he has been detained for a continuous period exceeding six months provided the delay in trial of juvenile has not been occasioned by his act or omission or any person acting on his behalf. It is settled law that the period of delay in conclusion of trial is to be counted from the date of the detention of the accused in the case. Since, late commencement of trial is not the fault of the petitioner, therefore, he is entitled to the concession of bail on the ground of statutory delay in conclusion of his trial.

case_info: C.R No. 237-M of 2022 Abdul Hakim  Vs  Assistant Director NADRA, Tehsil Matta, Swat and others
remarks:
1. The purpose of the law of limitation is to establish certainty in the affairs of men, to bring repose and to bring an end to litigation after a certain period has expired from accrual of an actionable right, as such, the same is to be applied with strict observance and adherence.
2. The question of limitation is not to be left at the option of the parties but the Court, which is cognizant of a lis, is required to advert to the same on its own.
3. In revisional jurisdiction, the High Court can only set aside the concurrent findings, if the same are the result of any misreading or non- reading of evidence or improper exercise of jurisdiction or is the result of illegality or material irregularity.

case_info: CR No. 6-P of 2024 with CM No. 11/2024  Rozi Khan etc  Vs  Fazil etc
remarks: Principle of \"merger of decrees\" is equally applicable to the setting aside of ex-parte decrees.

case_info: STR No. 95-P of 2022 DG Khyber Pakhtunkhwa  VS M/S Bee Line, Peshawar
remarks:
Where tax statutes prescribe a definitive time frame using prohibitive or restrictive language, such provisions are deemed mandatory, rendering any action beyond the prescribed period invalid. This approach prevents tax authorities from exercising unfettered discretion in initiating or concluding proceedings outside the statutory framework.
The statutory provisions governing the exclusion of time due to adjournments sought by the taxpayer are enshrined in Sections 40(4) and 68(5) of the Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Finance Act, 2013. Although both provisions share similar language in principle, a fundamental distinction exists: Section 68(5) explicitly imposes a maximum cap of thirty days on the exclusion of time due to such adjournments, whereas Section 40(4) does not prescribe any such limitation. This distinction is pivotal in determining the correct computation of the limitation period in each case.

case_info: W.P No. 1418-P of 2022 Muhammad Naeem, Assistant Vice President (AVP), National Bank of Pakistan, Nodeh Toru, Mardan  Vs  Federation of Pakistan through Secretary Finance, Government of Pakistan, Islamabad and others
remarks:
Based on our review of Bye-law 51 of the 2015 Bye-laws and Sections 11, 13, and 20 of the Act of 1974, it is evident that these provisions grant the Board of Directors of NBP broad authority over policymaking, administration, and management of the bank\'s affairs. While the Board is vested with executive powers to oversee operations, shape policies, and ensure compliance with banking regulations, these provisions do not extend its authority to framing rules governing the terms and conditions of bank employees. In contrast, Section 32 of the Ordinance of 1949 explicitly empowers the Central Board, with prior approval of the Federal Government, to formulate Bye-laws concerning the recruitment, employment terms, and service conditions of NBP officers and staff. Specifically, Section 32(2)(xxviii) underscores that the Board\'s authority extends to structuring the employment framework, including recruitment policies, service terms, and staff benefits. Thus, while the Board exercises overarching control over policy and operational matters, the power to regulate the service conditions of bank employees is exclusively conferred through Bye-laws framed under Section 32 of the Ordinance of 1949.
The primary contention raised by the learned counsel for the petitioners centered on the judgment rendered by the Apex Court in the case of Muhammad Zaman (supra). We have carefully examined the said judgment. However, with the utmost respect, we find that both the factual matrix and the legal principles enunciated therein bear no direct relevance to the present case. In Muhammad Zaman\'s case, the State Bank of Pakistan Act, 1956 (\"Act of 1956\") originally mandated, through Section 54, that the Central Board of Directors could frame regulations only with the prior approval of the Federal Government. However, this requirement was subsequently omitted through the State Bank of Pakistan (Amendment) Act, 1994 (\"Act of 1994\". Consequently, following the enactment of the Act of 1994, the rules framed by the Central Board of the State Bank of Pakistan no longer required the Federal Government\'s prior approval. In light of this legislative change, the Apex Court held that rules framed by the Central Board post-1994 were non-statutory. Thus, the Muhammad Zaman case hinged on a specific legislative amendment that removed the requirement for Federal Government approval, thereby altering the legal character of the rules. Since no such analogous amendment exists in the present matter, reliance on Muhammad Zaman\'s precedent is misplaced and legally inapplicable.
In the present case, no amendment has been introduced in the Ordinance of 1949. Likewise, the legal principles established by the Lahore High Court in Umar Asghar Quresh\'s case and by the Islamabad High Court in W.P. No. 3008/2021 are clearly distinguishable and bear no direct relevance to the present controversy. The core issue before the Lahore High Court and the Islamabad High Court was the status of the Rules of 2021, not their vires. Both Hon\'ble Courts held that the Rules of 2021 were non-statutory, and we respectfully concur with this observation to that extent. However, the present case concerns the vires of the Rules of 2021, and for the reasons elaborated above, we hold that the Rules of 2021 were framed without lawful authority by the NBP, rendering them legally untenable.

case_info: W.P No. 580-D of 2024 Illaudin  Vs  The State and others
remarks:
Petitioner Illaudin alleged illegal raid and detention by police personnel of Sarwakai Police Station, South Waziristan.
Detainees were allegedly tortured and deprived of legal representation.
Held:
The impugned order was set aside.
Directed immediate registration of a criminal case against the involved police officials.
Ordered forwarding of the bailiff’s report for legal action under Section 195 Cr.P.C.
(Writ petition was allowed).

case_info: RFA No. 204-P/2021 Shakeel Khan etc  Vs  LAC Mardan etc
