case_info: Tax Reference No. 22-P of 2013 Commissioner RTO Peshawar  Vs  Swat Ceramics Company (Pvt) Ltd, Shaidu Nowshera
remarks:
Surplus accruing to a member`s club from amounts received from its members in respect of activities/services provided to them could not be considered to be income as profit of the said Club.
Due to doctrine of mutuality, no one could make a profit out of oneself.

case_info: W.P No. 3730-P of 2022 with IR Sahibzada Muhammad Arif  Vs  Superintendent Engineer Irrigation Mardan and others
remarks:
(a)	The case concerns the removal of encroachment under the Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Public Property (Removal of Encroachment) Act, 1977. The law allows only a properly appointed \"authorized officer\" of the Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Government to issue notices for removal of encroachment on public property owned or controlled by the provincial government where is in the case in hand, the notice issued to the petitioner was found to be invalid because the property in question (Khara No. 1014) actually belongs to the Railway Department (Federal Government), not the Irrigation Department (Provincial Government). Therefore, the respondent department had no legal authority to issue the notice. 
(b)	The jurisdiction of the High Court under Article 199 of the Constitution of Pakistan is examined in relation to interfering with the order and judgment passed by the learned Tribunal. The writ of certiorari, as stipulated in Article 199(1)(a)(ii), empowers the High Court to correct errors committed by inferior courts or tribunals and annul acts or proceedings taken without lawful authority. 
C)	The High Court\'s power to issue a writ of certiorari is supervisory, not appellate, and is exercised to correct errors of jurisdiction, illegality, or violation of natural justice. The Court may intervene if there is an error apparent on the face of the record or if the tribunal has acted without lawful authority. 
(d)	However, the High Court\'s interference is limited to ensuring that the tribunal has acted in accordance with the law. If the tribunal\'s decision is lawful, the High Court will not substitute its own findings. The High Court\'s jurisdiction is confined to examining the legality and validity of the tribunal\'s decision, rather than re-evaluating the facts or law.

case_info: W.P No. 649-D of 2022 Muhammad Aslam Baloch  Vs  Govt: of KPK and others
remarks:
Held: (1) Earlier the name of the petitioner entered in Schedule IV vide notification, dated 17.11.2003. The comments furnished by the respondents in the instant as well as in the earlier petition, are substantially the same. The name of the petitioner is entered in Schedule-IV in a mechanical fashion. Therefore, a review under sub-section (3) and/or section (3B), ATA, would not be an efficacious remedy to the petitioner.
(2) The respondent-authority failed to substantiate the reasonable grounds that the petitioner is concerned in terrorism; associated with any proscribed organization; or suspected to be involved in terrorism or sectarianism.
(Writ petition was Allowed).

case_info: C.R No. 1057-P of 2024 Pakhtunkhwa Highway Authority through its Managing Director, Peshawar  Vs  Arshad Sher Ali and 1 another
remarks: Even, if the defendant are placed ex-parte, the Court can still demand sufficient evidence from the plaintiff to prove the claim. This is important for ensuring the judgment is based on solid proof.

case_info: C.R No. 86-B of 2023  Saif Ullah Khan through LRs  Vs  Joint Director, SIDB, Peshawar
remarks:
Revision Petition.
Held:
A lease deed in respect of an allotted plot cannot be unilaterally cancelled merely through an
office order issued by the issuing authority/Lessor. Such cancellation can only be effected in
accordance with the procedure prescribed under section 39 of the Specific Relief Act, 1877.
(Revision Petition allowed).

case_info: W.P No. 6552-P of 2024  & others CRs Rafi Ullah and othrs  Vs  Liaqat and others
remarks: Provisions of Order VIII, Rule 9 CPC reflects that it enables the Court to allow the plaintiff to file rejoinder in response of the written statement submitted by the defendants, but subject to certain conditions and circumstances. Under the circumstances, the writ petition is allowed and petitioners are permitted to file rejoinder to clarify, explain or deny the defences taken by the respondent in the written statement.

case_info: C.R No. 830-P of 2011 Tilawat Shah  Vs  Bahadar Khan and others
remarks: Non-framing of issues on the plea of the petitioner-defendant and its effect. A perusal of the impugned judgment of the learned lower for a clearly demonstrate that parties were aware thereabout and adduced evidence in that behalf and also made their submissions in relation thereto but did not raise objection over it. He is not permitted to raise this objection at revisional stage, hence revision petition is dismissed in the circumstances.

case_info: Cr.A No. 87-M of 2024 Shah Zamin  Vs  The State and another
remarks:
Though unimpeached ocular account, even from an interested party, does not require corroboration, however, the superior courts often advocate for independent corroboration of interested and related witnesses, particularly in cases involving capital punishment.
2. Timely report of the crime excludes the possibilities for collusion or fabrication of a false narrative.
3. The combination of coherent testimony of the eyewitness duly corroborated by circumstantial evidence and the prompt reporting of the incident all serve to highlight the reliability of the complainant`s narrative, lending substantial weight to the prosecution`s case.
4. The factors that the witness is interested being victim of the same occurrence and related to deceased being his son, coupled with the motive of land dispute between the parties would not render his testimony unreliable when the intrinsic worth of his statement has remained intact.
5. In view of the gravity of the situation and the immediate threat to the life of injured complainant, it is unreasonable to expect him to have acted in a manner that would involve resisting the fire or attempting to save his father. Such an expectation is out of line with the realistic reactions that individuals typically exhibit in life-threatening situations.
6. If the conduct of other eyewitnesses, who were not examined during the trial, is questionable or unusual, this does not diminish the veracity of the complainant’s statement who was examined as the sole eyewitness of the occurrence.
7. Even otherwise, when the deceased and his sons were unarmed at the time of occurrence while the appellant and absconding accused were having pistols, in such situation, non-intervening of the brothers of complainant or not catching the assailants armed with deadly weapons was normal conduct exhibited by them at the relevant time.
8. Since the other eyewitnesses were not called to testify, therefore, it is impossible to evaluate their conduct and ascertain whether it was usual or unusual. Therefore, applying the maxim ‘falsus in uno, falsus in omnibus’ in the current scenario of this case would be inappropriate. Even otherwise, the prevailing judicial thought indicates that the principle of ‘falsus in uno, falsus in omnibus’ does not rigidly apply within the framework of Pakistan’s criminal justice system instead, courts are required to sift grain from the chaff to reach at a just conclusion.
9. Every additional information provided by a witness relevant to understanding the context and circumstances leading up to the incident which were not mentioned by him in the FIR or his statement under Section 161, Cr.P.C. would not amount to dishonest improvement on his part.
10. The site plan is not classified as a substantive piece of evidence rather its primary role is to assist in visualizing the layout and events associated with the crime scene.
11. The objection that the non-production of some eyewitnesses may undermine the prosecution`s case, cannot be accepted as the legal precedents from the superior courts emphasize the importance of quality over quantity.
12. In a comparable situation involving the medical reports of two victims from the same incident, the interpretation of one report should be applied to analyze the medical report of the other victim.
13. Where ocular evidence is deemed trustworthy and compelling, it will typically take precedence over medical evidence.
14. The failure to conduct an internal postmortem is not fatal to the case of prosecution when there is trustworthy ocular account.
15. It is settled law that non recovery of any incriminating material from the spot would not render the prosecution case doubtful if it was otherwise proved.

case_info: W.P No. 272-B of 2024 Aziz-ur-Rehman  Vs  Anwar Hayat etc
remarks: Preliminary decree in suit of redemption of usufructuary  may, under clause (c ) (i) of Rule 7 (1) of order XXXIV, CPC , fixed a time for payment of the  amount declared due under the decree but default in making the payment of amount declared under the decree within the time fixed does not operate to debar the plaintiff/mortgagor from all rights to redeem the mortgaged property. If payment of the amount declared due under the preliminary decree is not made within time fixed by the court, then under clause ( c) (ii) of rule 7 a mortgagee/defendant is entitled to apply for a final decree in case of a mortgagor other than a sufructuary mortgage.

case_info: W.P No. 4601-P of 2024 with IR Arif Rauf  Vs  Government of KP through its Chief Secretary, Civil Secretariat, Peshawar and others
remarks:
It is by now the global idea that the public sector work such as water management, waste management, public services at the gross root level be governed through the formation of public sector companies. Not only the said companies would be independent in its work but would be more efficient, effective and accountable organizations that can deliver better services to citizens. The key principle behind the said idea is efficiency and effectiveness that the public sector companies can operate with more flexibility and autonomy, allowing them to respond quickly to changing circumstances and make decisions without being bogged down by bureaucratic processes; the said companies are to be managed by professionals with expertise in specific areas rather than general civil servants, enabling better decision making and more effective implementation of the policy of the government governing the management of public sector companies.
Therefore, once the public sector companies are established under the relevant legal dispensation with full autonomy then appointment of civil servant at the helms of affairs of such companies would run counter to the basic idea of creation of public sector companies.
Even otherwise, if it is assumed that the Government enjoys such powers, it is by now settled that such powers shall be exercised judiciously, fairly and to advance the purpose for which the discretion was conferred upon it. The Government in such like cases does not enjoy absolute discretion. Indeed, it is settled that when an authority has a discretion in relation to any matter then the said discretion is to be exercised according to rational reasons which means that;
(a) there be findings of primary facts based on good evidence; and (b) decision about facts be made for reasons which serve the purposes of statute in an intelligible and reasonable manner. Any action which do not meet these threshold requirements are considered arbitrary and misuse of power. 1
1 . Tariq Aziz-ud-Din and others (2011 PLC (CS) 1130)

case_info: Cr. Misc. BCA No. 25-B of 2024 Mst. Sabiha  Vs  Muhammad Asim alias Asim  etc.
remarks:
Bail Cancellation Applications
Held: The issuance of notice to the legal heirs of the deceased (in all cases involving qatl) and to the injured (in cases of hurt) is mandatory before a person accused of an offence referred to in sub-section (1) of section 497 Cr.P.C. is released on bail. In the instant case, the injured / victim was not arrayed as a respondent in the bail petition, nor was the victim issued a notice, let alone provided an opportunity of being heard, before the accused were released on bail. Therefore, the BCAs were allowed, and the bail granted to the accused was recalled.
(BCAs allowed)

case_info: Cr.Misc BA No. 645-B of 2024 Ilyas alias Patar  Vs  The State etc
remarks:
Bail Application
Held: A person accused of an offence punishable with death, who has been detained for a continuous period exceeding two years and whose trial has not concluded for no fault attributable to him, becomes entitled to be released on bail on statutory ground under clause (b) of the third proviso to section 497(1) Cr.P.C.
(Bail application allowed)

case_info: Cr.A No. 1531-P of 2023 Aurangzeb etc  Vs  The State etc.
remarks:
i.	The learned trial Court committed an illegality while convicting the appellants for the injuries caused to the injured who did not appear before the learned trial Court and that by doing so, the appellants have not been treated in accordance with law. As the injuries of the injured were attributed to specific accused, so under these circumstances the learned trial Court should have dealt with the matter in accordance with the allegations levelled and in accordance with the role played by each accused. It is settled that once an injured witness does not appear before the trial Court in support of his claim, then no conviction can be awarded for the injury caused to him.
ii.	We are conscious of the fact that the prosecution has the choice to produce the witnesses it likes, but at the same time when the best available witness is withheld, then an inference can be drawn that the witness, if produced, would not support the case. 
iii.	As the number of injuries do not commensurate with the number of accused and as the collected empties from the spot belies the stance of the complainant, so we are confident in holding that the number of accused has been exaggerated.
iv.	True that in counter version, the accused Bacha Said alias Usaid earned acquittal, but his acquittal under no circumstances would stop this Court from taking into consideration the circumstances of that particular case while appreciating the involvement or otherwise of the appellants of the present case. The learned trial Court was convinced with the acquittal of the accused charged in DD No. 30, so it focused mainly on the evidence collected against the appellants but we are afraid that the learned trial Court failed to appreciate the most crucial and the most essential aspects of the case. When the place of occurrence is the same, when people died from both the sides and when the motive was common to the parties, then the counter version can be and should have been taken into consideration. It was the uncertainty of events which led to the tragic incident and it was the concealment of facts which overshadowed the prosecution case, so if the accused could earn benefit of the same in DD No. 30, then the same benefit should have been extended to the appellants charged in the instant case.

case_info: C.R No. 280-A of 2012 Muhammad Aslam  Vs  Amir Mehmood Kiyani
remarks:
Petitioner filed two different suits for pre-emption in respect of two separate mutations. The respondents in both the suits appeared and filed application for rejection of plaint on the ground that the petitioner has failed to mention date of issuance of notice Talab-e-Ishhad in plaint. The learned trial Court accepted the application and rejected the plaint. Appeal filed by the petitioner was also dismissed on the ground that non-mentioning the date of issuance of notice Talab-e-Ishhad in the plaint was fatal.
Held;
	No doubt the right of pre-emption is a feeble right and slight omission in the plaint would be fatal for the plaintiff, because the law of pre-emption is a special statute and once a claim under such law is brought, the plaintiff would be precluded to further add or subtract in his plaint by seeking an amendment, particularly, when such an amendment is aimed at curing some significant omission in the plaint, therefore, the Courts below have rightly refused the petitioner to amend his plaint.
		Order-VII Rule 14 of the Civil Procedure Code, 1908 provides that when a suit is brought on the basis of some document, which is in possession or power of the plaintiff, the same should be presented to the Court along with plaint and when a document is relied as an evidence in support of certain claim, such document should be entered in a list to be added or annexed to the plaint.
	The notice of Talab-e-Ishhad is essentially a document, which is relied upon by the plaintiff during evidence in support of his claim of pre-emption and therefore, annexing its copy with the plaint would be sufficient for the plaintiff, enabling him to rely upon the same in support of his exercise of right of pre-emption.	
	Revision Petition allowed.

case_info: Cr.A No. 1400-P of 2023 Syed Nabi Shah  Vs  The State etc
remarks:
i.	Mere stamp of injuries would hardly qualify for the conviction, of an accused, as by doing so, the Courts of law would deviate from the set pattern, which in turn would lead to unfavorable results, not for the appellant, but for the prosecution as well. The stamp of injuries may indicate the presence of the injured on the spot, but that would hardly qualify a witness as a truthful witness and would hardly convince that what the witness spoke, was the whole truth.
ii.	The introduction of this witness was to fill up the lacuna, but he failed to fetch the desired results.
iii.	Right from the beginning till the end, the prosecution witnesses fell in dishonest improvements, trying to make its case a success, but instead they damaged the case beyond repair. Neither the complainant could tell that who prepared the site plan, on whose pointation nor the Investigating officer. It was the inconsistency between these witnesses which showered mist over the place of occurrence and even the witnesses could not confirm the venue, where the unfortunate incident occurred. 
iv.	Once it is disputed that on whose pointation the site plan was prepared and once the witness was left unexamined, then the site plan as a piece of evidence can hardly be taken into consideration.
v.	If such statement is taken into consideration, then this Court is to see that why the human hair found in possession of the deceased, collected by the doctor, handed over to the Investigating officer was not sent for DNA, knowing the fact that it was the best evidence, in possession of the prosecution, for establishing the identity of the accused. The reluctance on the part of the Investigating officer to transmit the same to the laboratory for DNA is a factor which this Court cannot ignore and in our understanding, the Investigating officer did not send the same apprehending that the same would exclude the involvement of the appellant and that his miseries, to investigate further, would increase.
vi.	Forensic science assists the law enforcement by providing accurate, objective analysis of criminal evidence. It helps to identify the culprit, investigate the duration and understanding the nature of the crime, forensic research scientists can aid investigators in learning how a crime was committed, including what tools were used, when the crime occurred and where it occurred. DNA evidence has revolutionized the field of forensic science, playing a crucial role in solving homicide cases. By analyzing genetic material found and crime scenes, investigators can identify suspects with a high degree of certainty, but it requires the strongest will and the desire to bring the actual culprits to books.
vii.	Here it lies with the prosecution to charge and to prove. We noticed that the prosecution charge hastily but it was lacking the courage to investigate diligently. We do not blame the prosecution, as no efforts were made to streamline the way the investigation is made and to encourage the collection of credible evidence which in turn would decide the fate of all concerned. Here much is expected from the Courts and less from the law enforcement agencies instead, it is the law enforcement agency which is to collect more and the courts would then appreciate the collected evidence.

case_info: Cr.R No. 311-P of 2024 Meeran shah  Vs  state
remarks: Provisions of Articles 133, 150 and 151 of the Qanun-e-Shahadat, 1984 has been discussed.

case_info: C.R No. 345-P of 2019 Muhammad Rafiq  Vs  Sultan Mahmood
remarks:
Question of limitation, estoppel, waivers and; acquiescence has been discussed.
Predecessor of the petitioner was well aware of the attestation of impugned inheritance mutation way back in the year 1925, however, she kept mum for long 83 years and; then, filed the instant suit in the year 2007, though in support of her stance, she also appeared before the court and; recorded her statement as PW-3, however, during cross examination, she has categorically admitted that the suit has been filed as the respondents are not paying due respect to her. It is worth mentioning that initially the suit was decreed, however, upon the appeal of the respondents, the matter was remanded to the learned Trial Court, where Muhammad Rafique, attorney of the petitioners appeared before the court as ARPW-1, who has clearly admitted during cross examination that the disputed property was privately partitioned amongst their maternal uncles, which clearly shows that they were not only in knowledge of the attestation of impugned mutation of the year 1925, but they were also aware of private partition between their maternal uncles, however, despite that their predecessor did not approach the court of competent jurisdiction for her rights in the disputed property within the prescribed period of limitation, as such, suit of the petitioner was not only barred by limitation but the same was also hit by the principle of estoppel and; acquiescence.
Akhtar Nashir Ahmad vs. Province of Punjab through District Collector, Gujrat and others [PLD 2024 Supreme Court 1268], Salamat Ali & others vs. Muhammad Din & others [PLD 2022 SC 353] and Mst. Grana through LRs Vs. Sahib Kamala Bibi & others [PLD 2014 SC 167].

case_info: C.R No. 411-P of 2020 Haji Zubair Ahmad & others  Vs  Project Director, KDA, Kohat & others
remarks: Plaint cannot be verified by a counsel alone, in cases of suits filed by corporation, but such a defect may subsequently be allowed to be remedied.

case_info: Tax Reference No. 86-P of 2024 Commissioner of Inland Revenue Peshawar Zone  Vs  Ishtiaq Hussain
remarks:
(a)	Section 126A as well as section 133 of the Income Tax Ordinance, 2001 were found having retrospective effect t to the extent as expressly provided in the amended sections of law.
(b)	Where legislature gives retrospective effect to provisions of an amending act by express words or necessary intendment then the principle enshrined in section 6 of the General Clauses Act does not apply and express intention of the legislature in new enactment has to be given effect.
